If you left out some of the revelation out of fear of their proposing signs to you, then you are only a warner, and you have nothing but what Allah wills. And if they say he fabricated it, then tell them to bring ten surahs like it, fabricated..." etc.

From what has passed, it becomes clear that presenting the discourse in the form of expectation and possibility is to observe what the station requires of the nature of far-fetchedness. The station is one of far-fetchedness, and its requirement is to mention every cause potentially influential in the far-fetched event. Consider this in the case of a king who is informed of the rebellion of some of his weak subjects; he sends one of his agents to call them to hearing and obedience, and writes a letter regarding that, ordering him to read it to them and blaming them for their rebellion and arrogance despite their weakness and humiliation, and despite their master's power, might, and glory. Then the king is informed that they rejected what reached them from him through his messenger. He writes to him a second letter, ordering him to read it to them, and in it: "Perhaps you did not read my letter to them for fear that they would propose to you what you are unable to do, or they claimed that the letter is not from me and that you only fabricated it against me. If it is the first, then you are a messenger whose only duty is the conveyance; and if it is the second, then the letter is in my handwriting, written by my hand and sealed with my seal, and no one other than me can imitate me in that."

Contemplation of this example shows that the station in what the second letter contains of address is a station of far-fetchedness, and that the intent of mentioning the two possibilities—leaving the conveyance and the claim of fabrication—is not to truly rebuke the messenger or to truly consider the claim of lying and fabrication. Rather, the two aspects were mentioned for the purpose of being like a prelude to mentioning what removes the two doubts: namely, that the messenger has no part in the matter such that he could be proposed to with what they propose, and that the letter of the king is beyond doubt or suspicion.

From here, it becomes clear that His saying, the Exalted: «فَلَعَلَّكَ تارِكٌ بَعْضَ ما يُوحى إِلَيْكَ وَضائِقٌ بِهِ صَدْرُكَ» (So maybe you are leaving out some of what is revealed to you, and your breast is straitened by it) etc., does not imply a serious expectation, nor is it directed at rebuking the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), nor is it intended for his consolation and the soothing of his soul following the grief and sorrow that used to reach him due to their disbelief and denial of the explicit truth he brought. Rather, the discourse is directed to lead to the mention of His saying: «إِنَّما أَنْتَ نَذِيرٌ وَاللهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَكِيلٌ» (You are only a warner, and Allah is guardian over all things).

Thus, what some commentators mentioned—that the discourse is arranged to forbid the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from grief and straitening of the breast due to the disbelief and denial they confronted him with, and that the prohibition is one of consolation and soothing of the soul, similar to what is in His saying: «وَلا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلا تَكُ فِي ضَيْقٍ مِمَّا يَمْكُرُونَ» (Do not grieve for them, and do not be upset by their plotting) [An-Nahl: 127], and His saying: «لَعَلَّكَ باخِعٌ نَفْسَكَ أَلَّا يَكُونُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ إِنْ نَشَأْ نُنَزِّلْ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ آيَةً فَظَلَّتْ أَعْناقُهُمْ لَها خاضِعِينَ» (You might kill yourself [with grief] that they will not have faith. If We wish, We will send down to them a sign from the sky before which their heads will remain bowed in humility) [Ash-Shu'ara: 3-4]—is a discourse that is out of place.

It also appears that His saying: «فَلَعَلَّكَ تارِكٌ» (So maybe you are leaving out) etc., and His saying: «أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ» (Or do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’) etc., are like two sides of a dilemma and both connect to what preceded them from a single perspective, as we mentioned.

And His saying: «تارِكٌ بَعْضَ ما يُوحى إِلَيْكَ» (leaving out some of what is revealed to you)—"some" was mentioned because the previous verses included the conveyance of the revelation in general. That is: perhaps you left out some of what We revealed to you of the Quran and did not recite it to them, so the truth was not fully disclosed to them such that they would not confront you with what they confronted you with of rejection and denial. This is because the Quran, some of it explains others, and one part of it brings another part closer to acceptance, such as the verses of argumentation (الاحتجاج) explaining the verses containing claims, and the verses of reward and punishment bringing the truth closer to acceptance through enticement and intimidation, and the verses of stories and lessons (القصص والعبر) winning over souls and softening hearts.

And His saying: «وَضائِقٌ بِهِ صَدْرُكَ أَنْ يَقُولُوا» (and your breast is straitened by it, that they should say) etc. It is said in al-Majma‘: "Dā’iq and dayyiq (straitened) have the same meaning, except that dā’iq here is better for two reasons: one, that it is transitory (عارض), and the other, that it matches the form of tārik (leaving out)." End quote.

The apparent meaning is that the pronoun in «بِهِ» (by it) refers to His saying: «بَعْضَ ما يُوحى» (some of what is revealed), although some mentioned that the pronoun refers to their saying: «لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ كَنْزٌ» (Why has a treasure not been sent down to him) etc., or to their proposal. This latter view is more consistent with His saying «أَنْ يَقُولُوا» (that they should say) etc., being a substitute (بدلا) for the pronoun in «بِهِ». What we mentioned is more consistent with it being an object of purpose (مفعولا له) for His saying: «تارِكٌ» (leaving out), and the estimation is: "Perhaps you are leaving that out for fear that they should say: 'Why has a treasure not been sent down to him, or an angel come with him?'"

And His saying: «إِنَّما أَنْتَ نَذِيرٌ» (You are only a warner) is a response to their proposal in their saying: «لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ كَنْزٌ أَوْ جاءَ مَعَهُ مَلَكٌ» (Why has a treasure not been sent down to him, or an angel come with him?). The mention of what they proposed has been repeated in various places of His speech, the Exalted; in some, it was limited to the mention of the coming of an angel, and in others, more was added, such as the proposal to bring Allah, the Glorified, to testify to the message, and that he should have a garden from which he eats, and that he should bring down from the heaven a book they could read. Allah, the Glorified, responded to all of them with the like of what He responded with here: namely, that His messenger has nothing but the message; it is not in his hand, being a human messenger, to respond to what they propose to him unless Allah wills something in that and permits the bringing of a sign, as He said: «وَما كانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ بِآيَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ» (It is not for any apostle to bring a sign except by Allah’s permission) [Al-Mu'min: 78].

Then He followed His saying: «إِنَّما أَنْتَ نَذِيرٌ» (You are only a warner) with His saying: «وَاللهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَكِيلٌ» (and Allah is guardian over all things) to complete the response to their proposal of miracles to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Its summary is: the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is a human like them and was only commanded with warning, which is the message by informing of the danger. The undertaking of all matters and their governance (تدبير), whether they run according to custom or are miraculous (خارقة لها), belongs only to Allah, the Glorified; so there is no reason for them to turn to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding what is not his.

This is because Allah, the Glorified, is the Originator of all things and their Creator, and He is the Sustainer of everything in the system that runs upon it. There is nothing except that He, the Exalted, is the beginning in its affair and state, and the end, whether the matters run according to custom or are miraculous. He, the Exalted, is the one to whom its affair is surrendered and who governs its state; thus, He, the Exalted, is the Guardian (الوكيل) over it. For the guardian is the one to whom the matter is surrendered and whose judgment is executed within it. Thus, He, the Exalted, is a Guardian over all things.

By this, it becomes clear that His saying: «وَاللهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَكِيلٌ» (and Allah is guardian over all things), with the aid of His saying: «إِنَّما أَنْتَ نَذِيرٌ» (You are only a warner), implies a restriction of the heart (قصر القلب); for they asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for a matter that was not his, but rather belonged only to Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: «أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ» (Or do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’ Say, ‘Then bring ten surahs’). Discourse has already passed by which it is correct to take "am" (أَمْ) as connected (متصلة), because His saying: «فَلَعَلَّكَ تارِكٌ» (So maybe you are leaving out) etc., is in the sense of an interrogation. The estimation is: "Are you leaving out some of what is revealed to you for fear of their proposing a miracle, or do they say that you fabricated it against Us?" For it is far-fetched that My speech be read to them then they do not believe in it. It has also been said: "am" is disjunctive (مقطعة), and the meaning is: "Rather, they say he fabricated it."

And His saying: «قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَياتٍ» (Say, ‘Then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated’)—in the speech is an explicit challenge (تحد). The pronoun refers to the Quran, or to the Surah insofar as it is Quran. The "fa" in «فَأْتُوا» (Then bring) indicates the branching of the command upon His saying: «افْتَراهُ» (He has fabricated it). In the speech, there is an omission and a connection for the sake of brevity. The estimation is: "Tell them: If this Quran is among what I fabricated against Allah, it was from me, and it would be possible for someone other than me to bring its like. If you are truthful in your claim and serious, not joking, then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated, and seek help in that by calling everyone you can besides Allah—from your idols whom you claim are gods you rush to in needs, and others from the rest of creation—until all causes and means are complete for you, and no one remains whose help might be expected to be effective and whose benefit is hoped for in that. If it were from me and not from Allah, it would then be possible for you to bring its like."

It has become clear by this explanation that the challenge with the Quran in the noble verse is not only from the perspective of its composition and eloquence. For He, the Exalted, orders them to seek help from everyone they can call besides Allah, whether their gods or others, and among them are those who do not know the Arabic language or the excellence of its composition and the quality of its eloquence. Thus, the challenge is general to all that the Noble Quran contains of true knowledge, shining proofs and evidences, good exhortations, noble ethics, divine laws, news of the Unseen, and eloquence and rhetoric, similar to what is in His saying, the Exalted: «قُلْ لَئِنِ اجْتَمَعَتِ الْإِنْسُ وَالْجِنُّ عَلى أَنْ يَأْتُوا بِمِثْلِ هذَا الْقُرْآنِ لا يَأْتُونَ بِمِثْلِهِ وَلَوْ كانَ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ ظَهِيراً» (Say, ‘Should all humans and jinn rally to bring the like of this Quran, they will not bring its like, even if they assisted one another’) [Al-Isra: 88]. An indication of this has already passed in the discussion on the inimitability (إعجاز) of the Quran in the first volume of the book.

By this, the falsity of the claim that the aspect of the Quran's inimitability is only eloquence and rhetoric in this specific composition becomes clear. For if the aspect of inimitability were other than that, He would not have been satisfied in the counter-challenge with fabrication and invention, because eloquence has three levels: the highest level is inimitable, and the lowest and middle are possible. Thus, the challenge in the verse only occurred in the highest level of it. If the aspect of inimitability were "turning away" (الصرفة) — the theory that Allah prevented the Arabs from producing the like of the Quran despite their ability — then feeble speech would be more eloquent in the chapter of inimitability.

The "like" (المثل) mentioned in the verse cannot mean its like in genus (الجنس), because its like in genus would be its narration (حكايته), and the challenge would not occur by it. Rather, it refers to what is customary among the Arabs in challenging one another, as is famous from the poetic contests (مناقضات) of Imru' al-Qays, 'Alqamah, 'Amr ibn Kulthum, al-Harith ibn Hillizah, Jarir, al-Farazdaq, and others. End quote.

For in this claim, firstly: if the aspect of inimitability in the Quran were its eloquence alone—which is a matter unknown to non-Arabs—then including others in the challenge would have no meaning. His saying: «وَادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ» (and call everyone you can besides Allah), with the generality it contains, and likewise His saying: «لَئِنِ اجْتَمَعَتِ الْإِنْسُ وَالْجِنُ» (Should all humans and jinn rally...) etc., would not lead to a coherent meaning. Rather, it would have been necessary to say: "Should all the Arabs rally," and "call everyone you can from your gods and from the people of your language."

Secondly: if the aspect of inimitability were eloquence only, the argumentation with the like of His saying: «وَلَوْ كانَ مِنْ عِنْدِ غَيْرِ اللهِ لَوَجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلافاً كَثِيراً» (Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would surely have found much discrepancy in it) [An-Nisa: 82] would not be correct. This verse is apparent in negating absolute discrepancy, whereas most discrepancies—those referring to meanings—do not harm the eloquence of the wording.

Thirdly: He, the Exalted, challenges with the like of His saying: «فَلْيَأْتُوا بِحَدِيثٍ مِثْلِهِ» (let them bring a discourse like it) [At-Tur: 34], and with His saying in Surah Yunus: «فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ وَادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ» (then bring a surah like it, and call everyone you can besides Allah) [Yunus: 38]. We have already learned that Surah Yunus was before Surah Hud in the order of revelation, and this is supported by tradition. Then He challenged in this Surah with: «فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَياتٍ وَادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ» (then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated, and call everyone you can besides Allah). If the aspect of inimitability were eloquence specifically, these challenges would be outside the natural order. For it would not be correct to charge the eloquent ones among the Arabs who deny the Quran being from Allah with bringing one surah like it, then after that with bringing ten fabricated surahs. Rather, nature requires that the challenge be by charging them with bringing the like of the entire Quran; then if they fail, with bringing ten surahs like it, fabricated; then if they fail, with bringing one surah like it.

Some have mentioned, in escaping this problem, that the order between the surahs and the revelation of some before others does not necessitate the order between the verses of the surahs. For many a Meccan verse is placed in a Medinan surah and vice versa. It is possible then that the verses of challenge with the entire Quran were revealed before others absolutely, then the verse of challenge with ten fabricated surahs was revealed after it, and the verse of challenge with one surah was revealed after all of them.

However, this only helps if the revelation of the verses occurred as he imagined; otherwise, the problem remains. The truth is that the Quran is inimitable in all its specific attributes: in its eloquence and rhetoric, in the true knowledge it contains, in its noble ethics, divine laws, stories and lessons, news of the Unseen, and in its authority over hearts and the beauty that rules over souls.

As for the reason for the challenge with ten surahs despite the challenge with one in Surah Yunus, it was said in al-Majma‘: "If it is asked: Why was the challenge mentioned once with ten surahs, once with one surah, and once with a discourse like it? The answer is: The challenge only occurs with what manifests inimitability in composed speech; so it is permissible to challenge once with the less and once with the more." End quote.

I say: This serves as a reason for the principle of challenging with one or many. But as for the challenge with ten after one—especially according to his view that its inimitability is by eloquence alone—it does not.

Some commentators have suggested, in justifying this, that the Quran is a miracle in everything it contains—including its knowledge (المعارف), ethics (الأخلاق), laws (الأحكام), stories (القصص), and other matters—and it is characterized by eloquence (الفصاحة) and rhetoric (البلاغة) and the absence of contradiction (انتفاء الاختلاف). The validity of a challenge (المعارضة) and the production of something like it (الإتيان بالمثل) only becomes apparent when a number of surahs are produced through which the absence of contradiction is demonstrated—especially among the stories contained therein—alongside other aspects such as eloquence, rhetoric, knowledge, and so forth.

This can only be achieved by producing the likes of the long surahs that encompass all the aforementioned affairs and include knowledge, stories, arguments, and other elements, such as the surahs of al-A‘raf and al-An‘am.

The long Quranic surahs that were revealed before Surah Hud—according to what has been mentioned in the narrations—and which encompass all the aforementioned arts, are: al-A‘raf, Yunus, Maryam, Ta-Ha, al-Shu‘ara’, al-Naml, al-Qasas, al-Qamar, and Sad. These are nine surahs, the tenth being Surah Hud. This perspective explains the challenge by their being commanded to bring ten surahs like it, forged. (End of the summary of his words, though he spoke at great length).

I say: Firstly, there is no reliance to be placed on the report (الأثر) he relied upon regarding the order of the revelation of the surahs; it is among the solitary reports (الآحاد) that are not free from weakness, and it is not appropriate to build exegetical research (البحث التفسيري) upon the likes of them.

Secondly, the apparent meaning of His saying: أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَياتٍ "Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’ Say, ‘Then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated’" (11:13) is that their accusing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of fabrication (الافتراء) against Allah, the Glorified, was a claim they made regarding all the Quranic surahs—both the long and the short—without specifying one surah over another. Therefore, it was necessary that they be answered in a way that uproots the doubt regarding every Quranic surah, and the challenge (التحدي) must be sufficient for that. Their inability to bring ten long, forged surahs that combine the Quranic arts does not prove that everything—even the short surahs like al-Kawthar and al-‘Asr—is from Allah, unless another explanation is joined to it, and the wording is devoid of that.

Thirdly, regarding His saying: بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ "ten surahs like it," if the pronoun therein refers to the Quran—as is the apparent meaning of this speaker's words—it would imply a challenge to bring ten forged surahs like it absolutely, whether long or short. Thus, restricting the challenge to ten long, comprehensive surahs is a restriction (تقييد) of the verse's wording without a restrictor (مقيد), which is arbitrary (تحكم). Even more arbitrary is the claim that "like it" refers to the likeness of the ten surahs he enumerated.

If the pronoun refers to Surah Hud, it would be a preposterous claim. How could it be correct to say to someone who claims that Surah al-Kawthar and the Mu‘awwidhatayn are fabrications against Allah: "Bring ten forged surahs like Surah Hud," and stop at that? Unless they were to babble that Surah Hud alone was a fabrication against Allah, the Exalted, in which case they would be challenged to bring its like; however, we have not heard of anyone among them uttering such a thing.

It can be said regarding the variation that appears in the verses of challenge—such as His saying: فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ "then bring a surah like it" (10:38), which is apparent in challenging with a single surah; and His saying: فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَياتٍ "then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated" (11:13), which is apparent in challenging with a specific number above one; and His saying: فَلْيَأْتُوا بِحَدِيثٍ مِثْلِهِ "let them bring a discourse like it" (52:34), which is apparent in challenging with a discourse (حديث) that resembles the Quran even if it is less than a surah—that each of these verses aims at a specific purpose in the challenge.

To explain this: the aspects and affairs of the Quran that constitute its reality as a Divine Book—in addition to the eloquence (الفصاحة) of its wording and the rhetoric (البلاغة) of its composition—ultimately return to its meanings (معانيه) and objectives (مقاصده). I do not mean by "meaning" (المعنى) what the scholars of rhetoric intend when they say: "Rhetoric is an attribute of the meaning, while the words are merely cast on the path," by which they mean concepts (المفاهيم) in terms of their natural order in the mind. For what they mean by "meaning" exists in speech that is an outright lie, in jest, in obscenity, in lampooning, and in slander, if it follows the style of rhetoric; and much of this is found in the speech inherited from the eloquent ones in both poetry and prose.

Rather, what is meant by the meaning and objective of the Quran is what He, the Exalted, describes as being a Wise Book (كتاب حكيم), a Manifest Light (نور مبين), a Grand Quran (قرآن عظيم), a Criterion (الفرقان) — the decisive standard that distinguishes truth from falsehood —, a Guide (هاد) that leads to the truth and to a straight path (صراط مستقيم), a Decisive Word (قول فصل) and not a jest, a Mighty Book (كتاب عزيز) that falsehood cannot approach from before it nor from behind it, a Reminder (ذكر), that it judges between people in that which they differ, that it is a healing (شفاء) and a mercy (رحمة) for the believers and increases the wrongdoers (الظالمين) in naught but loss, that it is an exposition (تبيان) of all things, and that none touch it except the purified (المطهرون).

It is evident that all of these are attributes of the meaning (معنى) of the Quran. They are not attributes of what the scholars of rhetoric intend by "eloquent meaning," which might be contained in false speech—which the Quran calls vain talk (لغو), and sin (إثم), and forbids man from engaging in or uttering, even if it be eloquent. Rather, the meaning characterized by these attributes is part of the Divine objectives (المقاصد الإلهية) that proceed upon the truth (الحق) which is unmixed with falsehood, and lie upon the path of guidance (الهداية). Speech containing a meaning of this description and a purpose of this stature is that which the Divine care (العناية الإلهية) is concerned with revealing and making a mercy for the believers and a reminder for all the worlds (العالمين).

This is what is correct to challenge with, as in His saying: فَلْيَأْتُوا بِحَدِيثٍ مِثْلِهِ "let them bring a discourse like it." For we do not call speech a "discourse" (حديث) unless it contains an important purpose that is discussed and transmitted from one mind to another. Likewise, His saying: فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ "then bring a surah like it," for Allah does not call a group of verses of His Book a "surah" (سورة)—even if they are numerous—unless they contain a Divine purpose (غرض إلهي) by which they are distinguished from others.

Were it not for this, the challenge with the Quranic verses would not be complete, and the opponent would have been able to choose a large number of individual verses, such as His saying: وَالضُّحى "By the morning brightness" (93:1), وَالْعَصْرِ "By time" (103:1), وَالطُّورِ "By the Mount" (52:1), فِي كِتابٍ مَكْنُونٍ "in a guarded Book" (56:78), مُدْهامَّتانِ "both of them dark green" (55:64), الْحَاقَّةُ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ وَما أَدْراكَ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ "The Besieger! What is the Besieger? And what will show you what the Besieger is?" (69:1-3), الرَّحْمنُ "The All-beneficent" (55:1), مَلِكِ النَّاسِ إِلهِ النَّاسِ "the King of mankind, the God of mankind" (114:2-3), وَخَسَفَ الْقَمَرُ "and the moon is eclipsed" (75:8), كَلَّا وَالْقَمَرِ "No indeed! By the moon" (74:32), سَنَدْعُ الزَّبانِيَةَ "We shall call the keepers of hell" (96:18), and other individual verses, and then match each of them with corresponding Arabic speech, without ensuring a connection between them or their containment of a purpose that unites them and presents them in a form of unity (الوحدة).

Thus, what the opponent was tasked with in these challenges was to bring speech that resembles the Quran, in addition to the rhetoric of its wording, in explaining some of the Divine objectives (المقاصد الإلهية) containing purposes characterized by the attributes mentioned by Allah, the Glorified.

The Divine Speech (الكلام الإلهي), along with what it challenged with in the verses of challenge, varies according to what appears of its specific properties (خاصته). The entirety of the Quran is distinguished by being a Book containing what the human species needs until the Day of Resurrection—in terms of fundamental knowledge (معارف أصلية), noble ethics (أخلاق كريمة), and subsidiary laws (أحكام فرعية). A surah of the Quran is distinguished by a comprehensive explanation of one of the Divine purposes related to guidance (الهدى) and the religion of truth (دين الحق), alongside its miraculous rhetoric (بلاغتها الخارقة); and this is a property different from the property that characterizes the Quran as a whole. A number of surahs, such as ten or twenty, are distinguished by another property: the exposition of various types of objectives and purposes and the diversity (التنوع) within them. This is further removed from the possibility of coincidence (الاتفاق). For if the opponent is unable to bring a single surah, it might occur to his mind that his inability only indicates the inability of people to bring its like, not that it is revealed from Allah, inspired by His knowledge. It might be thought to be like other human attributes and actions, in each of which it is possible for an individual to be unique among the members of the species by chance (اتفاقاً) due to the coincidence of necessitating causes—like a human individual described as being the tallest, or having the largest body, or being the bravest, most generous, most cowardly, or most miserly.

This possibility—even if it is refuted regarding a single surah of the Quran that the opponent intends to challenge—for it is eloquent speech containing true meanings with noble attributes, free from the material of falsehood, and such a thing does not occur by mere chance and accident (الصدفة) without being intended in itself with a purpose related to the will (الإرادة)—

However, the aforementioned possibility of chance and accident is even further removed regarding multiple surahs. For bringing surah after surah, explaining purpose after purpose, and uncovering one hidden truth after another, leaves no room for the possibility of chance and accident; this is obvious.

When what we have mentioned becomes clear, it becomes evident that it is possible for the challenge in His saying: قُلْ لَئِنِ اجْتَمَعَتِ الْإِنْسُ وَالْجِنُّ عَلى أَنْ يَأْتُوا بِمِثْلِ هذَا الْقُرْآنِ لا يَأْتُونَ بِمِثْلِهِ وَلَوْ كانَ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ ظَهِيراً "Say, ‘Should all humans and jinn rally to bring the like of this Quran, they will not bring its like, even if they assisted one another’" (17:88) to be a challenge regarding the entire Quran for all the Divine purposes it has gathered, and it is distinguished by being a compendium of everything people need until the Day of Resurrection. And His saying: قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ "Say, ‘Then bring a surah like it’" (10:38) is due to the manifest property within it, which is that it contains a complete and comprehensive explanation of one of the purposes of Divine guidance—a decisive explanation without jest. And His saying: قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ "Say, ‘Then bring ten surahs’" (11:13) is a challenge with ten Quranic surahs because of the variety in exposition and diversity in purposes due to the quantity. "Ten" (العشرة) is among the words used for multiplicity (الكثرة), like a hundred or a thousand.

The Exalted said: يَوَدُّ أَحَدُهُمْ لَوْ يُعَمَّرُ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ "Each of them is eager to live a thousand years" (2:96).

So, what is meant by "ten surahs"—and Allah knows best—is many surahs that possess some of the levels of multiplicity known among people. It is as if it were said: "Bring a number of its surahs, let them be ten, so that it may become clear that the diversity of Quranic purposes in its miraculous exposition is only from Allah."

As for His saying: فَلْيَأْتُوا بِحَدِيثٍ مِثْلِهِ "let them bring a discourse like it" (52:34), it is as if it were a challenge encompassing the three previous challenges. For "discourse" (الحديث) includes a surah, ten surahs, and the entire Quran; thus, it is a challenge with the absolute Quranic property (الخاصة القرآنية), and this is apparent.

Two matters remain here. First: in none of the mentioned verses of challenge was what the opponent brings described as "fabricated" (الافتراء) except in this verse, where it is said: فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَياتٍ "Then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated," unlike His saying: فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ "Then bring a surah like it," where it did not say: "bring a surah like it, fabricated," and similarly in the other verses of challenge.

Perhaps the reason for this is that the type of concern (العناية) in the verse under discussion is different from the type of concern in the other verses of challenge. The concern in the other verses is related to the fact that they are unable to bring the like of the Quran or the like of a surah because it is a Quran containing aspects that human power cannot reach, and none but Allah, the Exalted, can produce. The statement there was made absolutely.

As for this verse, since it was followed by His saying: فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكُمْ فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما أُنْزِلَ بِعِلْمِ اللهِ "But if they do not respond to you, then know that it has been sent down by Allah’s knowledge" (11:14), it indicates that the challenge therein is based on the Quran containing that which is exclusive to the knowledge of Allah, to which no one else has access. This is something that, by its very nature, does not accept fabrication (الافتراء). It is as if it were said: "This Quran, by its very nature, does not accept fabrication, for it contains matters of Divine knowledge (العلم الإلهي) to which none but He, the Exalted, has access. If you are in doubt about that, then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated—which you claim to be fabrication—and seek help from whomever you can besides Allah. If you are unable to do so, then know that it is from the knowledge exclusive to Him, the Exalted." Understand this.

Second: the meaning of the challenge with "the like" (المثل) where it is said: بِمِثْلِ هذَا الْقُرْآنِ "the like of this Quran," بِحَدِيثٍ مِثْلِهِ "a discourse like it," بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ "a surah like it," بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ "ten surahs like it." The apparent reason for this is that since the speech was a miraculous sign (آية معجزة), if a human were to bring what resembles it (يماثله), it would suffice to invalidate its being a miraculous sign; there would be no need to bring something that surpasses it in its attributes or excels it in its properties.

It might be objected that the inability of anyone other than him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to do that does not prove it is a miracle not attributed to him. For the attributes of perfection (صفات الكمال) found in the human species—such as eloquence, writing, bravery, generosity, and others—have varying and different levels, some excelling others. If this is the case, there must be a level that is above all others, which is the ultimate point to which the human soul can possibly ascend.

Thus, for every attribute of perfection, there exists among the individuals characterized by it one who carries the highest degree and the furthest limit of it, such that no one else equals him or opposes him. Necessarily, among human beings in general, there is one who is the most eloquent, the best writer, the bravest, or the most generous, just as there is among them one who is the tallest in stature or the largest in body. Why is it not possible that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was the most articulate and eloquent of all people, and the Quran is from his speech, which no one can oppose because he stands in a position where no one else has a foothold? In that case, the inability of others to bring its like would not be proof of its being Divine speech and not human, for it could be human speech exclusive to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and withheld from others.

This is refuted by the fact that human attributes in which excellence (التفاضل) occurs—even if they are as mentioned—are nonetheless things that human nature (الطبيعة الإنسانية) allows for through the potential (الاستعداد) Allah has placed within it, without arising from mere chance and without a cause that enables the characterized individual to possess them.

If this is the case, and an individual is assumed to be exclusive in a superior attribute that no one else equals or surpasses, it would be possible for others to follow the path he paved and become accustomed—through practice (التمرن), training (التدرب), and discipline (الارتياض)—to the actions that stem from the attribute of perfection he possesses. Thus, they could bring something resembling (يماثل) some of the perfection he is exclusive in and imitate him in a portion of his works, even if they cannot compete with him in everything or resemble him in the whole. The aforementioned genius individual would retain the position of originality (الأصالة), precedence (السبقة), and advancement in that. For example, Hatim [al-Ta'i], even if he was unique and unopposed in his generosity and bounty such that no one else could surpass him, it is possible for a disciplined person to follow his path, train, and practice in it, and thus bring something of the type of his generosity and bounty, even if he cannot compete with him in everything or in his original standing. All human perfections that are sources of actions follow this path; man is able, through practice and training, to follow the path of the preceding innovators therein and bring something of their works, even if he cannot compete with them in their original position.

So, if the Quran were from the speech of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—on the assumption that he was the most eloquent and articulate of humans—it would have been possible for someone else to take interest and train himself to follow the wondrous composition (النظم البديع) he innovated in his speech. He would then be able to imitate him in some of the speech and bring some discourse in a surah like it, even if he could not imitate the entire Quran or bring all of it.

He did not say in what He challenged with: "Let them bring a discourse more eloquent than it or better," or "a surah that is more eloquent or better," such that it could be said: "The Quran is the most eloquent or best human speech, and there is nothing more eloquent or better than it for anyone to bring," and thus the inability to bring that would not prove it is speech of other than humans. Rather, He said: فَلْيَأْتُوا بِحَدِيثٍ مِثْلِهِ "let them bring a discourse like it," قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ "Say, ‘Then bring a surah like it’," and so on. It is within human power to bring the like (مثل) of the speech of another human, even if that other is assumed to have a standing in speech that no one else opposes, as we have explained. Thus, the doubt is refuted by His saying, the Exalted: مِثْلِهِ "like it."

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكُمْ فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما أُنْزِلَ بِعِلْمِ اللهِ وَأَنْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ "But if they do not respond to you, then know that it has been sent down by Allah’s knowledge, and that there is no god except Him. Will you, then, submit?" (11:14). "Answering the call" (إجابة الدعوة) and "responding" (استجابتها) carry the same meaning.

It is apparent from the context that the address in the verse is to the polytheists (المشركين), and that it is a continuation of the speech of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) which he was commanded to deliver to them by His saying: قُلْ "Say." Accordingly, the plural pronoun in His saying: فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا "But if they do not respond" refers to the deities and everyone they sought help from, who are indicated by His saying: وَادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ "and invoke whomever you can besides Allah" (11:13).

The meaning is: If they do not respond to you, O assembly of polytheists—these whom you have called upon from among your deities, the eloquent ones of your tongue who are knowledgeable in the styles of speech, the scholars of the People of the Book who possess the Heavenly Books and the reports of the prophets and nations, the soothsayers who draw support from the suggestions of the devils among the jinn, and the masters of knowledge and understanding from among other people who delve into the various aspects of human knowledge—then know that this Quran was only sent down by the knowledge of Allah (بعلم الله). It was not fabricated from my own knowledge, nor from the knowledge of anyone else whom you claim teaches me and dictates to me. And know also that what I call you to of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — is the truth. For if there were a god besides Allah, he would have helped you in what you called him to. Will you then, O polytheists, be submissive (مسلمون) to Allah, the Exalted, and compliant with His command?

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكُمْ "But if they do not respond to you" is in the sense of our saying: "If you are unable to challenge it after seeking help and support from whomever you could call upon besides Allah." This is because the causes (الأسباب) that would necessitate their ability to challenge are the power of expression and the genius of eloquence they possess—and they believe that these are gifts from their deities besides Allah—as well as what their deities possess which they have not yet bestowed upon them but could support them with if they wished, according to their claim; and also the help from those other than their deities. If those they call upon do not respond to them in challenging the Quran, then all the causes necessitating their ability have been removed, and thereby their ability itself is removed. Thus, the failure of the partners to respond to the challenge of the Quran is inseparable from their own inability to do so, even with the power they themselves possess. So, there is a metonymy (كناية) in the speech.

And His saying: فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما أُنْزِلَ بِعِلْمِ اللهِ "then know that it has been sent down by Allah’s knowledge." It is apparent that what is meant by "Allah's knowledge" is the knowledge exclusive to Him, which is the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — to which no one else has access except by His permission, as He, the Exalted, said: لكِنِ اللهُ يَشْهَدُ بِما أَنْزَلَ إِلَيْكَ أَنْزَلَهُ بِعِلْمِهِ "But Allah bears witness to what He has sent down to you—He sent it down with His knowledge" (4:166); and He said: ذلِكَ مِنْ أَنْباءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ "That is from the news of the Unseen which We reveal to you" (12:102); and He said: عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى مِنْ رَسُولٍ "Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves of" (72:26-27); and He said: إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ فِي كِتابٍ مَكْنُونٍ لا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ تَنْزِيلٌ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ "This is indeed a noble Quran, in a guarded Book—no one touches it except the pure—a revelation from the Lord of all the worlds" (56:77-80).

The meaning is: If you are unable to challenge it by any supporting cause you have clung to besides Allah, then be certain that it was not sent down except from an Unseen cause, and that it is from the news of the Unseen which is exclusive to Him, the Exalted. He is the one who sent it down to me, spoke to me with it, and intended for me and for you to understand the true knowledge and treasures of guidance within it.

Some have mentioned that the meaning is that it was sent down based on Allah's knowledge of its revelation and His testimony for it. Others mentioned that the meaning is that it was sent down with the knowledge from Allah that it does not accept challenge, or with the knowledge from Allah of its composition and order, which no one else knows. These are weak meanings, far from the [correct] understanding.

The sentence—meaning His saying: أَنَّما أُنْزِلَ بِعِلْمِ اللهِ "that it has been sent down by Allah’s knowledge"—is one of the two conclusions drawn from the failure of their partners to respond to them. The other conclusion is His saying: وَأَنْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ "and that there is no god except Him." The necessity of this conclusion arises from two aspects: First, when they called upon their deities for their important affairs and they did not respond, it revealed that they are not gods. For the [true] God is only He who responds to the distressed when he calls upon Him, especially if he calls Him for something that benefits the deity called upon. For the Quran brought by the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was cutting off their roots, killing their mention, and turning people away from them. If they did not respond to their devotees when they called them to challenge a Book of this nature, it was the clearest evidence for the negation of their divinity.

Second, if it is established that the Quran is the truth revealed from Allah, truthful in what it informs, and among what it informs is that there is no other god with Allah, then it is known thereby that there is no god but Allah, the Glorified.

And His saying: فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ "Will you, then, submit?"—meaning: since you have known and it has become clear to you through the failure of your partners besides Allah to respond and your own inability to challenge, will you then be submissive (مسلمون) to what your knowledge has settled upon regarding the monotheism of Allah, the Glorified, and the fact that this Quran is a Book revealed by His knowledge? This is a command to submit (الإسلام) in the form of an interrogation. All of this is what the apparent meaning of the verse requires.

It has been said that the address in His saying: فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكُمْ "But if they do not respond to you" etc., is to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), addressed in the plural form to honor him and magnify his status, and the third-person plural pronoun refers to the polytheists. That is: "If the polytheists do not respond to what you have called them to of challenging it, O Prophet, then know that it is revealed by Allah's knowledge and that Allah is One; so are you submissive to His command?"

The problem with this is that it is established that honorifics using the plural form and multiplicity are exclusive in Arabic speech to the speaker (المتكلم); as for the second person (الخطاب) and third person (الغيبة), there is no honoring in them through the plural form.

In addition, the attribution of Divine revelation (الوحي الإلهي) and Lordly speech (التكليم الرباني) to Him, the Exalted, is a necessary attribution that does not accept doubt such that it would require help from evidence. What the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) receives, its indication of being speech from Allah is a necessary indication (دلالة ضرورية) that does not need an argument (حجة) such that it would be argued for by the polytheists' failure to respond to the challenge of the Quran and their inability to do so. This is unlike the speech of creatures—humans, jinn, angels, or any other caller—for obtaining knowledge of its attribution to its speaker requires external evidence from the senses or the intellect. An indication of this was previously mentioned in the story of Zakariyya in Surah Al ‘Imran, and a full discussion of this will come in its appropriate place, Allah willing.

Furthermore, addressing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with the likes of His saying: وَأَنْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ "and that there is no god except Him," and His saying: فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ "Will you, then, submit?" is not free from preposterousness. Moreover, the reasoning itself is incomplete, as we shall explain.

It has also been said that the address in the verse is to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers together, or to the believers specifically, because the believers share with him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in the religious call and the challenge with the Quran, which is the Book of their Lord revealed to them. The meaning would be: "If the polytheists do not respond to you in the challenge, then know that the Quran is revealed by Allah's knowledge and that there is no god but Him; so will you submit to Allah?"

When some realized that there is no meaning in calling the believers—while they are believers in Allah alone and in His Book—to the knowledge that it is a Book revealed from Allah and that He, the Exalted, is One without partner, they corrected it by saying the meaning is: "Then remain firm in your knowledge that it was only sent down by Allah's knowledge, and increase thereby in faith and certainty, and that there is no god but Him and none deserves worship besides Him; so are you firm in your submission (إسلامكم) and sincerity therein?"

The problem with this is that it is a restriction (تقييد) of the verse without a restrictor, and the argument is incomplete. This is because if the polytheists had taken the position of challenge with the resources they had and sought help therein by calling upon their deities and others they hoped for among jinn and humans, and then failed, that would have been clear evidence indicating to them that the Quran is above human speech, and the argument would have been completed against them. As for the disbelievers' failure to respond to the challenge, it does not prove it is from Allah, because they did not comply with what they were commanded by His saying: فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَياتٍ "Then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated." This was either because they knew it was the true speech of Allah and their saying: افْتَراهُ "He has fabricated it" was a statement arising from stubbornness (العناد) and persistence (اللجاج), not from conviction or doubt; or because they were despairing of their partners responding to the call for challenge; or because they were jesting in saying that, babbling nonsense.

In short, the polytheists' failure to respond to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), or to the believers, or to all of them, does not in itself prove that the Quran is revealed from Allah, unless that failure to respond occurred after they had actually called their partners to the challenge and they failed to respond to them. No such call was realized from the polytheists. The mere failure of the polytheists themselves to respond is of no use. It remains only to be said that the meaning of the verse is: "If the polytheists call upon whomever they can besides Allah and they do not respond to them, and the polytheists do not respond to you, O Prophet and assembly of believers, then know that it was sent down by Allah's knowledge," etc. This is what we alluded to just now as being a restriction of the verse without a restrictor.

Furthermore, it contains a command to the believers to be guided in their faith and certainty by a hypothetical matter that did not occur, and His speech, the Exalted, is far above that. If the intention was to indicate that they are unable to do that even if they called their partners to the challenge, it would have been proper for the speech to say: "But if they do not respond to you—and they will never respond—then know," etc., as was said in its counterpart: وَإِنْ كُنْتُمْ فِي رَيْبٍ مِمَّا نَزَّلْنا عَلى عَبْدِنا فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِنْ مِثْلِهِ وَادْعُوا شُهَداءَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ فَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلُوا وَلَنْ تَفْعَلُوا فَاتَّقُوا النَّارَ الَّتِي وَقُودُهَا النَّاسُ وَالْحِجارَةُ أُعِدَّتْ لِلْكافِرِينَ "And if you are in doubt concerning what We have sent down to Our servant, then bring a surah like it, and invoke your witnesses besides Allah, should you be truthful. But if you do not—and you will not—then beware the Fire whose fuel is humans and stones, prepared for the faithless" (2:23-24).

His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا وَزِينَتَها نُوَفِّ إِلَيْهِمْ أَعْمالَهُمْ فِيها وَهُمْ فِيها لا يُبْخَسُونَ "As for those who desire the life of this world and its glitter, We shall pay them in full for their works here, and they shall not be defrauded here" (11:15). "Paying in full" (التوفية) is delivering the right to its owner and giving it to him completely. "Defrauding" (البخس) is the reduction of the reward.

In the verse is a threat to those who do not submit to the truth when it comes to them and do not surrender to it, preferring the life of this world and forgetting the Hereafter. It is an exposition of something from the sunnah of causes (سنة الأسباب) that condemns them to despair of the bliss of the life of the Hereafter.

This is because an action, whatever it may be, only grants man the goal he intended by it and for which he performed it. If it was a worldly goal—to rectify the affairs of the life of this world such as wealth, beauty, or a good state—the action leads him—if other active causes assist—to what he hopes for by the action. As for the goals of the Hereafter, there is no news of them because they were not intended so as to occur. The mere suitability of an action to lie on the path of the Hereafter and benefit in attaining its bliss—such as righteousness (البر), kindness (الإحسان), and good character—does not necessitate reward and the raising of degrees as long as it was not intended for the sake of Allah and the abode of His reward.

Therefore, He followed it with His saying, the Exalted: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَيْسَ لَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ إِلَّا النَّارُ وَحَبِطَ ما صَنَعُوا فِيها وَباطِلٌ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ "They are the ones for whom there shall be nothing in the Hereafter but the Fire: what they have wrought here will come to naught, and what they used to do will be in vain" (11:16). He informed that when they enter the life of the Hereafter, they will fall into an abode whose reality is a fire that consumes all their actions in life just as fire consumes wood, and it destroys and annihilates everything that their souls found pleasant of the beauties of existence. It causes everything they wrought therein to come to naught (حبط) and invalidates what they sent forth of actions in the world. Therefore, He, the Glorified, called it in another place the "Abode of Ruin" (دار البوار)—meaning destruction—saying: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ كُفْراً وَأَحَلُّوا قَوْمَهُمْ دارَ الْبَوارِ جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَوْنَها "Have you not regarded those who substituted ungratefulness for Allah’s blessing, and settled their people in the abode of ruin? Hell, which they shall enter" (14:28-29). Thereby it becomes clear that both His saying: وَحَبِطَ ما صَنَعُوا فِيها "what they have wrought here will come to naught" and His saying: وَباطِلٌ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ "and what they used to do will be in vain" explain His saying: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَيْسَ لَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ إِلَّا النَّارُ "They are the ones for whom there shall be nothing in the Hereafter but the Fire" with a certain type of explanation.

From what has preceded, it becomes clear, firstly: that what is meant by "paying them in full for their works" is paying them the results of their works and delivering the effects they have according to the system of causes and effects (نظام الأسباب والمسببات), not what the actor intends by his act and hopes for by his endeavor. For what the actor attains in this world by his act is the result of the act which other active causes assist him in, not whatever the actor aims for; for not everything a man wishes for does he attain.

Allah, the Exalted, expressed this reality in another place by His saying: وَمَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ حَرْثَ الدُّنْيا نُؤْتِهِ مِنْها وَما لَهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ مِنْ نَصِيبٍ "and whoever desires the tillage of the world, We give him of it, and he will have no share in the Hereafter" (42:20). He, the Exalted, said: نُؤْتِهِ مِنْها "We give him of it," and did not say: "We give it to him." And He said in another place: مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْعاجِلَةَ عَجَّلْنا لَهُ فِيها ما نَشاءُ لِمَنْ نُرِيدُ ثُمَّ جَعَلْنا لَهُ جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلاها مَذْمُوماً مَدْحُوراً "Whoever desires the transitory [life], We expedite for him therein whatever We wish, for whomever We desire. Then We appoint hell for him, to enter it, blamed and spurned" (18:18). He mentioned what man desires of the world and attains from it, and added an explanation that not everyone who desires a matter attains it, nor is everything desired attained; rather, the matter is up to Allah, the Glorified: He gives what He wills and withholds what He wills, and He advances whom He wills and delays whom He wills, according to how the sunnah of causes proceeds.

Secondly: the two verses—meaning His saying: مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا وَزِينَتَها نُوَفِّ إِلَيْهِمْ أَعْمالَهُمْ "As for those who desire the life of this world and its glitter, We shall pay them in full for their works" until the end of the two verses—explain one of the Divine realities.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَلا إِنَّهُمْ يَثْنُونَ صُدُورَهُمْ "Behold! They fold up their breasts" (11:5), with its chain of narration from Ibn Mahbub, from Jamil ibn Salih, from Sudayr, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "Jabir ibn ‘Abdullah informed me that the polytheists, when they passed by the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) around the House, one of them would lower his head and back like this—and cover his head with a garment so that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would not see him—so Allah revealed: أَلا إِنَّهُمْ يَثْنُونَ (Behold! They fold up)."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Abu Razin, who said: "One of them would bend his back and cover himself with his garment."

In al-Majma‘: It is narrated from ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn, Abu Ja‘far, and Ja‘far ibn Muhammad (peace be upon them): يثنوني on the pattern of yaf‘aw‘ilu.

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi: from Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl, from Jabir, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "A man from the desert came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, I have sons and daughters, brothers and sisters—and grandsons and granddaughters, and nephews and nieces—and our livelihood is meager. If you see fit, O Messenger of Allah, to pray to Allah to expand [our provision] for us—'"

He said: "And he wept, so the Muslims felt pity for him. Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: ما مِنْ دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ ـ إِلَّا عَلَى اللهِ رِزْقُها وَيَعْلَمُ مُسْتَقَرَّها وَمُسْتَوْدَعَها ـ كُلٌّ فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ 'There is no animal on the earth, but that its sustenance is on Allah, and He knows its habitation and its repository: all is in a manifest Book' (11:6). Whoever takes charge of these mouths—whose sustenance is guaranteed by Allah—Allah will pour sustenance upon him in abundance—like pouring water. If it is little, then a little; and if it is much, then much." He said: "Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prayed, and the Muslims said 'Amen' for him."

He said: Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: "One who saw the man in the time of ‘Umar narrated to me; he asked him about his state, and he said: '[I am] among the best of his people in lawful wealth and the most abundant of them in riches.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur: al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi in Nawadir al-Usul, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in Shu‘ab al-Iman narrated from Ibn Mas‘ud, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who said: "When the appointed time (أجل) of one of you is in a land, a need is facilitated for him toward it—until when he reaches the furthest extent of his trace therein, he is taken—then the earth says on the Day of Resurrection: 'This is what you deposited with me (استودعتني).'"

I say: The narration is not clearly an explanation of the verse.

In al-Kafi: with its chain of narration from Abu Hamzah al-Thumali, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said during the Farewell Pilgrimage: 'Behold, the Trustworthy Spirit (الروح الأمين) breathed into my heart—that no soul shall die until it has completed its sustenance (رزقها)—so be God-conscious (اتقوا الله) and be moderate in seeking. Let not the slowness of any sustenance lead you—to seek it through an act of disobedience to Allah—for Allah, the Exalted, has divided the sustenances among His creation as lawful (حلالاً)—and He has not divided them as unlawful (حراماً). So whoever is God-conscious and patient, his sustenance will come to him from its lawful source; and whoever tears the veil of Allah's protection, Mighty and Majestic is He—and takes it from other than its lawful source, it will be deducted from his lawful sustenance—and he will be held accountable for it.'"

I say: The narration is among the famous ones, narrated by both the general and specific [scholars] through many paths.

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi: from Abu al-Hudhayl, from Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him), who said: "Allah has divided the sustenances among His servants—and He has bestowed a great surplus which He has not divided among anyone. Allah said: وَسْئَلُوا اللهَ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ 'and ask Allah of His grace' (4:32)."

I say: The narration is narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and some reports of this meaning have preceded under His saying, the Exalted: وَتَرْزُقُ مَنْ تَشاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ "and You provide for whomever You wish without any reckoning" (3:27), and His saying, the Exalted: وَسْئَلُوا اللهَ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ "and ask Allah of His grace" (4:32).

In al-Kafi: from Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him), who said: "Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) used to say often: 'Know with certain knowledge that Allah, Majestic and Sublime is He, has not allowed for a servant—even if his effort is intense, his ingenuity great, and his schemes numerous—to precede what has been named for him in the Wise Reminder (الذكر الحكيم). O people, no man shall increase by a speck through his cleverness, nor shall any man decrease by a speck due to his foolishness—so the one who knows this and acts upon it is the most relaxed of people in his benefit—and the one who knows this and abandons it is the most occupied of people in his harm. Many a person who is blessed is being led to ruin (مستدرج) through the kindness shown to him—and many a person who is deceived among people is being favored—.'"

"So be God-conscious, O striver, regarding your striving, and curtail your haste, and wake up from the slumber of your heedlessness—and reflect upon what has come from Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, upon the tongue of His Prophet (صلى الله عليه واله وسلم)." (The tradition).

In al-Kafi: with its chain of narration from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr, from ‘Abdullah ibn al-Hajjaj, from Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him), who said: "Muhammad ibn al-Munkadir used to say: 'I did not think that ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn would leave behind a creation—better than him until I saw his son Muhammad ibn ‘Ali—so I wanted to admonish him, but he admonished me—.' His companions said to him: 'With what did he admonish you?' He said: 'I went out to some outskirts of Medina at a hot hour—and I met Abu Ja‘far Muhammad ibn ‘Ali, who was a stout and heavy man—leaning on two black youths or two freed slaves—so I said to myself: "Glory be to Allah—a sheikh from the sheikhs of Quraysh at this hour in such a state—seeking the world? Indeed, I shall admonish him—."'"

"So I approached him and greeted him, and he returned the greeting with a panting breath while he was dripping with sweat—so I said: 'May Allah rectify you, a sheikh from the sheikhs of Quraysh at this hour—in this state seeking the world—have you considered if your appointed time (أجلك) came while you were in this state?' He said: 'If death came to me while I was in this state—it would come to me while I am in an act of obedience from the acts of obedience to Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He—by which I protect myself and my family from you and from the people. I only feared that death would come to me—while I was in an act of disobedience from the acts of disobedience to Allah.' So I said: 'You have spoken the truth, may Allah have mercy on you—I wanted to admonish you, but you admonished me.'"

In the same [source]: with its chain of narration from ‘Abd al-A‘la, the freed slave of the family of Sam, who said: "I met Abu ‘Abdullah in one of the paths of Medina—on a summer day of intense heat—so I said: 'May I be your ransom, your status with Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He—and your kinship to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—and yet you exert yourself on such a day?' He said: 'O ‘Abd al-A‘la, I went out seeking sustenance (الرزق)—to be independent thereby of the likes of you.'"

I say: There is no contradiction between the decree (القضاء) of sustenance and the command to seek it. This is obvious.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: al-Tayalisi, Ahmad, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it hasan), Ibn Majah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Abu al-Shaykh in al-‘Azamah, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Asma’ wa al-Sifat narrated from Abu Razin, who said: "I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, where was our Lord—before He created His creation?' He said: 'He was in a cloud (عماء), below which was air and above which was air, and He created His Throne upon the water.'"

I say: العماء (the cloud) is the mist that prevents sight from penetrating it. The "what" (ما) in his saying: "what was below it was air and what was above it was air" is a relative pronoun, and what is meant by "air" (الهواء) is that which is empty of everything, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَأَفْئِدَتُهُمْ هَواءٌ "and their hearts are empty" (14:43). Or it is a negating particle, and what is meant by "air" is its well-known meaning; the meaning being that it was a cloud that the air did not encompass, unlike all other clouds.

The narration is among the reports of anthropomorphism (التجسم), and therefore it was interpreted that his saying: "in a cloud" etc., is a metonymy for the Unseen of the Essence (غيب الذات) before which sights fail and intellects are bewildered.

In the same [source]: Ahmad, al-Bukhari, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa’i, Abu al-Shaykh in al-‘Azamah, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Asma’ wa al-Sifat narrated from ‘Imran ibn Husayn, who said: "The people of Yemen said: 'O Messenger of Allah, inform us about the beginning of this matter—how was it?' He said: 'Allah was before everything, and His Throne was upon the water, and He wrote in the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) the mention of everything, and He created the heavens and the earth.' Then a caller called out: 'Your camel has gone, O son of Husayn!'—so I went off and behold, the mirage was cutting off [the view] before it—by Allah, I wished that I had left it."

I say: Several traditionists narrated this narration from Buraydah; and Buraydah said at the end of it: "Then a comer came to me and said: 'This camel of yours has gone'—so I went out and the mirage was cutting off [the view] before it—and I wished that I had left it." This is among the things that weaken the two traditions.

In the same [source]: regarding His saying, the Exalted: لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً "that He may test you [to see] which of you is best in conduct" (11:7): Dawud ibn al-Muhabbir in the Book of Intellect, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Hakim in the History, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn ‘Umar, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited this verse: لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً so I said: 'What is the meaning of that, O Messenger of Allah?' He said: 'That He may test you which of you is best in intellect (عقلاً).' Then he said: 'And the best of you in intellect is the most wary of you (أورعكم) regarding the prohibitions of Allah—and the most knowledgeable of you (أعلمكم) regarding the obedience of Allah.'"

In al-Kafi: narrated from Sufyan ibn ‘Uyaynah, from Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He: لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً he said: "He said: 'It does not mean the one of you with the most actions—but the one of you with the most correct action (أصوبكم عملاً). Correctness is only the fear of Allah and the sincere intention (النية الصادقة).'"

Then he said: "Maintaining the action—until it is sincere is harder than the action itself. The sincere action (العمل الخالص) is that which you do not want anyone to praise you for—except Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He. And the intention (النية) is better than the action—behold, the intention is the action—." Then he recited the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: قُلْ كُلٌّ يَعْمَلُ عَلى شاكِلتِهِ "Say, ‘Everyone acts according to his character’" (17:84)—meaning according to his intention.

I say: His saying "behold, the intention is the action" means that the action has no effect except for what it possesses of intention.

In the Tafsir of al-Nu‘mani: with its chain of narration from Ishaq ibn ‘Abd al-‘Aziz, from Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him): regarding His saying: لَئِنْ أَخَّرْنا عَنْهُمُ الْعَذابَ إِلى أُمَّةٍ مَعْدُودَةٍ "And if We defer their punishment until a reckoned community" (11:8), he said: "The punishment (العذاب) is the rising of the Qa’im (peace be upon him)—and the reckoned community (الأمة المعدودة) are the people of Badr and his companions."

I say: al-Kulayni narrated this meaning in al-Kafi, and al-Qummi and al-‘Ayyashi in their two Tafsirs from ‘Ali, al-Baqir, and al-Sadiq (peace be upon them).

In al-Majma‘: It was said: "The reckoned community are the companions of the Mahdi—three hundred and ten-odd men, like the number of the people of Badr—they gather in a single hour just as the autumn clouds gather—." He said: "And it is what is narrated from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon them)."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: regarding His saying: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ "excepting those who are patient and do righteous deeds" (11:11), he said: "He said: 'They were patient in hardship and did righteous deeds in ease.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur: regarding His saying: مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا "As for those who desire the life of this world" (11:15): al-Bayhaqi in al-Shu‘ab narrated from Anas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'When the Day of Resurrection comes, my nation will become three groups: a group who worshiped Allah sincerely, a group who worshiped Allah out of ostentation (رياءً), and a group who worshiped Allah to attain the world thereby. He will say to the one who used to worship Allah for the world: "By My might and My majesty, what did you intend by My worship?" He will say: "The world." He will say: "No doubt, what you gathered shall not benefit you—nor shall you return to it; take him to the Fire." And He will say to the one who worshiped Allah out of ostentation: "By My might and My majesty, what did you intend by My worship?" He said: "Ostentation." He will say: "Your worship by which you used to show off—nothing of it ascended to Me, and it shall not benefit you today—take him to the Fire—."'"

"'And He will say to the one who used to worship Allah sincerely: "By My might and My majesty, what did you intend by My worship?" He will say: "By Your might and Your majesty, You are more knowledgeable of it than I—I used to worship You for Your sake and for Your abode—." He said: "My servant has spoken the truth; take him to Paradise."'"

Ayahs

أَفَمَن كَانَ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ وَيَتْلُوهُ شَاهِدٌ مِّنْهُ وَمِن قَبْلِهِۦ كِتَـٰبُ مُوسَىٰٓ إِمَامًا وَرَحْمَةً ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ ۚ وَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ فَٱلنَّارُ مَوْعِدُهُۥ ۚ فَلَا تَكُ فِى مِرْيَةٍ مِّنْهُ ۚ إِنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٧)
Is he who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord, and whom a witness of his own [family] follows? And before him there was the Book of Moses, a guide and mercy. It is they who have faith in it, and whoever defies him from among the factions, the Fire is their tryst. So do not be in doubt about it; it is the truth from your Lord, but most people do not have faith. (17)
وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ وَيَقُولُ ٱلْأَشْهَـٰدُ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا۟ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ ۚ أَلَا لَعْنَةُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٨)
Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah? Such shall be presented before their Lord, and the witnesses will say, ‘It is these who lied against their Lord.’ Behold! The curse of Allah is upon the wrongdoers (18)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (١٩)
—those who bar [others] from the way of Allah, and seek to make it crooked, and disbelieve in the Hereafter. (19)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَمْ يَكُونُوا۟ مُعْجِزِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا كَانَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِنْ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۘ يُضَـٰعَفُ لَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ ۚ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَسْتَطِيعُونَ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ يُبْصِرُونَ (٢٠)
They cannot frustrate [Allah] on the earth, nor do they have besides Allah any guardian. For them the punishment shall be doubled, for they could neither listen, nor did they use to see. (20)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (٢١)
They are the ones who have ruined their souls, and what they used to fabricate has forsaken them. (21)
لَا جَرَمَ أَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمُ ٱلْأَخْسَرُونَ (٢٢)
Undoubtedly, they are the ones who will be the biggest losers in the Hereafter. (22)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَأَخْبَتُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٢٣)
Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds and are humble before their Lord—they shall be the inhabitants of paradise, and they shall remain in it [forever]. (23)
۞ مَثَلُ ٱلْفَرِيقَيْنِ كَٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْأَصَمِّ وَٱلْبَصِيرِ وَٱلسَّمِيعِ ۚ هَلْ يَسْتَوِيَانِ مَثَلًا ۚ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٢٤)
The parable of the two parties is that of one who is blind and deaf and one who sees and hears. Are they equal in comparison? Will you not then take admonition? (24)

(Commentary)

The apparent context of the verses is that they serve to comfort the soul of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), strengthen his faith in the Book of Allah, and affirm the insight (البصيرة) he possesses regarding his mission. The discourse continues in the same mode of addressing him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم); the speech was directed toward him until it reached the point of what they accused him of—fabricating lies against Allah, the Glorified. Thus, He commanded him to challenge them to bring ten fabricated surahs like it. Then, He commanded him to be of good cheer and to remain firm in the knowledge he possesses—that it is revealed from Allah—for he is indeed upon the truth and is not a fabricator. Therefore, he should not feel desolated by the turning away of the majority, nor should he doubt.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَمَنْ كانَ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ وَيَتْلُوهُ شاهِدٌ مِنْهُ وَمِنْ قَبْلِهِ كِتابُ مُوسى إِماماً وَرَحْمَةً — Is he who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord, and whom a witness from him follows, and before him was the Book of Moses, a guide and a mercy? (11:17). This sentence is a derivation from the preceding discourse, which functions as an argument for the Quran being a Book revealed from Allah, the Glorified. The word "who" (مَنْ) is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (خبر) is omitted; the estimated meaning is: "Is he like others?" or something to that effect. The evidence for this is His subsequent saying: أُولئِكَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ وَمَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِهِ مِنَ الْأَحْزابِ فَالنَّارُ مَوْعِدُهُ — They believe in it, and whoever of the factions disbelieves in it, the Fire is his promised place.

The interrogative is one of denial (إنكاري), and the meaning is: One who is such-and-such is not like others who are not so; and since you possess these attributes, do not be in doubt concerning the Quran.

Regarding His saying: عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ — on a manifest proof from his Lord, the manifest proof (البينة) — the clear evidence that makes the truth distinct — is an adjective meaning that which is apparent and clear. However, apparent and clear things often clarify what is joined to them or related to them, much like light, which is manifest (بين) in itself and makes other things manifest. For this reason, the term "manifest proof" (البينة) is frequently used for that by which something else is made clear, such as an argument (الحجة) or a sign (الآية). A witness to a claimant's case is also called a "manifest proof" (بينة).

Allah, the Exalted, has called an argument a "manifest proof" (بينة), as in His saying: لِيَهْلِكَ مَنْ هَلَكَ عَنْ بَيِّنَةٍ — so that he who perishes might perish by a manifest proof (8:42). He also called His sign a "manifest proof," as in His saying: قَدْ جاءَتْكُمْ بَيِّنَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ هذِهِ ناقَةُ اللهِ لَكُمْ آيَةً — There has certainly come to you a manifest proof from your Lord. This is the she-camel of Allah, a sign for you (7:73). He also called the specific divine insight (البصيرة) granted to the prophets a "manifest proof," as in His saying, narrating from Noah (عليه السلام): يا قَوْمِ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي وَآتانِي رَحْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِهِ — O my people! Tell me, should I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord, and He has granted me a mercy from Himself (11:28). Or it refers to absolute divine insight, as is apparent in His saying, the Exalted:

أَفَمَنْ كانَ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ كَمَنْ زُيِّنَ لَهُ سُوءُ عَمَلِهِ وَاتَّبَعُوا أَهْواءَهُمْ — Is he who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord like those to whom the evil of their conduct has been made to seem fair and who follow their desires? (47:14). Regarding its meaning, He has also said: أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَنْ مَثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُماتِ لَيْسَ بِخارِجٍ مِنْها — Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and provided him with a light by which he walks among the people, like one who is in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge? (6:122).

It appears that the intended meaning of "manifest proof" (البينة) here is this latter general sense, based on the context of His subsequent saying: أُولئِكَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ — They believe in it, even if the specific instance intended is the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), for the discourse is structured so that His saying: فَلا تَكُ فِي مِرْيَةٍ مِنْهُ — so do not be in doubt about it — may be derived from it.

Thus, what is intended is the divine insight (البصيرة) granted to the Prophet (عليه السلام), not the Quran itself revealed to him. This is because it would not be outwardly appropriate to derive the command "do not be in doubt about it" from the Quran itself when the Quran is the very thing being doubted. This is clear, and it does not contradict the fact that the Quran is in itself a manifest proof from Allah by virtue of being a sign (آية) from Him, as in His saying: قُلْ إِنِّي عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِِّي وَكَذَّبْتُمْ بِهِ — Say, ‘Indeed I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord, and you have denied it’ (6:57), for that context is different from this one.

From what has passed, it becomes clear that the view of those who say that "he who stands..." refers exclusively to the Prophet as the primary linguistic intent (إرادة استعمالية) is misplaced; rather, he is intended in terms of the application to the specific case (انطباق المورد). Similarly, the view of those who say it refers to the believers among the companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is also without evidence for such restriction.

It also reveals the invalidity of the view that "manifest proof" (البينة) refers to the Quran, as well as the view that it refers to the proof of reason (حجة العقل) and is attributed to the Lord because He establishes both rational and traditional evidences. The reason for its invalidity is that there is no evidence for such restriction, and the manifest proof established for the Prophet (عليه السلام) from Allah's side through divine introduction (التعريف الإلهي) cannot be compared to the proof established for us through our intellects.

Regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَيَتْلُوهُ شاهِدٌ مِنْهُ — and whom a witness from him follows, the intended meaning of witnessing (الشهادة) is the bearing of testimony (تأدية الشهادة) which confirms the validity of the matter witnessed, rather than the mere receiving of it (تحملها). This is because the context is one of affirming the truth of the Quran, which is suitable for witnessing in the sense of bearing testimony, not merely receiving it.

It appears that this witness (شاهد) refers to someone who attained certainty in the truth of the Quran and possessed divine insight regarding it, thus believing in it out of insight and testifying that it is the truth revealed from Allah, just as one testifies to monotheism (التوحيد) and the messengership (الرسالة). Indeed, the testimony of a person of certainty and insight regarding a matter removes from a person the desolation of doubt and the uncertainty of being alone. For when a person adheres to a matter and is solitary in it, that solitude may cause him distress if no one supports him in his claim. However, if others among the people affirm it and support his view, his distress vanishes, his heart is strengthened, and his resolve is fortified. Allah has argued using a similar meaning in His saying: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كَانَ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ وَكَفَرْتُمْ بِهِ وَشَهِدَ شَاهِدٌ مِنْ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ عَلَى مِثْلِهِ فَآمَنَ وَاسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ — Say, ‘Tell me, if it is from Allah and you disbelieve in it, and a witness from the Children of Israel has testified to its like and believed, while you are disdainful?’ (46:10).

Accordingly, His saying: يَتْلُوهُ — follows him — is derived from "following" (التلو), not from "recitation" (التلاوة).
