Those are the ones that the common folk imagine the Prophets and the Friends of Allah (الأولياء) are given the keys to, and by which they possess the power to do what they wish and decree what they want, as was proposed to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) and Allah the Exalted narrated it when He says: وَقالُوا لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّى تَفْجُرَ لَنا مِنَ الْأَرْضِ يَنْبُوعاً أَوْ تَكُونَ لَكَ جَنَّةٌ مِنْ نَخِيلٍ وَعِنَبٍ فَتُفَجِّرَ الْأَنْهارَ خِلالَها تَفْجِيراً أَوْ تُسْقِطَ السَّماءَ كَما زَعَمْتَ عَلَيْنا كِسَفاً أَوْ تَأْتِيَ بِاللهِ وَالْمَلائِكَةِ قَبِيلاً أَوْ يَكُونَ لَكَ بَيْتٌ مِنْ زُخْرُفٍ أَوْ تَرْقى فِي السَّماءِ وَلَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لِرُقِيِّكَ حَتَّى تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنا كِتاباً نَقْرَؤُهُ قُلْ سُبْحانَ رَبِّي هَلْ كُنْتُ إِلَّا بَشَراً رَسُولاً — "They say, ‘We will not believe you until you make a spring gush forth from the ground for us, or until you have a garden of date palms and vines and you make rivers gush through them, or until you make the sky fall upon us in pieces as you claim, or until you bring Allah and the angels face to face [with us], or until you have a house of gold, or until you ascend into the sky. And we will not believe your ascent until you bring down for us a book that we may read.’ Say, ‘Immaculate is my Lord! Am I anything but a human messenger?’" (17:90-93).

He only said: وَلا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ (nor do I know the Unseen) and did not say: "nor do I say that I know the Unseen," because this type of knowledge, since it was something that is begrudged and not permitted to be shown, the saying of a speaker "I do not say that I know the Unseen" would not negate its existence with the speaker. Rather, it needs to be said: "I do not know the Unseen" to provide the negation. This is in contrast to his saying: لا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللهِ and his saying: وَلا أَقُولُ إِنِّي مَلَكٌ. He did not repeat his saying: لَكُمْ (to you) because one occurrence was sufficient.

Allah, the Glorified, commanded His Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny) to address his people with what Nuh (peace be upon him) addressed his people, then He appended it with that which makes the intent clear when He said: قُلْ لا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللهِ وَلا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ وَلا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ إِنِّي مَلَكٌ إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا ما يُوحى إِلَيَّ قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِي الْأَعْمى وَالْبَصِيرُ أَفَلا تَتَفَكَّرُونَ — "Say, ‘I do not say to you that I possess the treasuries of Allah, nor do I know the Unseen, nor do I say to you that I am an angel. I follow only what is revealed to me.’ Say, ‘Are the blind and the seeing equal? Will you not then reflect?’" (6:50).

Look at His saying: لا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ etc., then at His saying: إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا ما يُوحى إِلَيَ, then at His saying: قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِي الْأَعْمى وَالْبَصِيرُ etc. He first negates the merit that the common people expect from their Prophet, then He affirms for the messenger the message only. Then He hastens to affirm merit from another aspect, other than the aspect that the people expect, which is that he is seeing by the sight given by Allah the Exalted, and that others in relation to him are like the blind in relation to the seeing. This is the motive for their following him, just as the blind follows the seeing, and it is what permits him to call them to follow him.

(A Philosophical and Quranic Discourse on the Power of the Prophets and the Friends of Allah)

People are in ignorance of the station of their Lord and in heedlessness of the meaning of His encompassment and dominance. Despite what the human primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — guides them to of His existence and His oneness, the trial of the world of matter and nature, the immersion in natural laws and rules, then the social customs and laws, and the familiarity with multiplicity and separation, leads them to measure the Divine Realm by what they are familiar with in the world of matter. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, is to them, along with His creation, like a tyrant among the tyrants of humanity with his slaves and subjects.

There is an individual among humans whom we call, for example, a king or a tyrant, beneath whom are ministers, princes, soldiers, and henchmen who carry out what he commands or forbids. He has gifts and boons for whom he wishes, and will and dislike, taking and returning, seizing and releasing, mercy and wrath, decree and abrogation, and so on.

Each of the king, his servants, his working hands, his subjects, and what is in their hands of blessings and the goods of life, is an existing, limited, independent entity (موجود محدود مستقل الوجود), separated from others. They are only linked to one another by conventional rules, laws, and customs that have no place except the mind of the thinker and the belief of the believer.

They have applied the Divine Realm—meaning what prophecy informs of the station of the Lord, the Exalted, His attributes, His acts, His angels, His books, and His messengers—to this system. Thus, He, the Exalted, wills and dislikes, gives and withholds, and manages the system of creation just as one of us called a king does. He is limited in existence, isolated in being; and each of His angels and the rest of His creation is independent in existence, possessing what they have of existence and bestowed blessings apart from Allah, the Glorified. And Allah was in the pre-eternity of time (أزل الزمان) alone, with nothing of His creation with Him, then He originated the creation in the side of post-eternity, so they were with Him.

They have affirmed—as you see—a limited existent whose existence is coincident with time, except that His temporal existence is perpetual. He has power over everything, knowledge of everything, a will that is not broken, and a decree that is not returned. He is independent in what He has of attributes and acts, just as one of us is independent, thus possessing what he has of life, knowledge, power, and so on. So his life is a life for him and is not for Allah; his knowledge is his knowledge, not the knowledge of Allah; his power is his power, not the power of Allah, and so on. It is only said of our existence, or our life, or our knowledge, or our power that they are for Allah just as it is said of what the subjects have of blessing that it is for the king, in the sense that it was with him, then he took it out from himself and placed it with us for us to dispose of. All of this—as you see—stands on the basis of limitation and isolation.

However, certain proofs (البراهين اليقينية) decree the corruption of all of that. For they judge the permeation of poverty and need into the possible existents (الموجودات الممكنة) in their essences and the effects of their essences. If the need for Him, the Exalted, is in the station of the essence (الذات), it is impossible to be independent of Him or isolated from Him at all. For if independence were assumed for a thing from Him, the Exalted, in its existence or any of the effects of its existence—in whatever way assumed, in origination or survival—it would be independent of Him from that aspect, which is impossible.

So every possible being (ممكن) is non-independent in anything of its essence and the effects of its essence. And Allah, the Glorified, is the One Who is independent in His essence, and He is the Rich (الغني) Who does not lack in anything, nor does He lose anything of existence and the perfection of existence such as life, power, and knowledge; thus He has no limit by which He is limited. Some clarification of this issue has already preceded in the commentary of His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّ اللهَ ثالِثُ ثَلاثَةٍ — "They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the third [person] of a trinity’..." (5:73).

Based on what has preceded, what the possible being has of existence, life, power, or knowledge is dependent in its existence upon Him, the Exalted, and is not independent of Him in any way. There is no difference in that between the little and the much, as long as the characteristic of non-independence is preserved in it. Thus, there is no barrier to assuming a possible being that has knowledge of everything, or power over everything, or perpetual life, as long as it is non-independent in existence from Allah, the Glorified, nor isolated in being from Him; just as there is no barrier to the realization of a possible being with a temporary existence having a term, or knowledge or power related to some things and not others. Yes, the assumption of independence (الاستقلال) nullifies the possible need, and there is no difference in it between the much and the little, as you have known. This is from the aspect of the intellect.

As for the aspect of transmission (النقل), the Divine Book, although it speaks of the exclusivity of some attributes and acts to Him, the Exalted—such as knowledge of the hidden things, reviving, causing death, and creating, as in His saying: وَعِنْدَهُ مَفاتِحُ الْغَيْبِ لا يَعْلَمُها إِلَّا هُوَ — "With Him are the treasures of the Unseen; no one knows them except Him..." (6:59), and His saying: وَأَنَّهُ هُوَ أَماتَ وَأَحْيا — "...and that it is He who causes death and gives life..." (53:44), and His saying: اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها — "Allah takes the souls at the time of their death..." (39:42), and His saying: اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "Allah is the creator of all things..." (39:62), and other verses—yet they are all interpreted by other verses, such as His saying: عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى مِنْ رَسُولٍ — "[He is] the Knower of the Unseen, and He does not expose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves..." (72:26-27), and His saying: قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ — "Say, ‘The angel of death... will take your souls...’" (32:11), and His saying regarding ‘Isa (peace be upon him): وَأُحْيِ الْمَوْتى بِإِذْنِ اللهِ — "...and I revive the dead by Allah’s leave..." (3:49), and His saying: وَإِذْ تَخْلُقُ مِنَ الطِّينِ كَهَيْئَةِ الطَّيْرِ بِإِذْنِي فَتَنْفُخُ فِيها فَتَكُونُ طَيْراً بِإِذْنِي — "...and when you would fashion from clay the form of a bird by My leave, and you would breathe into it and it would become a bird by My leave..." (5:110), and other verses.

The joining of verses to verses leaves no doubt that what is meant by the negating verses is the exclusivity of these matters to Him, the Exalted, by way of originality and independence (الأصالة والاستقلال); and what is meant by the affirming verses is the possibility of their realization in other than Him, the Exalted, by way of following and non-independence.

So whoever affirms something of the hidden knowledge or the unseen power—meaning knowledge not through the path of thought and power not through its usual natural course—for other than Him, the Exalted, from His Prophets and Friends, as has occurred frequently in the reports and traditions, and negates with it originality and independence—such that the knowledge and power, for example, are for Him, the Exalted, and what appeared from him only appeared through mediation and what occurred from him only occurred by His bestowing existence—then there is no restriction upon him.

But whoever affirms something of that by way of originality and independence, according to what the common understanding affirms, even if he ascribes it to Allah, the Glorified, and the outpouring of His mercy, has not escaped from exaggeration (الغلو) and was included in the like of His saying: لا تَغْلُوا فِي دِينِكُمْ وَلا تَقُولُوا عَلَى اللهِ إِلَّا الْحَقَّ — "...Do not exceed the bounds in your religion, and do not attribute to Allah anything but the truth..." (4:171).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا أَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ تَزْدَرِي أَعْيُنُكُمْ لَنْ يُؤْتِيَهُمُ اللهُ خَيْراً اللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ إِنِّي إِذاً لَمِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "...nor do I say of those whom your eyes despise that Allah will never grant them any good—Allah knows best what is in their souls—for then I would indeed be one of the wrongdoers.’" (11:31). It is said in al-Mufradat: زريت (I disparaged) him means I found fault with him; and azraytu bihi means I belittled him, and likewise izdaraytu bihi, and its root is ifta‘altu. He said: "whom your eyes despise" (تزدري أعينكم) means they deem them insignificant. Its estimation is: "their eyes despise them," meaning they deem them insignificant and hold them in contempt. [End of quote].

This section of his speech (peace be upon him) is an allusion to what the elite who disbelieved from his people believed and upon which they built the custom of nobility and the way of lordship. It is that the individuals of humanity are divided into two types: the strong and the weak. As for the strong, they are the possessors of wealth and the masters of power, supported by money and numbers. As for the weak, they are the rest. The strong are the masters in human society; for them is the blessing and the dignity, and for their sake society is formed. Others from the weak are created for their sake, intended for them, sacrifices for their interests, like the subjects in relation to the throne of an absolute government, the slaves in relation to the masters, the servants and workers in relation to the served, and women in relation to men; and ultimately, every weak one in relation to the strong one who is superior over him.

In short, their belief was that the weak one in society is a degraded human or an animal in the form of a human, who only enters into society and participates with them in life so that the noble may benefit from his work and profit from the toil of his right hand for his life, without the reverse. Rather, he is deprived of dignity, driven away from the enclosure of nobility, and despairing of mercy and care.

This is what they used to see, and it was what was relied upon in their society. Nuh (peace be upon him) refuted that to them by his saying: وَلا أَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ تَزْدَرِي أَعْيُنُكُمْ لَنْ يُؤْتِيَهُمُ اللهُ خَيْراً.

Then he explained their error in their belief by His saying: اللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ (Allah knows best what is in their souls). Meaning: "Your eyes only despise them, belittle them, and hold their affair in contempt because they perceive the outward appearance of their weakness and insignificance. But that is not the criterion for attaining good and reaching dignity. Rather, the criterion in that—and especially the divine dignities and rewards—is the matter of the soul and its being adorned with the ornaments of virtue and spiritual merit. There is no way for me nor for you to the knowledge of the inner depths of the souls and the secrets of the hearts except for Allah, the Glorified. So it is not for me nor for you to judge their deprivation of good and felicity."

Then he explained by His saying: إِنِّي إِذاً لَمِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ (for then I would indeed be one of the wrongdoers) the reason for his recoiling from this saying. Its meaning is that it is a saying without knowledge, and the prohibition of good to one who may deserve it, arbitrarily without evidence, is an injustice (ظلم) that a person should not intend, thereby entering into the ranks of the wrongdoers.

This meaning is what the Exalted alludes to in what He narrates of the speech of the People of the Elevations (أصحاب الأعراف) on the Day of Resurrection, addressing these tyrants, when He says: وَنادى أَصْحابُ الْأَعْرافِ رِجالاً يَعْرِفُونَهُمْ بِسِيماهُمْ قالُوا ما أَغْنى عَنْكُمْ جَمْعُكُمْ وَما كُنْتُمْ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ أَهؤُلاءِ الَّذِينَ أَقْسَمْتُمْ لا يَنالُهُمُ اللهُ بِرَحْمَةٍ — "The occupants of the Elevations will call out to certain men whom they recognize by their marks, saying, ‘Your gathering did not avail you, nor what you used to be arrogant about. Are these the ones of whom you swore that Allah would never grant them any mercy?’" (7:48-49).

In the speech—meaning the saying of Nuh (peace be upon him): وَلا أَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ تَزْدَرِي أَعْيُنُكُمْ etc.—is an exposure of them: that just as they used to prohibit the weak of society from the social advantages of life, they likewise used to prohibit them from religious dignity and say: "They will not be felicitous through religion; rather, only the nobles of society and its strong ones will be felicitous by it." In it is also an exposure that they are wrongdoers.

Nuh (peace be upon him) only followed his saying: وَلا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللهِ وَلا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ وَلا أَقُولُ إِنِّي مَلَكٌ—in which he negates the aspects of distinction that they expected in the messenger from himself—with his saying: وَلا أَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ تَزْدَرِي أَعْيُنُكُمْ لَنْ يُؤْتِيَهُمُ اللهُ خَيْراً etc., even though it refers to the weak who believed in him from his people, because the elite had linked them to him in their saying: وَما نَرى لَكُمْ عَلَيْنا مِنْ فَضْلٍ.

Its clarification is that the meaning of this saying of theirs is: "Our following you and those who believed in you of these lowest ones would only be consistent due to a merit that is established for you over us, and we do not see you to have any merit over us. As for you, you do not have what is exclusive to the messenger of celestial power or knowledge of the Unseen, or that you be an angel purified from the pollutions of matter and nature. As for those who believe in you, they are only our lowest ones who are despairing of human dignity, deprived of mercy and care."

So Nuh answered them with what means: "As for me, I do not claim anything of what you expect from my messengership; for the messenger has nothing but the message. As for these weak ones who have insignificance with you, it is possible that Allah knows good from their souls, so He gives them good and merit; for He is most knowing of their souls. The criterion for religious dignity and divine mercy is the purity of the soul and the soundness of the heart, not the outward appearance that your eyes despise. So I do not say: 'Allah will never grant them any good,' for it is an injustice that would enter me into the ranks of the wrongdoers."

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا يا نُوحُ قَدْ جادَلْتَنا فَأَكْثَرْتَ جِدالَنا فَأْتِنا بِما تَعِدُنا إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ — "They said, ‘O Noah, you have disputed with us and you have disputed with us much. Now bring us what you threaten us with, if you are truthful.’" (11:32). This is speech they delivered to Nuh (peace be upon him) after they were unable to refute his argument and nullify the truth he called to. it is formulated in the manner of challenging (التعجيز). What is meant by their saying: بِما تَعِدُنا (with what you threaten us) is what he warned them of at the beginning of his call of the punishment of a painful day.

Allah, the Glorified, has presented this saying of theirs as a separate section without branching (تفريع), because they only said it after he had remained among them for a long period calling them to monotheism, contending with them, and arguing with them with various types of contention and argumentation until he cut off all their excuses and made the truth clear to them, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted, in what He narrates from him (peace be upon him) in his supplication: قالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي دَعَوْتُ قَوْمِي لَيْلاً وَنَهاراً—until he said—ثُمَّ إِنِّي دَعَوْتُهُمْ جِهاراً ثُمَّ إِنِّي أَعْلَنْتُ لَهُمْ وَأَسْرَرْتُ لَهُمْ إِسْراراً — "He said, ‘My Lord! Indeed I have called my people night and day... Then I called them aloud. Then I spoke to them publicly and confided with them privately." (71:5-9). And in Surah al-Ankabut: فَلَبِثَ فِيهِمْ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ إِلَّا خَمْسِينَ عاماً — "...and he remained among them for a thousand years less fifty years..." (29:14). So this which Allah has presented of his argumentation with his people and their response in the form of a single dialogue actually occurred over hundreds of years. This has many parallels in the Noble Quran and there is nothing strange in it; for the One Who narrates that is Allah, the Glorified, Who encompasses time and everything in it, and the one who hears it through revelation is the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny), who has been given such breadth of vision that the various nations and the ends of time gather with him.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: "O Nuh, you have disputed with us and increased your disputation with us until we have become weary and bored, and we are not going to believe in you. So bring us what you threaten us with of the punishment." They do not admit to the inability to contend and dispute with him; rather, they make him despair of themselves in argumentation and ask him to occupy himself with what the caller who despairs of hearing and obedience occupies himself with, which is the evil with which he threatens them and mentions alongside his advice.

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ إِنَّمَا يَأْتِيكُم بِهِ اللَّهُ إِن شَاءَ وَمَا أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ "He said, ‘Allah will bring it to you only if He wishes, and you cannot frustrate [Him].’" (11:33) Since their saying: فَأْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَا "so bring us what you threaten us with" (11:32) etc., was a demand for him to bring the punishment upon them—and that is not within his power, for he is but a messenger—he responded to this suggestion of theirs as well. In the context of a "reversal restriction" (قصر القلب), he clarified that bringing the punishment is not up to him; rather, it is solely up to Allah. He is the One who owns your affair, so He will bring you the punishment I promised you by His command. He is your Lord, and to Him the return of your entire affair belongs. Nothing of the administrative governance (التدبير) returns to me, such that my promising you the punishment or your suggestion to me to demand it would affect anything in the presence of His grandeur. If He wishes, He will bring it to you, and if He does not wish, He will not.

From here, it becomes clear that his saying (peace be upon him): إِن شَاءَ "if He wishes" is among the most subtle conditions in this context. Through it, the right of "transcendental sanctification" (التنزيه) — the recognition of Allah’s absolute freedom from any external compulsion or limitation — was conveyed. This means that nothing governs Allah the Glorified, nor does any subduer overpower Him; He does what He wills, and none other than He does what He wills. This is similar to what will come at the end of the Surah regarding the exception in His saying: خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا مَا دَامَتِ السَّمَاوَاتُ وَالْأَرْضُ إِلَّا مَا شَاءَ رَبُّكَ ۖ عَطَاءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ "to remain in it as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord may wish—an endless bounty." (11:108)

And his saying: وَمَا أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ "and you cannot frustrate [Him]" is another sanctification of Allah the Glorified. At the same time, it is a response to the "defiant challenge" (الأمر التعجيزي) they threw at him (peace be upon him). Its outward appearance suggested they paid no heed to the punishment he threatened them with, as if they were capable of frustrating Him (معجزون) and could not be overcome.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا يَنفَعُكُمْ نُصْحِي إِنْ أَرَدْتُ أَنْ أَنصَحَ لَكُمْ إِن كَانَ اللَّهُ يُرِيدُ أَن يُغْوِيَكُمْ "My advice will not benefit you, were I to advise you, if Allah should desire to cause you to perish." (11:34) etc. Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "Advice (النصح) is the pursuit of an action or word in which there is the well-being (صلاح) of its recipient." He said: "It is from their saying: 'I advised (نصحت) him in affection,' meaning I was sincere (أخلصته) to him. Pure honey is called nasih (ناصح). Or it is from their saying: 'I advised (نصحت) the skin,' meaning I sewed it; the tailor is called nasih (ناصح) and the thread nisah (نصاح)."

He also said: "Error (الغي) is ignorance (جهل) arising from a corrupt belief (اعتقاد فاسد). This is because ignorance may sometimes be a person having no belief—neither sound nor corrupt—and sometimes it arises from believing in something corrupt. This second type is called ghayy (غي). The Exalted said: مَا ضَلَّ صَاحِبُكُمْ وَمَا غَوَىٰ 'Your companion has neither gone astray, nor is he gone into error' (53:2), and He said: وَإِخْوَانُهُمْ يَمُدُّونَهُمْ فِي الْغَيِّ 'But their brethren, they draw them into error' (7:202)." End quote.

Based on this, the difference between "leading into error" (الإغواء) and "misguiding" (الإضلال) is that misguiding (الإضلال) — the act of leading someone away from the correct path — is a removal from the path while the destination remains present in the mind of the one straying. Leading into error (الإغواء) — the act of causing one to lose their way through ignorance or corruption of belief — is a removal from the path while the destination itself vanishes from his mind due to his preoccupation with something else out of ignorance.

Will (الإرادة) and volition (المشية) are nearly synonymous. From Allah the Glorified, they refer to the "causation of causes" (تسبيب الأسباب) that lead to the existence of a thing by necessity. Thus, a thing being "willed" by Him, the Exalted, means He has completed and perfected the causes of its existence; therefore, it exists inevitably. As for the essence of the ongoing "causality" (السببية), it is willed by itself. Hence it is said: "Allah created things by volition (المشية), and the volition by itself."

In short, his saying: وَلَا يَنفَعُكُمْ نُصْحِي "My advice will not benefit you" etc., is like one side of a "disjunctive dilemma" (الترديد), and the other side is his saying: وَمَا أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ "and you cannot frustrate [Him]." It is as if he (peace be upon him) is saying: Your affair is with Allah; if He wishes to punish you, He will bring the punishment upon you, and nothing can repel His punishment or overpower His volition. Thus, you cannot frustrate Him, nor will my advice benefit you—were I to desire to advise you—once Allah has willed to lead you into error so that you disbelieve in Him, and the word of punishment becomes due against you. He conditioned his advice with a "stipulation" (الشرط) because they did not concede to him that he was indeed advising them.

Leading into error (الإغواء), like misguiding (الإضلال), is not permissible to attribute to Him, the Exalted, if it is "initial" (ابتدائي). However, it is permissible if it is by way of "requital" (المجازاة). For instance, when a person sins and deserves error thereby, Allah denies him the "means of success" (أسباب التوفيق) and leaves him to his own soul (يخليه ونفسه); thus, he falls into error and strays from the path of truth. The Exalted said: يُضِلُّ بِهِ كَثِيراً وَيَهْدِي بِهِ كَثِيراً وَمَا يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفَاسِقِينَ "He misleads many thereby, and He guides many thereby; and He misleads no one thereby except the transgressors" (2:26).

In the discourse, there is an indication that the descent of the "punishment of extirpation" (عذاب الاستئصال) upon them is preceded by divine "leading into error" (الإغواء), as alluded to in His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا أَرَدْنَا أَن نُّهْلِكَ قَرْيَةً أَمَرْنَا مُتْرَفِيهَا فَفَسَقُوا فِيهَا فَحَقَّ عَلَيْهَا الْقَوْلُ فَدَمَّرْنَاهَا تَدْمِيراً "When We desire to destroy a town, We command its affluent ones [to obey Us]. But they commit transgression in it, and so the word becomes due against it, and We destroy it utterly" (17:16). And He said: وَقَيَّضْنَا لَهُمْ قُرَنَاءَ فَزَيَّنُوا لَهُم مَّا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ وَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلُ "We assigned them companions who glosssed over for them what was before them and what was behind them. So the word became due against them" (41:25).

His saying: هُوَ رَبُّكُمْ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ "He is your Lord, and to Him you will be brought back" (11:34) is a "justification" (تعليل) for His saying: وَلَا يَنفَعُكُمْ نُصْحِي "My advice will not benefit you" etc., or for His saying: إِنَّمَا يَأْتِيكُم بِهِ اللَّهُ إِن شَاءَ "Allah will bring it to you only if He wishes" up to His saying: يُرِيدُ أَن يُغْوِيَكُمْ "desire to cause you to perish" altogether. The sum of it is that the matter of "governing the servants" (تدبير العباد) belongs to the Lord to Whom all matters return. Allah the Glorified is your Lord and to Him you return; thus, it is not for me to bring you a promised punishment, nor is it for you to frustrate Him if He wishes to bring the punishment upon you—and so He brings it to extirpate you—nor is it for my advice to benefit you if He wills to lead you into error in order to punish you.

Regarding His saying: إِن كَانَ اللَّهُ يُرِيدُ أَن يُغْوِيَكُمْ "if Allah should desire to cause you to perish," they have mentioned several modes of "interpretation" (التأويل):

First: That the meaning is "He punishes you for your disbelief." Allah the Exalted has called punishment ghayy (غياً) in His saying: فَسَوْفَ يَلْقَوْنَ غَيًّا "so they will soon face [the rewards of their] straying" (19:59).

Second: That the intended meaning is: "If Allah desires the punishment for your leading the creation into error (إغوائكم) and your misguiding them." It is the custom of the Arabs to name the punishment by the name of the thing being punished. Of this category is His saying: اللَّهُ يَسْتَهْزِئُ بِهِمْ "Allah shall deride them" (2:15), meaning He punishes them for their derision; and His saying: وَمَكَرُوا وَمَكَرَ اللَّهُ "And they plotted, and Allah devised" (3:54), meaning He punished them for their plotting, and so on.

Third: That leading into error (الإغواء) is in the sense of "destruction" (الإهلاك). Thus, the meaning is "He desires to destroy you." This is from their saying: "The young camel ghawiya (غوي)," when it perishes from drinking too much milk.

Fourth: That the people of Noah used to believe that Allah the Exalted misguides His servants from the religion, and that what they were upon was by the will of Allah; had it not been so, He would have changed it and compelled them to the contrary. So Noah said to them, by way of "amazement" (تعجب) at their statement and "denial" (إنكار) of it, that "my advice will not benefit you if the matter is as you say."

By reflecting on what we have previously presented, you will realize that the discourse is in no need of these interpretations.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَرَاهُ ۖ قُلْ إِنِ افْتَرَيْتُهُ فَعَلَيَّ إِجْرَامِي وَأَنَا بَرِيءٌ مِّمَّا تُجْرِمُونَ "Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it’? Say, ‘If I have fabricated it, my guilt will be upon me, and I am quit of your guilt.’" (11:35). The root of "guilt" (الجرم) — as mentioned by al-Raghib in his Mufradat — is the cutting of fruit from the tree. Ajrama (أجرم) means he became a possessor of jurm (جرم). It was then used metaphorically for every "disliked acquisition" (اكتساب مكروه). Thus, al-jurm (الجرم), with either a damma or fatha on the jim, means a disliked acquisition, which is "sin" (المعصية).

The verse occurs as a "parenthetical interjection" (اعتراض). The "subtle point" (النكتة) in it is that the call of Noah and his "arguments" (احتجاجات) against the idolatry of his people—especially what Allah the Exalted has cited in this Surah of his argumentation—is the thing most similar to the call of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his argumentation against the idolatry of his nation.

If you wish for further confirmation of this, return to Surah al-An'am—which is in reality the Surah of Argumentation—and compare what Allah the Exalted narrated from Noah in this Surah with what Allah commanded the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in that Surah in His saying: قُل لَّا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِندِي خَزَائِنُ اللَّهِ وَلَا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ وَلَا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ إِنِّي مَلَكٌ "Say, ‘I do not say to you that I possess the treasuries of Allah, nor do I know the Unseen, nor do I say to you that I am an angel’" (6:50) until He said: وَلَا تَطْرُدِ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُم بِالْغَدَاةِ وَالْعَشِيِّ "And do not drive away those who supplicate their Lord morning and evening" (6:52) until He said: قُل لَّا أَتَّبِعُ أَهْوَاءَكُمْ ۙ قَدْ ضَلَلْتُ إِذًا وَمَا أَنَا مِنَ الْمُهْتَدِينَ قُلْ إِنِّي عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّي وَكَذَّبْتُم بِهِ "Say, ‘I do not follow your desires, for then I would have gone astray, and I would not be among the rightly guided.’ Say, ‘Indeed I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord, and you have denied it.’" (6:56-57).

You may also apply the rest of what was mentioned of his arguments (peace be upon him) in Surah Nuh and al-A'raf to what was mentioned of arguments in Surah al-An'am and in this Surah; you will then witness the truth of what we have claimed.

Due to this similarity and "correspondence" (المناسبة), it was appropriate to transition—after mentioning the arguments of Noah (peace be upon him) in his warning to his people by the command of Allah the Glorified—to what they accused the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) of and the "fabrication" (الافتراء) against Allah they charged him with. Yet he does not warn them nor present arguments to them except as Noah (peace be upon him) warned and presented arguments to his people. This is like a king's messenger who warns his people and the rebels who disdain obedience, offering them advice and "completing the proof" (إتمام الحجة) against them, only for them to charge him with being a fabricator against the king and that there is no duty of obedience. He returns to them with advice a second time, mentioning the story of another sincere messenger from the king to another people whom he advised just as he advised them, but they did not gain insight and thus perished. Whenever he mentions his arguments and "exhortations" (مواعظ) to them, "intense emotion" (الوجد) and regret move him to remember their charge of fabrication against him. He regrets this, saying: You charge me with fabrication, yet I have mentioned to you nothing but what this messenger spread among his people of the word of wisdom and advice. Certainly, if I have fabricated it, then my guilt is upon me; do not accept my word, but I am quit of your deeds.

He, the Glorified, returned to the command for such a "confrontation" (المباراة) a second time at the end of the Surah after citing several stories of the messengers, where He said: وَكُلًّا نَّقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ أَنبَاءِ الرُّسُلِ مَا نُثَبِّتُ بِهِ فُؤَادَكَ "And We relate to you every significant saga of the apostles with which We strengthen your heart" (11:120) until He said: وَقُل لِّلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ اعْمَلُوا عَلَىٰ مَكَانَتِكُمْ إِنَّا عَامِلُونَ وَانتَظِرُوا إِنَّا مُنتَظِرُونَ "And say to those who do not believe, ‘Act according to your ability; we too are acting. And wait! We too are waiting.’" (11:121-122).

Some commentators mentioned that the verse is part of the completion of the story and the address in it is to Noah. The meaning would be: Do the people of Noah say Noah fabricated it? Say, O Noah, if I have fabricated it, then my guilt is upon me and I am quit of the guilt you commit. According to this, the discourse contains a type of "shift" (التفات) from the third person to the second person, but this is extremely far from the "context" (سياق) of the speech.

In His saying: وَأَنَا بَرِيءٌ مِّمَّا تُجْرِمُونَ "and I am quit of your guilt," there is an affirmation of a "continuous guilt" (إجرام مستمر) on their part, which has been presented as a "foregone conclusion" (إرسال المسلمات), just as in His saying: فَعَلَيَّ إِجْرَامِي "my guilt will be upon me" there is an affirmation of guilt. This is because what was mentioned of Noah's arguments, if it were fabrication, would be a lie in the sense that Noah (peace be upon him) did not use these arguments—even though they are true. However, insofar as they are "decisive rational arguments" (حجج عقلية قاطعة) that do not admit of falsehood, they establish for these disbelievers a continuous guilt in rejecting what he guides them to of faith and righteous deeds. Thus, in their departure from the requirement of these arguments, they are definitely guilty. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is guilty not definitely, but on the "supposition" (تقدير) that he is a fabricator—and he is not a fabricator.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Ibn Abi Nasr al-Bazanti, from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him), he said: Allah said regarding Noah (peace be upon him): وَلَا يَنفَعُكُمْ نُصْحِي إِنْ أَرَدْتُ أَنْ أَنصَحَ لَكُمْ إِن كَانَ اللَّهُ يُرِيدُ أَن يُغْوِيَكُمْ "My advice will not benefit you, were I to advise you, if Allah should desire to cause you to perish." He said: "The matter is up to Allah—He guides and He misleads."

I say: Its explanation has already passed.

In Tafsir al-Burhan: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَرَاهُ "Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it’?" the verse: al-Shaybani in Nahj al-Bayan narrated from Muqatil, who said: "The disbelievers of Mecca said: 'Indeed Muhammad fabricated the Quran.'" He said: "The like of that has been narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon them)."

Ayahs

وَأُوحِىَ إِلَىٰ نُوحٍ أَنَّهُۥ لَن يُؤْمِنَ مِن قَوْمِكَ إِلَّا مَن قَدْ ءَامَنَ فَلَا تَبْتَئِسْ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ (٣٦)
It was revealed to Noah: ‘None of your people will believe except those who already have faith; so do not sorrow for what they used to do. (36)
وَٱصْنَعِ ٱلْفُلْكَ بِأَعْيُنِنَا وَوَحْيِنَا وَلَا تُخَـٰطِبْنِى فِى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّهُم مُّغْرَقُونَ (٣٧)
Build the ark before Our eyes and by Our revelation, and do not plead with Me for those who are wrongdoers: they shall indeed be drowned.’ (37)
وَيَصْنَعُ ٱلْفُلْكَ وَكُلَّمَا مَرَّ عَلَيْهِ مَلَأٌ مِّن قَوْمِهِۦ سَخِرُوا۟ مِنْهُ ۚ قَالَ إِن تَسْخَرُوا۟ مِنَّا فَإِنَّا نَسْخَرُ مِنكُمْ كَمَا تَسْخَرُونَ (٣٨)
As he was building the ark, whenever the elders of his people passed by him, they would ridicule him. He said, ‘If you ridicule us [today], we shall ridicule you [tomorrow] just as you ridicule us [now]. (38)
فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ مَن يَأْتِيهِ عَذَابٌ يُخْزِيهِ وَيَحِلُّ عَلَيْهِ عَذَابٌ مُّقِيمٌ (٣٩)
Soon you will know whom a disgraceful punishment will overtake and on whom a lasting punishment will descend.’ (39)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا وَفَارَ ٱلتَّنُّورُ قُلْنَا ٱحْمِلْ فِيهَا مِن كُلٍّ زَوْجَيْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ وَأَهْلَكَ إِلَّا مَن سَبَقَ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْقَوْلُ وَمَنْ ءَامَنَ ۚ وَمَآ ءَامَنَ مَعَهُۥٓ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ (٤٠)
When Our edict came and the oven gushed [a stream of water], We said, ‘Carry in it a pair of every kind [of animal], along with your family—except those [of them] against whom the edict has already been given—and those who have faith.’ And none believed with him except a few. (40)
۞ وَقَالَ ٱرْكَبُوا۟ فِيهَا بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ مَجْر۪ىٰهَا وَمُرْسَىٰهَآ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٤١)
He said, ‘Board it: In the Name of Allah it shall set sail and cast anchor. Indeed my Lord is all-forgiving, all-merciful.’ (41)
وَهِىَ تَجْرِى بِهِمْ فِى مَوْجٍ كَٱلْجِبَالِ وَنَادَىٰ نُوحٌ ٱبْنَهُۥ وَكَانَ فِى مَعْزِلٍ يَـٰبُنَىَّ ٱرْكَب مَّعَنَا وَلَا تَكُن مَّعَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٤٢)
And it sailed along with them amid waves [rising] like mountains. Noah called out to his son, who stood aloof, ‘O my son! ‘Board with us, and do not be with the faithless!’ (42)
قَالَ سَـَٔاوِىٓ إِلَىٰ جَبَلٍ يَعْصِمُنِى مِنَ ٱلْمَآءِ ۚ قَالَ لَا عَاصِمَ ٱلْيَوْمَ مِنْ أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا مَن رَّحِمَ ۚ وَحَالَ بَيْنَهُمَا ٱلْمَوْجُ فَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُغْرَقِينَ (٤٣)
He said, ‘I shall take refuge on a mountain; it will protect me from the flood.’ He said, ‘There is none today who can protect from Allah’s edict, except someone upon whom He has mercy.’ Then the waves came between them, and he was among those who were drowned. (43)
وَقِيلَ يَـٰٓأَرْضُ ٱبْلَعِى مَآءَكِ وَيَـٰسَمَآءُ أَقْلِعِى وَغِيضَ ٱلْمَآءُ وَقُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ وَٱسْتَوَتْ عَلَى ٱلْجُودِىِّ ۖ وَقِيلَ بُعْدًا لِّلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٤٤)
Then it was said, ‘O earth, swallow your water! O sky, leave off!’ The waters receded; the edict was carried out, and it settled on [Mount] Judi. Then it was said, ‘Away with the wrongdoing lot!’ (44)
وَنَادَىٰ نُوحٌ رَّبَّهُۥ فَقَالَ رَبِّ إِنَّ ٱبْنِى مِنْ أَهْلِى وَإِنَّ وَعْدَكَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَأَنتَ أَحْكَمُ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ (٤٥)
Noah called out to his Lord, and said, ‘My Lord! My son is indeed from my family. Your promise is indeed true, and You are the fairest of all judges.’ (45)
قَالَ يَـٰنُوحُ إِنَّهُۥ لَيْسَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ عَمَلٌ غَيْرُ صَـٰلِحٍ ۖ فَلَا تَسْـَٔلْنِ مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَعِظُكَ أَن تَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ (٤٦)
Said He, ‘O Noah! Indeed, He is not of your family. Indeed, he is [personification of] unrighteous conduct. So do not ask Me [something] of which you have no knowledge. I advise you lest you should be among the ignorant.’ (46)
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّىٓ أَعُوذُ بِكَ أَنْ أَسْـَٔلَكَ مَا لَيْسَ لِى بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ ۖ وَإِلَّا تَغْفِرْ لِى وَتَرْحَمْنِىٓ أَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٤٧)
He said, ‘My Lord! I seek Your protection lest I should ask You something of which I have no knowledge. If You do not forgive me and have mercy upon me I shall be among the losers.’ (47)
قِيلَ يَـٰنُوحُ ٱهْبِطْ بِسَلَـٰمٍ مِّنَّا وَبَرَكَـٰتٍ عَلَيْكَ وَعَلَىٰٓ أُمَمٍ مِّمَّن مَّعَكَ ۚ وَأُمَمٌ سَنُمَتِّعُهُمْ ثُمَّ يَمَسُّهُم مِّنَّا عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٤٨)
It was said, ‘O Noah! Disembark in peace from Us and with [Our] blessings upon you and upon nations [to descend] from those who are with you, and nations whom We shall provide for, then a painful punishment from Us shall befall them.’ (48)
تِلْكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهَآ إِلَيْكَ ۖ مَا كُنتَ تَعْلَمُهَآ أَنتَ وَلَا قَوْمُكَ مِن قَبْلِ هَـٰذَا ۖ فَٱصْبِرْ ۖ إِنَّ ٱلْعَـٰقِبَةَ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ (٤٩)
These are accounts of the Unseen, which We reveal to you. Neither you nor your people used to know them before this. So be patient. Indeed the outcome will be in favour of the Godwary. (49)

(Commentary)

This is the completion of the story of Nuh (peace be upon him). It comprises several sections, such as his (peace be upon him) informing his people of the descent of the punishment, his being commanded to build the Ark, the manner in which the punishment—the Flood—descended, the story of his drowned son, and the story of his salvation and the salvation of those with him. However, all of these return, from one perspective, to a single section: the section of the decisive judgment (فصل القضاء) between him (peace be upon him) and his people.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأُوحِيَ إِلى نُوحٍ أَنَّهُ لَنْ يُؤْمِنَ مِنْ قَوْمِكَ إِلَّا مَنْ قَدْ آمَنَ فَلا تَبْتَئِسْ بِما كانُوا يَفْعَلُونَ — "It was revealed to Noah: ‘None of your people will believe except those who have already believed; so do not be saddened by what they used to do.’" (11:36). Being saddened (الابتئاس) is derived from misery (البؤس), which is a grief accompanied by submission and helplessness.

His saying: لَنْ يُؤْمِنَ مِنْ قَوْمِكَ إِلَّا مَنْ قَدْ آمَنَ — "None of your people will believe except those who have already believed" is a cause for him (peace be upon him) to lose hope and despair regarding the faith of the disbelievers (الكفار) among his people thereafter. For this reason, He followed it with: فَلا تَبْتَئِسْ بِما كانُوا يَفْعَلُونَ — "so do not be saddened by what they used to do." This is because one who calls to a matter only feels sadness and grief over the opposition and rebellion of those called as long as he hopes for their faith and their response to his call. However, once he despairs of their response, he no longer concerns himself with them, nor does he exhaust himself in calling them to listen and obey, or in persisting with them to turn toward him. If he calls them after that, it is only for another purpose, such as the completion of the proof (إتمام الحجة) and the establishing of an excuse (إبراز المعذرة).

Accordingly, in His saying: فَلا تَبْتَئِسْ بِما كانُوا يَفْعَلُونَ — "so do not be saddened by what they used to do," there is a consolation from Allah for Nuh (peace be upon him) and a soothing of his noble soul, due to the indication in the speech that the time for the decisive judgment (فصل القضاء) between him and his people had arrived. It also serves to protect his soul from the anguish and grief caused by what he witnessed of their actions toward him and the believers (المؤمنين) among his people—their harming of them over a long period (nearly a thousand years) during which he remained among them.

It appears from the words of some commentators that they inferred from His saying: لَنْ يُؤْمِنَ مِنْ قَوْمِكَ إِلَّا مَنْ قَدْ آمَنَ — "None of your people will believe except those who have already believed" that whoever among them disbelieved would never believe after this moment, just as those who believed in him were firm in their faith and constant upon it. However, the focus in the speech is only concerned with explaining the lack of faith of the disbelievers thereafter. As for the faith of the believers, it was only mentioned in terms of its prior realization, and there is no indication in the exception for anything more than that. As for their steadfastness and permanence in faith, there is no evidence for it here.

From the verse, it is inferred, firstly: that the disbelievers are not punished as long as faith is hoped for from them. But when the disposition of disbelief (ملكة الكفر) and the filth of polytheism (رجس الشرك) become fixed in them, the word of punishment becomes due upon them.

Secondly: that what Allah, the Glorified, narrated of the supplication of Nuh in his saying: وَقالَ نُوحٌ رَبِّ لا تَذَرْ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ دَيَّاراً إِنَّكَ إِنْ تَذَرْهُمْ يُضِلُّوا عِبادَكَ وَلا يَلِدُوا إِلَّا فاجِراً كَفَّاراً — "And Noah said, ‘My Lord! Do not leave on the earth any inhabitant from among the faithless. If You leave them, they will lead Your servants astray, and will beget none but vicious ingrates.’" (71:26-27) occurred between His saying: أَنَّهُ لَنْ يُؤْمِنَ مِنْ قَوْمِكَ إِلَّا مَنْ قَدْ آمَنَ — "None of your people will believe except those who have already believed" etc., and His saying: وَاصْنَعِ الْفُلْكَ — "Build the Ark" until His saying: إِنَّهُمْ مُغْرَقُونَ — "they shall indeed be drowned."

This is because—as some have mentioned—there is no way to know the lack of future faith of the disbelievers through reason; rather, the way to it is through hearing via revelation (الوحي). Thus, he (peace be upon him) first learned from His revelation to him that "none of your people will believe except those who have already believed"—that none of them would believe after that, nor would there be among their progeny those who would believe in Allah. Then he supplicated against them for punishment and mentioned in his supplication what had been revealed to him. When Allah answered his supplication and willed their destruction, He commanded him (peace be upon him) to build the ship and informed him that they were to be drowned.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاصْنَعِ الْفُلْكَ بِأَعْيُنِنا وَوَحْيِنا وَلا تُخاطِبْنِي فِي الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا إِنَّهُمْ مُغْرَقُونَ — "Build the Ark under Our eyes and by Our revelation, and do not plead with Me for those who are wrongdoers; they shall indeed be drowned." (11:37). The Ark (الفلك) is the ship; its singular and plural forms are the same. "Eyes" (الأعين) is a plural of paucity for "eye" (العين); it is pluralized to indicate the abundance and intensity of oversight, for the phrase is a metaphor for oversight during the construction.

The mention of "eyes" is an indicator that what is meant by "revelation" (الوحي) is not this specific revelation—meaning His saying: "Build the Ark" etc.—such that it would be a revelation of a ruling. Rather, it is a revelation in the context of action, which is a practical directing and guidance through the support of the Holy Spirit (روح القدس), which indicates: "do this and do that," as He, the Exalted, mentioned regarding the Imams from the progeny of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) in His saying: وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ الْخَيْراتِ وَإِقامَ الصَّلاةِ وَإِيتاءَ الزَّكاةِ وَكانُوا لَنا عابِدِينَ — "and We revealed to them the performance of good deeds, the maintenance of prayer, and the giving of zakat, and they used to be Our worshippers." (21:73). This was previously alluded to in earlier discussions and will come, Allah willing, in the commentary on the verse.

His saying: وَلا تُخاطِبْنِي فِي الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا — "and do not plead with Me for those who are wrongdoers" means: do not ask Me for anything regarding their affair to repel the evil and punishment, nor intercede for them to avert the harm from them, because the judgment is decided and the decree is final. By this, it becomes clear that His saying: إِنَّهُمْ مُغْرَقُونَ — "they shall indeed be drowned" is in the position of providing the reason for His saying: "and do not plead with Me" etc., or for the entirety of His saying: "Build the Ark under Our eyes and by Our revelation, and do not plead with Me for those who are wrongdoers." It also becomes clear that His saying: "and do not plead with Me" etc., is a metaphor for intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah.

The meaning is: Build the ship under Our complete oversight and Our instruction to you, and do not ask Me to avert the punishment from these who have done wrong, for drowning has been decreed for them as a final judgment that cannot be turned back.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَصْنَعُ الْفُلْكَ وَكُلَّما مَرَّ عَلَيْهِ مَلَأٌ مِنْ قَوْمِهِ سَخِرُوا مِنْهُ قالَ إِنْ تَسْخَرُوا مِنَّا فَإِنَّا نَسْخَرُ مِنْكُمْ كَما تَسْخَرُونَ — "As he was building the Ark, whenever the elders of his people passed by him, they would ridicule him. He said, ‘If you ridicule us [now], we shall ridicule you [later] just as you ridicule [us now].’" (11:38). It is said in al-Majma': Ridicule (السخرية) is to manifest the opposite of what is hidden in a way that implies a weakness of intellect. From it comes "subjugation" (التسخير) for a humbling that is a weakness through coercion. The difference between ridicule and play (اللعب) is that in ridicule there is deception and belittlement, and it only occurs regarding living beings, whereas play can be with inanimate objects. End quote.

Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: I ridiculed him (سخرت منه) and sought to ridicule him (استسخرته) to mock him. He, the Exalted, said: "If you ridicule us, we shall ridicule you just as you ridicule [us]" and "so you will soon know," and "Nay, you wonder, while they ridicule." It is said: a man is sukhrah (سُخَرَة)—with a damma then a fatha—for one who ridicules, and sukhrah (سُخْرَة)—with a damma then a sukun—for one who is ridiculed. Sukhriyyah (السُّخْرِيَّة)—with a damma—and sikhriyyah (السِّخْرِيَّة)—with a kasra—refer to the act of the ridiculer. End quote.

His saying: وَيَصْنَعُ الْفُلْكَ — "As he was building the Ark" is a narration of a past state, depicting what Nuh (peace be upon him) underwent of harm from his people, and how one group after another stood to insult and mock him during the construction of the ship, and his patience upon that for the sake of the divine call and the establishing of the proof against them, without failing or turning back.

His saying: كُلَّما مَرَّ عَلَيْهِ مَلَأٌ مِنْ قَوْمِهِ سَخِرُوا مِنْهُ — "whenever the elders of his people passed by him, they would ridicule him" is a circumstantial clause (حال) for the subject of "building." The "elders" (الملأ) here refers to the group that is taken into account. In the speech, there is an indication that they used to come to him while he was building the Ark, group after group, passing by him in ridicule, and that he (peace be upon him) was building it in their sight and in a public thoroughfare.

His saying: قالَ إِنْ تَسْخَرُوا مِنَّا فَإِنَّا نَسْخَرُ مِنْكُمْ كَما تَسْخَرُونَ — "He said, ‘If you ridicule us, we shall ridicule you just as you ridicule [us]’" is in the position of an answer to a hypothetical question, as if a speaker said: "So what did Nuh (peace be upon him) say?" It was replied: "He said: If you ridicule us, we shall ridicule you." Therefore, the sentence is detached without a conjunction.

He (peace be upon him) did not say: "If you ridicule me, I shall ridicule you," in order to defend himself and the band of believers with him. It is as if he was seeking help from his family and followers in that, and they were participating with him in the construction of the ship, so the ridicule encompassed them all. The apparent meaning of the speech is that the elders were confronting Nuh and those with him during the construction of the ship with ridicule of Nuh and accusing him (peace be upon him) of confusion and madness, so their mockery included Nuh and those with him, even if they only mentioned Nuh specifically in their mockery.

Furthermore, nature and custom dictate that they would also ridicule his followers just as they ridiculed him, for they were members of one society linked by social interaction, even if their ridicule of his followers was, in reality, a ridicule of him, because he is the foundation upon whom the call stands. Therefore, it was said: "they ridiculed him" (سخروا منه) and it was not said: "they ridiculed him and the believers."

Ridicule, although it is ugly and stems from ignorance when it is initiated, is permissible when it is a recompense and by way of reciprocation, especially when it results in a rational benefit, such as carrying out a resolution and completing the proof. He, the Exalted, said: فَيَسْخَرُونَ مِنْهُمْ سَخِرَ اللهُ مِنْهُمْ وَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ — "they ridicule them: Allah shall ridicule them, and there is a painful punishment for them." (9:79). The consideration of recompense and reciprocation in kind in the verse is indicated by His saying: كَما تَسْخَرُونَ — "just as you ridicule."

His saying, the Exalted: فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ يَأْتِيهِ عَذابٌ يُخْزِيهِ وَيَحِلُّ عَلَيْهِ عَذابٌ مُقِيمٌ — "Soon you will know who will be overtaken by a punishment that will disgrace him, and on whom there will descend a lasting punishment." (11:39). The context dictates that His saying: "Soon you will know" is a derivation from the preceding conditional sentence: "If you ridicule us, we shall ridicule you." The derived sentence is the core of the ridicule that Nuh (peace be upon him) brought, and His saying: "who will be overtaken by a punishment that will disgrace him" etc., is related to "you will know" as the object of knowledge.

The meaning is: If you ridicule us, we shall ridicule you, saying to you: Soon you will know who will be overtaken by the punishment? We or you? And this is a ridicule with a truthful word.

His saying: مَنْ يَأْتِيهِ عَذابٌ يُخْزِيهِ — "who will be overtaken by a punishment that will disgrace him" refers to the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال) in this world, which is the drowning that disgraced and humbled them. What is meant by His saying: وَيَحِلُّ عَلَيْهِ عَذابٌ مُقِيمٌ — "and on whom there will descend a lasting punishment" is that a fixed, inseparable punishment will descend upon him, which is the punishment of the Fire in the Hereafter. The evidence for what we have mentioned—that the first punishment is in this world and the second is the punishment of the Hereafter—is the contrast, the repetition of "punishment" (عذاب) as an indefinite noun, and the description of the first as "disgraceful" (الإخزاء) and the second as "lasting" (الإقامة).

Some have taken His saying: "Soon you will know" as a complete sentence without mentioning the object of knowledge, and His saying: "who will be overtaken by a punishment that will disgrace him" etc., as the beginning of a new speech from Nuh, but this is far from the context.

His saying, the Exalted: حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَمْرُنا وَفارَ التَّنُّورُ — "Until when Our edict came and the oven gushed," (11:40) to the end of the verse. It is said: the pot "fawrs" (يفور) when it boils and its boiling intensifies; and the fire "fawrs" when it ignites and its flame rises. The oven (التنور) is the bread oven; it is a word in which the two languages, Arabic and Persian, agree, or the word is Persian in origin.

The gushing of the oven (فوران التنور) is the springing of water and its rising from it. It has been mentioned in narrations that the first beginning of the Flood on that day was through the bursting forth of water from an oven. Accordingly, the definite article in "the oven" (التنور) is for specification (للعهد), referring to an oven known in the discourse. It is also possible that the phrase is a metaphor for the intensification of the wrath of Allah, the Exalted, being of the same type as their saying: "the furnace grew hot" (حمي الوطيس) when the war intensifies.

So His saying: حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَمْرُنا وَفارَ التَّنُّورُ means: the matter continued in that way until Our command came—meaning the Lordly command was realized and applied to them—and the water gushed from the oven, or the wrath of the Lord, the Exalted, intensified, We said to him such and such.

Regarding "the oven" (التنور), there are other opinions far from understanding, such as the one who said it refers to the break of dawn, which was the first appearance of the Flood; and the saying of some that it refers to the highest and most noble part of the earth, meaning the water burst forth from the elevated places and highlands; and the saying of others that "the oven" is the face of the earth.

His saying: قُلْنَا احْمِلْ فِيها مِنْ كُلٍّ زَوْجَيْنِ اثْنَيْنِ — "We said, ‘Carry in it a pair of every kind [of animal]," means We commanded Nuh (peace be upon him) to carry in the ship from every species of animal two mates, which are the male and the female.

His saying: وَأَهْلَكَ إِلَّا مَنْ سَبَقَ عَلَيْهِ الْقَوْلُ — "and your family, except him against whom the word has already gone forth," means: and carry in it your family—those specifically related to him, such as his wife, children, the wives of his children, and their children—except the one against whom Our word has already passed and Our covenant has preceded that he is to perish. This exception was his treacherous wife, whom Allah, the Exalted, mentions in His saying: ضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا امْرَأَتَ نُوحٍ وَامْرَأَتَ لُوطٍ كانَتا تَحْتَ عَبْدَيْنِ مِنْ عِبادِنا صالِحَيْنِ فَخانَتاهُما — "Allah draws an example for the faithless: the wife of Noah and the wife of Lot. They were under two of Our righteous servants, yet they betrayed them." (66:10). And the son of Nuh, whom Allah, the Exalted, mentions in the following verses. Nuh (peace be upon him) used to think that the exception was only his wife, until Allah, the Glorified, clarified that his son was not of his family and that he was an unrighteous deed; at that point, he knew that he was among the wrongdoers.

His saying: وَمَنْ آمَنَ وَما آمَنَ مَعَهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ — "and those who have faith.’ And none believed with him except a few." (11:40). That is: and carry in it those who believed in you from your people other than your family, because those of his family who believed were commanded to be carried by His saying: "and your family." And none believed in him from the people except a few.

In His saying: وَما آمَنَ مَعَهُ — "And none believed with him," rather than saying "believed in him," there is an allusion that the meaning is: none believed in Allah along with Nuh except a few. This is more appropriate to the context, which is the context of mentioning those whom Allah saved from the punishment of drowning, and the criterion for that is faith in Allah and submission to His Lordship. Likewise, in His saying: إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ — "except a few," rather than saying "a few of them," it emphasizes their independence—that those who believed were few in themselves, not just in comparison to the people, for they were in a state of extreme fewness.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ ارْكَبُوا فِيها بِسْمِ اللهِ مَجْراها وَمُرْساها إِنَّ رَبِّي لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — "He said, ‘Board it: In the Name of Allah it shall set sail and cast anchor. Indeed my Lord is all-forgiving, all-merciful.’" (11:41). Majraha (مَجْراها) is read with a fatha on the mim, meaning the course of the ship and its sailing; and mujraha (مُجْراها) with a damma on the mim, meaning the sailing of the ship and its driving. Mursaha (مُرْساها) with a damma on the mim is a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) synonymous with irsa’ (الإرساء), which is fixing and stopping. He, the Exalted, said: وَالْجِبالَ أَرْساها — "and the mountains, He made them firm." (79:32).

His saying: وَقالَ ارْكَبُوا فِيها — "He said, ‘Board it’" is conjoined to His saying in the previous verse: "Our edict came"—meaning: until when Nuh said, etc. His address was to his family and the rest of the believers, or to everyone in the ship.

His saying: بِسْمِ اللهِ مَجْراها وَمُرْساها — "In the Name of Allah it shall set sail and cast anchor" is a naming (تسمية) by him (peace be upon him) through which he draws goodness and blessing for the sailing of the ship and its anchoring. For in the linking of an act or an affair to the Name of Allah, and its connection to Him, there is a protection for it from destruction and corruption, and a safeguarding from misguidance and loss. This is because He, the Exalted, is high in degrees and unassailable in side; there is no way for extinction, non-existence, fatigue, or exhaustion to reach Him. Thus, whatever is connected to Him is inevitably protected from the occurrence of accidental evil.

So he (peace be upon him) connects the sailing of the ship and its anchoring to the Name of Allah, and these are the two apparent causes for the salvation of the ship and those in it from drowning. These two causes only succeed if the divine care encompasses those who boarded it, and the care only encompasses through the inclusion of divine forgiveness for the mistakes of its passengers and divine mercy for them, so that they may be saved from drowning and live at ease on the earth. Therefore, he (peace be upon him) reasoned his naming by his saying: إِنَّ رَبِّي لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — "Indeed my Lord is all-forgiving, all-merciful." That is: I only mention the Name of Allah over the sailing of my ship and its anchoring because He is my Lord, the All-Forgiving, the Most Merciful; it is for Him to preserve its sailing and its anchoring from disorder and confusion so that we may be saved thereby from drowning through His forgiveness and mercy.

Nuh (peace be upon him) is the first human about whom Allah, the Glorified, narrated the naming by His noble Name in what He revealed of His Book. Thus, he (peace be upon him) is the first opener to open this door, just as he is the first to establish the proof for monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts, the first to bring a Book and a Law (شريعة), and the first to rise for the balancing of classes and the removal of contradiction from human society.

What we have presented of the meaning of His saying: "In the Name of Allah it shall set sail and cast anchor" is based on what is apparent—that the sentence is a naming by Nuh (peace be upon him), and that majra and mursa are mim-infinitives. It is also possible that it is a naming by those with Nuh by his command, or that majraha and mursaha are nouns of time or place, in which case the meaning would differ.

It is said in al-Kashshaf regarding the verse: It is permissible for it to be one sentence or two sentences. As for the single sentence, "In the Name of Allah" is connected to "Board it" as a circumstantial clause (حال) from the "waw" [in irkabu], meaning: board it naming Allah or saying "In the Name of Allah" at the time of its sailing and the time of its anchoring—either because majra and mursa are for time, or because they are infinitives like ijra’ and irsa’ from which the added time has been omitted, like their saying: "the setting of the star" and "the arrival of the pilgrim." It is also permissible that the place of sailing and anchoring is intended, and their accusative case is due to the meaning of the verb in "In the Name of Allah" or due to the intention of "saying" therein.

As for the two sentences, "In the Name of Allah it shall set sail and cast anchor" would be a sentence of a subject and predicate, extemporized (مقتضبة)—meaning: In the Name of Allah is its sailing and its anchoring. It is narrated that when he wanted it to sail, he said: "In the Name of Allah," and it sailed; and when he wanted it to anchor, he said: "In the Name of Allah," and it anchored. It is also permissible that the "Name" (الاسم) is inserted (يقحم) as in the saying: "Then the Name of Peace be upon you both," and Allah is intended for its sailing and its anchoring.

He said: Majraha and mursaha are read with a fatha on the mim from jara and rasa, either as infinitives, times, or places. Mujahid read: mujriha and mursiha in the form of the active participle, in the genitive position as attributes of Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهِيَ تَجْرِي بِهِمْ فِي مَوْجٍ كَالْجِبالِ — "It sailed with them amid waves like mountains," (11:42). The pronoun refers to the ship. "Waves" (الموج) is a collective noun like "dates" or the plural of "wave" (موجة)—according to what is said—which is a great piece that rises from the body of the water. In the verse, there is an indication that the ship was traveling upon the water and was not swimming inside the water like fish, as has been suggested.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنادى نُوحٌ ابْنَهُ وَكانَ فِي مَعْزِلٍ يا بُنَيَّ ارْكَبْ مَعَنا وَلا تَكُنْ مَعَ الْكافِرِينَ — "and Noah called out to his son, who stood aloof, ‘O my son! Board with us, and do not be with the faithless!’" (11:42). Ma'zil (مَعْزِل) is a noun of place from "isolation" (العزل); his son had isolated himself from his father and the believers in a place not near them. Therefore, He said: "and Noah called out to his son" and did not say: "and Noah said to his son."

The meaning is: Nuh called his son, and his son was in an isolated place far from them, and he said in his call: "O my son"—using the diminutive and the possessive to indicate compassion and mercy—"board the ship with us and do not be with the disbelievers," thereby sharing with them in the affliction as you shared with them in companionship and in not boarding the ship. He (peace be upon him) did not say: "and do not be of the disbelievers" because he did not know of his hypocrisy and that he was not a believer except in words; therefore, he called him to board.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ سَآوِي إِلى جَبَلٍ يَعْصِمُنِي مِنَ الْماءِ قالَ لا عاصِمَ الْيَوْمَ مِنْ أَمْرِ اللهِ — "He said, ‘I shall take refuge on a mountain; it will protect me from the water.’ He said, ‘There is no protector today from Allah’s edict," (11:43) etc. Al-Raghib said: The refuge (المأوى) is the infinitive of awa ya’wi awyan and ma’wa. You say: he took refuge (أوى) in such a thing: he joined it; and someone else gave him refuge (آواه). End quote.

The meaning is: The son of Nuh said, answering his father and rejecting his command: I shall join a mountain that will protect me and guard me from the water so I do not drown. Nuh said: There is no protector today—and it was a day when the wrath of Allah intensified and He decreed drowning for the people of the earth except those who took refuge with Allah—from Allah, neither a mountain nor anything else. And the waves came between Nuh and his son, so his son was among the drowned. Had the waves not come between them and the speech not been cut off thereby, he would have recognized his disbelief and disassociated from him.

In the speech, there is an indication that their land was a mountainous land, requiring no extra effort for a person to ascend some of the mountains that were there.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقِيلَ يا أَرْضُ ابْلَعِي ماءَكِ وَيا سَماءُ أَقْلِعِي وَغِيضَ الْماءُ وَقُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ وَاسْتَوَتْ عَلَى الْجُودِيِّ وَقِيلَ بُعْداً لِلْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ — "Then it was said, ‘O earth, swallow your water! O sky, leave off!’ The waters subsided; the edict was fulfilled, and it settled on [Mount] Judi. Then it was said, ‘Away with the wrongdoing lot!’" (11:44). Swallowing (البلع) is the passing of a thing through the throat to the interior. Leaving off (الإقلاع) is the ceasing and abandoning of a thing from its root. Subsiding (الغيض) is the earth's drawing of the liquid and moist from its surface to its interior; it is like drying up. It is said: the earth "subsided" (غاضت) the water, meaning it diminished it.

Al-Judi (الجودي) is a general term for a mountain and hard ground. It is said: it is a mountain in the land of Mosul in a chain of mountains ending at Armenia, which is called "Ararat."

His saying: وَقِيلَ يا أَرْضُ ابْلَعِي ماءَكِ وَيا سَماءُ أَقْلِعِي — "Then it was said, ‘O earth, swallow your water! O sky, leave off!’" is a call issued from the court of Grandeur and Majesty. The name of its speaker—who is Allah, exalted is His Name—is not explicitly mentioned for the sake of glorification. The command is an existential one (تكويني) carried by the word "Be" (كن) issued from the Possessor of the Throne, the Exalted. It results, without any gap, in the earth swallowing what is on its surface of the water bursting from its springs, and the sky ceasing its rains.

In this, there is an indication that the earth and the sky were partners in the overflowing of the water by the command of Allah, as explained by His saying, the Exalted: فَفَتَحْنا أَبْوابَ السَّماءِ بِماءٍ مُنْهَمِرٍ وَفَجَّرْنَا الْأَرْضَ عُيُوناً فَالْتَقَى الْماءُ عَلى أَمْرٍ قَدْ قُدِرَ — "Then We opened the gates of the sky with pouring water, and We made the earth gush forth with springs, and the waters met for a matter already ordained." (54:11-12).

His saying: وَغِيضَ الْماءُ — "The waters subsided" means the water diminished and dried up from the surface of the earth, and the land was uncovered. This only happens naturally by the gathering of what can be gathered of it in pools and the formation of seas and lakes, and the drying up of what is on the rest of the land.

His saying: وَقُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ — "the edict was fulfilled" means that what was promised to Nuh (peace be upon him) of the punishment of the people was accomplished, and the divine command for their drowning and the cleansing of the earth from them was executed. That is: what was told "Be" was as it was told. For the "fulfillment of the edict" (قضاء الأمر), just as it is said regarding the making of a ruling and its issuance, it is likewise said regarding its implementation, execution, and realization in the external world. However, the divine decree (القضاء الإلهي) and the Lordly ruling, which is the very external existence, its making and its execution are one; the difference is only according to the expression.

His saying: وَاسْتَوَتْ عَلَى الْجُودِيِ — "and it settled on [Mount] Judi" means the ship settled on the mountain or on the well-known Mount Judi. It is an informing of the conclusion of what Nuh and those with him were facing of the matter of the Flood.

His saying: وَقِيلَ بُعْداً لِلْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ — "Then it was said, ‘Away with the wrongdoing lot!’" means Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: "Away with the wrongdoing people," meaning: let them be far away. Thus, He distanced them thereby from His mercy and expelled them from the abode of His honor. The discussion regarding the omission of the subject of "it was said" (قيل) here is the same as the discussion regarding it in the previous "it was said."

The command also in His saying: "Away with the wrongdoing lot!" is like the two previous commands: "O earth, swallow your water! O sky, leave off!"—it is existential (تكويني). Thus, it is the very thing that Allah executed upon them of drowning, leading to their disgrace in this world and their loss in the Hereafter, even if from another perspective it is of the genus of the legislative command (الأمر التشريعي) because it is derived from their opposition to the divine command for faith and action, and it is a recompense for them for their arrogance and their seeking of superiority over Allah, the Mighty and Majestic.

For the turning away from mentioning the subjects in His saying: "Then it was said, ‘O earth...’" etc., and His saying: "the edict was fulfilled," and His saying: "Then it was said, ‘Away...’" etc., in the verse, there is another common reason: these great, formidable, and staggering matters can only be accomplished by the One, the Subduer, who has no partner in His affair. Thus, the imagination does not go to anyone else even if He is not mentioned for His act; for it is nothing but His act, whether mentioned or not.

For a similar point, the subject of "the waters subsided" (غِيضَ الْماءُ)—which is the earth—was omitted, as was the subject of "it settled on [Mount] Judi" (اسْتَوَتْ عَلَى الْجُودِيِ)—which is the ship. The wrongdoing people were not specified as being the people of Nuh, nor were the saved specified as being Nuh (peace be upon him) and those with him in the ship. For the verse reached, in its wondrous eloquence regarding the context of the story, a level where there is only a sky sending down its rains, an earth that burst with its springs and was submerged in water, a ship sailing in its waves, a fulfilled edict, wrongdoing people who are the people of Nuh, and a divine command promising the people destruction. So if the water subsided, it is only the earth that subsides it; and if something settled and became level, it is only the ship that settles on the earth. Just as if it is said: "O earth, swallow your water! O sky, leave off!" and it is said: "Away with the wrongdoing lot!" then the speaker is only Allah, exalted is His Name, and the wrongdoing people are those against whom the punishment was decreed. If it is said: "the edict was fulfilled," then the judge is only Allah, the Glorified, and the edict is what He promised Nuh and forbade him from reviewing with Him—which is that they were to be drowned. If it is said to the sky: "leave off" after it was said to the earth: "swallow your water," then what is intended is its leaving off and its holding back its water.

Thus, in the noble verse, there is a wondrous gathering of the causes of conciseness and a subtle agreement among them. The verse stands at a wondrous position of the miraculous eloquence of the Quran that dazzles the intellects and staggers the minds, even though all the Quranic verses are miraculous in their eloquence.

The masters of eloquence and the scholars of rhetoric have concerned themselves with its matter, diving into the depths of its sea and bringing out what they were able to attain of its pearls. Yet it is—as they have admitted—nothing but like a handful from a sea or a pebble from a desert.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنادى نُوحٌ رَبَّهُ فَقالَ رَبِّ إِنَّ ابْنِي مِنْ أَهْلِي وَإِنَّ وَعْدَكَ الْحَقُّ وَأَنْتَ أَحْكَمُ الْحاكِمِينَ — "Noah called out to his Lord, and said, ‘My Lord! My son is indeed from my family. Your promise is indeed true, and You are the fairest of all judges.’" (11:45). This is the supplication of Nuh (peace be upon him) for his son who stayed behind from boarding the ship. His last encounter with him was on the day he boarded the ship and found him in isolation, so he called him and commanded him to board the ship, but he did not obey. Then the waves came between them, and Nuh (peace be upon him)—while he thought he was a believer in Allah—found him to be of his family, and Allah had promised him the salvation of his family.

Due to the anguish and grief he felt, he raised his voice in supplication, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: "Noah called out to his Lord" and He did not say: "he asked" or "he said" or "he supplicated." Raising the voice in a cry for help by one in distress, whose harm has intensified and whose anguish has been stirred, is a natural matter. The supplication—meaning Nuh's calling out to his Lord regarding his son—even if mentioned in the story after the mention of the completion of the drowning of the people, and its apparent meaning is that the call was after the conclusion of the matter and the settling of the Ark, yet the requirement of the apparent state is that the call was after the waves came between them. Accordingly, its mention after the mention of the conclusion of the Flood is only for the sake of the concern with explaining all that is in the story of the formidable form in one place, to complete the depiction of the event, and then taking up the explanation of some of its remaining aspects.

He (peace be upon him) was a messenger, one of the Arch-Prophets (أولي العزم), knowing Allah, cognizant of the station of his Lord, and insightful regarding his own position in servitude (العبودية). The circumstance was one in which the sign of Lordship and divine subdual appeared in its most complete manifestation, drowning the world and its people, and a call was issued from the court of Grandeur and Majesty against the wrongdoers for distance. So Nuh (peace be upon him) began to supplicate for his son. In this circumstance, he did not dare—as required by the etiquette of prophethood—to ask for what he wanted of his son's salvation explicitly. Rather, he presented the speech like one inquiring about the reality of the matter, and he hastened to mention what Allah had promised him of the salvation of his family when He commanded him to gather the saved with him in the ship, saying to him: "Carry in it a pair of every kind, and your family."

His family—other than his wife—even this son of his, were believers in him outwardly. Had this son of his not been, as Nuh (peace be upon him) saw him, a believer, he would never have called him to board the ship. For he (peace be upon him) was the one who supplicated against the disbelievers, asking for their destruction by his saying: "My Lord! Do not leave on the earth any inhabitant from among the faithless." He saw this son of his as a believer, and his opposition to his father's command when he ordered him to board the ship was not, in his view, disbelief or leading to disbelief; rather, it was a sin short of disbelief.

For all of that, he (peace be upon him) said: "My Lord! My son is indeed from my family, and Your promise is indeed true." So he mentioned the promise of his Lord and joined to it that his son was of his family—along with what is in the speech of the indication of "My Lord" (رَبِّ) for seeking mercy, the indication of the possessive in "my son" (ابْنِي) for the proof in his saying "from my family" (مِنْ أَهْلِي), and the indication of emphasis with inna and the generic lam in his saying "and Your promise is indeed true" (وَإِنَّ وَعْدَكَ الْحَقُّ) for the fulfillment of the right of faith.

The two sentences: "My son is indeed from my family" and "Your promise is indeed true" would result, by joining one to the other, in the judgment of the necessity of his son's salvation. However, he (peace be upon him) did not take what his speech resulted in of judgment, out of etiquette in the station of servitude—for there is no judgment except for Allah. Rather, he submitted the true judgment and the decisive decree to Allah, the Glorified, saying: "and You are the fairest of all judges."

The meaning is: My Lord, my son is of my family, and Your promise is true, all of the truth, and that indicates that he should not be taken with the punishment of the people by drowning. Nevertheless, the true judgment is Yours, for You are the fairest of all judges. It is as if he (peace be upon him) was seeking clarification of what the reality of the matter was, and he did not mention the salvation of his son, nor did he add to this which Allah narrated of him anything else.
