The explanation of this will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ يا نُوحُ إِنَّهُ لَيْسَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ إِنَّهُ عَمَلٌ غَيْرُ صالِحٍ فَلا تَسْئَلْنِ ما لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ — "He said, ‘O Noah! He is indeed not of your family. He is indeed an unrighteous deed. So do not ask Me for what you have no knowledge of," (11:46) etc. Allah, the Glorified, clarified for Nuh (peace be upon him) the correct view regarding what he had mentioned in his saying: "My son is indeed from my family and Your promise..." etc., by which he was necessitating the salvation of his son. He, the Exalted, said: "He is indeed not of your family," and thus the effect of his proof was removed.

What is meant by his not being of his family—and Allah knows best—is that he was not of his family whom Allah had promised him to save. For the meaning of "family" in His saying: "and your family, except him against whom the word has already gone forth" is the righteous family, and he was not righteous, even if he was his son and of his family in the sense of specific relation. Therefore, He reasoned His saying: "He is indeed not of your family" by His saying: "He is indeed an unrighteous deed."

If you say: That necessitates that his disbelieving wife be of his family, because she only left the ruling through the exception, while being included in the subject in His saying: "and your family"; and it would mean his son was not of his family and was excluded from the subject, not by the exception, which is far-fetched.

I say: The meaning of "family" in His saying: "and your family, except him against whom the word has already gone forth" is the family in the sense of specific relation, and by the excepted—"him against whom the word has already gone forth"—is meant the unrighteous ones, and its instances are his wife and this son of his. As for the "family" occurring in this saying: "He is indeed not of your family," it refers to the righteous ones among those specifically related to him (peace be upon him), in accordance with what occurred in his saying: "My Lord! My son is indeed from my family." For he (peace be upon him) did not mean by "family" in this saying of his anyone other than the righteous among those of specific relation; otherwise, it would have included his wife and his proof would have been void. So understand that.

This is the apparent meaning of the verse, and it is supported by some of what has been narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), which will come in the following traditional discussion, Allah willing.

They have mentioned other meanings in the commentary of the verse:

Among them: That the meaning is that he was not upon your religion, as if his disbelief removed him from having the rulings of his family. This is attributed to a group of commentators. In itself, this is a meaning that is not objectionable, except that it is not inferred from the context of the verse. This is because Allah, the Glorified, negates from him the "family-ness" in the sense that Nuh (peace be upon him) was affirming it for him. Nuh did not mean by his "family-ness" that he was a believer and not a disbeliever; rather, he only meant that he was his family in the sense of specific relation and righteousness, even if its necessary consequence was faith. Unless, of course, it returns to the previous meaning.

Among them: That he was not his son in reality, but was born on his bed. So Nuh (peace be upon him) said: "he is my son" based on the outward appearance of the matter, and Allah informed him that the matter was contrary to that, and alerted him to the betrayal of his wife. This is attributed to al-Hasan and Mujahid.

In this: Besides what it contains of attributing shame and disgrace to the court of the Prophets (peace be upon them)—and the taste acquired from His speech, the Exalted, repels that from their court and purifies their side from such falsehoods—it is not something that the wording indicates with clarity or even appearance. There is nothing in the story except His saying: "He is indeed not of your family. He is indeed an unrighteous deed," and it is not apparent in what they dared to suggest. His saying regarding the wife of Nuh: "the wife of Noah and the wife of Lot. They were under two of Our righteous servants, yet they betrayed them" (66:10) is only apparent in that they were disbelievers who were loyal to the enemies of their husbands, kept secrets from them, and sought help against them.

Among them: That he was the son of his wife (peace be upon him) and was his stepson, not his son from his loins. In this: There is no evidence for it from the perspective of the wording. Furthermore, it does not suit His saying in the reasoning for his not being of his family: "He is indeed an unrighteous deed." Had it been so, it would have been appropriate for the speech to say: "He is the son of the woman."

Moreover, it is very unlikely that Nuh (peace be upon him) would not know that he was his stepson and not his son, such that he would address his Lord saying: "My son is indeed from my family," or that he would know that and speak metaphorically and argue with his Lord, the All-Knowing, the All-Aware, with that, only to be alerted that he was not his son but only a stepson.

His saying: إِنَّهُ عَمَلٌ غَيْرُ صالِحٍ — "He is indeed an unrighteous deed." The appearance of the context is that the pronoun refers to the son of Nuh (peace be upon him), so he himself is the unrighteous deed. Counting him as an unrighteous deed is a type of hyperbole, like "Zayd is justice" meaning "possessor of justice," and the saying: "she is but approach and retreat," meaning "possessor of approach and retreat."

The meaning is: This son of yours is a possessor of unrighteous deeds, so he is not of your family whom I promised you I would save. This meaning is supported by the reading of those who read: innahu 'amila 'amalan ghayra salih with the past tense verb, meaning "he did an unrighteous deed."

Some have mentioned that the pronoun refers to the request of Nuh (peace be upon him) understood from his saying: "My Lord! My son is indeed from my family," meaning: your asking for the salvation of your son is an unrighteous deed, because it is a request for what you have no knowledge of, and it is not befitting for a Prophet to address his Lord with the like of that.

This is among the most absurd of commentaries, for it is a meaning that does not suit either of the two sentences surrounding it: neither His saying: "He is indeed not of your family" nor His saying: "So do not ask Me for what you have no knowledge of," as is apparent. Had it been so, it would have been appropriate for the speech to precede His saying: "He is indeed not of your family" and be connected to the words of Nuh (peace be upon him).

Furthermore, you have learned that the words of Nuh (peace be upon him): "My Lord! My son is indeed from my family" etc., do not contain a request; rather, he was leading it—had it continued in his speech—toward a request, but the divine care came between him and the request.

His saying: فَلا تَسْئَلْنِ ما لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ — "So do not ask Me for what you have no knowledge of." The words of Nuh (peace be upon him): "My Lord! My son is indeed from my family and Your promise is indeed true" were in a position where they might lead him to ask for the salvation of his son, while he did not know that he was not of his family. So the divine care took hold of him, and the hidden directing (التسديد الغيبي) came between him and the request. Thus, the prohibition reached him by His saying: "So do not ask Me for what you have no knowledge of," deriving the prohibition from what preceded—meaning: since he is not of your family due to his being an unrighteous deed, and you have no way to know that, then beware of hastening to ask for his salvation, because it is a request for what you have no knowledge of.

The prohibition against asking without knowledge does not necessitate that such a request actually occurred from him (peace be upon him), neither independently nor within his saying: "My Lord! My son is indeed from my family." This is because the prohibition of a thing does not necessitate its prior commission. He, the Exalted, said: لا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلى ما مَتَّعْنا بِهِ أَزْواجاً مِنْهُمْ — "Do not extend your glance toward what We have provided certain groups of them with" (15:88), thus prohibiting the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) from loving the world and being infatuated with its adornment, and he is far above that.

Rather, the prohibition only requires, for the correctness of its connection to an act, that it be a voluntary act that the person subject to the ruling (المكلف) could possibly be tested with. What the Prophets (peace be upon them) were prohibited from is of this description, even though they possess divine protection (عصمة) and hidden directing. For it is part of the protection and directing that Allah, the Glorified, monitors them in their actions, and whenever they approach what is of a nature that a human might slip in, He alerts them to the correct view and calls them to the right course and the adherence to the path of servitude. He, the Exalted, said: وَلَوْ لا أَنْ ثَبَّتْناكَ لَقَدْ كِدْتَ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْئاً قَلِيلاً إِذاً لَأَذَقْناكَ ضِعْفَ الْحَياةِ وَضِعْفَ الْمَماتِ ثُمَّ لا تَجِدُ لَكَ عَلَيْنا نَصِيراً — "Had We not fortified you, you would have certainly leaned toward them a little. Then We would have surely made you taste a double [punishment] in this life and a double [punishment] after death, and then you would not have found for yourself any helper against Us." (17:74-75). Thus, He, the Exalted, informed that it was He who fortified him and did not let him approach leaning toward them, let alone the leaning itself.

And He, the Exalted, said: وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكَ وَرَحْمَتُهُ لَهَمَّتْ طائِفَةٌ مِنْهُمْ أَنْ يُضِلُّوكَ وَما يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَما يَضُرُّونَكَ مِنْ شَيْءٍ وَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَعَلَّمَكَ ما لَمْ تَكُنْ تَعْلَمُ وَكانَ فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكَ عَظِيماً — "Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy on you, a group of them were bent on leading you astray; but they do not mislead anyone except themselves, and they cannot do you any harm. Allah has sent down to you the Book and wisdom, and He has taught you what you did not know, and Allah’s grace on you has been very great." (4:113).

Among the evidence that the prohibition—"So do not ask Me" etc.—is a prohibition against what had not yet occurred is the words of Nuh (peace be upon him) after hearing this prohibition: رَبِّ إِنِّي أَعُوذُ بِكَ أَنْ أَسْئَلَكَ ما لَيْسَ لِي بِهِ عِلْمٌ — "My Lord! I seek Your protection from asking You anything of which I have no knowledge." (11:47). Had he asked for something, it would have been said: "I seek Your protection from my asking that," so that the infinitive added to the object would indicate realization and commission.

Among the evidence also that he (peace be upon him) did not ask for that is the following of His saying: "So do not ask Me for what you have no knowledge of" with His saying: إِنِّي أَعِظُكَ أَنْ تَكُونَ مِنَ الْجاهِلِينَ — "I advise you lest you should be among the ignorant." Its meaning is: I advise you in speech so that you do not become, by your asking that, among the ignorant. Had Nuh asked for that, he would have been among the ignorant because he asked for what he had no knowledge of.

If you say: He, the Exalted, said: "lest you should be among the ignorant" (أَنْ تَكُونَ مِنَ الْجاهِلِينَ)—meaning from those in whom the attribute of ignorance has become established. Its establishment only occurs through repetition, not by a single instance. By this, it is known that he asked what he asked and ignorance was realized from him once, and Allah only advised him with what He advised so that he would not return to the like of it, such that it would be repeated from him and he would enter the group of the ignorant.

I say: The form of the active participle (اسم الفاعل) like "ignorant" (جاهل) does not indicate establishment and repetition; rather, that is provided by the intensive form (الصفة المشبهة) like "very ignorant" (جهول), according to what they have mentioned. This is evidenced by His saying, the Exalted, in the story of the Cow: قالُوا أَتَتَّخِذُنا هُزُواً قالَ أَعُوذُ بِاللهِ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِنَ الْجاهِلِينَ — "They said, ‘Do you mock us?’ He said, ‘I seek Allah’s protection lest I should be one of the ignorant!’" (2:67); and His saying in the story of Yusuf: وَإِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّي كَيْدَهُنَّ أَصْبُ إِلَيْهِنَّ وَأَكُنْ مِنَ الْجاهِلِينَ — "If You do not turn away their stratagems from me, I might lean toward them and become one of the ignorant." (12:33); and His saying addressing His Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny): وَلَوْ شاءَ اللهُ لَجَمَعَهُمْ عَلَى الْهُدى فَلا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْجاهِلِينَ — "Had Allah willed, He would have gathered them on guidance; so do not be one of the ignorant." (6:35).

Also, had the meaning of the prohibition against asking been that he should not repeat that after it had occurred once, it would have been more appropriate to explicitly prohibit the return to its like, rather than prohibiting its root, as occurred in a similar case in His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ تَلَقَّوْنَهُ بِأَلْسِنَتِكُمْ وَتَقُولُونَ بِأَفْواهِكُمْ ما لَيْسَ لَكُمْ بِهِ عِلْمٌ — "when you were receiving it on your tongues, and were uttering with your mouths that of which you had no knowledge," until He said: يَعِظُكُمُ اللهُ أَنْ تَعُودُوا لِمِثْلِهِ أَبَداً — "Allah advises you lest you should ever repeat the like of it," (24:15-17).

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي أَعُوذُ بِكَ أَنْ أَسْئَلَكَ ما لَيْسَ لِي بِهِ عِلْمٌ وَإِلَّا تَغْفِرْ لِي وَتَرْحَمْنِي أَكُنْ مِنَ الْخاسرِينَ — "He said, ‘My Lord! I seek Your protection from asking You anything of which I have no knowledge. If You do not forgive me and have mercy upon me, I shall be among the losers.’" (11:47). When it became clear to Nuh (peace be upon him) that if the nature of the speech with which he addressed his Lord had led him to the request, he would have been asking for what he had no knowledge of and would have been among the ignorant, and that the care of Allah had come between him and destruction, he thanked his Lord and sought refuge in His forgiveness and mercy from that request that leads to loss, saying: "My Lord! I seek Your protection from asking You anything of which I have no knowledge."

The discussion regarding seeking refuge from what has not yet occurred of destructive matters and ruinous sins is like the discussion regarding the prohibition of what has not occurred of sins and offenses, which has already been discussed. Allah has commanded His Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) to seek refuge from Satan, while he is protected (معصوم) and Satan has no way to him. He, the Exalted, said: قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ النَّاسِ — "Say, ‘I seek the protection of the Lord of humans," until He said: مِنْ شَرِّ الْوَسْواسِ الْخَنَّاسِ الَّذِي يُوَسْوِسُ فِي صُدُورِ النَّاسِ — "from the evil of the sneaking whisperer, who whispers into the breasts of humans," (114:1-5). And He said: وَأَعُوذُ بِكَ رَبِّ أَنْ يَحْضُرُونِ — "and I seek Your protection, my Lord, from their presence." (23:98). Revelation is protected from the touch of devils, as He, the Exalted, said: عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى مِنْ رَسُولٍ فَإِنَّهُ يَسْلُكُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ رَصَداً لِيَعْلَمَ أَنْ قَدْ أَبْلَغُوا رِسالاتِ رَبِّهِمْ — "Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves of. Then He dispatches a sentinel before him and behind him, so that He may know that they have delivered the messages of their Lord," (72:26-28).

His saying: وَإِلَّا تَغْفِرْ لِي وَتَرْحَمْنِي أَكُنْ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ — "If You do not forgive me and have mercy upon me, I shall be among the losers" is a speech whose form is the form of repentance (التوبة), but its reality is gratitude (الشكر) for what Allah bestowed upon him of instruction and discipline.

As for the form of his repentance, it is because in that there is a return to his Lord, the Exalted, by seeking refuge, and its necessary consequence is the seeking of Allah's forgiveness and mercy—meaning His covering over the human what contains his slip and destruction, and the inclusion of His care for his state. It has already been explained in the latter parts of the sixth volume of the book that "sin" (الذنب) is more general than the opposition to a legislative command; rather, it is every consequence and evil effect that reaches a human in any way. And "forgiveness" (المغفرة) is more general than the covering over of the sin known among the followers of the Law; rather, it is every divine covering that brings happiness to a human and gathers his affairs.

As for the reality of gratitude, it is because the divine care that came between him and the request—which would have caused his entry into the group of the ignorant—and His protecting him by clarifying the correct view, was a divine covering over a slip in his path, and a mercy and blessing that Allah, the Glorified, bestowed upon him. So his saying (peace be upon him): "If You do not forgive me and have mercy upon me, I shall be among the losers" means: if You do not protect me from slips, I would surely lose. This is a praise and gratitude for His beautiful act.

His saying, the Exalted: قِيلَ يا نُوحُ اهْبِطْ بِسَلامٍ مِنَّا وَبَرَكاتٍ عَلَيْكَ وَعَلى أُمَمٍ مِمَّنْ مَعَكَ — "It was said, ‘O Noah! Disembark with peace from Us and blessings on you and on nations [to issue] from those who are with you," (11:48) etc. Peace (السلام) is safety or greeting. However, the mention of the touch of punishment at the end of the verse supports that what is meant by it at its beginning is safety from punishment. Likewise, the changing of "blessing" (البركة) at the end of the verse to "enjoyment" (التمتع) indicates that what is meant by "blessings" is not absolute favors and the goods of life, but rather favors insofar as they lead the human to goodness, happiness, and a praised end.

So His saying: قِيلَ — "It was said"—and the speaker, who is Allah, the Glorified, is not mentioned for the sake of glorification—يا نُوحُ اهْبِطْ بِسَلامٍ مِنَّا وَبَرَكاتٍ عَلَيْكَ — "O Noah! Disembark with peace from Us and blessings on you" means—and Allah knows best—O Nuh, descend with safety from the punishment—the Flood—and favors possessing blessings and goodnesses descending from Us upon you; or descend with a greeting and blessings descending from Us upon you.

His saying: وَعَلى أُمَمٍ مِمَّنْ مَعَكَ — "and on nations [to issue] from those who are with you" is conjoined to His saying: "on you." The indefiniteness of "nations" (أمم) indicates their partiality, because among the nations are those whom He, the Exalted, mentions afterward in His saying: وَأُمَمٌ سَنُمَتِّعُهُمْ — "and [there will be other] nations whom We shall provide for."

The address—meaning His saying, the Exalted: يا نُوحُ اهْبِطْ بِسَلامٍ مِنَّا وَبَرَكاتٍ عَلَيْكَ "O Noah, disembark with peace from Us and blessings upon you..." (11:48) until the end of the verse—is characterized by the circumstances of its issuance. At that moment, there was no living soul on the face of the earth, whether human or animal, for they had all been drowned. None remained except the small group in the Ark, which had come to rest and settled upon Mount Judi (الجودي). It had been decreed that they should descend to the earth to populate it and live therein for a time.

This is a general address encompassing all of humanity from the moment of their emergence from the Ark until the Day of Resurrection. It is analogous to the divine address issued on the day Adam (peace be upon him) was sent down from Paradise to the earth, which Allah the Exalted recounted in one place by His saying: وَقُلْنَا اهْبِطُوا بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتاعٌ إِلى حِينٍ "And We said, ‘Get down, being enemies of one another! On the earth shall be your abode and sustenance for a time’" (2:36)—until He said—قُلْنَا اهْبِطُوا مِنْها جَمِيعاً فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ مِنِّي هُدىً فَمَنْ تَبِعَ هُدايَ فَلا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ "We said, ‘Get down from it, all together! Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me, those who follow My guidance shall have no fear, nor shall they grieve. As for those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]’" (2:38-39). And in another place, He said: قالَ فِيها تَحْيَوْنَ وَفِيها تَمُوتُونَ وَمِنْها تُخْرَجُونَ "He said, ‘In it you shall live, and in it you shall die, and from it you shall be brought forth’" (7:25).

This address is a second address, similar to that first one, directed toward Noah (peace be upon him) and those believers with him—to whom the lineage of humanity today traces back—concerning them and those of their progeny who would follow them until the Day of Resurrection. It entails the decreeing of their earthly life, the permission for their descent to it, their settlement therein, and their being sheltered by it.

Allah divided those granted this permission into two groups: He expressed His permission for one group through "peace" (السلام) and "blessings" (البركات)—these being Noah (peace be upon him) and the nations (أمم) among those with him—and for another group through "enjoyment" (التمتيع), followed by the touch of punishment. Just as the two words "peace" and "blessings" are not devoid of the glad tidings of goodness and felicity for those to whom they pertain, the word "enjoyment" followed by "punishment" signifies the opposite.

Thus, it becomes clear that the command to disembark in this verse, along with the associated peace, blessings, and enjoyment, is directed toward all of humanity from the moment the occupants of the Ark descended until the Day of Resurrection. Its weight is equal to the weight of the command of descent directed toward Adam and his spouse (peace be upon them). In this address is the permission for earthly life, a promise for those who obey Allah, the Glorified, and a threat for those who disobey Him, just as that previous address was identical in every detail.

From this, it is evident that the meaning of His saying: وَعَلى أُمَمٍ مِمَّنْ مَعَكَ "and upon nations [to descend] from those with you" refers to the righteous nations among the companions of the Ark and the righteous ones who would appear from their progeny. Based on this, it appears that the preposition "from" (مِنْ) in His saying "from those with you" (مِمَّنْ مَعَكَ) is initiatory (ابتدائية), not explanatory (بيانية). The meaning is: "and upon nations whose existence begins from those with you," namely the companions of the Ark and the righteous among their descendants.

The outward sense of this meaning is that all the companions of the Ark were felicitous and saved. Reason supports this, for they had been thoroughly purified by affliction and had preferred the proximity (زلفى) of Allah. Allah the Glorified had twice confirmed their faith during the story, where He, the Mightiest of Speakers, said: إِلَّا مَنْ قَدْ آمَنَ "...except those who have already believed" (11:36) and said: وَمَنْ آمَنَ وَما آمَنَ مَعَهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ "...and those who believed; and none believed with him except a few" (11:40).

As for His saying: وَأُمَمٌ سَنُمَتِّعُهُمْ ثُمَّ يَمَسُّهُمْ مِنَّا عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ "and [there will be] nations whom We shall give enjoyment, then a painful punishment from Us will visit them," it is as if it were a subject (مبتدأ) for an omitted predicate (خبر). The estimation is: "And among those with you are nations..." or "And there are nations whom We shall give enjoyment..." Allah the Glorified excluded them from the group addressed with the permission of honor; He did not say, "and enjoyment for other nations who will be punished," thereby expelling them from the station of dignity. Thus, He informed that there are other nations whom We shall grant enjoyment and then punish, and they are not granted permission to utilize the enjoyments of life by way of honor and proximity.

In the verse, there are aspects of the Speaker's magnification that are not hidden, such as the use of the passive voice in قِيلَ "It was said," the singling out of Noah (peace be upon him) with the command to disembark, and the use of the first-person plural in His saying مِنَّا "from Us" in two places and سَنُمَتِّعُهُمْ "We shall give them enjoyment," among other instances.

It also becomes clear that the interpretation some have given for His saying: عَلى أُمَمٍ مِمَّنْ مَعَكَ "upon nations from those with you"—claiming it means "upon nations from the progeny of those with you"—is not as it should be, as it excludes those who were actually with him from the address. Likewise, the opinion of those who said it refers to the rest of the animals that were with him because Allah placed blessing in them is even more clearly erroneous.

His saying, the Exalted: تِلْكَ مِنْ أَنْباءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيها إِلَيْكَ "These are accounts of the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — which We reveal to you" (11:49). That is, these stories, or this story, are from the reports of the Unseen which We reveal to you.

And His saying: ما كُنْتَ تَعْلَمُها أَنْتَ وَلا قَوْمُكَ مِنْ قَبْلِ هذا "Neither you nor your people knew them before this." That is, while they are of pure truth and accuracy, they were unknown to you and your people before this. What the People of the Book possess of it is distorted and inverted from the correct path, as will be shown to you regarding what is in the current Torah concerning his story (peace be upon him).

And His saying: فَاصْبِرْ إِنَّ الْعاقِبَةَ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ "So be patient; indeed the outcome is in favor of the God-conscious (المتقين) — those who maintain a protective shield of piety and awareness of Allah." This is a command derived from the details of the story. That is: since you have learned what the matter of Noah (peace be upon him) and his people led to—the destruction of his people and his salvation along with the believers with him, and how Allah made them inherit the earth for their patience, and aided Noah against his enemies for his patience—then be patient upon the truth, for the final outcome belongs to the God-conscious, who are the patient ones in the way of Allah the Glorified.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ishaq ibn Bishr and Ibn Asakir narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "Noah (peace be upon him) used to be beaten and then wrapped in a felt cloth and cast into his house, with people thinking he had died. Then he would emerge and call them again. When he despaired of his people's faith, a man came with his son while leaning on a staff and said: 'O my son, look at this old man; do not let him deceive you.' The son said: 'O father, let me have the staff.' Then he took the staff and said: 'Set me on the ground.' He set him down, and the boy walked to Noah and struck him, fracturing his skull until the blood flowed.

Noah (peace be upon him) said: 'O Lord, You see what Your servants do to me. If You have any need for Your servants, then guide them; but if it be otherwise, then grant me patience until You judge, and You are the best of judges.' Then Allah revealed to him, making him despair of his people's faith, and informed him that there remained no believer in the loins of the men nor the wombs of the women. He said: 'O Noah, none of your people will believe except those who have already believed, so do not be despondent at what they used to do'—meaning do not grieve over them—'and build the Ark.' He said: 'O Lord, what is the Ark?' He said: 'A house of wood that runs upon the surface of the water, so that I may drown the people of My disobedience and purify My earth of them.' He said: 'O Lord, where is the water?' He said: 'I am powerful over whatever I will.'"

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from al-Mufaddal, who said: "I was with Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) in Kufa during the days he came to Abu al-Abbas. When we reached al-Kunasa, he said: 'Here is the place where my uncle Zayd (may Allah have mercy on him) was crucified.' Then he proceeded until he reached the Arch of the Oil-Sellers (طاق الزياتين), which is the end of the Saddlers (السراجين). He dismounted and said: 'Dismount, for this spot was the first Mosque of Kufa, which Adam marked out, and I dislike entering it while riding.' I said: 'Who changed it from its original plan?' He said: 'The first of that was the Flood in the time of Noah. Then the companions of Kisra and al-Nu'man changed it, and then it was changed after Ziyad ibn Abi Sufyan.' I said: 'Were Kufa and its mosque there in the time of Noah?' He said to me: 'Yes, O Mufaddal. The dwelling of Noah and his people was in a village at a distance of one station from the Euphrates, toward the west of Kufa.'

He said: 'Noah was a carpenter, and Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, made him a prophet and chose him. Noah was the first to build a ship that runs upon the surface of the water.' He said: 'Noah remained among his people for a thousand years less fifty, calling them to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, while they mocked and ridiculed him. When he saw that from them, he prayed against them, saying: رَبِّ لا تَذَرْ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ دَيَّاراً ـ إِنَّكَ إِنْ تَذَرْهُمْ يُضِلُّوا عِبادَكَ وَلا يَلِدُوا ـ إِلَّا فاجِراً كَفَّاراً (My Lord! Do not leave on the earth any inhabitant from among the faithless. If You leave them, they will lead Your servants astray, and will beget none but vicious ingrates). Then Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, revealed to Noah to build a ship, to make it spacious, and to hasten its construction. So Noah built the ship in the Mosque of Kufa with his own hand, bringing the wood from afar until he finished it.'"

Al-Mufaddal said: "Then the discourse of Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) was interrupted at the time of the sun's decline. Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) stood and prayed the Dhuhr and Asr prayers. Then he departed from the mosque, turned to his left, and pointed with his hand to the site of the House of the Two Dwellings (دار الدارين)—which is the site of the house of Ibn Hakim, and that is the Euphrates today—and said: 'O Mufaddal, here were set up the idols of Noah's people: Yaghuth, Ya'uq, and Nasr.' Then he proceeded until he mounted his beast.

I said: 'May I be your ransom, in how much time did Noah build his ship?' He said: 'In two cycles (دورين).' I said: 'And how long is a cycle?' He said: 'Eighty years.' I said: 'But the common people say he built it in five hundred years?' He said: 'Not at all. How could that be when Allah says: وَوَحْيِنا (and Our revelation)?' I said: 'Then inform me about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَمْرُنا وَفارَ التَّنُّورُ (Until when Our command came and the oven gushed forth). Where was its location and how was it?' He said: 'The oven (التنور) was in the house of a believing old woman, behind the prayer niche (القبلة) on the right side of the mosque.' I said to him: 'Where is that?' He said: 'The location of the corner of the Elephant Gate (باب الفيل) today.' Then I said to him: 'Was the beginning of the water's emergence from that oven?' He said: 'Yes. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, desired to show the people of Noah a sign. Then Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, sent down rain upon them in a torrential flood, and all the springs gushed forth, so Allah drowned them and saved Noah and those with him in the Ark...'" (the tradition continues).

I say: The narration, despite its length, is not entirely related to the exegesis, but we have cited it as a sample of many narrations that have come regarding these meanings through both Shi'a and Sunni paths, and to serve as an aid in understanding the stories of the verses through the medium of traditions.

In the narration, the inference of haste in building the Ark is taken from His saying, the Exalted: وَاصْنَعِ الْفُلْكَ بِأَعْيُنِنا وَوَحْيِنا "Build the Ark under Our eyes and [by] Our revelation" (11:37). In the narration, Ziyad is attributed to Abu Sufyan; perhaps what was uttered by the Imam was "Ziyad," and "ibn Abi Sufyan" was added by some narrators.

In the same source, with its chain of narration from Abu Razin al-Asadi from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), who said: "When Noah (peace be upon him) finished the Ark, and his appointed time between him and his Lord for the destruction of his people was for the oven to gush forth, the oven gushed forth in the house of a woman. She said: 'The oven has gushed forth!' He went to it and sealed it, so the water stopped. He entered those he wished to enter and brought out those he wished to bring out. Then he came to his seal and removed it. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: فَفَتَحْنا أَبْوابَ السَّماءِ بِماءٍ مُنْهَمِرٍ ـ وَفَجَّرْنَا الْأَرْضَ عُيُوناً فَالْتَقَى الْماءُ عَلى أَمْرٍ قَدْ قُدِرَ ـ وَحَمَلْناهُ عَلى ذاتِ أَلْواحٍ وَدُسُرٍ (Then We opened the gates of heaven with pouring water, and We made the earth gush forth with springs, so the waters met for a purpose already decreed. And We carried him on a vessel of planks and nails). He said: 'His carpentry was in the middle of your mosque, and its area has decreased by seven hundred cubits.'"

I say: The gushing of the oven being a sign for him (peace be upon him) by which he would know the approach of the Flood is mentioned in several narrations from both the elite and the common paths. The context of the verse: حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَمْرُنا وَفارَ التَّنُّورُ قُلْنَا احْمِلْ "Until when Our command came and the oven gushed forth, We said, ‘Carry...’" (11:40) is not devoid of indicating that it was a prearranged signal.

In the same source, with its chain of narration from Isma'il al-Ju'fi from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "The law (شريعة) of Noah was to worship Allah with monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and sincerity, and the casting off of rivals (الأنداد). This is the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — upon which He originated mankind. Allah took His covenant from Noah and the prophets to worship Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, and not to associate anything with Him. He commanded prayer, the bidding of good, the forbidding of evil, and the lawful and the prohibited. He did not impose laws of legal punishments (حدود) nor the ordinances of inheritance; this was his law. Noah remained among them for a thousand years less fifty, calling them secretly and openly. When they refused and became arrogant, he said: رَبِّ أَنِّي مَغْلُوبٌ فَانْتَصِرْ (My Lord! I am overcome, so help me!). Then Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, revealed to him: لَنْ يُؤْمِنَ مِنْ قَوْمِكَ إِلَّا مَنْ قَدْ آمَنَ ـ فَلا تَبْتَئِسْ بِما كانُوا يَفْعَلُونَ (None of your people will believe except those who have already believed, so do not be despondent at what they used to do). That is the saying of Noah: وَلا يَلِدُوا إِلَّا فاجِراً كَفَّاراً (and will beget none but vicious ingrates). Then Allah revealed to him: أَنِ اصْنَعِ الْفُلْكَ (Build the Ark)."

I say: Al-Ayyashi narrated it from al-Ju'fi in a disconnected (مرسل) form. The outward sense of the narration is that he (peace be upon him) had two prayers against his people: the first was his saying: رَبِّ أَنِّي مَغْلُوبٌ فَانْتَصِرْ "My Lord! I am overcome, so help me!" which occurs in Surah al-Qamar; and the second was after Allah made him despair of his people's faith, which was his saying: رَبِّ لا تَذَرْ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ دَيَّاراً إِنَّكَ إِنْ تَذَرْهُمْ يُضِلُّوا عِبادَكَ وَلا يَلِدُوا إِلَّا فاجِراً كَفَّاراً "My Lord! Do not leave on the earth any inhabitant from among the faithless. If You leave them, they will lead Your servants astray, and will beget none but vicious ingrates," which occurs in Surah Nuh.

In Ma'ani al-Akhbar, with its chain of narration from Humran from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَما آمَنَ مَعَهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ "and none believed with him except a few," he said: "They were eight."

I say: Al-Ayyashi also narrated it from Humran from him (peace be upon him). People have other opinions regarding their number: six, seven, ten, seventy-two, or eighty, but there is no definitive proof for any of them.

In al-Uyun, with its chain of narration from Abd al-Salam ibn Salih al-Harawi, who said: "Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) said: 'When Noah descended to the earth, Noah, his children, and those who followed him were eighty souls. He built a village where he landed and named it the Village of the Eighty (قرية الثمانين).'"

I say: There is no contradiction between the two narrations, as it is possible that those other than the eight were from Noah's family (peace be upon him), and he had lived nearly a thousand years by then.

In the same source, with its chain of narration from al-Hasan ibn Ali al-Washsha from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him), who said: "I heard him saying: 'My father said: Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said to Noah: إِنَّهُ لَيْسَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ (Indeed he is not of your family) because he was in opposition to him, and He made those who followed him part of his family.'"

He said: "He asked me: 'How do they recite this verse regarding the son of Noah?' I said: 'People recite it in two ways: إِنَّهُ عَمَلٌ غَيْرُ صالِحٍ (Indeed it is an unrighteous deed) and إِنَّهُ عَمِلَ غَيْرَ صالِحٍ (Indeed he did unrighteous work).' He said: 'They have lied; he was his son, but Allah disowned him from him when he opposed him in his religion.'"

I say: Perhaps he (peace be upon him) refers by his saying "and He made those who followed him part of his family" to the saying of the Exalted: فَنَجَّيْناهُ وَأَهْلَهُ مِنَ الْكَرْبِ الْعَظِيمِ "So We delivered him and his family from the great distress" (21:76), for it appears that the intended meaning of "his family" is all those who were saved with him.

The intended meaning of "reciting the verse" was its interpretation. The narrator, by mentioning the two recitations, refers to the interpretation of those who explained the verse by saying that Noah's wife bore the son from someone else and he attributed him to his own bed. For this reason, some recited: وَنادى نُوحٌ ابْنَهَا "And Noah called her son" or وَنادى نُوحٌ ابْنَه "And Noah called his son" with a lightened ha for "her son," attributing these recitations to Ali and some of the Imams from his progeny (peace be upon him).

It is said in al-Kashshaf: "Ali (may Allah be pleased with him) recited ابْنَهَا (her son), with the pronoun referring to his wife. Muhammad ibn Ali and Urwah ibn al-Zubayr recited ابْنَه with a fatha on the ha, meaning 'her son,' sufficing with the fatha instead of the alif. This supports the view of al-Hasan. Qatadah said: 'I asked him and he said: By Allah, he was not his son.' I said: 'But Allah recounted from him: إِنَّ ابْنِي مِنْ أَهْلِي (Indeed my son is of my family), yet you say he was not his son? And the People of the Book do not disagree that he was his son!' He replied: 'And who takes his religion from the People of the Book?' He argued from the phrase 'of my family' (من أهلي) and noted he did not say 'from me' (مني)." (End of quote).

His argument is weak, for Allah promised him the salvation of his family (أهله) and did not promise him the salvation of "whoever was from him" (من كان منه) such that he would be forced to say "my son is from me" when asking for his salvation. It has already been explained that the wording of the verses does not accommodate this view.

As for the claim that there is no disagreement among the People of the Book, it is questionable, as the Torah is silent regarding the story of this drowned son of Noah.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn al-Anbari in al-Masahif and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Ali (may Allah be pleased with him) that he recited: وَنادى نُوحٌ ابْنَهَا "And Noah called her son."

In the same source, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Abu Ja'far Muhammad ibn Ali regarding His saying: وَنادى نُوحٌ ابْنَهُ "And Noah called his son," he said: "It is in the dialect of Tayy—he was not his son, but the son of his wife."

I say: Al-Ayyashi narrated it in his Tafsir from Muhammad ibn Muslim from him (peace be upon him).

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Musa from al-Ala ibn Siyabah from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: وَنادى نُوحٌ ابْنَهُ "And Noah called his son," he said: "He was not his son; he was only the son of his wife. It is the dialect of Tayy; they call the son of a man's wife 'his son.'" (the tradition continues).

In the same source, from Zurarah from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Noah: يا بُنَيَّ ارْكَبْ مَعَنا "O my son! Board with us," he said: "He was not his son." I said: "But Noah said: 'O my son'?" He said: "Noah said that while he did not know."

I say: What is to be relied upon is what was previously mentioned in the narration of al-Washsha from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him).

In the same source, from Ibrahim ibn Abi al-Ala from one of the two [Imams al-Baqir or al-Sadiq] (peace be upon them), who said: "When Allah said: يا أَرْضُ ابْلَعِي ماءَكِ ـ وَيا سَماءُ أَقْلِعِي (O earth, swallow your water! O heaven, leave off!), the earth said: 'I was only commanded to swallow my own water, and I was not commanded to swallow the water of the heaven.' So the earth swallowed its water, and the water of the heaven remained and became the sea surrounding the world."

In the same source, from Abu Basir from Abu al-Hasan Musa (peace be upon him) in a tradition mentioning al-Judi: "It is a mountain in Mosul."

In the same source, from al-Mufaddal ibn Umar from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): "اسْتَوَتْ عَلَى الْجُودِيِ (It settled on al-Judi)—it is the Euphrates of Kufa."

I say: The previous narration is supported by other traditions.

In the same source, from Abd al-Hamid ibn Abi al-Daylam from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "When Noah (peace be upon him) boarded the Ark, it was said: 'Away with the wrongdoing people!'"

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: قِيلَ يا أَرْضُ ابْلَعِي ماءَكِ "It was said, ‘O earth, swallow your water!’"—he said: "It is narrated that the faithless of the Quraysh wanted to attempt to challenge the Qur'an. They devoted themselves to the finest wheat, mutton, and choice wine for forty days so that their minds might become clear. When they began what they intended, they heard this verse. Some said to others: 'This is speech that nothing resembles, and it does not resemble the speech of created beings.' So they abandoned what they had started and dispersed."

Researches on the Story of Noah in Chapters

These are Quranic, Traditional, Historical, and Philosophical Researches

1 - Reference to His Story: His name (peace be upon him) is mentioned in the Qur'an in over forty places, referring to parts of his story either briefly or in detail. His story (peace be upon him) is not fully exhausted in any single place in the manner of a historical chronicle—mentioning his lineage, household, birth, dwelling, upbringing, occupation, age, death, burial, and other matters related to his personal life—because the Qur'an was not revealed as a history book to recount the chronicles of people, whether righteous or wicked.

Rather, it is a book of guidance (الهدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — that describes for people what contains their felicity and clarifies for them the manifest truth so they may adopt it and succeed in their life of this world and the Hereafter. It may refer to parts of the stories of prophets and nations to demonstrate the sunnah (سنة) of Allah among His servants, so that those encompassed by divine care and granted honor may take heed, and the proof (الحجة) may be completed against the rest.

The story of Noah (peace be upon him) is detailed in six of the Quranic Surahs: Surah al-A'raf, Surah Hud, Surah al-Mu'minun, Surah al-Shu'ara, Surah al-Qamar, and Surah Nuh. The most detailed of these is Surah Hud, where his story (peace be upon him) is mentioned in twenty-five verses (25–49).

2 - His Story (peace be upon him) in the Qur'an:

His Commissioning and Sending:

After Adam (peace be upon him), people lived as a single nation in simplicity and innocence, following the human primordial nature (الفطرة), until the spirit of arrogance spread among them. This led gradually to the dominance of some over others and the taking of some as lords over others. This is the original seed which, if it grows and flourishes, yields nothing but the religion of idolatry and severe disparity between social classes—through the strong exploiting the weak, the powerful enslaving the lowly, and the occurrence of disputes and quarrels among people.

Thus, in the time of Noah (peace be upon him), corruption became widespread on the earth. People turned away from the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) and the path of social justice, turning instead to the worship of idols. Allah the Glorified named some of them: Wadd, Suwa', Yaghuth, Ya'uq, and Nasr (Surah Nuh). Social classes drifted apart; those strong in wealth and children began to trample the rights of the weak, and tyrants oppressed those beneath them, ruling over them according to their own desires (al-A'raf, Hud, Nuh).

So Allah commissioned Noah (peace be upon him) and sent him to them with the Book and the Law (الشريعة), calling them to the monotheism (التوحيد) of Allah the Glorified, the casting off of rivals, and equality among themselves (2:213) through glad tidings and warnings.

His Religion and Law (peace be upon him):

He (peace be upon him) used to call them to the monotheism (التوحيد) of Allah the Glorified and the rejection of partners (as appears from all his Quranic stories), and to submission (الإسلام) to Allah (as appears from Surahs Nuh and Yunus, and 3:19), and to the bidding of good and forbidding of evil (as appears from 11:27), and to prayer (as appears from 4:103 and 42:8), and to equality and justice, and that they should not approach indecencies and vices, and to truthfulness in speech and fulfillment of covenants (6:151–152). He (peace be upon him) is the first from whom the Qur'an recounts the naming of the Name of Allah (التسمية) in important matters (11:41).

His Diligence (peace be upon him) in His Mission:

He (peace be upon him) used to call his people to faith in Allah and His signs, exerting his utmost effort in that. He would summon them to the truth night and day, openly and secretly, but they responded with nothing but stubbornness and arrogance. Every time he increased his call, they increased in their rebellion and disbelief. None believed in him except his family and a few others, until he despaired of their faith, complained of that to his Lord, and sought victory (Surahs Nuh, al-Qamar, and al-Mu'minun).

His Stay Among His People:

He (peace be upon him) remained among his people for a thousand years less fifty, calling them to Allah the Glorified, but they responded with nothing but mockery and ridicule, accusing him of madness and claiming he only sought to gain superiority over them, until he sought help from his Lord (Surah al-Ankabut). His Lord revealed to him that none of his people would believe except those who had already believed, and He comforted him regarding them (Surah Hud). So he prayed against them for destruction and ruin, and that Allah should purify the earth of them to the last man (Surah Nuh). Then Allah revealed to him: "Build the Ark under Our eyes and [by] Our revelation" (Surah Hud).

His Construction (peace be upon him) of the Ark:

Allah the Exalted commanded him to build the Ark with His support and guidance. He began building it, and the people would pass by him group after group, mocking him as he built it on dry land without water. He (peace be upon him) would say: إِنْ تَسْخَرُوا مِنَّا فَإِنَّا نَسْخَرُ مِنْكُمْ كَما تَسْخَرُونَ فَسوفَ تَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ يَأْتِيهِ عَذابٌ يُخْزِيهِ وَيَحِلُّ عَلَيْهِ عَذابٌ مُقِيمٌ "If you mock us, we [too] mock you [now] just as you mock [us]. Soon you will know who will be overtaken by a punishment that will disgrace him, and on whom a lasting punishment will descend" (11:38–39). Allah had set a sign for the descent of the punishment: that water would gush forth from the oven (Surahs Hud and al-Mu'minun).

The Descent of Punishment and the Coming of the Flood:

When the construction of the Ark was complete, the command of Allah came, and the oven gushed forth. Allah the Exalted revealed to him to carry in the ship a pair of every kind of animal, and to carry his family—except those against whom the divine word of drowning had already passed, namely his treacherous wife and his son who refused to board—and to carry those who believed (Surahs Hud and al-Mu'minun). When he had loaded them and they had all boarded, Allah opened the gates of heaven with pouring water and made the earth gush forth with springs, so the waters met for a purpose already decreed (Surah al-Qamar). The water rose, and the ship ascended upon it, sailing amidst waves like mountains (Surah Hud). The Flood overtook the people while they were wrongdoers. Allah the Exalted had commanded him that when he and those with him were settled upon the Ark, he should praise Allah for saving him from the wrongdoing people and ask Him for blessing in his landing, saying: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي نَجَّانا مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ "All praise belongs to Allah, who delivered us from the wrongdoing people" (23:28), and saying: رَبِّ أَنْزِلْنِي مُنْزَلاً مُبارَكاً وَأَنْتَ خَيْرُ الْمُنْزِلِينَ "My Lord! Land me with a blessed landing, for You are the best of those who bring to land" (23:29).

The Conclusion of the Matter and the Descent to Earth:

When the Flood had encompassed everything and drowned the people (as appears from 37:77), Allah commanded the earth to swallow its water and the heaven to leave off. The water subsided, and the ship settled upon Mount Judi. It was said: "Away with the wrongdoing people!" It was revealed to Noah (peace be upon him) to disembark to the earth with peace from Us and blessings upon you and upon nations from those with you; no general flood would overtake them after this. Among them would be nations whom Allah would grant the enjoyments of life, then a painful punishment would visit them. He and those with him emerged and settled on the earth, worshipping Allah with monotheism (التوحيد) and submission (الإسلام). His progeny (peace be upon him) inherited the earth, and Allah made his descendants the survivors (Surahs Hud and al-Saffat).

The Story of Noah's Drowned Son:

When Noah (peace be upon him) boarded the Ark, one of his sons did not board. He did not believe his father that whoever stayed behind would certainly be drowned. His father saw him in a place apart and called out to him: "O my son! Board with us, and do not be with the faithless!" But he replied to his father, saying: "I shall take refuge on a mountain; it will protect me from the water." Noah (peace be upon him) said: "There is no protector today from Allah’s decree except for one whom He has mercy on"—meaning the people of the Ark. The son paid no heed to his words, and the waves came between them, so he was among the drowned.

Noah (peace be upon him) did not know that he harbored disbelief, as he knew regarding his wife. Had he known that, he would not have been grieved by his matter, for he was the one who said in his prayer: رَبِّ لا تَذَرْ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ دَيَّاراً إِنَّكَ إِنْ تَذَرْهُمْ يُضِلُّوا عِبادَكَ وَلا يَلِدُوا إِلَّا فاجِراً كَفَّاراً "My Lord! Do not leave on the earth any inhabitant from among the faithless. If You leave them, they will lead Your servants astray, and will beget none but vicious ingrates" (71:26–27), and he was the one who said: فَافْتَحْ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَهُمْ فَتْحاً وَنَجِّنِي وَمَنْ مَعِيَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ "So judge decisively between me and them, and deliver me and the believers who are with me" (26:118). He had also heard the saying of the Exalted in what was revealed to him: وَلا تُخاطِبْنِي فِي الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا إِنَّهُمْ مُغْرَقُونَ "and do not plead with Me for those who are wrongdoers: they shall indeed be drowned" (11:37).

So Noah (peace be upon him) felt distress and grief, and he called out to his Lord from his sorrow, saying: "My Lord! My son is of my family, and Your promise is true; You promised me the salvation of my family, and You are the most just of judges; You do not act unjustly in Your judgment nor are You ignorant in Your decree. So what has happened to my son?" Then divine care overtook him and prevented him from explicitly asking for his son's salvation—which would have been a request for that of which he had no knowledge. Allah revealed to him: "O Noah! Indeed he is not of your family. Indeed he is unrighteous conduct. So do not ask Me for that of which you have no knowledge. I advise you lest you should be among the ignorant."

The matter became clear to Noah (peace be upon him), and he took refuge with his Lord, the Exalted, saying: "My Lord! I seek Your protection lest I should ask You that of which I have no knowledge. I ask You to encompass me with Your care, to cover me with Your forgiveness, and to show me Your mercy; were it not for that, I would have been among the losers."

3 - Characteristics of Noah (peace be upon him): He (peace be upon him) is the first of the Arch-Prophets (أولي العزم) — the possessors of steadfast resolve and the masters of the prophets. Allah sent him to all of humanity with a Book and a Law (الشريعة). His Book is the first of the heavenly books containing the laws of Allah, and his Law is the first of the divine laws.

He (peace be upon him) is the second father of the present generation of humans; their lineages trace back to him, and all are his descendants, according to the saying of the Exalted: وَجَعَلْنا ذُرِّيَّتَهُ هُمُ الْباقِينَ "and We made his descendants the survivors" (37:77). He (peace be upon him) is the father of the prophets mentioned in the Qur'an, except for Adam and Idris (peace be upon them). The Exalted said: وَتَرَكْنا عَلَيْهِ فِي الْآخِرِينَ "and We left [this praise] for him among the later generations" (37:78).

He (peace be upon him) is the first to open the door of legislation (التشريع) and bring a Book and a Law, and he spoke to people with the logic of reason and the method of argumentation, in addition to the path of revelation (الوحي). Thus, he is the source to which the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) in the world traces back. He holds a favor over all monotheists until the Day of Resurrection. For this reason, Allah the Exalted singled him out with a general salutation shared by no one else, saying: سَلامٌ عَلى نُوحٍ فِي الْعالَمِينَ "Peace be to Noah throughout all the worlds!" (37:79).

Allah chose him over all creation (3:33), counted him among the virtuous (6:84, 37:80), named him a grateful servant (17:3), counted him among His believing servants (37:81), and named him a righteous servant (66:10).

The last of what is recounted of his prayer is his saying: رَبِّ اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِوالِدَيَّ وَلِمَنْ دَخَلَ بَيْتِيَ مُؤْمِناً وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ وَلا تَدَرْ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا تَباراً "My Lord! Forgive me and my parents, and whoever enters my house in faith, and the faithful men and women, and do not increase the wrongdoers in anything but ruin" (71:28).

4 - His Story (peace be upon him) in the Current Torah: It happened (Genesis, Chapter 6) when men began to multiply on the face of the earth and daughters were born to them, that the sons of God saw the daughters of men that they were beautiful; and they took wives for themselves of all whom they chose. And the Lord said, "My Spirit shall not strive with man forever, for he is indeed flesh; yet his days shall be one hundred and twenty years." There were giants on the earth in those days, and also afterward, when the sons of God came in to the daughters of men and they bore children to them. Those were the mighty men who were of old, men of renown.

Then the Lord saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth, and that every intent of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually. And the Lord was sorry that He had made man on the earth, and He was grieved in His heart. So the Lord said, "I will destroy man whom I have created from the face of the earth, both man and beast, creeping thing and birds of the air, for I am sorry that I have made them." But Noah found grace in the eyes of the Lord.

This is the genealogy of Noah. Noah was a just man, perfect in his generations. Noah walked with God. And Noah begot three sons: Shem, Ham, and Japheth. The earth also was corrupt before God, and the earth was filled with violence. So God looked upon the earth, and indeed it was corrupt; for all flesh had corrupted their way on the earth.

And God said to Noah, "The end of all flesh has come before Me, for the earth is filled with violence through them; and behold, I will destroy them with the earth. Make yourself an ark of gopherwood; make rooms in the ark, and cover it inside and outside with pitch. And this is how you shall make it: The length of the ark shall be three hundred cubits, its width fifty cubits, and its height thirty cubits. You shall make a window for the ark, and you shall finish it to a cubit from above; and set the door of the ark in its side. You shall make it with lower, second, and third decks. And behold, I Myself am bringing floodwaters on the earth, to destroy from under heaven all flesh in which is the breath of life; everything that is on the earth shall die. But I will establish My covenant with you; and you shall go into the ark—you, your sons, your wife, and your sons’ wives with you. And of every living thing of all flesh you shall bring two of every sort into the ark, to keep them alive with you; they shall be male and female. Of the birds after their kind, of animals after their kind, and of every creeping thing of the earth after its kind, two of every sort will come to you to keep them alive. And you shall take for yourself of all food that is eaten, and you shall gather it to yourself; and it shall be food for you and for them." Thus Noah did; according to all that God commanded him, so he did.

Then (Genesis, Chapter 7) the Lord said to Noah, "Come into the ark, you and all your household, because I have seen that you are righteous before Me in this generation. You shall take with you seven each of every clean animal, a male and his female; two each of animals that are unclean, a male and his female; also seven each of birds of the air, male and female, to keep species alive on the face of all the earth. For after seven more days I will cause it to rain on the earth forty days and forty nights, and I will destroy from the face of the earth every living thing that I have made." And Noah did according to all that the Lord commanded him.

Noah was six hundred years old when the floodwaters were on the earth. So Noah, with his sons, his wife, and his sons’ wives, went into the ark because of the waters of the flood. Of clean animals, of animals that are unclean, of birds, and of everything that creeps on the earth, two by two they went into the ark to Noah, male and female, as God had commanded Noah.

And it came to pass after seven days that the waters of the flood were on the earth. In the six hundredth year of Noah’s life, in the second month, the seventeenth day of the month, on that day all the fountains of the great deep were broken up, and the windows of heaven were opened. And the rain was on the earth forty days and forty nights. On that very same day Noah and Noah’s sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth, and Noah’s wife and the three wives of his sons with them, entered the ark—they and every beast after its kind, all cattle after their kind, every creeping thing that creeps on the earth after its kind, and every bird after its kind, every bird of every sort. And they went into the ark to Noah, two by two, of all flesh in which is the breath of life. So those that entered, male and female of all flesh, went in as God had commanded him; and the Lord shut him in.

Now the flood was on the earth forty days. The waters increased and lifted up the ark, and it rose high above the earth. The waters prevailed and greatly increased on the earth, and the ark moved about on the surface of the waters. And the waters prevailed exceedingly on the earth, and all the high hills under the whole heaven were covered. The waters prevailed fifteen cubits upward, and the mountains were covered.

And all flesh died that moved on the earth: birds and cattle and beasts and every creeping thing that creeps on the earth, and every man. All in whose nostrils was the breath of the spirit of life, all that was on the dry land, died. So He destroyed all living things which were on the face of the ground: both man and cattle, creeping thing and bird of the air. They were destroyed from the earth. Only Noah and those who were with him in the ark remained alive. And the waters prevailed on the earth one hundred and fifty days.

Then (Genesis, Chapter 8) God remembered Noah, and every living thing, and all the animals that were with him in the ark. And God made a wind to pass over the earth, and the waters subsided. The fountains of the deep and the windows of heaven were also stopped, and the rain from heaven was restrained. And the waters receded continually from the earth. At the end of the hundred and fifty days the waters decreased. Then the ark rested in the seventh month, the seventeenth day of the month, on the mountains of Ararat. And the waters decreased continually until the tenth month. In the tenth month, on the first day of the month, the tops of the mountains were seen.

So it came to pass, at the end of forty days, that Noah opened the window of the ark which he had made. Then he sent out a raven, which kept going to and fro until the waters had dried up from the earth. He also sent out from himself a dove, to see if the waters had receded from the face of the ground. But the dove found no resting place for the sole of her foot, and she returned to him into the ark, for the waters were on the face of the whole earth. So he put out his hand and took her, and drew her into the ark to himself. And he waited yet another seven days, and again he sent the dove out from the ark. Then the dove came to him in the evening, and behold, a freshly plucked olive leaf was in her mouth; and Noah knew that the waters had receded from the earth. So he waited yet another seven days and sent out the dove, which did not return to him anymore.

And it came to pass in the six hundred and first year, in the first month, the first day of the month, that the waters were dried up from the earth; and Noah removed the covering of the ark and looked, and behold, the surface of the ground was dry. And in the second month, on the twenty-seventh day of the month, the earth was dried.

Then God spoke to Noah, saying, "Go out of the ark, you and your wife, and your sons and your sons’ wives with you. Bring out with you every living thing of all flesh that is with you: birds and cattle and every creeping thing that creeps on the earth, so that they may abound on the earth, and be fruitful and multiply on the earth." So Noah went out, and his sons and his wife and his sons’ wives with him. Every animal, every creeping thing, every bird, and whatever creeps on the earth, according to their families, went out of the ark.

Then Noah built an altar to the Lord, and took of every clean animal and of every clean bird, and offered burnt offerings on the altar. And the Lord smelled a soothing aroma. Then the Lord said in His heart, "I will never again curse the ground for man’s sake, although the imagination of man’s heart is evil from his youth; nor will I again destroy every living thing as I have done. While the earth remains, seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, winter and summer, and day and night shall not cease."

So God blessed (Genesis, Chapter 9) Noah and his sons, and said to them: "Be fruitful and multiply, and fill the earth. And the fear of you and the dread of you shall be on every beast of the earth, on every bird of the air, on all that move on the earth, and on all the fish of the sea. They are given into your hand. Every moving thing that lives shall be food for you. I have given you all things, even as the green herbs. But you shall not eat flesh with its life, that is, its blood. Surely for your lifeblood I will demand a reckoning; from the hand of every beast I will require it, and from the hand of man. From the hand of every man’s brother I will require the life of man. Whoever sheds man’s blood, by man his blood shall be shed; for in the image of God He made man. And as for you, be fruitful and multiply; bring forth abundantly in the earth and multiply in it."

Then God spoke to Noah and to his sons with him, saying: "And as for Me, behold, I establish My covenant with you and with your descendants after you, and with every living creature that is with you: the birds, the cattle, and every beast of the earth with you, of all that go out of the ark, every beast of the earth. Thus I establish My covenant with you: Never again shall all flesh be cut off by the waters of the flood; never again shall there be a flood to destroy the earth." And God said: "This is the token of the covenant which I make between Me and you, and every living creature that is with you, for perpetual generations: I set My rainbow in the cloud, and it shall be for the sign of the covenant between Me and the earth. It shall be, when I bring a cloud over the earth, that the rainbow shall be seen in the cloud; and I will remember My covenant which is between Me and you and every living creature of all flesh; the waters shall never again become a flood to destroy all flesh. The rainbow shall be in the cloud, and I will look on it to remember the everlasting covenant between God and every living creature of all flesh that is on the earth."

And God said to Noah, "This is the sign of the covenant which I have established between Me and all flesh that is on the earth."

Now the sons of Noah who went out of the ark were Shem, Ham, and Japheth. And Ham was the father of Canaan. These three were the sons of Noah, and from these the whole earth was populated. And Noah began to be a farmer, and he planted a vineyard. Then he drank of the wine and was drunk, and became uncovered in his tent. And Ham, the father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father, and told his two brothers outside. But Shem and Japheth took a garment, laid it on both their shoulders, and went backward and covered the nakedness of their father. Their faces were turned away, and they did not see their father’s nakedness.

So Noah awoke from his wine, and knew what his younger son had done to him. Then he said: "Cursed be Canaan; a servant of servants he shall be to his brethren." And he said: "Blessed be the Lord, the God of Shem, and may Canaan be his servant. May God enlarge Japheth, and may he dwell in the tents of Shem; and may Canaan be his servant."

And Noah lived after the flood three hundred and fifty years. So all the days of Noah were nine hundred and fifty years; and he died. (End of the cited passage).

This account—as you see—differs from what has come in the Noble Qur'an in several ways:

Among them: It does not mention the exception of Noah's wife; rather, it explicitly states her entry into the Ark and her salvation with her husband. Some have excused this by saying it is possible Noah had two wives, one of whom was drowned and the other saved.

Among them: It does not mention Noah's drowned son, whose story the Qur'an recounts.

Among them: It does not mention any believers other than Noah and his family; rather, it restricts the survivors to him, his sons, his wife, and his sons' wives.

Among them: It mentions Noah's total age as nine hundred and fifty years, whereas the outward sense of the Mighty Book is that this was the duration he remained among his people calling them to Allah before the Flood. The Exalted said: وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا نُوحاً إِلى قَوْمِهِ فَلَبِثَ فِيهِمْ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ إِلَّا خَمْسِينَ عاماً فَأَخَذَهُمُ الطُّوفانُ وَهُمْ ظالِمُونَ "Certainly We sent Noah to his people, and he remained among them a thousand years less fifty years. Then the flood overtook them while they were wrongdoers" (29:14).

Among them: What it mentions regarding the rainbow, the story of sending the raven and the dove for information, the specifics of the Ark's width, length, height, and its three decks, the duration of the Flood, the height of the water, and other such details. these are specifics not mentioned in the Qur'an, and some are far-fetched, such as the covenant of the rainbow. Recounting such meanings in the story of Noah (peace be upon him) became frequent in the speech of the Companions and the Successors, and most of it resembles the Isra'iliyyat (Judeo-Christian traditions).

5 - What has come regarding the matter of the Flood in the reports and legends of nations: The author of al-Manar said in his Tafsir: "There has appeared in the histories of ancient nations mention of the Flood, some of which agrees with the report of the Book of Genesis except for a little, and some of which differs from it except for a little.

The closest of narrations to it is the narration of the Chaldeans, who were the ones in whose land the Flood occurred. It is narrated from Berossus and Josephus that Xisuthrus saw in a dream after the death of his father, Otiartes, that the waters would overflow and drown all of humanity, and he was commanded to build a ship in which he, his household, and his close friends could take refuge, so he did. This agrees with the Book of Genesis in that there was a generation of giants on the earth who rebelled and increased corruption, so Allah punished them with the Flood.

Some Englishmen discovered clay tablets inscribed with this narration in cuneiform script from the era of Ashurbanipal, about six hundred and sixty years before the birth of Christ, and that it was copied from an ancient writing from the seventeenth century before Christ or earlier; thus, it is older than the Book of Genesis.

The Greeks narrated a report about the Flood cited by Plato, which is that the Egyptian priests told Solon—the Greek sage—that the heaven sent a flood that changed the face of the earth, and humanity perished many times in different ways, so nothing remained for the new generation of the traces or knowledge of those before them.

Manetho cited a report of a flood that occurred after the first Hermes, who was after the first Menes; this is also older than the history of the Torah. It is narrated from the ancient Greeks a report of a flood that encompassed the entire earth except for Deucalion and his wife, Pyrrha, who were saved from it.

It is narrated from the ancient Persians a flood with which Allah drowned the earth due to the corruption and evils spread therein by the action of Ahriman, the god of evil. They said: 'This flood first gushed from the oven of the old woman (Zul Kufa) as she was baking her bread in it.' However, the Magi denied the universality of the Flood and said it was specific to the region of Iraq and ended at the borders of Kurdistan.

Likewise, the ancient Indians affirm the occurrence of the Flood seven times in another mythical form, the last of which was that their king was saved along with his wife in a great ship that his god, Vishnu, commanded him to build and seal with nails until it settled on Mount Himavat—the Himalayas. However, the Brahmins, like the Magi, deny the occurrence of a general flood that drowned all of India. The plurality of floods is also narrated from Japan, China, Brazil, Mexico, and others. All these narrations agree that the cause was Allah's punishment of humanity for their wrongdoing and evils." (End of quote).

It has appeared (1) in the Avesta—the sacred book of the Magi—that Ahura Mazda revealed to Yima (whom the Magi believe to be King Jamshid) that a flood would occur that would drown the earth. He commanded him to build a high wall whose purpose was to protect those inside from drowning, and to gather inside it a group of righteous men and women for procreation, and to enter into it two of every kind of animal, and to build inside the wall houses and domes in different levels to be inhabited by the people gathered there and to shelter the beasts and birds. He was also to plant inside it what would be useful for human life of fruit-bearing trees and to sow the noble grains by which people are sustained, thereby preserving what sustains the life of the world and its cultivation.

In the History of Indian Literature (2), regarding the story of the Flood, it is mentioned that while "Manu" (who is the son of God according to the pagans) was washing his hands, a fish came into his hand. To his astonishment, the fish spoke to him, asking to be saved from destruction, and promised him in return that it would save Manu in the future from a great danger. The imminent great danger foretold by the fish was a flood that would sweep away all creatures. Consequently, Manu kept the fish in a jar.

When it grew large, it informed Manu of the year in which the flood would come. It then instructed Manu to build a large ship and enter it when the floodwaters rose, saying: "I shall save you from the flood." So Manu built the ship, and the fish grew larger than the capacity of the jar, so he cast it into the sea.

Then the flood came as the fish had foretold. When Manu entered the ship, the fish swam to him, so he tied the ship to a horn on its head, and it pulled it to the northern mountains. There, Manu tied the ship to a tree. When the water receded and dried up, Manu remained alone. (End of quote).

6 – Was his prophethood (عليه السلام) universal for all humanity? This is an issue on which the opinions of scholars have differed. What is well-known among the Shi‘ah is the universality of his message. Narrations from the paths of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) have been transmitted indicating this, and that the "Arch-Prophets" (أولو العزم) — who are Nuh, Ibrahim, Musa, ‘Isa, and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) — were sent to all people.

As for the Sunnis, some of them affirmed the universality of his message, relying on the apparent meaning of the verses speaking of the flood encompassing all the inhabitants of the earth, such as His saying: رَبِّ لَا تَذَرْ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ دَيَّارًا — "My Lord! Do not leave on the earth any inhabitant from among the faithless" (Nuh: 26); and His saying: لَا عَاصِمَ الْيَوْمَ مِنْ أَمْرِ اللَّهِ إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ — "Today there is no protector from Allah’s edict except for those on whom He has mercy" (Hud: 43); and His saying: وَجَعَلْنَا ذُرِّيَّتَهُ هُمُ الْبَاقِينَ — "And we made his descendants the survivors" (as-Saffat: 77). Also, what is mentioned in the Sahih narrations regarding the Hadith of Intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — that Nuh was the first messenger Allah sent to the people of the earth, which necessitates his being sent to all of them.

Others denied this, relying on what is mentioned in the Sahih from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Every prophet used to be sent to his people specifically, but I have been sent to all people." They responded to the verses by stating they are open to interpretation (التأويل); it is possible that "the earth" (الأرض) refers to the land they inhabited and their homeland, similar to Pharaoh’s saying to Musa and Harun: وَتَكُونَ لَكُمَا الْكِبْرِيَاءُ فِي الْأَرْضِ — "and so that the two of you may have the sway in the land" (Yunus: 78).

Thus, the meaning of the first verse would be: "Do not leave on this land any inhabitant from the disbelievers of my people." Likewise, the meaning of the second: "Today there is no protector for my people from Allah’s edict." And the meaning of the third: "And we made his descendants the survivors from among his people."

The truth is that the research has not been fully exhausted in their discourse. What ought to be said is that prophethood (النبوة) only appeared in human society due to a factual need for it and a real link between people and their Lord. It relies on an ontological reality (حقيقة تكوينية), not an arbitrary or conventional one (اعتبارية جزافية). Among the real laws governing the system of the universe is the law of the perfection of species and their guidance toward their existential goals. The Exalted has said: الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّىٰ وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدَىٰ — "who created and fashioned, who determined and guided" (al-A‘la: 2-3); and He said: الَّذِي أَعْطَىٰ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدَىٰ — "He who gave everything its creation and then guided it" (Taha: 50).

Every species in the universe is directed, from the first moment of its formation, toward the perfection of its existence and the goal of its creation, wherein lies its good and happiness. The human species is one of these species, not an exception among them; it has a perfection and happiness toward which its individuals, both singly and collectively, are directed.

It is necessary in our view that this perfection cannot be achieved by the human being alone due to the abundance of his vital needs and the multitude of tasks he must perform to fulfill them. The practical intellect (العقل العملي) that drives him to benefit from everything he can—utilizing minerals, plants, and animals for his interests—also drives him to benefit from the labor of others of his kind.

However, individuals are equals, and each possesses the same practical intellect and specific human consciousness as the other, which drives him to seek benefit just as it drives the other. This forced them into a cooperative society (الاجتماع التعاوني) where everyone works for everyone, and one benefits from the labor of others just as others benefit from his labor. Thus, each is subjugated to the other to the extent that he subjugates them, as the Exalted said: نَحْنُ قَسَمْنَا بَيْنَهُمْ مَعِيشَتَهُمْ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَرَفَعْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ دَرَجَاتٍ لِيَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُهُمْ بَعْضًا سُخْرِيًّا — "It is We who dispense their livelihood between them in the present life, and raise some of them above others in rank, so that some may take others into service" (az-Zukhruf: 32).

This foundation of man upon cooperative society is a matter of necessity for him, imposed by the needs of life and the power of competitors. He is, in reality, social and cooperative by a "secondary nature" (بالطبع الثاني); otherwise, his "primary nature" (طبعه الأولي) is to benefit from everything possible, even the labor of his fellow humans. Therefore, whenever a human becomes powerful, self-sufficient, and finds others weak, he transgresses against them and begins to enslave and exploit people without compensation.
