Therefore, it was a religious duty for man to believe in Brahm (who is Allah, the origin of everything), to seek proximity to him through offerings and worships, and to be adorned with noble character and righteous deeds. If his soul turns away from the world and he adopts noble character, performs righteous deeds, and knows Brahm through the knowledge of his own self, he becomes a "Brahmana" and unites with Brahm, becoming him. This is the great happiness and pure life. Otherwise, let him believe in Brahm and perform righteous deeds so that he may be happy in his next life, which is his hereafter.

However, since Brahm is an absolute essence (ذات مطلقة) encompassing everything and not encompassed by anything, he was too high and exalted for man to know him except through a kind of negation of deficiencies (نفي النقائص) or to reach him through worship or offering. Therefore, it is incumbent upon us to seek proximity through worship to his favorites and the powerful among his creation so that they may be intercessors (شفعاء) for us with him. These are the gods who are worshipped besides Allah through the worship of their idols. Despite their numbers, they are either from the angels, the jinn, or the souls of the perfected among the Brahmins. The jinn are worshipped only out of fear of their evil, and others out of desire for their mercy and fear of their wrath. Among them are the spouses, sons, and daughters of Allah.

These are the summaries of what Brahmanism contains and what the scholars of the creed among the Brahmins teach.

However, what is obtained from the Upanishads (١)—which is the fourth part of the sacred Vedas—may not agree with the aforementioned generalities of their beliefs, even if the scholars of the creed among the Brahmins interpret them.

For the critical researcher finds that the treatises of the Upanishads teaching divine knowledge (المعارف الإلهية), although they describe the divine world and the affairs related to it—such as the names, attributes, and acts of initiation, return, creation, sustenance, giving life, and causing death, and other things—using descriptions applied to physical, material matters—such as division, partitioning, rest, motion, transition, indwelling (الحلول), union (الاتحاد), greatness, smallness, and other physical, material states—they nevertheless explicitly state in several places that Brahm (٢) is an absolute essence (ذات مطلقة) transcendent beyond being encompassed by any limit. To him belong the Beautiful Names and the exalted attributes of life, knowledge, and power; he is transcendent beyond the descriptions of deficiency and the accidents of matter and body; there is nothing like him.

They explicitly state (٣) that He, the Exalted, is a single essence (إحدى الذات), was not born from anything, did not give birth to anything, and has no peer or likeness whatsoever.

They explicitly state (٤) that the truth is that none should be worshipped besides Him, the Exalted, nor should proximity be sought to any other through an offering; rather, the one worthy of worship is He alone, without any partner.

They frequently state (٥) the Resurrection (القيامة) and that it is the term to which creation ends. They describe the reward of deeds and their punishment after death in a way that does not refuse correspondence to the intermediate realm (البرزخ) without being necessarily interpreted as reincarnation.

There is no mention in these divine discussions presented therein of idols, statues, the directing of worship, or the presentation of offerings to them.

These which we have quoted from the Upanishads—and what we have left out is more—are noble truths and true knowledge (معارف حقة) in which the sound human primordial nature (الفطرة) finds tranquility. They—as you see—negate all the principles of idolatry presented at the beginning of the discussion.

What deep reflection guides to is that these were high truths discovered by individuals from the people of Allah's guardianship (أهل ولاية الله), who then informed some of their students who took from them of what they found. However, they mostly spoke in symbols (الرمز) and used parables (الأمثال) in their teachings.

Then, what was taken from these individuals was made the foundation upon which the way of life—which is the religion agreed upon by the general public—is built. These are subtle knowledges that none can bear except individuals from the people of knowledge (أهل المعرفة) because their level is higher than sense and imagination, which are the public's share of perception, and because of the extreme difficulty of perceiving them for ordinary intellects not trained in true knowledge.

Restricting their attainment to the few among people and depriving the majority of that is the first pitfall for a human religion. For the primordial nature (الفطرة) established the human world based on social gathering (الاجتماع المدني), and the separation of some from others in the way of life—which is religion—is an annulment of the law of the primordial nature and the method of creation.

Furthermore, in that is an abandonment of the path of the intellect (العقل), which is one of the three paths: Revelation (الوحي), Intuition (الكشف), and Intellect. It is the most general and important of them with regard to human worldly life. Revelation is not attained except by the people of infallibility (أهل العصمة) among the honored prophets; Intuition is not honored except for individuals from the people of sincerity and certainty; and people—even the people of Revelation and Intuition—are in dire need of employing intellectual proof (الحجة العقلية) in all affairs of worldly life and cannot do without it. In neglecting this path, compulsory imitation (التقليد الإجباري) is imposed upon all affairs of social life—beliefs, ethics, and actions—and in that is the fall of humanity.

Moreover, in that is the implementation of the law of enslavement (الاستعباد) in human society, as evidenced by long historical experience in human nations that lived in the religion of idolatry or in which the customs of enslavement were practiced by taking lords besides Allah.

2 - The Spread of These Pitfalls to Other Religions:

Other general religions, despite their profession of the monotheism of divinity (توحيد الألوهية), were not safe from the polytheism of worship (شرك العبادة). This led them to be afflicted with the very same pitfalls that Brahmanical idolatry was afflicted with, the most important of which are the three aforementioned.

As for Buddhism and the Sabians, this is manifest in them, and history bears witness to it. Something regarding their beliefs and actions has already been presented.

As for the Magians, they unify "Ahura Mazda" in divinity, but they submit in sanctification to Yazdan, Ahriman, the angels entrusted with the affairs of lordship, the sun, fire, and other things. History recounts the law of enslavement, class distinction, and management that was practiced among them. Reflection and consideration dictate that all of this only seeped into them from the direction of the distortion of the original religion. It has been narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding them: "They had a prophet whom they killed, and a book which they burned."

As for the Jews, the Quran recounts much of their actions, their distortion of the Book of Allah, their taking of scholars as lords besides Allah, and what Allah afflicted them with of the inversion of the primordial nature and the badness of disposition.

As for the Christians, we have detailed the discourse regarding what they deviated in of view and action in the third volume of the book, so refer to it. If you wish, apply the opening of the Gospel of John and the epistles of Paul to the other Gospels and complete it by reviewing the history of the Church; the discourse on that is long.

Deep research into all of that yields that the general calamities in religious societies in the human world are from the legacies of the first idolatry, which took divine knowledges and high true truths—unveiled and exposed—and made them the foundation of religious customs, imposing them upon the general understandings that only find familiarity with the sensible and the perceived. This produced what it produced.

3 - Islam's Reform of These Corruptions:

As for Islam, it reformed these corruptions by casting these high knowledges into the mold of simple expression (البيان الساذج) that is suitable for the digestion of simple understandings and ordinary intellects. Thus, they began to touch them from behind a veil and receive them wrapped and surrounded. This is what is suitable for the state of the general public. As for the elite (الخاصة), they attain them unveiled and exposed in their magnificent beauty and exquisite goodness, safe and tranquil, and they are in the company of those whom Allah has blessed among the prophets, the truthful, the martyrs, and the righteous, and excellent are those as companions. Allah the Exalted said: وَالْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتابِ لَدَيْنا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ — "By the Manifest Book! We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And indeed it is in the Mother Book with Us, [and it is] surely sublime and wise" (43:2-4). And He said: إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ فِي كِتابٍ مَكْنُونٍ لا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ — "This is indeed a noble Quran, in a protected Book—no one touches it except the pure" (56:77-79). And the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "We, the assembly of prophets, have been commanded to speak to people according to the capacity of their intellects."

It treated the disaster of polytheism and idolatry in the stage of monotheism by negating independence (الاستقلال) in essence and attributes from everything except Allah, the Glorified; for He, the Exalted, is the Sustainer (القيوم) over everything. It focused understandings in the knowledge of divinity between anthropomorphism (التشبيه) and transcendence (التنزيه), describing Him, the Exalted, as having life but not like our life, knowledge but not like our knowledge, power but not like our power, hearing but not like our hearing, and sight but not like our sight. In short, there is nothing like Him, and He is greater than can be described. Along with this, it commanded people not to say anything regarding that except based on knowledge, and not to lean toward a belief except based on an intellectual proof that their intellects and understandings can digest.

Thus, it succeeded, firstly, in presenting the religion to the general public and the elite as a single law; secondly, in allowing the sound intellect to function without leaving this divine gift in vain and unutilized; and thirdly, in bringing the different classes in human society as close as possible without blessing one and depriving another, or advancing one and delaying another. The Exalted said: إِنَّ هذِهِ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً وَأَنَا رَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُونِ — "Indeed this community of yours is one community, and I am your Lord. So worship Me" (21:92). And He said: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْناكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ وَأُنْثى وَجَعَلْناكُمْ شُعُوباً وَقَبائِلَ لِتَعارَفُوا إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ أَتْقاكُمْ — "O mankind! Indeed We created you from a male and a female, and made you nations and tribes that you may identify yourselves with one another. Indeed the noblest of you in the sight of Allah is the most God-conscious among you" (49:13).

This is a summary of the discourse; you can find the detailed discourse on its aspects in various discussions that have preceded in this book, and Allah is the One whose help is sought.

4 - It may be thought that what is mentioned in supplications regarding seeking intercession (الاستشفاع) through the Prophet and his immaculate progeny (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), asking Him, the Exalted, by their right (بحقهم), visiting their graves, kissing them, seeking blessing from their soil, and venerating their traces is from the forbidden polytheism—which is idolatrous polytheism—arguing that this type of devotional orientation involves granting a lordly influence (تأثير ربوبي) to other than Him, the Exalted, which is polytheism. They argue that the practitioners of idolatry only associated partners because they said of their idols: "These are our intercessors with Allah," and their saying: "We only worship them so that they may bring us closer to Allah in rank." They claim there is no difference in worshipping other than Allah, the Glorified, whether that other is a prophet, a saint (ولي), a tyrant among tyrants, or others; all are from the forbidden polytheism.

What they have missed, firstly, is that the establishment of influence (التأثير)—whether material or non-material—in other than Him, the Exalted, is necessary and cannot be denied. He, the Exalted, has attributed influence of all kinds to other than Himself in His speech. Negating influence from other than Him, the Exalted, absolutely necessitates the nullification of the general law of cause and effect (العلية والمعلولية), which is the pillar in all proofs of monotheism, and in that is the destruction of the foundation of monotheism. Yes, what is negated of influence from other than Him, the Exalted, is independence (الاستقلال) in influence, and no one disputes this. As for negating absolute influence, in that is the denial of the self-evident truth of the intellect and a departure from the human primordial nature.

And whoever seeks intercession through the people of intercession (أهل الشفاعة) whom Allah mentioned in such as His saying: وَلا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ الشَّفاعَةَ إِلَّا مَنْ شَهدَ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ — "Those whom they invoke besides Him do not possess [the power of] intercession, except those who bear witness to the truth and they know [it]" (43:86), and His saying: وَلا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضى — "and they do not intercede except for him with whom He is pleased" (21:28).

Or asks Allah by their status (بجاههم) and adjures Him by their right (بحقهم) which He has granted them over Himself in such as His saying absolutely: وَلَقَدْ سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنا لِعِبادِنَا الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنْصُورُونَ وَإِنَّ جُنْدَنا لَهُمُ الْغالِبُونَ — "Certainly Our decree has gone forth for Our servants, the apostles, that they will indeed be the ones to be helped, and that Our host will indeed be the victors" (37:171-173), and His saying: إِنَّا لَنَنْصُرُ رُسُلَنا وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا — "Indeed We shall help Our apostles and those who have faith" (40:51).

Or venerates them and manifests love for them by visiting their graves, kissing them, and seeking blessing from their soil insofar as they are the signs (آيات) of Allah and His symbols (شعائره), holding fast to such as the saying of the Exalted: وَمَنْ يُعَظِّمْ شَعائِرَ اللهِ فَإِنَّها مِنْ تَقْوَى الْقُلُوبِ — "And whoever venerates the sacraments of Allah—indeed that arises from the God-consciousness of hearts" (22:32), and the verse of kinship (آية القربى) and other things from the Book and the Sunnah.

He is, in all of that, seeking the means (الوسيلة) through them to Allah, and He, the Exalted, has said: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ وَابْتَغُوا إِلَيْهِ الْوسِيلَةَ — "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and seek the means [of recourse] to Him" (5:35). Thus, He legislated the seeking of the means through Him and made them—through what He legislated of loving them, honoring them, and venerating them—means to Him. There is no meaning to necessitating the love of something and its veneration while forbidding the traces of that. Therefore, there is no barrier to seeking proximity to Allah through loving them and venerating their affair and the traces of that, provided it is in the manner of recourse (التوسل) and seeking intercession (الاستشفاع) without granting them independence of influence or worship whatsoever.

Secondly, they have overlooked the distinction between worshipping other than Allah in the hope that they might intercede with Allah or bring one closer to Him, and worshipping Allah alone while seeking intercession (الاستشفاع) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — and proximity through them to Him. In the first case, independence (الاستقلال) is attributed to them and worship (العبادة) is devoted sincerely to other than the Exalted; this is polytheism (الشرك) in both servitude and worship. In the second case, independence is reserved solely for Allah, the Exalted, and worship is restricted to Him alone, without any partner.

Indeed, the Exalted only censured the polytheists (المشركين) for their saying: مَا نَعْبُدُهُمْ إِلَّا لِيُقَرِّبُونَا إِلَى اللَّهِ زُلْفَىٰ "...We only worship them so that they may bring us near to Allah." (39:3), because they granted them independence and directed their worship toward them instead of Allah, the Glorified. Had they said: "We worship Allah alone, yet we hope that His angels, His messengers, or His friends (أولياؤه) will intercede for us by His permission, or we seek a means (نتوسل) to Allah by honoring His symbols (شعائره) and loving His friends," they would not have disbelieved thereby. Rather, their "partners" would have become like the Kaaba (الكعبة) in Islam; it is a direction (وجهة) and not an object of worship, for it is Allah alone who is worshipped by turning toward it.

I wonder what these people would say regarding the Black Stone (الحجر الأسود) and what has been legislated in Islam concerning its touching and kissing? Likewise, what of the Kaaba? Is all of that a form of polytheism (الشرك) that has been exempted from the ruling of prohibition? The ruling [against polytheism] is a necessary rational judgment (حكم ضروري عقلي) that accepts neither specification (تخصصا) nor exception. Or is it that such acts are purely the worship of Allah, while the Stone serves as a path or a direction? If so, then what is the difference between it and anything else, provided its veneration is not done by attributing independence to it or devoting worship to it? The general proofs (مطلقات) for honoring the symbols of Allah (شعائر الله), venerating the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, loving him, and having affection for him and his Household (أهل بيته) remain valid in their respective places.

Ayahs

وَإِلَىٰ عَادٍ أَخَاهُمْ هُودًا ۚ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ ۖ إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا مُفْتَرُونَ (٥٠)
And to ‘Ad [We sent] Hud, their brother. He said, ‘O my people! Worship Allah. You have no other god besides Him: you merely fabricate [the deities that you worship]. (50)
يَـٰقَوْمِ لَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْرًا ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱلَّذِى فَطَرَنِىٓ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (٥١)
‘O my people! I do not ask you any reward for it. My reward lies only with Him who originated me. Do you not exercise your reason? (51)
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ ٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ يُرْسِلِ ٱلسَّمَآءَ عَلَيْكُم مِّدْرَارًا وَيَزِدْكُمْ قُوَّةً إِلَىٰ قُوَّتِكُمْ وَلَا تَتَوَلَّوْا۟ مُجْرِمِينَ (٥٢)
‘O my people! Plead with your Lord for forgiveness, then turn to Him penitently: He will send copious rains for you from the sky, and add power to your [present] power. So do not turn your backs [on Him] as guilty ones.’ (52)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰهُودُ مَا جِئْتَنَا بِبَيِّنَةٍ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِتَارِكِىٓ ءَالِهَتِنَا عَن قَوْلِكَ وَمَا نَحْنُ لَكَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ (٥٣)
They said, ‘O Hud, you have not brought us any manifest proof. We are not going to abandon our gods for what you say, and we are not going to believe you. (53)
إِن نَّقُولُ إِلَّا ٱعْتَرَىٰكَ بَعْضُ ءَالِهَتِنَا بِسُوٓءٍ ۗ قَالَ إِنِّىٓ أُشْهِدُ ٱللَّهَ وَٱشْهَدُوٓا۟ أَنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ (٥٤)
All we say is that some of our gods have visited you with some evil.’ He said, ‘I call Allah to witness—and you too be [my] witnesses—that I repudiate what you take as [His] partners (54)
مِن دُونِهِۦ ۖ فَكِيدُونِى جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ لَا تُنظِرُونِ (٥٥)
besides Him. Now try out your stratagems against me, together, without granting me any respite. (55)
إِنِّى تَوَكَّلْتُ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ رَبِّى وَرَبِّكُم ۚ مَّا مِن دَآبَّةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ ءَاخِذٌۢ بِنَاصِيَتِهَآ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٥٦)
Indeed I have put my trust in Allah, my Lord and your Lord. There is no living being but He holds it by its forelock. Indeed my Lord is on a straight path. (56)
فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَقَدْ أَبْلَغْتُكُم مَّآ أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِۦٓ إِلَيْكُمْ ۚ وَيَسْتَخْلِفُ رَبِّى قَوْمًا غَيْرَكُمْ وَلَا تَضُرُّونَهُۥ شَيْـًٔا ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ حَفِيظٌ (٥٧)
But if you turn your backs [on me], then [know that] I have communicated to you whatever I was sent to you with. My Lord will make another people succeed you, and you will not hurt Allah in the least. Indeed my Lord is watchful over all things.’ (57)
وَلَمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا نَجَّيْنَا هُودًا وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنَّا وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُم مِّنْ عَذَابٍ غَلِيظٍ (٥٨)
When Our edict came, We delivered Hud and the faithful who were with him, by a mercy from Us, and We delivered them from a harsh punishment. (58)
وَتِلْكَ عَادٌ ۖ جَحَدُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَعَصَوْا۟ رُسُلَهُۥ وَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَ كُلِّ جَبَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ (٥٩)
Such were [the people of] Ad: they impugned the signs of their Lord and disobeyed His apostles, and followed the dictates of every obstinate tyrant. (59)
وَأُتْبِعُوا۟ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلدُّنْيَا لَعْنَةً وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ عَادًا كَفَرُوا۟ رَبَّهُمْ ۗ أَلَا بُعْدًا لِّعَادٍ قَوْمِ هُودٍ (٦٠)
So they were pursued by a curse in this world and on the Day of Resurrection. Behold! ‘Ad indeed defied their Lord. Now, away with ‘Ad, the people of Hud! (60)

(Commentary)

The verses recount the story of the Prophet Hud and his people, who were the first ‘Ad (عاد الأولى). He (peace be upon him) is the first prophet mentioned by Allah, the Exalted, in His Book after Noah (peace be upon him). Allah commends his efforts in establishing the true call and rising against idolatry (الوثنية). The mention of the people of Hud follows the mention of the people of Noah; as the Exalted says in several places of His speech: قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَعادٍ وَثَمُودَ — "the people of Noah, and ‘Ad, and Thamud."

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِلى عادٍ أَخاهُمْ هُوداً — "And to ‘Ad [We sent] their brother Hud" (11:50). He was their brother in lineage because he was one of them; members of a tribe are called brothers due to their shared descent from the tribal patriarch. The sentence is conjoined to the previous saying of the Exalted: نُوحاً إِلى قَوْمِهِ — "Noah to his people" (11:25). The implied meaning is: "And We certainly sent to ‘Ad their brother Hud." Perhaps the omission of the verb is what necessitated placing the prepositional phrase before the object in the conjoined clause, contrary to the initial clause where it was said: وَإِلى عادٍ أَخاهُمْ etc., rather than saying "and Hud to ‘Ad," for example, as was said: نُوحاً إِلى قَوْمِهِ. This is because the indication of the prepositional phrase—namely, إِلى عادٍ—toward the implied act of "sending" is more apparent and clear.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ يا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرُهُ إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا مُفْتَرُونَ — "He said, ‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him. You are nothing but fabricators’" (11:50). This speech occurs as a response, as if the listener, upon hearing His saying: "And to ‘Ad [We sent] their brother Hud," asks: "So what did he say to them?" Thus it was said: "He said, ‘O my people, worship Allah...’" For this reason, the sentence is disconnected (الفصل) without a conjunction.

His saying: اعْبُدُوا اللهَ — "worship Allah" is in the context of exclusivity (الحصر); meaning: worship Him alone and do not worship others among the deities you have taken as lords besides Allah, worshipping them so they may be intercessors for you with Allah without you worshipping Him, the Exalted. The evidence for this exclusivity is his subsequent saying: ما لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرُهُ إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا مُفْتَرُونَ — "You have no other god besides Him. You are nothing but fabricators," as it indicates they had taken deities whom they worshipped as a fabrication (افتراء) against Allah regarding partnership and intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: يا قَوْمِ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً — "O my people! I do not ask you any reward for it" (11:51) to the end of the verse. Al-Tabarsi said in al-Majma‘: "The term al-fatr (الفطر) means the splitting (الشقق) of a matter by Allah’s command, just as leaves split forth from a tree. From this comes 'Allah originated (فطر) the creation,' because it is as if they split forth from Him and appeared." End quote. Al-Raghib said: "The root of al-fatr is splitting lengthwise... and 'Allah originated (فطر) the creation' refers to the bringing of a thing into existence (إيجاد) and its origination (إبداع) upon a form predisposed for a particular action. Thus, His saying: فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها — 'the nature (فطرة) of Allah according to which He originated mankind' (30:30) is an allusion by Him, the Exalted, to what He originated and instilled in people regarding the knowledge of Him. The primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — is that which He instilled in them of the capacity for the knowledge of faith (الإيمان), which is alluded to in His saying: وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَهُمْ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللهُ — 'If you ask them who created them, they will surely say, "Allah"'" (43:87). End quote.

It appears that al-fatr (الفطر) is bringing into existence from absolute non-existence (العدم البحت). The specific nuance understood from verses like: "the primordial nature (الفطرة) of Allah according to which He originated mankind," arises from the construction of the "type" (بناء النوع) contained within the word fitrah (فطرة), which follows the morphological pattern fi‘lah (فعلة). Based on this, the interpretation by some of al-fitrah as merely "creation" (الخلقة) is far from correct. Rather, creation (الخلق) is the bringing of a form into existence from matter by way of assembling parts; the Exalted says: وَإِذْ تَخْلُقُ مِنَ الطِّينِ كَهَيْئَةِ الطَّيْرِ — "and when you would create from clay the form of a bird" (5:110).

The speech is intended to remove suspicion and the charge of futility (العبث). The meaning is: "O my people, I do not ask you for a reward or compensation for what I call you to, such that you might suspect me of seeking a benefit for myself even if it harms you. Nor am I calling you without any sought reward at all, which would make the action futile. Rather, I only seek the reward for it from Allah, Who brought me into existence (أوجدني) and originated me (أبدعني). Will you not then use your intellect (أفلا تعقلون) regarding what I say to you, so it becomes clear that I am a sincere advisor to you in my call, desiring nothing but to lead you to the truth?"

His saying, the Exalted: وَيا قَوْمِ اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوا إِلَيْهِ يُرْسِلِ السَّماءَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِدْراراً — "‘O my people! Plead with your Lord for forgiveness, then turn to Him penitently: He will send copious rains for you from the sky’" (11:52) to the end of the verse. The discussion regarding the meaning of His saying: "Plead with your Lord for forgiveness, then turn to Him penitently" was presented at the beginning of the Surah.

His saying: يُرْسِلِ السَّماءَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِدْراراً — "He will send copious rains for you from the sky" is in the position of the result (الجزاء) for his saying: "Plead with your Lord for forgiveness..." meaning: if you seek His forgiveness and turn to Him, He will send the sky upon you in abundance. What is meant by "the sky" (السماء) here is the clouds, for everything that is high and overshadows is called sama’. It is also said to mean rain, which is a common usage. The term copious (مدرار) is an intensive form derived from al-darr (الدر). The root of al-darr is milk, then it was used metaphorically for rain and for every benefit and profit. Thus, sending the sky midraran means sending clouds that pour down continuous, beneficial rains by which the earth is revived, crops and grass grow, and gardens and orchards flourish.

His saying: وَيَزِدْكُمْ قُوَّةً إِلى قُوَّتِكُمْ — "and add power to your power" (11:52). It is said that this refers to adding the power of faith (الإيمان) to the power of their bodies, as the people were possessors of great physical strength and vigor; had they believed, the power of faith would have been added to their physical strength. It is also said that it refers to physical strength, just as Noah said to his people: اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ إِنَّهُ كانَ غَفَّاراً يُرْسِلِ السَّماءَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِدْراراً وَيُمْدِدْكُمْ بِأَمْوالٍ وَبَنِينَ — "‘Plead with your Lord for forgiveness—indeed He is all-forgiving—He will send copious rains for you from the sky, and aid you with wealth and sons’" (71:10-12). Perhaps a general application is more appropriate.

His saying: وَلا تَتَوَلَّوْا مُجْرِمِينَ — "and do not turn away as guilty ones" (11:52) serves as an explanation for his saying: "Plead with your Lord for forgiveness, then turn to Him penitently." That is: your worship of the deities you have taken besides Allah is a crime (إجرام) and a sin (معصية) that necessitates the descent of divine wrath upon you; so seek Allah’s forgiveness for your crimes and return to Him with faith so that He may have mercy on you by sending down pouring, rainy clouds and adding power to your power.

In the verse, there is, firstly, an indication or evidence that they were afflicted with the withholding of rain, drought (الجدب), and famine (السنة), as might be alluded to by his saying: "He will send the sky," as well as their saying as recounted by Allah, the Exalted, in another place: فَلَمَّا رَأَوْهُ عارِضاً مُسْتَقْبِلَ أَوْدِيَتِهِمْ قالُوا هذا عارِضٌ مُمْطِرُنا بَلْ هُوَ مَا اسْتَعْجَلْتُمْ بِهِ رِيحٌ فِيها عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ — "When they saw it as a cloud advancing toward their valleys, they said, ‘This is a cloud that will bring us rain.’ ‘No, it is what you sought to hasten: a hurricane containing a painful punishment’" (46:24).

Secondly: there is a complete correlation between human actions and the cosmic events that affect them. Righteous deeds (الصالحات) necessitate the overflowing of goodness and the descent of blessings (البركات), while evil deeds invite a succession of calamities and trials, and bring about vengeance, misery, and destruction. This is indicated by the saying of the Exalted: وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرى آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "If the people of the towns had been faithful and God-conscious, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven and the earth" (7:96). A detailed discussion of this was provided in the commentary on verses 94–102 of Surah al-A‘raf in the eighth volume of this book, and in the section on the rulings of actions in the second volume.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا يا هُودُ ما جِئْتَنا بِبَيِّنَةٍ وَما نَحْنُ بِتارِكِي آلِهَتِنا عَنْ قَوْلِكَ وَما نَحْنُ لَكَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ — "They said, ‘O Hud, you have not brought us any manifest proof. We are not going to abandon our gods [just] because of what you say, and we do not believe in you’" (11:53). Hud had asked two things of them in his saying: "O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him" through the end of the three verses: that they abandon their deities and return to the worship of Allah alone, and that they believe in him and obey him in what he advised them. They responded to him with what is in this verse, both generally and specifically:

As for the general response, it was their saying: ما جِئْتَنا بِبَيِّنَةٍ — "you have not brought us any manifest proof," meaning that your call is devoid of proof (الحجة) and miraculous signs (الآية المعجزة), and there is no reason to listen to something of this nature.

As for the specific response, they answered his call to reject the partners by saying: وَما نَحْنُ بِتارِكِي آلِهَتِنا عَنْ قَوْلِكَ — "We are not going to abandon our gods [just] because of what you say," and they answered his call to faith and obedience by saying: وَما نَحْنُ لَكَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ — "and we do not believe in you." Thus, they made him despair in both matters.

Then they mentioned to him the opinion they held regarding him, so that he would completely lose hope of them responding: إِنْ نَقُولُ إِلَّا اعْتَراكَ بَعْضُ آلِهَتِنا بِسُوءٍ — "All we say is that some of our gods have visited you with some evil" (11:54). The term visitation (الاعتراء) means to confront or afflict. They were saying: "We believe regarding your situation that some of our gods have afflicted you with an evil such as confusion or madness because of your insulting them and mentioning them poorly; thus your intellect has departed, and no weight is given to what you utter in the form of a call."

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ إِنِّي أُشْهِدُ اللهَ وَاشْهَدُوا أَنِّي بَرِيءٌ مِمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ فَكِيدُونِي جَمِيعاً ثُمَّ لا تُنْظِرُونِ — "He said, ‘I call Allah to witness, and you [too] bear witness, that I am quit of what you polytheize besides Him. So scheme against me, all of you, then do not give me any respite’" (11:54-55). Hud (peace be upon him) responded to their statement by declaring his disavowal (البراءة) of their partners besides Allah, and then by challenging them to scheme against him all together and not give him any respite.

His saying: أَنِّي بَرِيءٌ مِمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ — "that I am quit of what you polytheize besides Him" is a performative declaration (إنشاء) rather than a mere report (إخبار), as is appropriate for the station of disavowal. This does not contradict his being quit of them from the very beginning, for the outward manifestation of disavowal does not negate its prior existence. His saying: فَكِيدُونِي جَمِيعاً ثُمَّ لا تُنْظِرُونِ — "So scheme against me, all of you, then do not give me any respite" is a command and a prohibition intended to demonstrate their incapacity (تعجيزيان).

He (peace be upon him) responded in this manner so that the people might witness that their deities could not touch him with any harm despite his open disavowal. If they were deities possessing knowledge and power, they would have overcome him and taken revenge for themselves, as they claimed that some of their gods had visited him with evil. This is a manifest proof (حجة بينة) that they are not deities and that they had not visited him with any evil as they claimed. Furthermore, they would witness for themselves that they were unable to kill or punish him, despite being possessors of such strength and power that none could equal them in severity and assault. Were he not a prophet from Allah, truthful in what he says and protected by his Lord, they would have been able to inflict upon him whatever punishment or repulsion they desired.

From this, the reason for his calling his Lord, the Glorified, and his people to witness his disavowal becomes clear. As for calling Allah to witness, it was so that his disavowal would be in its true reality and from the depths of the heart, without embellishment or hypocrisy. As for calling them to witness, it was so they would know of it and then witness how the matter would unfold—the silence of their deities and their own inability to take revenge or punish him.

It also becomes clear that the suggestion of some is correct: that this demonstration of incapacity (التعجيز) is the miracle (معجزة) of Hud (peace be upon him). This is because the apparent nature of the response is to cut off the rejection they presented in the form of an argument, which included their saying: "you have not brought us any manifest proof." It is highly unlikely that the Prophet Hud (peace be upon him) would neglect to address this in his call and argument, especially since this challenge and demonstration of incapacity is inherently suitable to be taken as a miraculous sign (آية معجزة), just as the disavowal of the partners besides Allah is suitable to reveal that they are not deities and that none of them had visited him with evil.

The truth is that his saying: إِنِّي أُشْهِدُ اللهَ وَاشْهَدُوا — "I call Allah to witness, and you [too] bear witness" through the end of the two verses, contains both a rational proof (حجة عقلية) for the falsehood of the divinity of the partners, and a miraculous sign for the truth of Hud’s (peace be upon him) messengership.

In his saying: جَمِيعاً — "all of you," there is an indication that his intent was to demonstrate the incapacity of both them and their deities together, making it a more complete proof of his being upon the truth and their being upon falsehood.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنِّي تَوَكَّلْتُ عَلَى اللهِ رَبِّي وَرَبِّكُمْ — "‘I have indeed put my trust in Allah, my Lord and your Lord’" (11:56) through the end of the verse. Since a command in the form of a challenge (التعجيز) can be motivated by the desire to show the opponent's weakness and lack of power, or it can be issued with the motive that the commander does not fear the opponent—even if the opponent is capable of doing what is commanded, but is unable to frighten the commander, coerce him into obedience, or force him to do what he wants—as in the saying of the magicians to Pharaoh: فَاقْضِ ما أَنْتَ قاضٍ إِنَّما تَقْضِي هذِهِ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا — "‘So decide what you will decide. You can only decide about the life of this world’" (20:72).

And since his saying: "So scheme against me, all of you, then do not give me any respite" could potentially be interpreted as merely showing that he does not fear them regardless of what they do to him, he followed it up to repel this possibility by saying: إِنِّي تَوَكَّلْتُ عَلَى اللهِ رَبِّي وَرَبِّكُمْ — "‘I have indeed put my trust in Allah, my Lord and your Lord’." He mentioned that he is reliant in his affair upon Allah, Who manages his affair and their affair. Then he followed this with: ما مِنْ دَابَّةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ آخِذٌ بِناصِيَتِها إِنَّ رَبِّي عَلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ — "‘There is no living being but He holds it by its forelock. Indeed my Lord is on a straight path’" (11:56). He mentioned that he is successful in this trust (التوكل), for Allah encompasses them all, overpowers them, and judges according to a single sunnah (سنة) — a divine law or way of acting — which is the aiding of the truth and its triumph over falsehood when they confront and struggle against each other.

Thus, his disavowal of their idols and his challenging them in their current state by saying: "So scheme against me, all of you, then do not give me any respite," followed by his remaining among them in well-being and safety—they not touching him with any evil nor being able to reach him with harm—is a miraculous sign (آية معجزة) and a heavenly proof that he is the Messenger of Allah to them.

In his saying: ما مِنْ دَابَّةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ آخِذٌ بِناصِيَتِها إِنَّ رَبِّي عَلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ, the term living being (الدابة) refers to everything that crawls upon the earth among the various types of animals. "Holding by the forelock" (الأخذ بالناصية) is a metaphor for complete authority and ultimate power. His being, the Exalted, "on a straight path" (على صراط مستقيم) means that His sunnah in creation is one, firm, and unchanging; it is the management of affairs according to the path of justice and wisdom. Thus, He establishes the truth and nullifies falsehood when they conflict.

The meaning is: "I have put my trust in Allah, my Lord and your Lord, regarding the success of the proof I have presented to you—which is the manifestation of disavowal of your deities and the fact that you and your deities cannot harm me in the least—for He is the Owner (المالك) who possesses authority over me, over you, and over every living being. His just sunnah is firm and unchanging; He will soon aid His religion and protect me from your evil."

He did not say: "Indeed my Lord and your Lord is on a straight path," in parallel with his saying: "in Allah, my Lord and your Lord." This is because he is in the station of supplicating for himself against his people, expecting that Allah will protect him from their evil. He takes Him, the Exalted, as a Lord for himself, whereas the people do not; thus it was more appropriate to count Him as a Lord for himself and hold fast to the bond of servitude (العبودية) between him and his Lord so that his request might succeed. This differs from the station of his saying: "I have put my trust in Allah, my Lord and your Lord," where he intended to explain the universality of authority and encompassment.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَقَدْ أَبْلَغْتُكُمْ ما أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِ إِلَيْكُمْ — "‘But if you turn your backs, I have already delivered to you what I was sent with to you’" (11:57). This sentence from his speech (peace be upon him) looks toward their saying at the end of their dispute: "All we say is that some of our gods have visited you with some evil," which indicates they were certain they would not believe in him and were persistent in rejection. The meaning is: "If you turn away and refuse to believe in me and obey my command, I have already delivered to you the message of my Lord, the proof has been completed against you, and the calamity has become binding upon you."

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَسْتَخْلِفُ رَبِّي قَوْماً غَيْرَكُمْ وَلا تَضُرُّونَهُ شَيْئاً إِنَّ رَبِّي عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ حَفِيظٌ — "‘and my Lord will situate another people in your stead, and you cannot do Him any harm. Indeed my Lord is watchful (حفيظ) over all things’" (11:57). This is a threat and an announcement of the consequence that their crime entails. He had promised them that if they sought Allah’s forgiveness and turned to Him, He would send copious rains and add power to their power, and he forbade them from turning away as guilty ones; thus, in this is a severe punishment.

His saying: وَيَسْتَخْلِفُ رَبِّي قَوْماً غَيْرَكُمْ — "and my Lord will situate another people in your stead" means He will make a people other than you successors (خلفاء) on the earth in your place, for man is a successor from Him on the earth, as the Exalted said: إِنِّي جاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً — "‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth’" (2:30). He (peace be upon him) had already explained to them that they were successors on the earth after the people of Noah, as the Exalted said, recounting his words to his people: وَاذْكُرُوا إِذْ جَعَلَكُمْ خُلَفاءَ مِنْ بَعْدِ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَزادَكُمْ فِي الْخَلْقِ بَصْطَةً — "‘Remember when He made you successors after the people of Noah, and increased you in stature’" (7:69).

The apparent context is that the declarative sentence is conjoined to another implied one, the meaning being: "My Lord will take you away and situate another people in your stead," similar to His saying: إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ وَيَسْتَخْلِفْ مِنْ بَعْدِكُمْ ما يَشاءُ — "If He wishes, He will take you away, and make whomever He wishes succeed you" (6:133).

His saying: وَلا تَضُرُّونَهُ شَيْئاً — "and you cannot do Him any harm." The apparent context is that it is a completion of what preceded it; meaning: you are not able to harm Him in the least by escaping or otherwise if He intends to destroy you, nor does your punishment and destruction cause Him to lose anything He intends. For my Lord is watchful (حفيظ) over all things; nothing escapes His knowledge, and nothing eludes His power. The commentators have other views on this verse that are far from the truth, which we have set aside.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمَّا جاءَ أَمْرُنا نَجَّيْنا هُوداً وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِنَّا وَنَجَّيْناهُمْ مِنْ عَذابٍ غَلِيظٍ — "When Our edict came, We delivered Hud and those who had faith with him by a mercy from Us, and We delivered them from a harsh punishment" (11:58). What is meant by the "coming of the edict" (مجيء الأمر) is the descent of the punishment, or more precisely, the issuance of the divine command that entails the decisive judgment (القضاء الفاصل) between the Messenger and his people, as the Exalted said: وَما كانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ بِآيَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ فَإِذا جاءَ أَمْرُ اللهِ قُضِيَ بِالْحَقِّ وَخَسِرَ هُنالِكَ الْمُبْطِلُونَ — "No apostle may bring a sign except by Allah’s leave. But when Allah’s edict comes, judgment is made with justice, and it is then that the falsifiers are losers" (40:78).

His saying: بِرَحْمَةٍ مِنَّا — "by a mercy from Us." It appears that what is meant is the specific mercy for the believers that necessitates their victory in their religion and their deliverance from being included in the divine wrath and the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال). The Exalted said: إِنَّا لَنَنْصُرُ رُسُلَنا وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَيَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْأَشْهادُ — "Indeed We shall help Our apostles and those who have faith in the life of the world and on the day when the witnesses rise up" (40:51).

His saying: وَنَجَّيْناهُمْ مِنْ عَذابٍ غَلِيظٍ — "and We delivered them from a harsh punishment." The apparent context is that it refers to the punishment that encompassed the disbelievers among the people; thus it would be a form of explanatory conjunction (عطف التفسير) relative to what preceded it. It is also said that it refers to the punishment of the Hereafter, but this is of no consequence.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتِلْكَ عادٌ جَحَدُوا بِآياتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَعَصَوْا رُسُلَهُ وَاتَّبَعُوا أَمْرَ كُلِّ جَبَّارٍ عنيدٍ — "Such were ‘Ad: they denied the signs of their Lord and disobeyed His apostles, and followed the bid of every obstinate tyrant" (11:59). This verse and the one following it are a summary after a summary of the story of ‘Ad. The first of the two summaries is His saying: "Such were ‘Ad..." through His saying: "...and on the Day of Resurrection." It mentions that they denied the signs of their Lord—consisting of wisdom, exhortation, and miraculous signs—which had made clear to them the path of rectitude and distinguished the truth from falsehood for them; yet they denied them after knowledge had come to them.

They disobeyed the apostles of their Lord—namely Hud and the apostles before him—for the disobedience of one of them is the disobedience of all, as they all call to one religion. Thus, they disobeyed the person of Hud, and by disobeying him, they disobeyed the rest of Allah’s apostles. This is apparent in His saying in another place: كَذَّبَتْ عادٌ الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِذْ قالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ هُودٌ أَلا تَتَّقُونَ — "‘Ad impugned the apostles, when Hud, their brother, said to them, ‘Will you not be God-conscious?’" (26:123-124). It is also suggested by His saying: وَاذْكُرْ أَخا عادٍ إِذْ أَنْذَرَ قَوْمَهُ بِالْأَحْقافِ وَقَدْ خَلَتِ النُّذُرُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ — "And remember the brother of ‘Ad, when he warned his people at Ahqaf—and warners have passed away before him and after him" (46:21). It is possible they had other apostles sent to them between Hud and Noah (peace be upon them) who are not mentioned in the Mighty Book, but the context of the verses does not support this.

They followed the bid of every obstinate tyrant (جبار عنيد) among their tyrants, which distracted them from following Hud and what he called to. The tyrant (الجبار) is the great one who overpowers people with his will and coerces them into what he desires, and the obstinate (العنيد) is the one of much stubbornness who does not accept the truth. This is the summary of their state: denial of the signs, disobedience of the apostles, and obedience to tyrants.

Then Allah mentioned the consequence of their affair by His saying: وَأُتْبِعُوا فِي هذِهِ الدُّنْيا لَعْنَةً وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ — "They were pursued by a curse in this world and on the Day of Resurrection" (11:60). That is, Allah pursued them in this world with a curse and banishment from mercy. The manifestation of this curse is the punishment that followed them and caught up with them, or the sins and evil deeds recorded against them as long as the world lasts, for they established a sunnah of polytheism and disbelief for those after them; the Exalted said: وَنَكْتُبُ ما قَدَّمُوا وَآثارَهُمْ — "and We write what they have sent ahead and their effects" (36:12).

It is also said: the meaning is that a curse caught up with them in this world, such that everyone who learned of their state after them, everyone who perceived their ruins, and everyone to whom the apostles after them brought their news, would curse them.

As for the curse on the Day of Resurrection, its manifestation is the eternal punishment that will catch up with them on that day, for the Day of Resurrection is a day of recompense and nothing else.

In the following of His saying in the verse: وَأُتْبِعُوا (they were pursued) with His saying: وَأُتْبِعُوا, there is a manifest subtlety.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلا إِنَّ عاداً كَفَرُوا رَبَّهُمْ أَلا بُعْداً لِعادٍ قَوْمِ هُودٍ — "Behold! ‘Ad indeed disbelieved in their Lord. Behold! Begone with ‘Ad, the people of Hud!" (11:60). Meaning they disbelieved in their Lord; the word "Lord" is in the accusative case due to the removal of the preposition (منصوب بنزع الخافض). This is the second summary we alluded to, which summarizes the first summary. Thus, His saying: "Behold! ‘Ad indeed..." etc., corresponds to the description of their state mentioned in His saying: "Such were ‘Ad: they denied..." etc., and His saying: "Behold! Begone with ‘Ad..." etc., corresponds to His saying: "They were pursued by a curse in this world..." etc.

This sentence supports the view that the intended curse previously mentioned is the divine curse rather than the curse of people. The most appropriate interpretation for it would be one of the first two views among the three previous views, specifically the second view rather than the third.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, narrated from Abu ‘Amr al-Sa‘di, who said: ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him) said regarding His saying: إِنَّ رَبِّي عَلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ — "meaning He is upon truth; He rewards goodness with goodness, and evil with evil, and He pardons whom He wills and forgives, glorified and exalted is He."

I say: This has already been clarified. It has been narrated in reports from them (peace be upon them) that: "‘Ad’s lands were in the desert, and they had many crops and palm trees. They had long lives and tall bodies. They worshipped idols, so Allah sent Hud to them to call them to Islam and to cast off peers (الأنداد) — but they refused and did not believe in Hud and they harmed him — so the sky withheld from them for seven years until they suffered drought." (The hadith).

The withholding of the sky from them was also narrated through Sunni channels from al-Dahhak, who said: "Rain was withheld from ‘Ad for three years — so Hud said to them: ‘Plead with your Lord for forgiveness, then turn to Him penitently — He will send copious rains for you from the sky’ — but they only persisted in their ways." It has already been mentioned that the verses are not devoid of an indication toward this.

Know that the narrations regarding the story of Hud and ‘Ad are many, but they contain matters that cannot be authenticated through the Book nor supported by rational consideration; therefore, we have omitted their mention.

Other reports have also been transmitted through both Shi‘i and Sunni channels describing the "Garden of ‘Ad" attributed to King Shaddad, which is mentioned in the saying of the Exalted: إِرَمَ ذاتِ الْعِمادِ الَّتِي لَمْ يُخْلَقْ مِثْلُها فِي الْبِلادِ — "Iram, [the city] of the columns, the like of which was not created in [any of] the lands" (89:7-8). The discussion regarding this will come, Allah willing, in the commentary on Surah al-Fajr.

(A Discourse on the Story of Hud)

1 — ‘Ad, the People of Hud:

These were a people from the Arabs of pre-history who inhabited the Peninsula. Their news has been cut off and their traces erased; history preserves nothing of their lives except legends that cannot be relied upon, and there is no mention of them in the existing Torah.

What the Holy Qur’an mentions of their story is that ‘Ad—and sometimes it calls them the "First ‘Ad" (53:50), which contains an indication that there was a "Second ‘Ad"—were a people who inhabited the Ahqaf (46:21) of the Arabian Peninsula after the people of Noah (7:69).

They had tall bodies (54:20, 69:7) and were given an increase in stature (7:69). They were possessors of great strength and severe assault (41:15, 26:130). They had progress and advancement in civilization and culture, possessing flourishing cities and fertile lands with gardens, palm trees, crops, and noble dwellings (Surah al-Shu‘ara’ and others). Sufficient for you regarding their advancement and great civilization is the saying of the Exalted in describing them: أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِعادٍ إِرَمَ ذاتِ الْعِمادِ الَّتِي لَمْ يُخْلَقْ مِثْلُها فِي الْبِلادِ — "Have you not regarded how your Lord dealt with ‘Ad, Iram, [the city] of the columns, the like of which was not created in [any of] the lands?" (89:6-8).

The people continued to enjoy Allah’s blessings until they changed what was within themselves. Idolatry became deep-rooted among them; they built a monument on every high place for vain play and took up strongholds as if they would live forever. They obeyed their arrogant tyrants, so Allah sent to them their brother Hud to call them to the truth and guide them to worship Allah, reject idols, and act with justice and mercy (26:130). He went to great lengths in exhorting them and disseminating sincere advice among them; he illuminated the path, clarified the way, and cut off their excuses. They met him with refusal and resistance, and confronted him with denial and rejection. None believed in him except a small group, while the majority persisted in transgression and obstinacy. They accused him of folly and madness, and insisted that he bring down upon them the punishment with which he was warning and threatening them. He said: إِنَّمَا الْعِلْمُ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَأُبَلِّغُكُمْ ما أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِ وَلكِنِّي أَراكُمْ قَوْماً تَجْهَلُونَ — "‘The knowledge is only with Allah, and I deliver to you what I have been sent with. But I see that you are an ignorant people’" (46:23).

So Allah sent down the punishment upon them and sent against them the "barren wind" (الريح العقيم) that spared nothing it came upon but made it like decayed bones (51:42) — a "screeching wind" (ريحا صرصرا) during ill-fated days for seven nights and eight days in succession, such that you would see the people therein prostrate as if they were the hollow trunks of palm trees (69:7). It would pluck people away as if they were the trunks of uprooted palm trees (54:20).

When they first saw it as a cloud advancing toward their valleys, they rejoiced and said: "This is a cloud that will bring us rain." But they were mistaken; rather, it was that which they sought to hasten: a wind containing a painful punishment, destroying everything by the command of its Lord. Thus they became such that nothing could be seen except their dwellings (46:25). Allah destroyed them to the last man and delivered Hud and those who believed with him by a mercy from Him (11:58).

2 — The Spiritual Personality of Hud:

As for Hud (peace be upon him), he was from the people of ‘Ad and the second of the prophets mentioned by Allah who rose to defend the truth and refute idolatry. Allah recounted his story and the trials and harm he endured for the sake of Allah, the Glorified. He praised him as He praised His noble messengers and included him with them in beautiful remembrance; upon him be the peace of Allah.

Ayahs

۞ وَإِلَىٰ ثَمُودَ أَخَاهُمْ صَـٰلِحًا ۚ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥ ۖ هُوَ أَنشَأَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱسْتَعْمَرَكُمْ فِيهَا فَٱسْتَغْفِرُوهُ ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى قَرِيبٌ مُّجِيبٌ (٦١)
And to Thamud [We sent] Salih, their brother. He said, ‘O my people! Worship Allah. You have no other god besides Him. He brought you forth from the earth and made it your habitation. So plead with Him for forgiveness, then turn to Him penitently. My Lord is indeed nearmost [and] responsive.’ (61)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰصَـٰلِحُ قَدْ كُنتَ فِينَا مَرْجُوًّا قَبْلَ هَـٰذَآ ۖ أَتَنْهَىٰنَآ أَن نَّعْبُدَ مَا يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُنَا وَإِنَّنَا لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّمَّا تَدْعُونَآ إِلَيْهِ مُرِيبٍ (٦٢)
They said, ‘O Salih! You were a source of hope to us before this. Do you forbid us to worship what our fathers have been worshiping? Indeed we have grave doubts concerning that to which you invite us.’ (62)
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن كُنتُ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّى وَءَاتَىٰنِى مِنْهُ رَحْمَةً فَمَن يَنصُرُنِى مِنَ ٱللَّهِ إِنْ عَصَيْتُهُۥ ۖ فَمَا تَزِيدُونَنِى غَيْرَ تَخْسِيرٍ (٦٣)
He said, ‘O my people! Tell me, should I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord, and He has granted me His own mercy, who will protect me from Allah should I disobey Him? For then you will increase me in nothing but loss. (63)
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ هَـٰذِهِۦ نَاقَةُ ٱللَّهِ لَكُمْ ءَايَةً فَذَرُوهَا تَأْكُلْ فِىٓ أَرْضِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا تَمَسُّوهَا بِسُوٓءٍ فَيَأْخُذَكُمْ عَذَابٌ قَرِيبٌ (٦٤)
O my people! This she-camel of Allah is a sign for you. Let her graze [freely] in Allah’s land, and do not cause her any harm, for then you shall be seized by a prompt punishment.’ (64)
فَعَقَرُوهَا فَقَالَ تَمَتَّعُوا۟ فِى دَارِكُمْ ثَلَـٰثَةَ أَيَّامٍ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ وَعْدٌ غَيْرُ مَكْذُوبٍ (٦٥)
But they hamstrung her, whereupon he said, ‘Enjoy yourselves in your homes for three days: that is a promise not to be belied!’ (65)
فَلَمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا نَجَّيْنَا صَـٰلِحًا وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنَّا وَمِنْ خِزْىِ يَوْمِئِذٍ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ ٱلْقَوِىُّ ٱلْعَزِيزُ (٦٦)
So when Our edict came, We delivered Salih and the faithful who were with him by a mercy from Us and from the [punishment and] disgrace of that day. Your Lord is indeed the All-strong, the All-mighty. (66)
وَأَخَذَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دِيَـٰرِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ (٦٧)
The Cry seized those who were wrongdoers, and they lay lifeless prostrate in their homes, (67)
كَأَن لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا۟ فِيهَآ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ ثَمُودَا۟ كَفَرُوا۟ رَبَّهُمْ ۗ أَلَا بُعْدًا لِّثَمُودَ (٦٨)
as if they had never lived there. Behold! Thamud indeed defied their Lord. Now, away with Thamud! (68)

(Commentary)

The noble verses recount the story of the Prophet Salih (peace be upon him) and his people, Thamud. He (peace be upon him) is the third of the prophets who rose to establish the call of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and to stand against idolatry (الوثنية). He called Thamud to monotheism and endured harm and tribulation for the sake of Allah until the matter was decided between him and his people by their destruction and the salvation of himself and the believers who were with him.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِلى ثَمُودَ أَخاهُمْ صالِحاً قالَ يا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرُهُ And to Thamud [We sent] Salih, their brother. He said, ‘O my people! Worship Allah. You have no other god besides Him.’ (11:61). The discussion regarding the like of this verse occurred in the story of Hud.

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ أَنْشَأَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ وَاسْتَعْمَرَكُمْ فِيها He brought you forth from the earth and settled you therein... (11:61) until the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said: "Originating (الإنشاء) is the bringing of a thing into existence and its nurturing (تربيته), and it is most often said regarding animals." He said: هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْشَأَكُمْ وَجَعَلَ لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ وَالْأَبْصارَ It is He who created you, and made for you hearing and sight... (67:23). [End quote]. He also said: "Cultivation (العمارة) is the opposite of ruin (الخراب). It is said: 'He cultivated (عمر) his land' when he develops it." He said: وَعِمارَةَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ ...and the maintenance of the Holy Mosque... (9:19). It is said: 'I inhabited it (عمرته), so it became inhabited (عمر), thus it is inhabited (معمور).'" He said: وَعَمَرُوها أَكْثَرَ مِمَّا عَمَرُوها ...and they cultivated it more than they have... (30:9) and وَالْبَيْتِ الْمَعْمُورِ By the Inhabited House (52:4). "And 'I gave him the land to cultivate' (أعمرته الأرض) and 'I settled him to cultivate it' (استعمرته) when the task of cultivation is delegated to him." He said: وَاسْتَعْمَرَكُمْ فِيها ...and settled you therein. (11:61).

[End quote]. Thus, cultivation (العمارة) is the transformation of the earth into a state suitable for benefiting from its expected fruits, such as building a house for dwelling, a mosque for worship (العبادة), a field for tilling, and a garden for harvesting its fruit and for recreation. Settling for cultivation (الاستعمار) is the seeking of cultivation by requiring the human being to make the earth flourishing (عامرة) and fit for benefiting from the desired utilities.

Based on what has passed, the meaning of His saying: هُوَ أَنْشَأَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ وَاسْتَعْمَرَكُمْ فِيها — and the speech implies exclusivity (الحصر) — is that He, the Exalted, is the one who brought into existence upon the earthly materials this reality called "human," then perfected it through gradual nurturing and disposed his primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — to act upon the earth by transforming it into a state through which he benefits in his life, and by which he fulfills the needs and deficiencies he perceives. That is to say: you are not in need, for your existence and survival, of anyone except Him, the Exalted and Sanctified.

Thus, the saying of Salih: "He brought you forth from the earth and settled you therein" stands as a justification (التعليل) and an argument (حجة) used to prove what he presented to them in his call: "O my people! Worship Allah. You have no other god besides Him." For this reason, the sentence was brought as a detached clause (الفصل), as if it were said to him: "Why should we worship Him alone?" and he replied: "Because He is the one who brought you forth from the earth and settled you therein."

This is because they only worshipped idols and took them as partners to Allah because they used to say—according to their claim—that Allah, the Glorified, is too great for any understanding to encompass Him, and too exalted and distant for any worship to reach Him or any request to rise to Him. Since man inevitably needs such things, it is necessary for us to worship some of His noble creatures to whom the affairs of this earthly world and the management of its current system have been delegated. We seek proximity by supplicating to them so that they may be pleased with us and send down blessings upon us, and not be wrathful toward us, thereby ensuring safety from evils. These gods and lords are, in reality, our intercessors (شفعاؤنا) before Allah, for He is the God of gods and Lord of lords, and to Him all matters return.

Thus, the religion of idolatry is built upon the severance of the relationship (النسبة) between Allah, the Glorified, and the human being, and its establishment between him and those noble intermediaries to whom they turn, while assuming these intermediaries possess independence (استقلال) in their influence and their intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — with Allah.

However, since Allah, the Exalted, is the one who brought the human being forth from the earth and settled him therein, He, the Exalted, possesses a relationship with the human being and is near to him. None of these causes (الأسباب) which He has organized and set in motion in this world possess any independence such that good might be hoped for from them through pleasing them, or evil might be feared through angering them.

Therefore, Allah, the Glorified, is the one who must be worshipped, so that His pleasure is sought and His wrath is avoided, by virtue of the fact that He is the Creator of man and of everything, the Disposer (المدبر) of his affairs and the affairs of everything. Thus, His saying: "He brought you forth from the earth and settled you therein" is driven to justify the preceding statement and to argue for it by way of affirming the relationship between Him, the Exalted, and the human being, and denying independence to the secondary causes.

For this reason, He followed it with His saying: فَاسْتَغْفِرُوهُ ثُمَّ تُوبُوا إِلَيْهِ ...So plead with Him for forgiveness, then turn to Him penitently (11:61) as a derivation (التفريع). That is: if Allah, the Exalted, is the one whom you must worship and you must abandon others because He is your Creator and the Disposer of the affairs of your life, then ask Him to forgive your sin of worshipping others, and return to Him through faith in Him and worship of Him. Indeed, He is Near and Responsive.

He justified His saying: "So plead with Him for forgiveness..." with His saying: إِنَّ رَبِّي قَرِيبٌ مُجِيبٌ ...Indeed my Lord is near and responsive (11:61). This is because he concluded from his aforementioned argument that He, the Exalted, undertakes the creation of man, his nurturing, and the management of his life's affairs, and that none of the causes acting in the cosmos possess independence; rather, it is Allah, the Exalted, who drives this here and diverts that from there. Thus, He, the Exalted, is the one who stands between man and his needs and all the causes acting therein; He is near to him, not as they claim—that no understanding perceives Him and no worship or sacrifice reaches Him. If He is near, then He is responsive; and if He is near and responsive, and He is Allah besides whom there is no other god, then it is mandatory to plead with Him for forgiveness and then turn to Him penitently.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا يا صالِحُ قَدْ كُنْتَ فِينا مَرْجُوًّا قَبْلَ هذا أَتَنْهانا أَنْ نَعْبُدَ ما يَعْبُدُ آباؤُنا They said, ‘O Salih! You were a source of hope among us before this. Do you forbid us to worship what our fathers used to worship?...’ (11:62). Hope (الرجاء) only relates to a person not in terms of his essence, but in terms of his actions and effects, and nothing is hoped for from them except good and benefit. His being "a source of hope" means that he appeared as a person of maturity (رشد) and perfection in his person and his household, such that good was expected from him and benefit was anticipated. Their saying: "You were among us" is evidence that he was a source of hope for their general public and their masses.

So their saying: "O Salih! You were a source of hope among us before this" means that Thamud used to hope that you would be one of its righteous members, benefiting their society with your services and leading the nation upon the path of progress and transcendence, because of the signs of maturity and perfection they observed in you. However, they have despaired of you and of the soundness of your judgment today because of the novel speech you have produced and the call you have established.

Their saying: "Do you forbid us to worship what our fathers used to worship?" is a rhetorical question (استفهام إنكاري) intended for censure and blame. The question serves as a justification for what preceded it; its gist is that the reason for their despair of you today is that you forbid them from practicing a tradition (سنة) among the traditions of their creed and you erase the most prominent manifestation of their national identity. For the taking of idols is among the sacred traditions of this society, and the continued practice of sacred traditions by a society is evidence that they possess a deep-rooted, firm origin and a national unity that has stability in judgment and will.

The evidence for what we have mentioned is His saying: "to worship what our fathers worship" (أَنْ نَعْبُدَ ما يَعْبُدُ آباؤُنا), which indicates the meaning of continuous worship by the connection of the children's worship to the fathers' worship. He did not say: "to worship what our fathers used to worship" (ما كان يعبد آباؤنا). The difference between the two expressions in terms of meaning is clear.

From here, it becomes apparent that the interpretation of some commentators, such as the author of al-Manar and others, of His saying: "what our fathers worship" as "what our fathers used to worship" is an error.

His saying: وَإِنَّنا لَفِي شَكٍّ مِمَّا تَدْعُونا إِلَيْهِ مُرِيبٍ ...Indeed we are in disquieting doubt about what you call us to (11:62) is a second argument of theirs in rejecting the call of Salih (peace be upon him). Their first argument is what the beginning of the verse contains, the gist of which is: "That which you call us to—the rejection of idol worship—is a reprehensible innovation (بدعة) that does away with the sacred tradition of Thamud, destroys the foundation of their creed, and kills their memory; therefore, we must reject it." The second is: "You have not brought a clear proof (حجة بينة) for what you call to that would produce certainty and remove doubt from us; thus, we are in a disquieting doubt regarding what you call us to, and it is not for us to accept what you invite us to while we are in doubt about it."

Disquieting (الإرابة) implies suspicion and ill-thinking. It is said: "Such a thing caused me doubt (رابني)" when it necessitates doubt, and "Such a thing made me suspicious (أرابني)" when it leads you to suspect it and have an ill-opinion of it.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ يا قَوْمِ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي وَآتانِي مِنْهُ رَحْمَةً He said, ‘O my people! Tell me, should I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord, and should He have conferred a mercy upon me from Himself...’ (11:63) until the end of the verse. What is meant by the manifest proof (البينة) is the miraculous sign (الآية المعجزة), and by the mercy (الرحمة) is prophethood (النبوة). The discussion regarding the like of this verse occurred in the story of Noah (peace be upon him) in this Surah.

His saying: فَمَنْ يَنْصُرُنِي مِنَ اللهِ إِنْ عَصَيْتُهُ ...who will help me against Allah should I disobey Him? (11:63) is the apodosis (جواب الشرط). The overall meaning is: "Tell me, if I am supported by a miraculous sign that informs of the truth of my call, and Allah has given me the message and commanded me to convey His message, then who will save me from Allah and defend me if I obey you in what you ask and agree with you in what you want from me—which is to abandon the call?"

Thus, in the speech is an answer to both of their arguments and an excuse for what they blamed him for regarding the "innovated" call.

His saying: فَمَا تَزِيدُونَنِي غَيْرَ تَخْسِيرٍ ...You do not add [anything] to me except loss (11:63) is a derivation from his previous statement mentioned in the context of refuting the two arguments and excusing himself for opposing them and establishing the call against their national tradition. The meaning is: "You do not increase me, in your eagerness for me to abandon the call and return to you and join you, except in making me a loser; for opposing the truth is nothing but loss."

It has been said: the meaning is "You do not increase me, in your saying: 'Do you forbid us to worship what our fathers worship?', except in my attributing loss to you." It has also been said: the meaning is "You do not increase me except in insight regarding your loss." The first view is more appropriate.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيا قَوْمِ هذِهِ ناقَةُ اللهِ لَكُمْ آيَةً فَذَرُوها تَأْكُلْ فِي أَرْضِ اللهِ وَلا تَمَسُّوها بِسُوءٍ فَيَأْخُذَكُمْ عَذابٌ قَرِيبٌ ‘O my people! This is the she-camel of Allah, a sign for you. Let her graze in Allah’s earth, and do not cause her any harm, lest a speedy punishment should seize you.’ (11:64). The annexation of the she-camel to Allah is an annexation of honor (إضافة تشريف), like "the House of Allah" (بيت الله) and "the Book of Allah" (كتاب الله). The she-camel was a miraculous sign for him (peace be upon him) supporting his prophethood. He had brought it forth, upon their request, from the rock of the mountain by the permission of Allah. He told them that it should eat in the earth of Allah, set free, and he warned them not to touch it with evil—meaning to afflict it with a blow, a wound, or killing. He informed them that if they did so, a near, expedited punishment would seize them. This is the meaning of the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: فَعَقَرُوها فَقالَ تَمَتَّعُوا فِي دارِكُمْ ثَلاثَةَ أَيَّامٍ ذلِكَ وَعْدٌ غَيْرُ مَكْذُوبٍ But they hamstrung her, whereupon he said, ‘Enjoy yourselves in your homes for three days: that is a promise not to be belied.’ (11:65). Hamstringing the she-camel (عقر الناقة) is its slaughtering. The abode (الدار) is the place a person builds to reside in and take shelter with his family. What is meant by it in the verse is the city; it is called an "abode" because it gathers its people just as a house gathers its inhabitants. It has been said that the "abode" refers to the world, but that is far-fetched.

What is meant by their "enjoying themselves" in their city is living and delighting in life, because the life of this world is a temporary enjoyment (متاع) that is enjoyed, or taking pleasure in the various blessings they had prepared therein—such as pleasing sights, furnishings, food, and drink—and indulging in the desires of their souls.

His saying: "That is a promise not to be belied" refers to his saying: "Enjoy yourselves...", and "a promise not to be belied" is an explanation of it.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا جاءَ أَمْرُنا نَجَّيْنا صالِحاً So when Our edict came, We delivered Salih... (11:66) until the end of the verse. As for His saying: فَلَمَّا جاءَ أَمْرُنا نَجَّيْنا صالِحاً وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِنَّا So when Our edict came, We delivered Salih and those who had faith with him by a mercy from Us... (11:66), the discussion regarding its like occurred in the story of Hud.

As for His saying: وَمِنْ خِزْيِ يَوْمِئِذٍ ...and from the disgrace of that day (11:66), it is coordinated (معطوف) to an omitted element. The estimation is: "We delivered them from the punishment and from the disgrace of that day." Disgrace (الخزي) is the flaw whose scandal becomes apparent and which one is ashamed to show. Or the estimation is: "We delivered them from the people and from the disgrace of that day," similar to His saying: وَنَجِّنِي مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ ...and deliver me from the wrongdoing people (66:11).

His saying: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ الْقَوِيُّ الْعَزِيزُ ...Indeed your Lord is the All-strong, the All-mighty (11:66) is in the position of justifying the content of the beginning of the verse. In it is a transition (التفات) from the first-person plural ("Our edict", "We delivered") to the third-person ("your Lord"). Its like occurred at the end of the story of Hud in His saying: أَلا إِنَّ عاداً كَفَرُوا رَبَّهُمْ ...Behold! ‘Ad disbelieved in their Lord... (11:60). The reason for it is the mention of the attribute of Lordship (الربوبية) to indicate their departure from the garb of servitude (العبودية), their disbelief in Lordship, and their ingratitude for the blessings of their Lord.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَخَذَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا الصَّيْحَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا فِي دِيارِهِمْ جاثِمِينَ The Cry seized those who were wrongdoers, and they lay lifeless in their homes (11:67). It is said: "He lay lifeless (جثم جثوما)" when he falls upon his face. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: كَأَنْ لَمْ يَغْنَوْا فِيها ...as if they had never lived there (11:68). "He lived in the place" (غني بالمكان) means he resided in it. The pronoun refers to the homes (الديار).

His saying, the Exalted: أَلا إِنَّ ثَمُودَ كَفَرُوا رَبَّهُمْ أَلا بُعْداً لِثَمُودَ Behold! Thamud disbelieved in their Lord. Behold! Begone with Thamud! (11:68). The two sentences are a summary of what was previously detailed in the story. The first sentence is a summary of what the affair of Thamud and the call of Salih (peace be upon him) ended in, and the second is a summary of how Allah recompensed them. The like of this verse occurred at the end of the story of Hud.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, narrated with a chain of transmission from Abu Basir, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: I said to him: كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ بِالنُّذُرِ ـ فَقالُوا أَبَشَراً مِنَّا واحِداً نَتَّبِعُهُ ـ إِنَّا إِذاً لَفِي ضَلالٍ وَسُعُرٍ Thamud denied the warnings. They said, ‘Shall we follow a lone human from among us? Then we would indeed be in error and madness.’ (54:23-24). He said: "This was when they denied Salih. And Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, never destroyed a people until He first sent messengers to them so that they might establish the proof against them."

"So Allah sent Salih to them, but they did not respond to him and were insolent toward him. They said: 'We will never believe in you until you bring out for us from this rock a she-camel ten months pregnant'—and they used to venerate and worship that rock, and sacrifice near it at the beginning of every year, gathering around it. They said: 'If you are as you claim, a prophet and messenger, then call upon your God to bring out for us from this solid rock a she-camel ten months pregnant.' So Allah brought it out as they had requested of Him."

"Then Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, revealed to him: 'O Salih, tell them: Indeed, Allah has appointed for this she-camel a day for drinking and for you a day for drinking.' So when it was the she-camel's day, she would drink the water that day. They would then milk her, and no one, young or old, remained but that they drank of her milk on their day. When it was night and they reached the morning, they would go to their water and drink from it that day, and the she-camel would not drink that day. They remained in that state for as long as Allah willed."

"Then they became insolent toward Allah and went to one another, saying: 'Hamstring this she-camel and be rid of her; we are not satisfied that there should be a day for us to drink and a day for her to drink.' Then they said: 'Who will undertake its killing, and we shall give him whatever reward he desires?' There came to them a man—red-complexioned, fair-haired, blue-eyed, a child of fornication whose father was unknown, called Qudar—a wretched one among the wretched, a source of ill-omen for them. So they set a reward for him."

"When the she-camel headed toward the water she used to frequent, he left her until she had drunk and was returning. He sat in wait for her on her path and struck her with a sword, but it did nothing. Then he struck her another blow and killed her. She fell to the ground on her side, and her foal fled until it ascended the mountain and cried out three times to the heaven. The people of Salih approached, and not one of them remained but that he shared in the blow, and they divided its meat among themselves. No one, young or old, remained but that they ate of it."

"When Salih saw that, he approached them and said: 'O my people, what drove you to what you have done? Have you disobeyed the command of your Lord?' Then Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, revealed to Salih (peace be upon him): 'Indeed, your people have transgressed and rebelled and killed a she-camel that Allah sent to them as a proof against them, and there was no harm for them in it; rather, they had the greatest benefit.
