So tell them: I am sending My punishment upon them in three days. If they repent and return, I shall accept their repentance and turn [the punishment] away from them. But if they do not repent and do not return, I shall send My punishment upon them on the third day.'"

"So Salih came to them and said: 'O my people, I am the messenger of your Lord to you, and He says to you: If you repent, return, and plead for forgiveness, I shall forgive you and turn toward you.' When he said that to them, they were more insolent and wicked than they had ever been. They said: 'O Salih, bring us what you threaten us with, if you are of the truthful.'"

"He said: 'O my people, you will wake up tomorrow with your faces yellowed, on the second day your faces will be reddened, and on the third day your faces will be blackened.' When the first day came, they woke up with yellowed faces. They went to one another and said: 'What Salih said has come upon you.' But the insolent among them said: 'We will not listen to the word of Salih nor accept his saying, even if it be grave.' When the second day came, their faces were reddened. They went to one another and said: 'O my people, what Salih said to you has come upon you.' The insolent among them said: 'Even if we were all to perish, we would not listen to the word of Salih, nor would we abandon our gods that our fathers used to worship.' They did not repent and did not return. When the third day came, they woke up with blackened faces. They went to one another and said: 'O my people, what Salih said to you has come to you.' The insolent among them said: 'What Salih said to us has come to us.'"

"When it was midnight, Gabriel came to them and cried out to them a single Cry (صرخة) that pierced their ears, split their hearts, and ruptured their livers. During those three days, they had already embalmed themselves and shrouded themselves, knowing that the punishment was descending upon them. They all died in the blink of an eye: their young and their old. Not a bleating animal, nor a herder, nor anything remained but that Allah destroyed it. They lay in their homes and their beds, dead. Then Allah sent upon them, along with the Cry, fire from the heaven, and it burned them all. This was their story."

I say: The inclusion in the hadith of matters that break the norm (خارقة للعادة), such as all the people drinking from the she-camel's milk and the changing of their facial colors day by day, is of no harm, since the origin of its existence was through a miracle (إعجاز). The noble Quran has explicitly stated this, and that she had a day for drinking and all the people of the city had a known day for drinking.

As for the Cry being from Gabriel, it does not contradict its being a heavenly thunderbolt (صاعقة سماوية) descending upon them, killing them with its sound and burning them with its fire. There is no barrier to attributing a cosmic event—whether it breaks the norm or follows it—to a spiritual angel if he is in the course of its issuance, just as other cosmic events like death, life, provision, and others are attributed to the acting angels.

His saying (peace be upon him): "they had already embalmed themselves and shrouded themselves" is as if it were a metaphor for their preparation for death.

In some narrations describing the she-camel, it is mentioned that the distance between its two sides was a mile. This is among the things that weaken the narration—not because of the impossibility of its occurrence, for that could be defended on the grounds that its existence was through a miracle, but because considering the proportion between its limbs would then necessitate the height of its hump reaching nearly three miles. It is inconceivable that any human being could kill it with his sword, and that certainly did not occur through a miracle from the one who hamstrung the she-camel. Nevertheless, His saying, the Exalted: لَها شِرْبٌ وَلَكُمْ شِرْبُ يَوْمٍ مَعْلُومٍ ‘She shall have a [share of] drink, and you shall have a [share of] drink on a known day’ (26:155) is not devoid of an indication or a suggestion that its body was very large.

(A Discussion on the Story of Salih in Several Sections)

1 - Thamud, the People of Salih (peace be upon him): Thamud were a people from the original Arabs (العرب العاربة) who inhabited Wadi al-Qura between Medina and Sham. They are among the prehistoric peoples; history records only a very small portion of their news. The ages have effaced their traces, so there is no reliance on the details mentioned in their stories.

What the Book of Allah recounts of their news is that they were an Arab nation—as indicated by the name of their prophet, who was from them (11:61). They arose after the people of ‘Ad and possessed a civilization and culture, cultivating the earth, taking palaces in its plains, and carving houses out of the mountains, feeling secure (7:74). Among their occupations was agriculture, through the flowing of springs and the establishment of gardens, palm trees, and tilling (26:148).

Thamud lived according to the custom of tribes and clans; their masters and elders ruled over them. In the city where Salih was sent, there were nine groups (رهط) who spread corruption in the land and did not reform (27:48). They transgressed in the land, worshipped idols, and exceeded in insolence and injustice.

2 - The Mission of Salih (peace be upon him): When Thamud forgot their Lord and were excessive in their affairs, Allah sent to them the Prophet Salih (peace be upon him). He was from a house of honor and pride, known for intellect and competence (11:62, 27:49). He called them to the monotheism of Allah, the Glorified, to abandon the worship of idols, and to conduct their society with justice and excellence. He urged them not to be haughty in the land, nor to be excessive, nor to transgress, and he warned them of the punishment (11, 26, 91, and others).

He (peace be upon him) undertook the call to the religion of Allah with wisdom and goodly exhortation, and he was patient with harm for the sake of Allah. Only a small group of their weak ones believed in him (7:75). As for the arrogant transgressors and the masses who followed them, they persisted in their disbelief, belittled those who believed in him, and accused him of folly and being bewitched (7:66, 26:153, 27:47).

They demanded from him a manifest proof for his statement and asked him for a miraculous sign indicating his truthfulness in the claim of messengership. They proposed that he bring out for them a she-camel from the rock of the mountain. So he brought them a she-camel according to what they had described. He said to them: "Indeed, Allah commands you to drink from your spring of water one day and to refrain from it one day so that the she-camel may drink; she has a day for drinking and you have a known day for drinking. And leave her to eat in the earth of Allah as she wills, and do not touch her with evil, lest a near punishment seize you" (7:72, 11:64, 26:156).

The matter remained so for a time. Then they transgressed and plotted, and they sent the most wretched of them to kill the she-camel, so he hamstrung her. They said to Salih: "Bring us what you threaten us with, if you are of the truthful." Salih (peace be upon him) said: "Enjoy yourselves in your homes for three days: that is a promise not to be belied" (11:65).

Then the clans and groups of the city plotted against Salih and swore among themselves: "We will surely attack him and his family by night, then we will say to his heir, 'We did not witness the destruction of his family, and indeed we are truthful.'" They plotted a plot, and Allah plotted a plot while they were unaware (27:50). "So the thunderbolt seized them while they were looking on" (51:44), as did the earthquake and the Cry, and they lay lifeless in their abode. He turned away from them and said: "O my people! I have certainly delivered to you the message of my Lord and advised you, but you do not like advisers" (7:79, 11:67). Allah delivered those who believed and were God-conscious (41:18). After them, the divine caller cried out: "Behold! Thamud disbelieved in their Lord. Behold! Begone with Thamud!"

3 - The Personality of Salih (peace be upon him): There is no mention of this righteous prophet in the current Torah. He (peace be upon him) was from the people of Thamud, the third of the prophets mentioned in the Quran as standing for the command of Allah and rising for monotheism against idolatry. Allah mentions him after Noah and Hud, and He praises and commends him with that which He praises His prophets and messengers. He chose him and preferred him, like the rest of them, over all the worlds (peace be upon him).

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ جَآءَتْ رُسُلُنَآ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ بِٱلْبُشْرَىٰ قَالُوا۟ سَلَـٰمًا ۖ قَالَ سَلَـٰمٌ ۖ فَمَا لَبِثَ أَن جَآءَ بِعِجْلٍ حَنِيذٍ (٦٩)
Certainly Our messengers came to Abraham with the good news, and said, ‘Peace!’ ‘Peace!’ He replied. Presently he brought [for them] a roasted calf. (69)
فَلَمَّا رَءَآ أَيْدِيَهُمْ لَا تَصِلُ إِلَيْهِ نَكِرَهُمْ وَأَوْجَسَ مِنْهُمْ خِيفَةً ۚ قَالُوا۟ لَا تَخَفْ إِنَّآ أُرْسِلْنَآ إِلَىٰ قَوْمِ لُوطٍ (٧٠)
But when he saw their hands not reaching for it, he took them amiss and felt a fear of them. They said, ‘Do not be afraid. We have been sent to the people of Lot.’ (70)
وَٱمْرَأَتُهُۥ قَآئِمَةٌ فَضَحِكَتْ فَبَشَّرْنَـٰهَا بِإِسْحَـٰقَ وَمِن وَرَآءِ إِسْحَـٰقَ يَعْقُوبَ (٧١)
His wife, standing by, laughed as We gave her the good news of [the birth of] Isaac, and of Jacob, after Isaac. (71)
قَالَتْ يَـٰوَيْلَتَىٰٓ ءَأَلِدُ وَأَنَا۠ عَجُوزٌ وَهَـٰذَا بَعْلِى شَيْخًا ۖ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَشَىْءٌ عَجِيبٌ (٧٢)
She said, ‘Oh, my! Shall I, an old woman, bear [children], and [while] this husband of mine is an old man?! That is indeed an odd thing!’ (72)
قَالُوٓا۟ أَتَعْجَبِينَ مِنْ أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ رَحْمَتُ ٱللَّهِ وَبَرَكَـٰتُهُۥ عَلَيْكُمْ أَهْلَ ٱلْبَيْتِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ حَمِيدٌ مَّجِيدٌ (٧٣)
They said, ‘Are you amazed at Allah’s dispensation? [That is] Allah’s mercy and His blessings upon you, members of the household. Indeed He is all-laudable, all-glorious.’ (73)
فَلَمَّا ذَهَبَ عَنْ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ٱلرَّوْعُ وَجَآءَتْهُ ٱلْبُشْرَىٰ يُجَـٰدِلُنَا فِى قَوْمِ لُوطٍ (٧٤)
So when the awe had left Abraham and the good news had reached him, he pleaded with Us concerning the people of Lot. (74)
إِنَّ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ لَحَلِيمٌ أَوَّٰهٌ مُّنِيبٌ (٧٥)
Abraham was indeed most forbearing, plaintive, [and] penitent. (75)
يَـٰٓإِبْرَٰهِيمُ أَعْرِضْ عَنْ هَـٰذَآ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ قَدْ جَآءَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ ۖ وَإِنَّهُمْ ءَاتِيهِمْ عَذَابٌ غَيْرُ مَرْدُودٍ (٧٦)
‘O Abraham, let this matter alone! Your Lord’s edict has certainly come, and an irrevocable punishment shall overtake them.’ (76)

(Commentary)

The verses contain the story of the glad tidings (البشرى) given to Ibrahim (peace be upon him) regarding the birth of a son. This serves as a prelude to what follows—the story of the angels' journey to the Prophet Lut (peace be upon him) to destroy his people. Indeed, that story is a sequel to this one, and at the end of the story of the glad tidings, the reason for the story of destruction is clarified by His saying: "O Ibrahim, leave this aside! Indeed your Lord’s edict has come, and there is indeed coming to them a punishment that is irreversible" (Surah Hud 11:76).

His saying, the Exalted: "Certainly Our messengers came to Ibrahim with the good news" (Surah Hud 11:69) until the end of the verse. The term al-bushra (البشرى) means glad tidings. The ‘ijl (العجل) is the calf, the offspring of a cow. The term hanidh (حنيذ) is a pattern denoting the passive participle, meaning mahnuah (محنوذ), which is meat roasted on stones heated by fire. This is similar to qadid (القديد), which is meat roasted on stones heated by the sun, according to some linguists. Others mention that it refers to roasted meat from which water and fat drip. It is also said to mean roasted meat in general. His saying, the Exalted, in Surah al-Dhariyat regarding this story: "Then he went inconspicuously to his family and brought a fat calf" (Surah al-Dhariyat 51:26) lends some support to the second meaning.

His saying: "Certainly Our messengers came to Ibrahim with the good news" is conjoined to His previous saying: "Certainly We sent Noah to his people" (Surah Hud 11:25). It is mentioned in al-Majma‘ that the particle lam (اللام) is included to emphasize the report. The meaning of qad (قد) here is that the listener of the stories of the prophets expects one story after another, and qad is used for expectation (التوقع); thus, it serves to signal that the listener is in a state of anticipation. End quote.

The messengers (الرسل) were the angels sent to Ibrahim with glad tidings and to Lut for the destruction of his people. The commentators have differed regarding their number, though it is certain they were more than two, as indicated by the plural form "the messengers" (الرسل). In some narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), it is stated that they were four of the noble angels, and these will be cited, Allah willing, in the "Discussion on Traditions."

The glad tidings (البشرى) brought by the messengers to Ibrahim (peace be upon him) are not explicitly mentioned in the wording of this story; rather, what is mentioned is the glad tidings given to his wife. Ibrahim’s own glad tidings are mentioned elsewhere, such as in Surah al-Hijr and Surah al-Dhariyat. In neither place is it explicitly stated who the child promised to Ibrahim was—whether it was Ishaq or Isma‘il (peace be upon them), or if they gave him tidings of both. However, the apparent context of the story in this Surah is that it refers to the glad tidings of Ishaq, and a comprehensive discussion on this will follow at the end of the story.

His saying: "They said, ‘Peace!’ ‘Peace!’ he said" (Surah Hud 11:69) means they exchanged greetings of peace. They said salaman (سلاماً), meaning "We greet you with peace," and Ibrahim said salam (سلامٌ), meaning "Peace be upon you."

The word salam (السلام) occurring in Ibrahim’s greeting (peace be upon him) is indefinite (نكرة). Its occurrence as an indefinite noun in the context of greeting indicates that it refers to the genus (الجنس) of peace, or that it has an omitted adjective for the sake of magnification (التفخيم) and increased honor. The implied meaning is: "Upon you be a pure and goodly peace," or something similar. For this reason, some commentators noted that the nominative case (raf‘) of salam is more eloquent than the accusative (nasb), as he returned their greeting with a better one, going to great lengths to honor them, thinking they were guests.

His saying: "and it was not long before he brought a roasted calf" (Surah Hud 11:69) means he did not delay in presenting them with a roasted calf dripping with water and fat, but rather hastened to do so.

His saying, the Exalted: "But when he saw their hands not reaching for it, he took them for strangers and conceived a fear of them" (Surah Hud 11:70). Their hands not reaching for it is a metonymy (كناية) for the fact that they did not extend their hands toward the food. This was a sign of enmity and the harboring of ill intent. The terms nakirahum (نكرهم) and ankarahum (أنكرهم) carry the same meaning; he found them strange because he observed from them an unfamiliar behavior.

The term ijas (الإيجاس) refers to a thought occurring in the heart. Al-Raghib said: al-wajs (الوجس) is a faint sound, al-tawajjus (التوجس) is to listen intently, and al-ijas (الإيجاس) is the presence of that within the soul. He said: "and conceived a fear of them" (أوجس منهم خيفة). Regarding al-wajis (الواجس), they said it is a state that occurs in the soul after the hajis (الهاجس), because the hajis is the beginning of a thought, then the wajis becomes the settled thought. End quote. Thus, the sentence is a metonymy, as if the occurrence of khifah (الخيفة)—which is a type of fear—and its striking the soul was like a sound heard by the "hearing of the heart." The meaning is that he felt fear within himself, which is why they reassured him and put his mind at ease by saying: "Do not be afraid! We have been sent to the people of Lot" (Surah Hud 11:70).

The meaning of the verse is that when Ibrahim (peace be upon him) presented the roasted calf to them, he saw that they did not eat from it, as if refusing the food—which is a sign of ill intent. He felt fear of them within himself. They said, to reassure him and comfort his soul: "Do not fear, for we have been sent to the people of Lut." He then realized they were noble angels, who are transcendent (المنزهين) above eating, drinking, and similar requirements of the material body, and that they were sent for a momentous matter.

Attributing the feeling of fear to Ibrahim (peace be upon him) does not contradict his station of prophethood (النبوة), which necessitates divine protection (العصمة) from sin and moral vices. Absolute fear—which is the soul's reaction to witnessing something disliked that prompts it to take precaution and hasten to repel it—is not a vice. Rather, the vice is that reaction which results in the collapse of the soul's resistance, the appearance of helplessness, panic, and the loss of focus in planning to repel the harm; this is called cowardice (الجبن). Similarly, the total lack of reaction when witnessing something disliked—which is called recklessness (التهور)—is not a virtue at all.

This is because Allah, the Glorified, did not create these psychological states that appear in souls—including the reaction and affect (الانفعال) upon witnessing harm and evil, just like longing, inclination, and love upon witnessing the beloved and the good—in vain. Indeed, seeking the good and beneficial and repelling the evil and harmful is something upon which all types of existents, in their multiplicity, are naturally disposed (فطر), and upon this the wheel of existence turns in its general order.

Since this species called "human" only proceeds in the path of its survival through consciousness (الشعور) and will (الإرادة), the act of seeking and repelling in him stems from his consciousness and will. It is not completed except through a psychological affect called "inclination" and "desire" on the side of love, and "fear" and "apprehension" on the side of hatred and dislike.

Furthermore, since these internal psychological states might lead a human toward the extremes of excess (الإفراط) or deficiency (التفريط), it is mandatory for the human to stand in defense as is appropriate, which is the virtue of courage (الشجاعة). Similarly, it is mandatory for him to hasten toward seeking what is appropriate as is appropriate, which is the virtue of temperance (العفة). These two are the limits of moderation (الاعتدال) between excess and deficiency. As for the absence of reaction, such that a person throws himself into certain destruction in the context of defense, this is recklessness (التهور). Or if his soul never yearns for anything desirable in the context of seeking and desire, this is lethargy (الخمول). Likewise, if the reaction reaches such intensity that the person forgets himself and loses his judgment and planning, panicking at every shadow that appears to him in the context of defense, this is cowardice (الجبن). Or if he indulges in everything his soul craves and desires, like a beast over its fodder in the context of desire, this is greed (الشره). All of these are vices.

The protection (العصمة) with which Allah, the Glorified, has favored His prophets establishes in their souls the virtue of courage, not recklessness. Courage is not the opposite of fear—which is the absolute reaction to witnessing something disliked that calls the soul to perform the duty of defense. Rather, it is the opposite of cowardice, which is when the psychological reaction reaches the point where judgment and planning are nullified, leading to helplessness and flight.

The Exalted said: "such as deliver the messages of Allah and fear Him, and fear no one except Allah" (Surah al-Ahzab 33:39). He said, addressing Musa (peace be upon him): "Do not be afraid! Indeed you will have the upper hand" (Surah Ta Ha 20:68). He said, narrating the words of Shu‘ayb to him (peace be upon him): "Do not be afraid! You have been delivered from the wrongdoing people" (Surah al-Qasas 28:25). And He said, addressing His Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny): "And if you fear treachery from a people, break off with them in like manner" (Surah al-Anfal 8:58).

The Friend (الخليل) (peace be upon him) is the noble prophet who stood for the true call when the name of Allah alone was not mentioned. He contended with the idolatry of his people, arguing with his father Azar and his people, and he argued with the tyrannical king Nimrod, who claimed divinity. He broke the idols of the people until they threw him into the fire, and Allah saved him from the fire. None of those terrors made him a coward, nor did any defeater defeat him in his struggle in the way of Allah. A prophet such as this, with his spiritual standing, if he fears something or is apprehensive of someone or is startled by a matter—according to the varying expressions of the verses—he only fears with the fear of prudence (حزم), not the fear of cowardice (جبن). If he fears for himself, his honor, or his wealth, he only fears for the sake of Allah, not out of personal whim.

His saying, the Exalted: "And his wife was standing by, and she laughed. So We gave her the good news of Ishaq, and of Jacob after Ishaq" (Surah Hud 11:71). The word dahikat (ضحكت) is derived from dahk (الضحك) with a fathah on the dad, meaning she menstruated. This is supported by the fact that the glad tidings are branched from it in His saying immediately after: "So We gave her the good news" etc. Her menstruation would be a sign that brings the glad tidings closer to acceptance, and a sign that prepares her soul to submit to their truthfulness in what they promised. The mention of her standing serves to depict the scene—that it did not occur to her mind that she would menstruate while being an old woman; she was simply standing, watching what was transpiring between her husband and the guests who had descended upon him, and conversing with them.

The meaning is that Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was speaking to them and they to him regarding the matter of the food, while his wife was standing there, looking at what was happening between the guests and Ibrahim, with nothing else on her mind. Then it suddenly happened that she menstruated, and the angels gave her the glad tidings of the son.

Most commentators take the word from dihk (الضحك) with a kasrah on the dad, the opposite of crying. They then differed in justifying its cause. The closest view is to say: she was standing there and had been terrified by the guests' refusal to eat, which signaled evil. When it became clear to her that they were noble angels who had descended upon their house and that no evil was directed toward them, she became happy and rejoiced, so she laughed. Then they gave her the glad tidings of Ishaq, and after Ishaq, Ya‘qub.

There are other views mentioned that lack evidence, such as their saying she laughed in wonder at the heedlessness of the people of Lut; or their saying she laughed in wonder at the guests' refusal to eat while she herself was serving them; or their saying she had suggested to Ibrahim to bring Lut to him because the lewdness of his people would bring upon them punishment and destruction, so when she heard the angels say, "We have been sent to the people of Lut," she rejoiced and laughed because her opinion was correct; or their saying she laughed in wonder at the son they promised her while she was a barren old woman. According to this last view, there is a transposition (تقديم وتأخير) in the speech, and the implied order is: "So We gave her the good news of Ishaq, and of Jacob after Ishaq, and she laughed."

His saying: "So We gave her the good news of Ishaq, and of Jacob after Ishaq" (Surah Hud 11:71). Ishaq was her son from Ibrahim, and Ya‘qub was the son of Ishaq (peace be upon them). The meaning is that the angels gave her tidings that she would give birth to Ishaq, and Ishaq would have a son, Ya‘qub—one child after another. This is based on the recitation of Ya‘quba with a fathah, as it is stripped of the preposition. It has also been recited with the nominative Ya‘qubu, which serves as an explanation for the completion of the glad tidings; the first is more likely.

In this expression: "and of Jacob after Ishaq" (وَمِنْ وَراءِ إِسْحاقَ يَعْقُوبَ), there is an allusion to the reason for naming Ya‘qub (peace be upon him) with this name—that he would follow (ya‘qubu) his father Ishaq according to these glad tidings, as it is mentioned that he is "after" (wara’) him. This also serves to correct what is in the Torah regarding the reason for naming Ya‘qub.

It says in the current Torah: "Isaac was forty years old when he took Rebekah... to be his wife... Isaac prayed to the Lord on behalf of his wife, because she was childless. The Lord answered his prayer, and his wife Rebekah became pregnant. The babies jostled each other within her, and she said, 'Why is this happening to me?' So she went to inquire of the Lord. The Lord said to her, 'Two nations are in your womb, and two peoples from within you will be separated; one people will be stronger than the other, and the older will serve the younger.' When the time came for her to give birth, there were indeed twins in her womb. The first to come out was red, and his whole body was like a hairy garment; so they named him Esau. After this, his brother came out, with his hand grasping Esau’s heel; so he was named Jacob." End of the relevant portion. This is one of the subtleties of the Noble Quran.

His saying, the Exalted: "She said, ‘Oh, woe to me! Shall I give birth when I am an old woman, and this husband of mine is an old man? This is indeed a strange thing!’" (Surah Hud 11:72). Al-wayl (الويل) refers to ugliness and every misfortune that necessitates regret, whether from destruction, calamity, tragedy, or scandal. Calling upon it is a metonymy for its presence and arrival. One says "O my woe" (يا ويلي), meaning that which causes my regret has arrived and descended upon me. The form ya waylata (يا ويلتا) adds the ta in the vocative, like ya abata.

The ‘ajuz (العجوز) is an elderly woman, and the ba‘l (البعل) is the husband of the woman. The original meaning refers to one who stands over a matter and is independent of others. A palm tree that is independent of irrigation from rivers and springs, relying on rainwater, is called ba‘l. The term is also used for a companion and a lord (rabb). From this comes "Baalbek," because it contained a temple for one of their idols.

The ‘ajib (العجيب) is an adjective derived from ‘ajab (العجب), which is the state that occurs to a human upon witnessing something whose cause is unknown. Therefore, it occurs frequently in anomalous and rare matters due to the habitual ignorance of their cause. Her saying: "Oh, woe to me! Shall I give birth..." etc., is expressed in the context of wonder and regret. When she heard the glad tidings of the angels, she envisioned the situation of a child being born to a barren old woman and a decrepit old man who had reached an age where procreation is not expected. It is a strange matter, in addition to the shame and disgrace it carries among people, who would laugh at them and mock them, which would be a scandal.

His saying, the Exalted: "They said, ‘Are you amazed at Allah’s dispensation? May Allah’s mercy and His blessings be upon you, O people of the household! Indeed He is All-laudable, All-glorious’" (Surah Hud 11:73). Al-majd (المجد) is nobility, and al-majid (المجيد) is the noble one who is abundant in giving. The meanings of the rest of the vocabulary in the verse have been previously explained.

Their saying: "Are you amazed at Allah’s dispensation?" is a rhetorical question of disapproval (استفهام إنكاري). The angels disapproved of her wonder because wonder only arises from ignorance of the cause and finding the matter strange. A matter attributed to Allah, the Glorified—He who does what He wills and is powerful over all things—leaves no room for wonder.

Furthermore, He, the Exalted, singled out the household of Ibrahim for great care and high gifts that they alone possess among people. Thus, there is no harm if He adds to His past blessings descending upon them another blessing specific to them among people—a child from two elderly spouses from whom a child is not normally born.

For this reason we mentioned, the angels said to her, in disapproval of the wonder they saw from her, first: "Are you amazed at Allah’s dispensation?" They attributed the matter to Allah so that all amazement and strangeness would be cut off, because nothing is difficult for the station of Divinity, and He is the Creator of all things.

Second: "May Allah’s mercy and His blessings be upon you, O people of the household!" They alerted her by this that Allah has sent down His mercy and blessings upon them, the people of the household, and made these inseparable from them. Thus, it is not far-fetched that among these blessings is the birth of a child from parents who are not at the usual, familiar age for such a thing.

His saying: "Indeed He is All-laudable, All-glorious" serves as the justification (تعليل) for His saying: "May Allah’s mercy and His blessings be upon you, O people of the household!" That is, He, the Exalted, is the source of every praiseworthy act and the origin of all nobility and generosity, bestowing His mercy and blessings upon whom He wills of His servants.

His saying, the Exalted: "So when the fear had left Ibrahim and the good news had come to him, he pleaded with Us regarding the people of Lot" (Surah Hud 11:74). Al-raw‘ (الروع) is fear and terror. Al-mujadalah (المجادلة) is originally persistence in inquiry and questioning to prevail in an opinion. The meaning is that when the fear that had overcome Ibrahim left him—upon it becoming clear that those who had descended upon him intended no evil and harbored no ill intent—and the glad tidings came to him that Allah would grant him and his wife Ishaq, and after Ishaq, Ya‘qub, he began to plead with the angels regarding the people of Lut, desiring thereby to avert the punishment from them.

So His saying: "he pleaded with Us regarding the people of Lot" (يُجادِلُنا فِي قَوْمِ لُوطٍ) is to narrate a past state, or it implies a past tense verb before it, the estimation being: "he began to plead with Us," etc., because the original rule for the response to lamma (لما) is that it be a past tense verb.

It appears from the verse that the angels first informed him that they were sent to the people of Lut, then they gave him the glad tidings, then the conversation took place between them specifically regarding the punishment of the people of Lut. Ibrahim (peace be upon him) then began to plead with them to avert the punishment from them, so they informed him that the decree was final and the punishment was descending and irreversible.

What Allah mentioned of his (peace be upon him) pleading with the angels is His saying in another place: "When Our messengers came to Ibrahim with the good news, they said, ‘We are indeed going to destroy the people of this town. Its people are indeed wrongdoers.’ He said, ‘Lot is in it.’ They said, ‘We know better who is in it. We will surely deliver him and his family, except his wife: she shall be one of those who remain behind’" (Surah al-‘Ankabut 29:31-32).

His saying, the Exalted: "Ibrahim was indeed most forbearing, plaintive, [and] penitent" (Surah Hud 11:75). The halim (الحليم) is one who does not hasten with punishment and revenge. The awwah (الأواه) is one who sighs (ta’awwuh) much over the evil that befalls him or that he witnesses. The munib (المنيب) is from inabah (الإنابة), which is returning, and the meaning is returning in every matter to Allah.

The verse is presented to justify His saying in the previous verse: "he pleaded with Us regarding the people of Lot." In this is a profound praise for Ibrahim (peace be upon him) and a clarification that he only pleaded for them because he was forbearing (حليم), not hastening the descent of punishment upon the wrongdoers in the hope that success might reach them so they would reform and become upright. He was deeply affected by the misguidance of people and the descent of destruction upon them, referring back to Allah for their salvation. It was not that he (peace be upon him) disliked the punishment of the wrongdoers or supported them in their wrongdoing—far be it from him to do so.

His saying, the Exalted: "‘O Ibrahim, leave this aside! Indeed your Lord’s edict has come, and there is indeed coming to them a punishment that is irreversible’" (Surah Hud 11:76). This is a narration of the angels' words to Ibrahim (peace be upon him). By this, they cut off his pleading, and he ceased when he realized that persistence in averting the punishment from them would bear no fruit, for the decree was final and the punishment was inevitably occurring. So their saying: "O Ibrahim, leave this aside!" means turn away from this pleading and do not hope for their salvation, for it is a hope for that which cannot be hoped for.

Their saying: "Indeed your Lord’s edict has come" means His command has reached a point where it cannot be repelled by any repeller nor changed by any changer. This is supported by His saying in the following sentence: "and there is indeed coming to them a punishment that is irreversible," for its apparent meaning refers to the future. Had the command been issued, the decree would never fail to reach the decreed. This is also supported by His saying in the upcoming verses of the story of the people of Lut: "So when Our edict came, We made its topmost part its nethermost..." etc. (Surah Hud 11:82).

Their saying: "and there is indeed coming to them a punishment that is irreversible" means it cannot be repelled from them by any repeller, for to Allah belongs the judgment, and there is no reverser of His judgment. The sentence is an explanation of what was commanded, brought to emphasize the previous sentence, as the context is one of emphasis. For this reason, the first sentence began with the pronoun of the case (ضمير الشأن) and qad (قد) which denotes certainty, and both sentences were introduced with inna (إن). They attributed the command to the "Lord of Ibrahim" (peace be upon him) rather than the "command of Allah" to help him cease his pleading.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to Abu Yazid al-Hammar, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, Allah sent four angels for the destruction of the people of Lut: Jibra’il, Mika’il, Israfil, and Karubil. They passed by Ibrahim and greeted him while they were wearing turbans, so he did not recognize them. He saw a handsome appearance and said: 'None shall serve these except me personally'—and he was a man of hospitality. So he roasted a fat calf for them until it was well-cooked and presented it to them. When it was placed before them, he saw their hands not reaching for it, so he took them for strangers and conceived a fear of them. When Jibra’il saw that, he pulled back the turban from his face, and Ibrahim recognized him. He said: 'Is it you?' He replied: 'Yes.' Then his wife passed by him, and he gave her the glad tidings of Ishaq, and after Ishaq, Ya‘qub. She said what Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said, and they answered her with what is in the Book.

Ibrahim said to them: 'Why have you come?' They said: 'For the destruction of the people of Lut.' He said: 'If there are a hundred believers among them, will you destroy them?' Jibra’il said: 'No.' He said: 'And if there are fifty?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'And if there are thirty?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'And if there are twenty?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'And if there are ten?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'And if there are five?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'And if there is one?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'But Lut is among them.' They said: 'We know better who is in it. We will surely deliver him and his family, except his wife: she shall be one of those who remain behind.' Then they departed."

He said: "And al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali said: 'I do not know this speech except that he was trying to spare them,' and that is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: 'he pleaded with Us regarding the people of Lot.'" The hadith has a continuation that will reach you in the story of Lut.

I say: His saying: "I do not know this speech except that he was trying to spare them" can be inferred from His saying, the Exalted: "Ibrahim was indeed most forbearing, plaintive, [and] penitent," for it is more appropriate that his purpose was to spare the people, not just to spare the Prophet of Allah, Lut. Furthermore, His saying: "he pleaded with Us regarding the people of Lot" and His saying: "there is indeed coming to them a punishment that is irreversible" are only consistent with trying to spare the people.

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from ‘Abdullah ibn Sinan, who said: "I heard Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) say: 'He brought a roasted calf (hanidh), roasted and well-cooked.'"

In Ma‘ani al-Akhbar, with a sahih chain from ‘Abd al-Rahman ibn al-Hajjaj, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: "and she laughed. So We gave her the good news of Ishaq": "He said: 'She menstruated.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ishaq ibn Bishr and Ibn ‘Asakir narrated through the path of Juwaybir from al-Dahhak from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "When Ibrahim saw that their hands did not reach for the calf, he took them for strangers and feared them. Ibrahim’s fear was only because in those days, if one of them intended evil toward a man, he would not eat at his place. He would say: 'If I am honored with his food, it is forbidden for me to harm him.' So Ibrahim feared they intended evil toward him, and his joints trembled.

His wife Sarah was standing, serving them—for whenever he wanted to honor a guest, he would have Sarah stand to serve them. Sarah laughed, and she only laughed because she said: 'O Ibrahim, what do you fear? They are three individuals, and you have your family and your servants.' Jibra’il said to her: 'O laughing one, indeed you shall give birth to a boy called Ishaq, and after him a boy called Ya‘qub.' So she came forward with a cry and struck her face, coming forward in bewilderment, saying: 'O woe to me!' and she placed her hand on her face out of shyness. That is His saying: 'So she struck her face' (Surah al-Dhariyat 51:29), and she said: 'Shall I give birth when I am an old woman, and this husband of mine is an old man?'

He said: 'When Ibrahim was given the glad tidings, Allah says: "So when the fear had left Ibrahim and the good news had come to him... he pleaded with Us regarding the people of Lot." His pleading was that he said: "O Jibra’il, where are you heading? And to whom were you sent?" He said: "To the people of Lut, and we have been commanded to punish them."

Ibrahim said: "Lut is among them." They said: "We know better who is in it. We will surely deliver him and his family, except his wife"—and she was, as they claimed, named Waliqah. Ibrahim said: "If there are a hundred believers among them, will you punish them?" Jibra’il said: "No." He said: "If there are ninety believers, will you punish them?" Jibra’il said: "No." He said: "If there are eighty believers, will you punish them?" Jibra’il said: "No"—until he reached the number of one believer, and Jibra’il said: "No." When they did not mention to Ibrahim that there was even one believer in it, he said: "Lut is among them." They said: "We know better who is in it. We will surely deliver him and his family, except his wife."

I say: In the text of the hadith, there is some disorder in that it mentions Ibrahim’s saying "Lut is among them" both first and second, but the meaning is clear.

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from Abu Hamzah al-Thumali, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "When Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, decreed the punishment of the people of Lut and determined it, He wished to compensate Ibrahim for the punishment of the people of Lut with a wise boy (غلام عليم) to console him for his distress over the destruction of the people of Lut.

He said: 'So Allah sent messengers to Ibrahim to give him the glad tidings of Isma‘il.' He said: 'They entered upon him at night, and he was startled by them, fearing they were thieves. When the messengers saw him startled and terrified, they said: "Peace!" He said: "Peace! Indeed we are apprehensive of you." They said: "Do not be apprehensive! Indeed we give you the glad tidings of a wise boy." Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: 'The wise boy is Isma‘il from Hajar.' Ibrahim said to the messengers: 'Do you give me glad tidings despite old age having touched me? Of what then do you give me glad tidings?' They said: 'We give you glad tidings with the truth, so do not be of the despondent.'

Ibrahim said to the messengers: 'What then is your business after the glad tidings?' They said: 'We have been sent to a guilty people, the people of Lut—they were a transgressive people—to warn them of the punishment of the Lord of all the worlds.' Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: 'Ibrahim said: "Lut is among them." They said: "We know better who is in it. We will surely deliver him and his family, except his wife; we have determined that she shall be among those who remain behind."'

When Allah punished them, He sent messengers to Ibrahim to give him the glad tidings of Ishaq and to console him for the destruction of the people of Lut. That is His saying: "Certainly Our messengers came to Ibrahim with the good news... They said, ‘Peace!’ ‘Peace!’ he said"—an unfamiliar people—"and it was not long before he brought a roasted calf"—meaning a pure, roasted, well-cooked one—"But when he saw their hands not reaching for it, he took them for strangers and conceived a fear of them. They said, ‘Do not be afraid! We have been sent to the people of Lot.’ And his wife was standing by." Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: 'They meant Sarah was standing.' "So We gave her the good news of Ishaq, and of Jacob after Ishaq." She laughed, meaning she wondered at their words."

I say: The narration—as you see—makes the story of the glad tidings into two stories: the glad tidings of Isma‘il and the glad tidings of Ishaq, who was born years after Isma‘il. Then it interprets the verses of Surah al-Hijr—where the presentation of the roasted calf to the guests is not mentioned—as referring to the glad tidings of Isma‘il, when the punishment had not yet befallen the people of Lut. It interprets the verses of Surah al-Dhariyat and Surah Hud—which are mixed in the narration—as referring to the glad tidings to Sarah of Ishaq and Ya‘qub, and that this was only after the destruction of the people of Lut, when they returned to Ibrahim and informed him of the occurrence of the punishment and gave him the second glad tidings.

As for the verses of Surah al-Hijr, they could potentially be interpreted as referring to the glad tidings of Isma‘il. Similarly, the verses in Surah al-Dhariyat could potentially narrate what happened after the destruction of the people of Lut, with the glad tidings of Ishaq and Ya‘qub occurring then.

However, the verses of Surah Hud are explicit regarding the glad tidings of Ishaq and Ya‘qub. Yet, what is at their end—His saying: "he pleaded with Us regarding the people of Lot. Ibrahim was indeed most forbearing, plaintive, [and] penitent" until the end of the verses—refuses to be applied to the time after the destruction of the people of Lut. This is so even if what is at their beginning—His saying: "We have been sent to the people of Lot"—does not refuse being applied to the time after the destruction, nor does the sentence "Indeed your Lord’s edict has come," were it not for the surrounding context.

In short, the import of the verses in Surah Hud is that the glad tidings of Ishaq occurred before the destruction of the people of Lut, and it was at that time that Ibrahim’s (peace be upon him) pleading took place. This necessitates that the version of the story in Surah al-Dhariyat also occurred before the destruction of the people, not after. Likewise, the version in Surah al-Hijr—which explicitly states it was before their destruction and includes Ibrahim’s pleading—is devoid of the glad tidings of Ishaq and Ya‘qub, not the glad tidings of Isma‘il.

The conclusion is that the inclusion of the glad tidings of Ishaq and Ibrahim’s (peace be upon him) pleading in the verses of Hud—which clearly occurred before the destruction of the people of Lut—requires that the glad tidings mentioned in all three Surahs (Hud, al-Hijr, and al-Dhariyat) be a single story: the story of the glad tidings of Ishaq before the occurrence of the punishment. This greatly weakens the narration.

There is another point in the narration: it takes "laughing" (الضحك) to mean "wonder" (العجب) and interprets His saying: "and she laughed. So We gave her the good news of Ishaq, and of Jacob after Ishaq" as having a transposition, the implied order being: "So We gave her the good news of Ishaq, and of Jacob after Ishaq, and she laughed." This is contrary to the apparent meaning without any evident subtle point.

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, also from al-Fadl ibn Abi Qurrah, who said: "I heard Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) say: 'Allah revealed to Ibrahim that a son would be born to you. He told Sarah, and she said: "Shall I give birth when I am an old woman?" So Allah revealed to him: "She shall give birth, and her children shall be punished for four hundred years because of her returning the speech to Me."'

He said: 'When the punishment was prolonged for the Children of Israel, they cried out and wept to Allah for forty mornings. So Allah revealed to Musa and Harun to deliver them from Pharaoh, and He reduced their time by one hundred and seventy years.'

He said: 'And Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "You are the same. If you did so, Allah would grant us relief. But since you are not, the matter will reach its ultimate end."'"

I say: The existence of a link between the states and traits of a human and the specifics of his bodily composition is beyond doubt. Each side of the link has a specific demand and influence on the other. Furthermore, the sperm is taken from the bodily matter, naturally carrying the material and spiritual characteristics present in the body. Thus, it is possible for descendants to inherit some of the material and spiritual character traits of their ancestors.

It has been repeatedly mentioned in previous discussions that there is a complete link between a human's spiritual attributes and deeds and external events, both good and evil, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: "If the people of the towns had been faithful and God-conscious, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven and the earth" (Surah al-A‘raf 7:96), and His saying: "Whatever affliction may visit you is because of what your hands have earned" (Surah al-Shura 42:30).

Thus, it is possible for an individual or a society to perform a deed—good or bad—or for a trait to appear—virtue or vice—and then its beautiful effect or evil consequence appears in their descendants. The criterion for this is a type of inheritance, as mentioned. A discussion on this meaning was previously presented under His saying, the Exalted: "And let those fear who, if they were to leave behind them weak offspring, would be concerned for them" (Surah al-Nisa’ 4:9) in the fourth volume of this book.

In the same source, from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), and from ‘Abd al-Rahman from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: "Ibrahim was indeed most forbearing, plaintive, [and] penitent": "He said: 'Supplicating (دعاء).'" I say: A similar narration is found in al-Kafi from Zurarah from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him).

In the same source, from Abu Basir from one of the two [Imams al-Baqir or al-Sadiq] (peace be upon them), who said: "Ibrahim pleaded regarding the people of Lut and said: 'Lut is among them.' They said: 'We know better who is in it.' Ibrahim increased his pleading, so Jibra’il said: 'O Ibrahim, leave this aside! Indeed your Lord’s edict has come, and there is indeed coming to them a punishment that is irreversible.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn al-Anbari narrated in the book al-Waqf wa al-Ibtida’ from Hassan ibn Abjar, who said: "I was with Ibn ‘Abbas when a man from Hudhayl came to him. Ibn ‘Abbas said to him: 'What did so-and-so do?' He said: 'He died and left four children and three wara’.' Ibn ‘Abbas said: 'So We gave her the good news of Ishaq, and of Jacob after Ishaq'—he said: 'The child of the child.'"

(A Discourse on the Story of the Glad Tidings)

The story of the glad tidings—which Allah, the Exalted, called the "discourse of Ibrahim’s guests" (حديث ضيف إبراهيم)—occurs in five of the Quranic Surahs, all of which are Meccan. In the order of the Quran, they are: Surah Hud, al-Hijr, al-‘Ankabut, al-Saffat, and al-Dhariyat.

The first is in Surah Hud (11:69-76): "Certainly Our messengers came to Ibrahim with the good news. They said, ‘Peace!’ ‘Peace!’ he said, and it was not long before he brought a roasted calf. But when he saw their hands not reaching for it, he took them for strangers and conceived a fear of them. They said, ‘Do not be afraid! We have been sent to the people of Lot.’ And his wife was standing by, and she laughed. So We gave her the good news of Ishaq, and of Jacob after Ishaq. She said, ‘Oh, woe to me! Shall I give birth when I am an old woman, and this husband of mine is an old man? This is indeed a strange thing!’ They said, ‘Are you amazed at Allah’s dispensation? May Allah’s mercy and His blessings be upon you, O people of the household! Indeed He is All-laudable, All-glorious.’ So when the fear had left Ibrahim and the good news had come to him, he pleaded with Us regarding the people of Lot. Ibrahim was indeed most forbearing, plaintive, [and] penitent. ‘O Ibrahim, leave this aside! Indeed your Lord’s edict has come, and there is indeed coming to them a punishment that is irreversible.’"

The second is in Surah al-Hijr (15:51-60): "And inform them about Ibrahim’s guests. When they entered upon him, they said, ‘Peace!’ He said, ‘We are indeed apprehensive of you.’ They said, ‘Do not be apprehensive! Indeed we give you the good news of a wise boy.’ He said, ‘Do you give me good news despite old age having touched me? Of what then do you give me good news?’ They said, ‘We give you good news with the truth, so do not be of the despondent.’ He said, ‘And who despairs of his Lord’s mercy except the astray?’ He said, ‘What is your business, O messengers?’ They said, ‘We have been sent to a guilty people, except the family of Lot; we will indeed deliver them all, except his wife: we have determined that she shall be among those who remain behind.’"

The third is in Surah al-‘Ankabut (29:31-32): "When Our messengers came to Ibrahim with the good news, they said, ‘We are indeed going to destroy the people of this town. Its people are indeed wrongdoers.’ He said, ‘Lot is in it.’ They said, ‘We know better who is in it. We will surely deliver him and his family, except his wife: she shall be one of those who remain behind.’"

The fourth is in Surah al-Saffat (37:99-113): "He said, ‘I am going to my Lord; He will guide me. My Lord! Give me [a child] from among the righteous.’ So We gave him the good news of a forbearing boy. When he was old enough to assist him, he said, ‘My son! I see in a dream that I am sacrificing you. See what you think.’ He said, ‘Father! Do whatever you are bidden. You will find me, Allah willing, to be one of the patient.’ So when they had both surrendered [to Allah’s will] and he had laid him down on his forehead, We called out to him, ‘O Ibrahim! You have indeed fulfilled the vision! Thus indeed do We reward the virtuous! This was indeed a manifest trial.’ Then We ransomed him with a great sacrifice, and left for him [a good name] among posterity. ‘Peace be to Ibrahim!’ Thus do We reward the virtuous. He was indeed one of Our faithful servants. And We gave him the good news of Ishaq, a prophet from among the righteous. And We blessed him and Ishaq, and among their descendants are the virtuous and those who are manifest wrongdoers to themselves."

The fifth is in Surah al-Dhariyat (51:24-30): "Did the story of Ibrahim’s honored guests reach you? When they entered upon him, they said, ‘Peace!’ ‘Peace!’ he said, ‘[You are] an unfamiliar people.’ Then he went inconspicuously to his family and brought a fat calf. Then he put it before them, saying, ‘Will you not eat?’ Then he conceived a fear of them. They said, ‘Do not be afraid!’ and they gave him the good news of a wise boy. Then his wife came forward with a cry and struck her face, and said, ‘[I am] a barren old woman!’ They said, ‘So has your Lord said. Indeed He is the All-wise, the All-knowing.’"

The discussion on the story of the glad tidings proceeds from several aspects:

First: Is it a single glad tiding—the one comprising the glad tidings to Ibrahim and Sarah of Ishaq and Ya‘qub, occurring shortly before the destruction of the people of Lut—or are there two stories: one comprising the glad tidings of Isma‘il and the other containing the glad tidings of Ishaq and Ya‘qub?

The second view is sometimes preferred based on the fact that the version in Surah al-Dhariyat is explicit about the presentation of the roasted calf and that Ibrahim feared them when they refused to eat, after which they gave him and his wife—the barren old woman, who is certainly Sarah, the mother of Ishaq—the glad tidings. The end of the verses appears to place this after the destruction of the people of Lut, where the angels say: "We have been sent to a guilty people..." until they say: "So We brought out whoever was in it of the believers. But We did not find in it other than one house of Muslims. And We left in it a sign for those who fear the painful punishment" (Surah al-Dhariyat 51:32-37). Similar to this is what is in Surah Hud, where the angels said to remove Ibrahim’s fear initially: "We have been sent to the people of Lot."

As for the version in Surah al-Hijr, it does not include the account of presenting the roasted calf; rather, its apparent meaning is that Ibrahim and his family feared them upon their entry, so they calmed his terror with the glad tidings, as He, the Exalted, says: "When they entered upon him, they said, ‘Peace!’ He said, ‘We are indeed apprehensive of you.’ They said, ‘Do not be apprehensive! Indeed we give you the good news of a wise boy.’" The end of the verses appears to place this before the destruction of Lut.

Similar to this is the version in Surah al-‘Ankabut, which is even clearer in being before the destruction and includes Ibrahim’s pleading regarding the people of Lut. The narration of al-‘Ayyashi on this meaning was previously mentioned in the "Discussion on Traditions."

However, the truth is that the verses in all four Surahs—Hud, al-Hijr, al-‘Ankabut, and al-Dhariyat—only narrate the story of the glad tidings of Ishaq and Ya‘qub, not Isma‘il.

As for what is at the end of the verses of al-Dhariyat: "They said, ‘We have been sent...’" which appears to be in the past tense and after the completion of the matter, its like occurs in the verses of al-Hijr, despite the consensus that they narrate what happened before the completion.

Furthermore, there is no linguistic or customary barrier to the sent angels saying "We have been sent" while they are still on the way.

As for His saying: "So We brought out whoever was in it of the believers" until the end of the verses, this is from the speech of Him, the Exalted, and is not a continuation of the angels' speech to Ibrahim, as indicated by the context of the stories mentioned in Surah al-Dhariyat.

As for the mention of apprehension (wajal) in the verses of al-Hijr at the beginning of the story, unlike Surahs al-Dhariyat and Hud, the reason is the omission of the presentation of the roasted calf in al-Hijr, unlike the other two. Moreover, the complete link between the parts of a story allows for some parts to be presented before others at times and reversed at other times. For instance, He, the Exalted, mentions Ibrahim’s finding them unfamiliar in the verses of al-Dhariyat at the beginning of the story after their greeting, while in Surah Hud it is in the middle of the story after their refusal to eat. This occurs frequently in the composition of the Quran.

Furthermore, the verses of Hud are explicit regarding the glad tidings of Ishaq and Ya‘qub, and they include Ibrahim’s pleading regarding the people of Lut in a context that leaves no doubt that it was before the destruction of Lut. This necessitates that the glad tidings of Ishaq were before it, not after.

Additionally, it is agreed upon that Isma‘il was older than Ishaq, with years between their births. If these angels had given Ibrahim glad tidings of Isma‘il on their way to the destruction of the people of Lut shortly before the destruction, and gave him glad tidings of Ishaq on their return from the destruction shortly after, the interval between the two glad tidings would be a day or two. This would mean the interval between the glad tidings of Ishaq and his birth would be years. The term "glad tidings" (بشرى) is only applied to the reporting of something good when it is imminent, unless there is a specific consideration; otherwise, reporting absolute good is a "promise" (وعد) or the like.

Second: Is there a glad tiding of Isma‘il? The truth is that the glad tidings mentioned at the beginning of the verses of al-Saffat are indeed the glad tidings of Isma‘il. This is distinct from the glad tidings of Ishaq explicitly mentioned at the end of the verses. The context of the verses following His saying: "So We gave him the good news of a forbearing boy" and then the resumption of the glad tidings of Ishaq in His saying finally: "And We gave him the good news of Ishaq, a prophet from among the righteous" leaves no doubt for a doubter that the "forbearing boy" (الغلام الحليم) promised first is other than Ishaq promised second; and he is none other than Isma‘il.

Al-Tabari mentioned in his Tarikh that the first glad tidings in this Surah also refer to Ishaq, by analogy with the mention of glad tidings in other Surahs, but this is as you see [unfounded]. A discussion on this meaning was previously presented in the stories of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) in the seventh volume of this book.

Third: The study of the story from the perspective of applying what is in the current Torah to what is inferred from the Noble Quran. This will reach you when discussing the story of Lut (peace be upon him) under the following verses.

Fourth: The study of Ibrahim’s pleading with the angels, which includes His saying: "he pleaded with Us regarding the people of Lot" and His saying: "O Ibrahim, leave this aside!"

It has been previously mentioned that the context of the verses, especially His saying: "Ibrahim was indeed most forbearing, plaintive, [and] penitent," only indicates his praise with beauty. Thus, his pleading was only out of his eagerness for the salvation of Allah’s servants, hoping they would be guided to the path of faith.

Ayahs

وَلَمَّا جَآءَتْ رُسُلُنَا لُوطًا سِىٓءَ بِهِمْ وَضَاقَ بِهِمْ ذَرْعًا وَقَالَ هَـٰذَا يَوْمٌ عَصِيبٌ (٧٧)
When Our messengers came to Lot, he was distressed on their account and in a predicament for their sake, and he said, ‘This is a terrible day!’ (77)
وَجَآءَهُۥ قَوْمُهُۥ يُهْرَعُونَ إِلَيْهِ وَمِن قَبْلُ كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ ۚ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ بَنَاتِى هُنَّ أَطْهَرُ لَكُمْ ۖ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَلَا تُخْزُونِ فِى ضَيْفِىٓ ۖ أَلَيْسَ مِنكُمْ رَجُلٌ رَّشِيدٌ (٧٨)
Then his people came running toward him, and they had been committing vices aforetime. He said, ‘O my people, these are my daughters: they are purer for you. Be wary of Allah and do not humiliate me with regard to my guests. Is there not a right-minded man among you?’ (78)
قَالُوا۟ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ مَا لَنَا فِى بَنَاتِكَ مِنْ حَقٍّ وَإِنَّكَ لَتَعْلَمُ مَا نُرِيدُ (٧٩)
They said, ‘You certainly know that we have no interest in your daughters, and indeed you know what we want.’ (79)
قَالَ لَوْ أَنَّ لِى بِكُمْ قُوَّةً أَوْ ءَاوِىٓ إِلَىٰ رُكْنٍ شَدِيدٍ (٨٠)
He said, ‘If only I had the power to deter you, or could take refuge in a mighty support!’ (80)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰلُوطُ إِنَّا رُسُلُ رَبِّكَ لَن يَصِلُوٓا۟ إِلَيْكَ ۖ فَأَسْرِ بِأَهْلِكَ بِقِطْعٍ مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ وَلَا يَلْتَفِتْ مِنكُمْ أَحَدٌ إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَكَ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ مُصِيبُهَا مَآ أَصَابَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ مَوْعِدَهُمُ ٱلصُّبْحُ ۚ أَلَيْسَ ٱلصُّبْحُ بِقَرِيبٍ (٨١)
They said, ‘O Lot, we are messengers of your Lord. They will never get at you. Set out with your family in a watch of the night; and none of you shall turn round, except your wife; indeed she will be struck by what strikes them. Indeed their tryst is the dawn. Is not the dawn [already] near?’ (81)
فَلَمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا جَعَلْنَا عَـٰلِيَهَا سَافِلَهَا وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهَا حِجَارَةً مِّن سِجِّيلٍ مَّنضُودٍ (٨٢)
So when Our edict came, We made its topmost part its nethermost, and We rained on it stones of laminar shale, (82)
مُّسَوَّمَةً عِندَ رَبِّكَ ۖ وَمَا هِىَ مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ بِبَعِيدٍ (٨٣)
sent in from your Lord [for the profligate], never far from the wrongdoers. (83)

(Commentary)

The verses mention the punishment of the people of Lot (Lut). In one respect, they serve as a completion of the preceding verses which narrated the descent of the angels, their entry upon Abraham (peace be upon him), and their giving him the glad tidings of Isaac (Ishaq); for that was merely a preamble to the story of the punishment of the people of Lot.

His saying, the Exalted: «And when Our messengers came to Lot, he was distressed on their account and felt helpless for them, and said, ‘This is a terrible day.’» (11:77). It is said: ساءه (he was distressed) by the matter, meaning it brought evil upon him. سيء (he was distressed) is in the passive voice, meaning evil was brought upon him from their direction and because of them.

The term الذرع (reach/capacity) refers to the measurement of lengths, derived from الذراع (the arm), the well-known limb, because they used to measure with it; it is also applied to the measuring instrument itself. It is said: ضاق بالأمر ذرعا (he was distressed/felt helpless), which is an idiom for the blockage of the path of resourcefulness and the inability to find a way out to save oneself from a calamity—like one who measures something that his measuring tool cannot encompass.

The word العصيب (terrible/distressing) is of the form fa‘īl with the meaning of a passive participle, derived from العصب (binding/tightening). A يوم عصيب (terrible day) is a day that is tightly bound with affliction in a way that accepts no loosening, nor can any of its parts be separated from the others.

The meaning is: when Our messengers came to Lot—and they were the angels who had descended upon Abraham (peace be upon him)—their arrival distressed Lot, and he was unable to find a way to save them from the evil of the people. This was because they entered upon him in the form of handsome, beardless youths, and his people were intensely greedy for committing indecency (الفاحشة) — the act of sodomy and sexual transgression. It was not expected that they would turn away from them or leave them be. Therefore, Lot could not help but say: «This is a terrible day», meaning a severe day whose evils are entwined with one another.

His saying, the Exalted: «His people came running to him, and they had been committing vices before.» (11:78). Al-Raghib said: It is said هرع and أهرع when one is driven with violence and intimidation. It is narrated from Kitab al-Ayn that الإهراع means a hurried driving.

His saying: «and they had been committing vices before» means that before that event, they were perpetrating sins and committing abominations. They were audacious in performing indecency (الفاحشة), accustomed to it, and could not be diverted by any deterrent. Neither shame nor the heinousness of the act hindered them, nor were they restrained by admonition, blame, or censure, because habit eases every difficulty and beautifies every ugly and shameless thing.

The sentence acts as a parenthetical clause between His saying: «His people came running to him» and His saying: «He said, ‘O my people, these are my daughters...’» etc. It is beneficial to the content of both sides. As for what precedes it, it clarifies that what was driving and impelling them toward Lot (peace be upon him) was their habit of committing vices; they had become accustomed to performing indecency and were obsessed with it, which drove them to come to him and intend evil toward his guests.

As for what follows it, it indicates that due to the rootedness of this trait and the establishment of the habit, they were stripped of the ability to listen and accept, or to be restrained by any admonisher or advisor. Therefore, Lot began his speech to them by offering his daughters to them, then said to them: «So be God-conscious and do not disgrace me in my guests...» etc.

His saying, the Exalted: «He said, ‘O my people, these are my daughters; they are purer for you.’» (11:78) to the end of the verse. When he saw them gathered for evil, and that mere words of admonition or harshness of speech would not divert them, he wanted to divert them by substituting the indecency they desired with that which contains no sin—the lawful (الحلال). Thus, he offered his daughters to them and preferred them for them by stating they were "purer" for them.

The use of the superlative form أطهر (purer) here simply means the possession of purity (الطهارة) — the state of being clean and lawful according to divine law — without any mixture of filth; it refers to pure purity. This is a common usage. The Exalted says: «What is with Allah is better than diversion» (62:11), and He says: «and settlement is better» (4:128). It conveys the sense of taking that which is certain.

The qualification of His saying: «these are my daughters» with His saying: «they are purer for you» is a truthful witness that he only offered them for contact through marriage (النكاح), not through fornication (السفاح). Far be the station of a Prophet of Allah from such a thing. This is because fornication contains no purity at all, and the Exalted has said: «Do not approach fornication. It is indeed an indecency and an evil way» (17:32), and He said: «Do not approach indecencies, the outward among them and the inward» (6:151). It has already been mentioned in the commentary of this verse that what it contains are general rulings legislated in all divine laws (الشرائع) revealed to His prophets.

From here, the error of those who say he offered his daughters without the condition of marriage becomes apparent. I do not know what it means to treat one indecency with another indecency? And what would be the meaning of his saying then: «So be God-conscious»? If he only wanted to ward off scandal and shame from himself, he would have sufficed with saying: «and do not disgrace me in my guests.»

It has been suggested that the meaning of his saying: «these are my daughters» refers to the women of the people, because the Prophet is the father of his community (أمته), so their women are his daughters just as their men are his sons. He meant that seeking females—which is the natural (فطري) path—is better for you and purer than seeking males through the path of indecency.

This is an arbitrary interpretation with no linguistic evidence whatsoever. As for them being disbelievers (كفار) and his daughters being Muslims, and the impermissibility of marrying a Muslim woman to a disbeliever, it is not known if that was part of the law (شريعة) of Abraham such that Lot (peace be upon him) would follow it. It is possible that marrying a believing woman to a disbeliever was permissible in his law, just as it was permissible at the beginning of Islam; the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) married his daughter to Abu al-As ibn al-Rabi‘ while he was a disbeliever before the Hijrah, and then that was abrogated.

Furthermore, their reply to him: «You certainly know that we have no right to your daughters» does not align with the daughters being the women of the people rather than his biological daughters. They did not believe in him such that they would acknowledge their women as his daughters, unless it was meant as mockery, for which there is no contextual clue.

It should not be said that his (peace be upon him) use of the plural "daughters" (البنات) when he only had two daughters indicates he meant the women of his community, as the plural would not strictly apply to two.

We say: there is no evidence for this in the speech of the Exalted, nor has it occurred in any reliable report. Yes, it occurs in the current Torah that Lot had only two daughters, but what it contains is not to be relied upon.

His saying: «So be God-conscious and do not disgrace me in my guests» is an explanation of the requirement, and His saying: «and do not disgrace me in my guests» is an explanatory conjunction to His saying: «So be God-conscious». For he (peace be upon him) was only asking them not to interfere with his guests out of godwariness (تقوى) — the protective shield of the soul against Allah's punishment through obedience — toward Allah, not out of personal desire or tribalistic zeal (عصبية جاهلية). There was no difference to him between his guests and others in what he was deterring them from; he had admonished them against this heinous sin and persisted in that for many long years.

He only linked the deterrence to the concept of hospitality and attributed the guests to himself, mentioning the disgrace that would befall him from interfering with them, all in the hope of stirring the qualities of chivalry and honor in them. Therefore, he followed that with a plea for help and support, saying: «Is there not a right-minded man among you?» perhaps he might find among them someone with human rectitude (رشد) who would support him and save him and his guests from the hands of those wrongdoers (الظالمين). But the people were as Allah the Exalted said: «By your life, they were wandering blindly in their intoxication» (15:72). This had no effect on them, and they did not cease their pursuit; rather, they answered with that which made him despair of any further insistence.

His saying, the Exalted: «They said, ‘You certainly know that we have no right to your daughters, and you indeed know what we want.’» (11:79). This is the people's response to what Lot invited them to regarding lawful marriage. They responded by denying they had any right to his daughters, and that he knew this, and he knew what their objective was in this assault and what they desired.

It has been said regarding the meaning of their denial of "right" (الحق) that it means: "we have no need for your daughters," and since a person has no need for something, it is as if he has no right to it; thus, the speech contains a type of metaphor (استعارة).

It has also been said: the meaning is "we have no right to your daughters because we do not marry them," and whoever does not marry a woman has no right to her. Thus, the denial of the right is a denial of its cause, which is marriage.

It has also been said: the "right" refers to share and portion, rather than a legal or customary right; meaning, "we have no desire for them because they are women, and we have no inclination toward them."

What must be noted is that they did not say: "we have no right to your daughters," but rather: «You certainly know that we have no right to your daughters». They did not answer him with the fact itself, but with his knowledge of it. There is a difference between the two statements. It appears they reminded him of what he knew of the established communal custom (السنة القومية) among them, which was the prohibition of interfering with people's women—especially by force and coercion—or the total abandonment of approaching women and the legitimization of interfering with youths to satisfy their lust. Lot used to deter them from that custom of theirs, saying to them: «Do you come to men with lust instead of women?» (7:81); «Do you come to the males of the world, and leave what your Lord has created for you of your spouses?» (26:165-166); «Do you come to men, and cut off the way, and commit indecency in your gatherings?» (29:29). There is no doubt that a communal custom established upon doing something confirms a right in it, and one established upon abandoning it negates the right.

In short, they were drawing his (peace be upon him) attention to what he knew regarding the absence of their right to his daughters—insofar as they are women—according to the communal custom, and what he knew of their intent in assaulting his house. This is perhaps the best of the interpretations, followed by the third one.

His saying, the Exalted: «He said, ‘If only I had the power to [resist] you, or could take refuge in a strong pillar!’» (11:80). It is said: أوى to such-and-such, meaning he joined it, and آواه to him, meaning he brought him into his fold. The الركن (pillar) is that upon which the building relies after the foundation.

It appears that when Lot (peace be upon him) admonished them by commanding godwariness and stirring their chivalry to protect his position and respect his sanctity by not interfering with his guests—which would bring him shame and disgrace—and after he had cut off their excuse by offering his daughters to them in marriage, and then pleaded for support from the right-minded among them in the hope of finding a man who would support him and repel them, yet no one answered his request, nor did any right-minded person emerge to help him; rather, they made him despair by saying: «You certainly know that we have no right to your daughters, and you indeed know what we want»—nothing remained for him but to express his grief and sorrow in the form of a wish. He wished he had a power (قوة) from them by which he could repel their oppressive tyrants—this being the "right-minded man" he was asking for in his plea—or that he had a "strong pillar" (ركن شديد) and a formidable clan (عشيرة منيعة) to join, so he could repel them through them.

So his saying: «If only I had the power to [resist] you» means: "I wish I had strength through you by the joining of a right-minded man from among you to me who would stand for my support, so I could repel you through him." And His saying: «or could take refuge in a strong pillar» means: "or if I could join a strong pillar," meaning a formidable clan that would protect me from you. This is what the apparent context provides.

It has been said: the meaning of his saying: «If only I had the power to [resist] you» is: "I wish I had protection, power, and a group to strengthen myself against you so I could repel you from my guests." The flaw in this is that it changes His saying «بِكُمْ» (through/by you) to "through them against you." This is as you see.

It has also been said: the meaning of «If only I had the power to [resist] you» is: "If only I were strong enough against you by myself." This is even further from the wording of the verse.

It has also been said: the address in the verse is to the guests rather than the people, and the meaning of the verse is that he said to his guests: "I wish I had power because of you to meet them with." The flaw here is that the transition from addressing the people to addressing the guests—with no apparent linguistic evidence—is an ambiguity and complication without cause, and the speech of the Exalted is too sublime for that.

His saying, the Exalted: «They said, ‘O Lot, we are the messengers of your Lord. They shall never reach you.’» (11:81) to the end of the verse. Their "not reaching him" is a metaphor for their inability to achieve what they desired. The meaning is: when the matter reached this point, the angels spoke, addressing Lot: "We are the messengers of your Lord." They revealed to him that they were angels and informed him they were sent from Allah. They comforted his soul, telling him the people would never reach him nor be able to inflict upon him what they intended. Thus occurred what Allah mentioned in another place in His speech: «They certainly solicited his guests from him, so We blotted out their eyes» (54:37). Allah took away the sight of those who persisted in evil and crowded at his door, so they became blind and stumbled about.

His saying: «So travel with your family in a watch of the night, and let none of you look back». الإسراء and السري mean traveling by night, so His saying: «in a watch of the night» (بِقِطْعٍ مِنَ اللَّيْلِ) is a type of clarification for it. The preposition bi is for accompaniment or means "in". A قطع (watch/segment) of something is a portion or part of it. الالتفات (looking back) is the reflexive form of اللفت (turning). Al-Raghib said: It is said لفته from such-and-such when he turned him away from it. The Exalted said: «They said, ‘Have you come to us to turn us away...’» (10:78). From this is "so-and-so looked back" (التفت) when he turned his face away from his direction. A "turning woman" (امرأة لفوت) is one who turns from her husband toward her child from another man.

The statement is an instruction from the angels to Lot (peace be upon him), guiding him to salvation from the punishment descending upon the people on the morning of that very night. It contains a sense of urgency, as indicated by His saying afterward: «Indeed their tryst is the morning.»

The meaning is: "We are sent for the punishment and destruction of the people, so save yourself and your family. Travel, you and your family, during a portion of this night and leave their lands, for they are to be destroyed by the punishment of Allah on the morning of this night of yours. There is not much time between you and the morning, and let none of you look behind."

What some have mentioned—that "looking back" (الالتفات) refers to looking back at wealth or goods in the city to take along, or that it means lagging behind in the night journey—is not to be considered.

His saying: «except your wife; indeed she will be struck by what strikes them». The apparent context is that it is an exception from His saying: «with your family», not from His saying: «none». In His saying: «indeed she will be struck by what strikes them» is the explanation of the reason for her exception. The Exalted said in another place: «except his wife; We have ordained that she shall be among those who remain behind» (15:60).

His saying: «Indeed their tryst is the morning. Is not the morning near?» (11:81). That is, the time of their destruction is the morning, which is the beginning of the day after the break of dawn at sunrise, as the Exalted said in another place: «So the Cry seized them at sunrise» (15:73).

The first sentence is a justification for His saying: «So travel with your family in a watch of the night», and it contains a type of urgency as previously mentioned. This is emphasized by His saying: «Is not the morning near?» It is possible that Lot (peace be upon him) was urging them to punish the people, so they answered him by saying: «Indeed their tryst is the morning.
