Is not the morning near?» meaning it is decreed they will perish in the morning, and it is not a distant appointment. Or the first sentence could be an urging from the angels, and the second a consolation from them to Lot in his urgency.

The verses do not mention the destination of their night journey or the place they were heading to. The Exalted said in another place in His speech: «So travel with your family in a watch of the night, and follow their rears, and let none of you look back, and go on where you are commanded» (15:65). Its apparent meaning is that the angels did not mention the destination to him and left that to what would come to him of guidance through divine revelation (الوحي الإلهي).

His saying, the Exalted: «So when Our command came, We made its uppermost its lowermost, and We rained upon it stones of shale, layered, marked with your Lord.» (11:82-83). The three feminine pronouns refer to the land of the people, the town, or their cities known from the context. السجيل (shale/baked clay), according to al-Majma‘, means al-Sijjin, which is fire. Al-Raghib said: al-Sijjin is stone and clay mixed together; its origin is said to be a Persianized word. He is referring to what was said that its origin is sang-gil. It was also said: it is derived from السجل (the scroll/record) as if what they contained of the act of destruction was written in them. It was also said: derived from أسجلت meaning "I sent forth."

It appears that the root of all these meanings is the Persianized compound giving the meaning of stone and clay. السجل meaning "book/scroll" is also from it, for they used to write on processed stone, then it was expanded so every book was called a sijill even if it was made of paper. الإسجال meaning "sending forth" is derived from that.

النضد (layering) is order and arrangement. التسويم (marking) is making something possess a mark (سيماء) — a distinctive sign or brand.

The meaning is: when Our command for punishment came—which is His command to the angels to punish them, and it is the word "Be" (كُنْ) which He pointed to in His saying: «All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it, ‘Be,’ and it is» (36:82)—We made the uppermost of their land and cities its lowermost by overturning it upon them, and We rained upon it stones of shale, layered, marked with your Lord and in His knowledge, such that they could not miss the target they were cast to hit.

Some mentioned that the overturning occurred on their cities and the raining of shale punished those of them who were absent. It was said: the town was rained upon while Gabriel (Jibril) was lifting it to sink it. It was said: the stones were rained upon them after their town was overturned to intensify the punishment. All these sayings are arbitrary without evidence from the text.

In the saying of the Exalted in another place: «So the Cry seized them at sunrise» (15:73), there was an overturning, a Cry (الصيحة), and a raining of stones. It is possible that this occurred through the eruption of a volcano near their cities, causing an earthquake in their land and a terrestrial explosion with a Cry that necessitated the overturning of their cities, and the volcano rained upon them pieces of stone that it stirred up and cast. Allah knows best.

His saying, the Exalted: «And it is not far from the wrongdoers.» (11:83). It was said the "wrongdoers" (الظالمين) refers to the wrongdoers among the people of Mecca or the polytheists (المشركون) from the people of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and the speech is intended as a threat. The meaning is: these stones are not far from the wrongdoers of Mecca. Or the meaning: these overturned towns are not far from the wrongdoers of your people, for they are on their path between Mecca and Syria, as the Exalted said in another place: «And it is indeed on a lasting road» (15:76), and He said: «And indeed you pass by them in the morning, and at night. Do you not exercise your reason?» (37:137-138).

This is supported by the shift from the first-person context to the third-person in His saying: «marked with your Lord». It is as if the Exalted shifted from saying "marked with Us" to this expression to address the people of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with a threat, or to bring the narrative to their senses so it would have a stronger impact in arguing against them.

It is also possible that the threat is intended for wrongdoers in general, meaning that the stones—i.e., their raining from Allah—are not far from the assembly of wrongdoers, among whom were the wrongdoing people of Lot. The reason for the shift (الالتفات) in His saying: «with your Lord» would also be to address the wrongdoing polytheists of the Prophet's people.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Zakariyya ibn Muhammad [from his father] from ‘Amr from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "The people of Lot were among the best people Allah created. So Iblis sought them with an intense seeking. Part of their virtue and goodness was that when they went out to work, they went out all together, leaving the women behind. Iblis continued to frequent them; whenever they returned, Iblis would have ruined what they had worked on."

"They said to one another: 'Come, let us lie in wait for this one who ruins our goods.' They lay in wait for him, and behold, he was a youth, the most handsome of youths. They said to him: 'Are you the one who ruins our goods time after time?' Their opinion converged on killing him, so they housed him with a man. When night came, he cried out to him. The man said: 'What is the matter with you?' He said: 'My father makes me sleep on his stomach.' The man said: 'Come and sleep on my stomach.'"

He said: "The man continued to rub him until he taught him to do it to himself. First, Iblis taught him, and second, he taught him. Then he slipped away and fled from them. In the morning, the man began to inform others of what he had done with the youth, and it pleased them while they did not know him. So they set their hands to it until the men were satisfied with one another. Then they began to lie in wait for travelers on the road and do it to them, until people avoided their city. Then they left their women and turned toward the youths."

"When he saw that he had perfected his matter among the men, he came to the women. He made himself into a woman and said to them: 'Do your men do it with one another?' They said: 'Yes, we have seen that.' All the while, Lot was admonishing them and advising them, while Iblis was seducing them, until the women were satisfied with women."

"When the proof was completed against them, Allah sent Gabriel, Michael (Mika'il), and Seraphiel (Israfil) in the guise of youths wearing tunics. They passed by Lot while he was plowing. He said: 'Where are you heading? I have never seen anyone more handsome than you.' They said: 'We are messengers of our master to the lord of this town.' He said: 'Has it not reached your master what the people of this town do? By Allah, they take men and do it with them until blood comes out.' They said: 'Our master commanded us to pass through its center.' He said: 'I have a request of you.' They said: 'What is it?' He said: 'That you wait here until the darkness mixes.'"

He said: "So they sat. He sent his daughter and said: 'Bring them bread, bring them water in the gourd, and bring them a cloak to cover themselves from the cold.' When the daughter went, rain and the valley [flood] came. Lot said: 'Now the valley will take the boys away.' He said: 'Rise so we may proceed.' Lot began to walk at the base of the wall, while Gabriel, Michael, and Seraphiel walked in the middle of the road. He said: 'O my sons, walk here.' They said: 'Our master commanded us to pass through its center.' Lot was trying to take advantage of the darkness."

"Iblis passed by and took a child from a woman's lap and threw him into the well. All the people of the city cried out at Lot's door. When they saw the youths in Lot's house, they said: 'O Lot, have you entered into our practice?' He said: 'These are my guests; do not disgrace me in my guests.' They said: 'They are three; take one and give us two.' He said: 'He brought them into the room and said: "If only I had a household to protect me from you."'"

He said: "They crowded at the door and broke Lot's door and threw Lot down. Gabriel said to him: 'We are the messengers of your Lord; they shall never reach you.' He took a handful of pebbles and struck their faces and said: 'May the faces be deformed!' So all the people of the city became blind. Lot said to them: 'O messengers of my Lord, what has my Lord commanded you regarding them?' They said: 'We were commanded to take them at dawn.' He said: 'I have a request of you.' They said: 'What is your request?' He said: 'Take them now, for I fear that my Lord may change His mind regarding them.' They said: 'O Lot, indeed their tryst is the morning. Is not the morning near?'—for one who wants to take. 'So take your daughters and proceed, and leave your wife.'"

Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: "May Allah have mercy on Lot. Had he known who was with him in the room, he would have known he was supported when he said: «If only I had the power to [resist] you, or could take refuge in a strong pillar!» What pillar is stronger than Gabriel being with him in the room? Then Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said to Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): «And it is not far from the wrongdoers»—from the wrongdoers of your community if they do what the people of Lot did." And the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Whoever persists in the sodomy of men shall not die until he invites men to himself."

I say: The narration is not free from some confusion in the wording. It mentions the sent angels were three, while in some narrations—like the one mentioned in the previous section from Abu Yazid al-Hammar from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him)—they were four, with the addition of Karubil. In some narrations from the Sunni paths, they were three: Gabriel, Michael, and Raphael. It is apparent from the narration that it takes Lot's saying: «If only I had the power to [resist] you» etc., as an address to the angels, not the people, as previously indicated in the explanation of the verses.

His (peace be upon him) saying: "May Allah have mercy on Lot, had he known..." is in the sense of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying—as narrated from him: "May Allah have mercy on Lot; he was taking refuge in a strong pillar."

His (peace be upon him) saying: "Then Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said to Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)..." etc., is an indication of what was previously mentioned regarding the possibility of the verse being intended as a threat to the Quraysh.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain to Abu Basir from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: «and We rained upon it stones of shale, layered», he said: "No servant leaves this world while deeming the practice of the people of Lot lawful except that Allah strikes him with a boulder from those stones in which his death lies, but the creation does not see it."

I say: It is narrated in al-Kafi, with its chain to Maymun al-Ban from him (peace be upon him) similarly. It adds: "Whoever spends the night persisting in sodomy (اللواط) shall not die until Allah strikes him with a stone in which his death lies, and no one sees it." In both hadiths, there is an indication that His saying: «And it is not far from the wrongdoers» is not exclusive to the Quraysh, and an indication that the mentioned punishment is spiritual rather than material.

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Ya‘qub ibn Shu‘ayb from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding Lot's saying: «these are my daughters; they are purer for you», he said: "He offered marriage to them."

In al-Tahdhib from al-Rida (peace be upon him) regarding a man approaching a woman from her rear, he said: "A verse from the Book of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has made it permissible: the saying of Lot: «these are my daughters; they are purer for you», for he knew they did not want the vagina."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu al-Shaykh narrated from ‘Ali (may Allah be pleased with him) that he gave a sermon and said: "A man's clan for the man is better than the man for his clan. If he withholds his hand from them, he withholds one hand, but they withhold many hands from him, along with their affection, protection, and support. A man might even get angry for another man whom he only knows by his lineage. I shall recite to you verses from the Book of Allah regarding that." Then he recited this verse: «If only I had the power to [resist] you, or could take refuge in a strong pillar!»

‘Ali (may Allah be pleased with him) said: "The strong pillar is the clan. Lot had no clan. By the One besides Whom there is no god, Allah did not send a prophet after Lot except within the wealth [and strength] of his people."

I say: The end of the narration is reported through both Sunni and Shi‘a paths.

In al-Kafi—in the hadith of Abu Yazid al-Hammar from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) mentioned in the previous tradition-based discussion—he said: "They—meaning the angels—came to Lot while he was farming near the town. They greeted him while they were wearing turbans. When he saw their handsome appearance, wearing white clothes and white turbans, he said to them: 'The house?' They said: 'Yes.' He preceded them and they walked behind him. He regretted offering the house to them and said: 'What have I done? I bring them to my people while I know them?' He said: 'You are coming to the worst of Allah's creation.' Gabriel said: 'We shall not hasten against them until he bears witness against them three times.' Gabriel said: 'This is one.' He walked for a while then turned to them and said: 'You are coming to the worst of Allah's creation.' Gabriel said: 'This is two.' Then he walked, and when he reached the gate of the city, he turned to them and said: 'You are coming to the worst of Allah's creation.' Gabriel said: 'This is the third.' Then he entered and they entered with him until he entered his house."

"When his wife saw them, she saw a handsome appearance, so she went up to the roof. She clapped but they did not hear, so she made smoke. When they saw the smoke, they came to the door running until they reached the door. She went down to them and said: 'We have people with us; I have never seen people with a more handsome appearance.' So they came to the door to enter."

"When Lot saw them, he stood before them and said to them: 'O my people, be God-conscious and do not disgrace me in my guests. Is there not a right-minded man among you?' Then he said: 'These are my daughters; they are purer for you,' inviting them all to the lawful. They said: 'We have no right to your daughters, and you indeed know what we want.' He said to them: 'If only I had the power to [resist] you, or could take refuge in a strong pillar!' Gabriel said: 'If only he knew what power he has!'"

"They overwhelmed him until they entered the door. Gabriel shouted at them and said: 'O Lot, let them enter.' When they entered, Gabriel gestured with his finger toward them and their eyes went away. This is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: «so We blotted out their eyes». Then Gabriel called him and said: 'O Lot, we are the messengers of your Lord; they shall never reach you. So travel with your family in a watch of the night.' Lot said to Gabriel: 'We were sent for their destruction.' He said: 'O Gabriel, hasten!' He said: 'Indeed their tryst is the morning. Is not the morning near?'"

"So he commanded him to carry [his belongings] and those with him except his wife. Then Gabriel uprooted it—meaning the city—with his wing from seven earths. Then he raised it until the inhabitants of the lowest heaven heard the howling of dogs and the crowing of roosters. Then he overturned it and rained upon it and upon those around the city stones of shale."

I say: What the end of the narration contains regarding uprooting it from seven earths then raising it to where the inhabitants of the lowest heaven heard the howling of their dogs and the crowing of their roosters is an extraordinary matter (خارق للعادة). While it is not far-fetched for the power of Allah, the Glorified, it is not established by such narrations, which are solitary reports (الآحاد).

Furthermore, the divine custom (السنة الإلهية) is established upon following wisdom (الحكمة) in miracles and wonders. What wisdom is there in raising them to this extent when it has no effect on their punishment or its intensification?

The saying of some theologians (أهل الكلام): "It is possible that this amazing, extraordinary act was a grace (لطف) from Allah so that the report of it through the Infallibles (المعصومين) would bring the believers closer to obedience and distance them from sin," is a flawed statement. For the creation of great, amazing things and extraordinary events to strengthen the faith of believers and serve as a lesson for those who take heed—even if it is not devoid of grace—is only a grace when its reach to them is through the senses or some other certain scientific path. As for a single or weak narration that lacks authoritative proof (حجية), it is not to be considered. There is no meaning in creating extraordinary things and amazing events for the sake of obtaining a lesson or fear through such a path. There is no reason to intensify the punishment of one people so another people may take heed, except in the custom of the ignorant among human tyrants and oppressors.

The author of al-Manar said in his tafsir: "Among the myths of the commentators narrated from the Israelites (الإسرائيليات) is that Gabriel uprooted it from the depths of the earth with his wing and ascended with it to the clouds of heaven until the inhabitants of heaven heard the sounds of dogs and chickens in it, then he overturned it completely, making its uppermost its lowermost."

"This is a conception built on the belief of its conceiver that the inhabited celestial bodies are such that the inhabitants of the earth and the animals in it can approach them and remain alive. It has been proven by observation and actual testing in these days in which this is being written that airplanes and balloons created in the atmosphere reach where the air pressure lightens and human life becomes impossible. They manufacture types of them in which they produce enough oxygen from the air for inhalation and breathing for life in the upper layers of the atmosphere and ascend in them."

"The Noble Book has pointed to the effect of ascending into the atmosphere of heaven on the constriction of the chest from difficulty in breathing in the saying of the Exalted: «Whomever Allah desires to guide, He expands his breast to Islam, and whomever He desires to lead astray, He makes his breast narrow and straitened as if he were climbing to heaven» (6:125)."

"If it is said: 'This act narrated from Gabriel is among the rational possibilities and its occurrence was from the extraordinary wonders, so it is not correct to make its belief dependent on what is known of the laws of the universe.'"

"I say: Yes, but the first condition for accepting a narration regarding a matter that comes contrary to the laws and norms by which Allah established the system of the world—of prosperity and ruin—is that the narration be from divine revelation (وحي إلهي) transmitted through mass-transmission (التواتر) from the Infallible, or at least with a sound, connected chain of transmission (سند صحيح متصل الإسناد) that has no anomaly (شذوذ) or defect (علة). It was not mentioned in the Book of Allah, nor was there a marfu‘ hadith regarding it to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), nor does the wisdom of Allah appear in it; it was only narrated from some of the Successors (التابعين) rather than the Companions (الصحابة). There is no doubt it is from the Israelites."

"Among what they said regarding it: that the number of its inhabitants was four million, and the entire land of Palestine cannot accommodate this number, so where were these millions living in those four towns?" End quote.

What he mentioned—that the hadith was only narrated from the Successors rather than the Companions—is incorrect, for this meaning is narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas and Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman. In the narration of Ibn ‘Abbas—as in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ishaq ibn Bishr and Ibn ‘Asakir through Juwaybir and Muqatil from al-Dahhak from him: "When it was at the face of the morning, Gabriel headed to the towns of Lot—with what was in them of its men, women, fruits, and birds—he gathered them and folded them, then uprooted them from the depths of the soil. Then he carried them under his wing and raised them to the lowest heaven. The inhabitants of the lowest heaven heard the sounds of dogs, birds, women, and men from under Gabriel's wing. Then he sent them down overturned, then followed them with stones. The stones were for the shepherds, merchants, and whoever was outside their cities."

In the narration of Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman—according to al-Durr al-Manthur from ‘Abd al-Razzaq, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim from him: "Gabriel asked permission for their destruction, and he was permitted. He carried the land they were on and descended with it until the inhabitants of the lowest heaven heard the barking of their dogs. He lit a fire under them then overturned it with them. Lot's wife heard the crash while she was with them, so she turned and the punishment struck her, and the stones followed their travelers."

As for the Successors, this meaning was narrated from Sa‘id ibn Jubayr, Mujahid, Abu Salih, Muhammad ibn Ka‘b al-Qurazi, and from al-Suddi what is even more extreme than that. He said: "When they reached the morning, Gabriel descended and uprooted the land from seven earths, then carried it until he reached the lowest heaven, then Gabriel descended with it to the earth."

As for what he mentioned that "it is a condition for accepting the narration that it be transmitted through mass-transmission from the Infallible or with a sound, connected chain of transmission that has no anomaly or defect," this is a matter of legal theory (أصولية). The view that has settled today on the matter is that if the report is mass-transmitted (متواتر) or surrounded by a definitive context, there is no doubt in its authority (حجية). As for anything else, there is no authority in it except for reports regarding subsidiary legal rulings (الأحكام الشرعية الفرعية) if the report is reliable in its issuance by general probability (الظن النوعي), for they have authority.

This is because legal authority (الحجية الشرعية) is among the rational considerations (الاعتبارات العقلائية), so it follows the existence of a legal effect in the case that accepts legal positing and consideration. Historical issues and matters of belief have no meaning for the positing of authority in them due to the absence of a legal effect; there is no meaning for the Lawgiver's ruling that non-knowledge is knowledge and making people worship through that. External subjects, although a legal effect can be realized in them, their effects are particular (جزئية), and legal positing only reaches universals (الكليات). Let the detailed discussion of the matter be sought from the science of Usul.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ubayy ibn Ka‘b, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'May Allah have mercy on Lot; he was taking refuge in a strong pillar.'"

I say: The requirement of the situation in which Lot was contending with his people and commanding them to be God-conscious and avoid profligacy, and the apparent context of the verses narrating the dispute between him and his people, is that Lot was only wishing for right-minded supporters from among his people or others. So his saying: «or could take refuge in a strong pillar» means he wanted supporters from other than the people, from a clan or brothers and friends in Allah who would support him in repelling [the people] from his guests. This "strong pillar" was with him in his house, and they were Gabriel, Michael, and Seraphiel; therefore, they answered him without delay and said: «O Lot, we are the messengers of your Lord. They shall never reach you.»

He would not, in any of those states, be heedless of his Lord and that all victory is from Him, such that he would forget Him and wish for a supporter other than Him. Far be the station of this noble Prophet from such blameworthy ignorance. Allah the Exalted has said regarding him: «We gave him judgment and knowledge...» until He said: «and We admitted him into Our mercy. Indeed he was one of the righteous» (21:74-75).

So the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: "he was taking refuge in a strong pillar" means that Gabriel and the other angels were with him while he did not know it; it does not mean that Allah the Glorified was with him while he was ignorant of the station of his Lord.

What is in some narrations transmitting the words of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of the indication that he meant by the "strong pillar" Allah the Glorified rather than the angels, only arose from the understanding of some narrators of the hadith, as from Abu Hurayrah who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'May Allah have mercy on Lot; he was taking refuge in a strong pillar'—meaning Allah the Exalted."

And as from him through another path: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'May Allah forgive Lot; he was taking refuge in a strong pillar.'" Perhaps there is a transmission of the meaning (نقل بالمعنى) here, and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "May Allah have mercy on Lot," but the narrator changed it to "May Allah forgive Lot," which suggests that Lot neglected an etiquette of servitude or committed a sin by his ignorance of the station of his Lord and his forgetting what he should not have forgotten.

(A Discussion on the Story of Lot and His People in Chapters)

1 - His story and the story of his people in the Quran: Lot (peace be upon him) was from the Chaldeans in the land of Babylon and among the foremost of those who believed in Abraham (peace be upon him). He believed in him and said: «I am migrating toward my Lord» (29:26). Allah saved him along with Abraham to the Holy Land, the land of Palestine (21:71). He settled in one of its cities (which was the city of Sodom according to histories, the Torah, and some narrations).

The people of the city and the surrounding cities—which Allah called in His speech "the overturned cities" (المؤتفكات) (9:70)—used to worship idols and commit indecency (الفاحشة): sodomy. They were the first people among whom that became widespread (7:80), until they were performing it in their gatherings without disapproval (29:29). The indecency continued to spread among them until it became a communal custom that afflicted their general population; they left women and cut off the way (29:29).

So Allah sent Lot to them (26:162). He invited them to godwariness toward Allah, the abandonment of indecency, and the return to the path of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being. He warned them and frightened them, but it only increased them in rebellion. Their only answer was to say: "Bring us the punishment of Allah if you are truthful." They threatened him with expulsion from their town and said to him: «If you do not desist, you will surely be among the expelled» (26:167), and they said: «Expel the family of Lot from your town; they are indeed a people who keep pure» (27:56).

2 - The end of their affair: Lot (peace be upon him) continued to invite them to the way of Allah, the adherence to the custom of the primordial nature, and the abandonment of indecency, while they persisted in committing foul deeds until rebellion settled within them and the word of punishment became due upon them. Allah sent messengers from the honored angels to destroy them. They first descended upon Abraham (peace be upon him) and informed him of what Allah had commanded them regarding the destruction of the people of Lot. Abraham (peace be upon him) argued with them, perhaps to avert the punishment from them, and reminded them that Lot was among them. They replied to him that they were more knowledgeable of the location of Lot and his family, and that the command of Allah had come, and that a punishment was coming to the people that would not be turned back (29:32, 11:76).

They proceeded to Lot in the form of beardless youths and entered upon him as guests. That was difficult for Lot and he felt helpless for them because of what he knew of his people—that they would interfere with them and would never leave them be. It was not long before the people heard of it and came running to him, rejoicing. They assaulted his house, so he went out to them and went to great lengths in admonishing them and stirring their chivalry and rectitude, even offering his daughters to them, saying: «O my people, these are my daughters; they are purer for you. So be God-conscious and do not disgrace me in my guests.» Then he pleaded for help and said: «Is there not a right-minded man among you?» They replied to him that they had no need for his daughters and that they would never leave his guests be, until Lot despaired and «said, ‘If only I had the power to [resist] you, or could take refuge in a strong pillar!’» (11:80).

The angels said then: "O Lot, we are the messengers of your Lord. Be at ease; the people shall never reach you." They blotted out the eyes of the people, so they became blind, stumbling about, and dispersed (54:37).

Then they commanded Lot (peace be upon him) to travel with his family during that night in a watch of the night, and to follow their rears, and that none of them should look back except his wife, for she would be struck by what struck them. They informed him that they would destroy the people in the morning (11:81, 15:66).

So the Cry seized the people at sunrise, and Allah sent upon them stones of clay, marked with your Lord for the profligate. He overturned their cities upon them, making their uppermost their lowermost, and He brought out whoever was in them of the believers (المؤمنين). He did not find in them other than one house of Muslims (المسلمين), which was the house of Lot, and He left in it a sign (آية) for those who fear the painful punishment (51:37, and others).

In the restriction of faith (الإيمان) and submission (الإسلام) to the house of Lot (peace be upon him), and the inclusion of the punishment for their cities, there is a indication—firstly—that the people were disbelievers, not believers; and secondly—that the indecency was not widespread among the men only. For if that were the case and the women were innocent of it, and Lot was inviting the people to return to the path of the primordial nature and the custom of creation—which is the union of men and women—a number of women would have followed him, gathered around him, and believed in him naturally. Nothing of that was mentioned in the speech of the Glorified.

In this is a confirmation of what was previously mentioned in the narrated reports: that the indecency spread among them, and the men were satisfied with men through sodomy (اللواط), and the women with women through lesbianism (السحق).

3 - The spiritual personality of Lot: He (peace be upon him) was a messenger from Allah to the people of the overturned cities, which was the city of Sodom and the surrounding cities—it is said they were four cities: Sodom, Gomorrah, Zoar, and Zeboiim. He shared in all the spiritual stations with which He described His noble prophets.

Among what He specifically described him with is what is in His saying: «And Lot, We gave him judgment and knowledge, and We delivered him from the town that used to commit vices. Indeed they were an evil and transgressing people. And We admitted him into Our mercy. Indeed he was one of the righteous» (21:74-75).

4 - Lot and his people in the Torah: The Torah mentions (1) that Lot was the nephew of Abram—Abraham—Haran son of Terah. He and Abram were in the house of Terah in Ur of the Chaldeans. Then Terah migrated from Ur intending for the land of the Canaanites, so he settled in the town of Haran with Abram and Lot, and he died there.

Then Abram, by command from the Lord, went out from Haran with Lot, and they had much wealth and servants they had acquired in Haran. He came to the land of Canaan. Abram would travel continuously toward the south, then he came to Egypt, then he ascended from there south toward Bethel and settled there.

(1) Chapters eleven and twelve of the Book of Genesis.

Lot, who was traveling with Abram, also possessed sheep, cattle, and tents. The land could not support them both dwelling together, and a dispute arose between the herders of their livestock. Consequently, they separated to avoid the occurrence of conflict and quarreling. Lot chose the plain of the Jordan and dwelt in the cities of the plain, moving his tents toward Sodom. The people of Sodom were exceedingly wicked and sinners before the Lord. Abram moved his tents and settled by the oaks of Mamre, which are in Hebron.

Subsequently, a war broke out between the kings of Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim, and Zoar on one side, and four of their neighbors on the other. The king of Sodom and the kings with him were defeated. The enemy seized all the property of Sodom and Gomorrah and all their food supplies. Lot was among those captured, and all his wealth was plundered. When the news reached Abram, he set out with the young men in his service—numbering more than three hundred—fought them, and defeated them. He rescued Lot and all his property from captivity and plunder, returning him to the place where he had been residing. (This is a summary of what is in the Torah regarding the beginning of the story of Lot).

The Torah says (1): And the Lord appeared to him—to Abram—by the oaks of Mamre while he was sitting at the entrance of the tent during the heat of the day. He lifted his eyes and looked, and behold, three men were standing before him. When he saw them, he ran from the entrance of the tent to meet them and prostrated to the ground. He said, "O Lord, if I have found favor in your eyes, do not pass by your servant. Let a little water be brought, wash your feet, and rest under this tree. I will bring a morsel of bread so you may refresh your hearts, then

(1) Genesis, Chapter Eighteen.

you may pass on, since you have come to your servant." They said, "Do as you have spoken."

So Abraham hurried into the tent to Sarah and said, "Hurry, take three measures of fine flour, knead it, and make hearth cakes." Then Abraham ran to the herd, took a tender and good calf, and gave it to the servant, who hurried to prepare it. Then he took butter and milk and the calf he had prepared and set them before them. While he stood by them under the tree, they ate.

They said to him, "Where is Sarah your wife?" He said, "There she is, in the tent." He said, "I will surely return to you about the time of life, and Sarah your wife shall have a son." Sarah was listening at the entrance of the tent, which was behind him. Now Abraham and Sarah were old, advanced in years, and the manner of women had ceased for Sarah. So Sarah laughed within herself, saying, "After I have grown old, shall I have pleasure, my lord being old also?" The Lord said to Abraham, "Why did Sarah laugh, saying, 'Shall I indeed bear a child, now that I am old?' Is anything impossible for the Lord? At the appointed time I will return to you, about the time of life, and Sarah shall have a son." Sarah denied it, saying, "I did not laugh," for she was afraid. He said, "No, but you did laugh."

Then the men rose from there and looked toward Sodom, and Abraham walked with them to send them on their way. The Lord said, "Shall I hide from Abraham what I am doing? Abraham shall surely become a great and mighty nation, and all the nations of the earth shall be blessed in him. For I have known him, so that he may command his children and his household after him to keep the way of the Lord by doing righteousness and justice, so that the Lord may bring upon Abraham what He has spoken concerning him."

The Lord said, "Because the outcry against Sodom and Gomorrah is great and their sin is very grave, I will go down and see whether they have done altogether according to the outcry that has come to Me; and if not, I will know." The men turned from there and went toward Sodom, but Abraham remained standing before the Lord.

Abraham drew near and said, "Will You indeed sweep away the righteous with the wicked? Suppose there are fifty righteous within the city; will You then sweep away the place and not forgive it for the sake of the fifty righteous who are in it? Far be it from You to do such a thing, to put to death the righteous with the wicked, so that the righteous fare as the wicked! Far be it from You! Shall not the Judge of all the earth do justice?" The Lord said, "If I find in Sodom fifty righteous in the city, I will forgive the whole place for their sake."

Abraham answered and said, "Behold, I have taken it upon myself to speak to the Lord, I who am but dust and ashes. Suppose

five of the fifty righteous are lacking; will You destroy the whole city for lack of five?" The Lord said, "I will not destroy it if I find forty-five there." He spoke to Him again and said, "Suppose forty are found there." He said, "For the sake of forty I will not do it." Then he said, "Oh let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak. Suppose thirty are found there." He said, "I will not do it if I find thirty there." He said, "Behold, I have taken it upon myself to speak to the Lord. Suppose twenty are found there." He said, "For the sake of twenty I will not destroy it."

He said, "Oh let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak again but this once. Suppose ten are found there." He said, "For the sake of ten I will not destroy it." The Lord went His way when He had finished speaking with Abraham, and Abraham returned to his place.

The two angels (1) came to Sodom in the evening, and Lot was sitting in the gateway of Sodom. When Lot saw them, he rose to meet them and prostrated with his face to the ground. He said, "My lords, please turn aside to your servant’s house and spend the night and wash your feet; then you may rise early and go on your way." They said, "No, we will spend the night in the square." But he urged them strongly, so they turned aside to him and entered his house. He prepared a feast for them and baked unleavened bread, and they ate.

Before they lay down, the men of the city, the men of Sodom, surrounded the house—both young and old, all the people from every quarter. They called to Lot and said to him, "Where are the men who came to you tonight? Bring them out to us, that we may know them." Lot went out to them at the entrance and shut the door behind him. He said, "I beg you, my brothers, do not act wickedly. Behold, I have two daughters who have not known a man; let me bring them out to you, and do to them as you please. Only do nothing to these men, for they have come under the shadow of my roof."

But they said, "Stand back!" Then they said, "This fellow came to sojourn, and he keeps acting as a judge! Now we will deal worse with you than with them." They pressed hard against the man Lot and drew near to break down the door. But the two men reached out their hands and pulled Lot into the house with them and shut the door. And they struck the men who were at the entrance of the house with blindness, both small and great, so that they wore themselves out groping for the door.

(1) Genesis, Chapter Nineteen.

The two men said to Lot, "Have you anyone else here? Sons-in-law, sons, daughters, or anyone you have in the city—bring them out of the place. For we are about to destroy this place, because the outcry against its people has become great before the Lord, and the Lord has sent us to destroy it." So Lot went out and spoke to his sons-in-law, who were to marry his daughters, and said, "Up, get out of this place, for the Lord is about to destroy the city." But he seemed to be jesting in the eyes of his sons-in-law.

As morning dawned, the angels urged Lot, saying, "Up, take your wife and your two daughters who are here, lest you be swept away in the punishment of the city." But he lingered, so the men seized him and his wife and his two daughters by the hand, the Lord being merciful to him, and they brought him out and set him outside the city.

And as they brought them out, one said, "Escape for your life! Do not look back or stop anywhere in the plain. Escape to the hills, lest you be swept away." And Lot said to them, "Oh, no, my lords. Behold, your servant has found favor in your eyes, and you have shown me great kindness in saving my life. But I cannot escape to the hills, lest the disaster overtake me and I die. Behold, this city is near enough to flee to, and it is a little one. Let me escape there—is it not a little one?—and my life will be saved!" He said to him, "Behold, I grant you this favor also, that I will not overthrow the city of which you have spoken. Hurry, escape there, for I can do nothing until you arrive there." Therefore the name of the city was called Zoar.

The sun had risen on the earth when Lot came to Zoar. Then the Lord rained on Sodom and Gomorrah sulfur and fire from the Lord out of heaven. And He overthrew those cities, and all the plain, and all the inhabitants of the cities, and what grew on the ground. But Lot’s wife, behind him, looked back, and she became a pillar of salt.

Abraham rose early in the morning to the place where he had stood before the Lord. And he looked down toward Sodom and Gomorrah and toward all the land of the plain, and he looked, and behold, the smoke of the land went up like the smoke of a furnace. So it was that, when God destroyed the cities of the plain, God remembered Abraham and sent Lot out of the midst of the overthrow when He overthrew the cities in which Lot had lived.

Lot went up out of Zoar and lived in the hills with his two daughters, for he was afraid to live in Zoar. So he lived in a cave with his two daughters. And the firstborn said to the younger, "Our father is old, and there is not a man on earth to come in to us after the manner of all the earth. Come, let us make our father drink wine, and we will lie with him, that we may preserve offspring from our father." So they made their father drink wine that night. And the firstborn went in and lay with her father. He did not know when she lay down or when she arose. The next day, the firstborn said to the younger, "Behold, I lay last night with my father. Let us make him drink wine tonight also. Then you go in and lie with him, that we may preserve offspring from our father." So they made their father drink wine that night also. And the younger arose and lay with him, and he did not know when she lay down or when she arose. Thus both the daughters of Lot became pregnant by their father.

The firstborn bore a son and called his name Moab. He is the father of the Moabites to this day. The younger also bore a son and called his name Ben-ammi. He is the father of the Ammonites to this day. (End of quote).

This is what the Torah narrates regarding Lot and his people. We have quoted it at length so that the ways in which it contradicts the Noble Quran—both in the aspects of the story and in other respects—may become clear.

Among these contradictions is that the angels sent for the glad tidings and the punishment are two in number, whereas the Quran uses the term "messengers" (الرسل)—a plural form, the minimum of which is three.

Another is that Abraham's guests ate from what he prepared and presented to them, while the Quran denies this and narrates that Abraham became fearful when he saw that their hands did not reach for the food.

Furthermore, it specifies two daughters for Lot, while the Quran uses the plural "daughters" (بنات). It also describes how the angels brought Lot out, the manner of the people's punishment, the wife becoming a pillar of salt, and so on.

Additionally, it contains explicit attributions of anthropomorphism (التجسم) to Allah, the Glorified, as well as what it mentioned at the end regarding the story of Lot with his two daughters. The Quran, however, sanctifies the presence of the Truth (الحق), the Glorified, from anthropomorphism and exonerates His prophets and messengers from committing that which is unbecoming of their station of sanctity (قدسهم).

Ayahs

۞ وَإِلَىٰ مَدْيَنَ أَخَاهُمْ شُعَيْبًا ۚ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥ ۖ وَلَا تَنقُصُوا۟ ٱلْمِكْيَالَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ ۚ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰكُم بِخَيْرٍ وَإِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ مُّحِيطٍ (٨٤)
And to Midian [We sent] Shu‘ayb, their brother. He said, ‘O my people! Worship Allah. You have no other god besides Him. Do not diminish the measure or the balance. Indeed I see that you are faring well, but I fear for you the punishment of an all-embracing day.’ (84)
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ أَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْمِكْيَالَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۖ وَلَا تَبْخَسُوا۟ ٱلنَّاسَ أَشْيَآءَهُمْ وَلَا تَعْثَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ (٨٥)
‘O my people! Observe fully the measure and the balance, with justice, and do not cheat the people of their goods, and do not act wickedly on the earth, causing corruption.’ (85)
بَقِيَّتُ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ ۚ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ عَلَيْكُم بِحَفِيظٍ (٨٦)
‘What remains of Allah’s provision is better for you, should you be faithful, and I am not a keeper over you.’ (86)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰشُعَيْبُ أَصَلَوٰتُكَ تَأْمُرُكَ أَن نَّتْرُكَ مَا يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُنَآ أَوْ أَن نَّفْعَلَ فِىٓ أَمْوَٰلِنَا مَا نَشَـٰٓؤُا۟ ۖ إِنَّكَ لَأَنتَ ٱلْحَلِيمُ ٱلرَّشِيدُ (٨٧)
They said, ‘O Shu‘ayb, does your worship require that we abandon what our fathers have been worshiping, or that we should not do with our wealth whatever we wish? You are indeed [a] gentle and sensible [person].’ (87)
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن كُنتُ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّى وَرَزَقَنِى مِنْهُ رِزْقًا حَسَنًا ۚ وَمَآ أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُخَالِفَكُمْ إِلَىٰ مَآ أَنْهَىٰكُمْ عَنْهُ ۚ إِنْ أُرِيدُ إِلَّا ٱلْإِصْلَـٰحَ مَا ٱسْتَطَعْتُ ۚ وَمَا تَوْفِيقِىٓ إِلَّا بِٱللَّهِ ۚ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَإِلَيْهِ أُنِيبُ (٨٨)
He said, ‘O my people! Have you considered, should I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord, who has provided me a good provision from Himself? I do not wish to oppose you by what I forbid you. I only desire to put things in order, as far as I can, and my success lies only with Allah: in Him I have put my trust, and to Him I turn penitently. (88)
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ لَا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شِقَاقِىٓ أَن يُصِيبَكُم مِّثْلُ مَآ أَصَابَ قَوْمَ نُوحٍ أَوْ قَوْمَ هُودٍ أَوْ قَوْمَ صَـٰلِحٍ ۚ وَمَا قَوْمُ لُوطٍ مِّنكُم بِبَعِيدٍ (٨٩)
O my people, do not let your defiance toward me lead you to be visited by the like of what was visited on the people of Noah, or the people of Hud, or the people of Salih, and the people of Lot are not distant from you. (89)
وَٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى رَحِيمٌ وَدُودٌ (٩٠)
Plead with your Lord for forgiveness, then turn to Him penitently. My Lord is indeed all-merciful, all-affectionate.’ (90)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰشُعَيْبُ مَا نَفْقَهُ كَثِيرًا مِّمَّا تَقُولُ وَإِنَّا لَنَرَىٰكَ فِينَا ضَعِيفًا ۖ وَلَوْلَا رَهْطُكَ لَرَجَمْنَـٰكَ ۖ وَمَآ أَنتَ عَلَيْنَا بِعَزِيزٍ (٩١)
They said, ‘O Shu‘ayb, we do not understand much of what you say. Indeed we see that you are weak amongst us, and were it not for your tribe, we would have stoned you, and you are not a formidable [challenge] for us.’ (91)
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَرَهْطِىٓ أَعَزُّ عَلَيْكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَٱتَّخَذْتُمُوهُ وَرَآءَكُمْ ظِهْرِيًّا ۖ إِنَّ رَبِّى بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطٌ (٩٢)
He said, ‘O my people! Is my tribe more formidable in your sight than Allah, to whom you pay no regard? Indeed my Lord comprehends whatever you do. (92)
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْمَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَكَانَتِكُمْ إِنِّى عَـٰمِلٌ ۖ سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ مَن يَأْتِيهِ عَذَابٌ يُخْزِيهِ وَمَنْ هُوَ كَـٰذِبٌ ۖ وَٱرْتَقِبُوٓا۟ إِنِّى مَعَكُمْ رَقِيبٌ (٩٣)
O my people! Act according to your ability; I too am acting. Soon you will know who will be overtaken by a punishment that will disgrace him, and who is a liar. So be on the watch; I too will be watching along with you.’ (93)
وَلَمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا نَجَّيْنَا شُعَيْبًا وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنَّا وَأَخَذَتِ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دِيَـٰرِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ (٩٤)
When Our edict came, We delivered Shu‘ayb and the faithful who were with him by a mercy from Us. And the Cry seized those who were wrongdoers, whereat they lay lifeless prostrate in their homes, (94)
كَأَن لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا۟ فِيهَآ ۗ أَلَا بُعْدًا لِّمَدْيَنَ كَمَا بَعِدَتْ ثَمُودُ (٩٥)
as if they had never lived there. Behold, away with Midian!—just as Thamud was done away with! (95)

(Commentary)

The verses recount the story of Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him) and his people, who were the inhabitants of Madyan. They used to worship idols, and the practice of defrauding (التطفيف) in weights and measures had become widespread among them, and corruption had intensified within their society. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, sent Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him) to them. He called them to monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and to fulfill the measure and the balance with justice, and to cease spreading corruption in the land. He gave them glad tidings and warned them, going to great lengths in his exhortation. It has been narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he said: "Shu‘ayb was the orator of the prophets."

However, the people responded only with rejection and disobedience. They threatened him with stoning and expulsion from among them, and they went to extremes in harming him and the small group of people who believed in him, barring them from the path of Allah. They persisted in this until he asked Allah to judge between him and them, whereupon Allah, the Exalted, destroyed them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِلى مَدْيَنَ أَخاهُمْ شُعَيْباً — "And to Madyan [We sent] Shu‘ayb, their brother" (11:84) until the end of the verse, is conjoined to the preceding stories of the prophets and their nations. Madyan is the name of the city inhabited by the people of Shu‘ayb. Attributing the sending of Shu‘ayb to "Madyan" while he was sent to its people is a type of figurative attribution (المجاز في الإسناد), similar to saying, "the waterspout ran" [meaning the water in it ran]. Describing Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him) as "their brother" indicates that he was genealogically related to them.

His saying: قالَ يا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرُهُ — "He said, ‘O my people! Worship Allah. You have no other god besides Him’" (11:84). The interpretation of this has been provided in similar instances previously.

His saying: وَلا تَنْقُصُوا الْمِكْيالَ وَالْمِيزانَ — "and do not reduce the measure and the balance" (11:84). The measure (المكيال) and the balance (الميزان) are names of instruments, meaning that by which things are measured and that by which things are weighed. They are not themselves described by "reduction"; rather, it is the measured and weighed goods that are described by reduction, increase, or equality. Thus, attributing reduction to the measure and the balance is a rational metaphor (المجاز العقلي).

Singling out the reduction of the measure and the balance for mention from among their sins indicates its prevalence among them, their preoccupation with it, and their excess in it to the point that its corruption became manifest and its evil effects apparent. This necessitated a strong emphasis from the caller to truth, who called them to abandon it by specifically mentioning it among other sins.

His saying: إِنِّي أَراكُمْ بِخَيْرٍ — "Indeed I see you in prosperity" (11:84), meaning: I witness you in a state of goodness, which refers to the blessings Allah has bestowed upon you of wealth, abundance of sustenance, low prices, and fertility. Therefore, you have no need to reduce the measure and the balance, or to embezzle small amounts of people's property out of greed through unlawful means, injustice, and rebellion. Based on this, His saying: إِنِّي أَراكُمْ بِخَيْرٍ serves as the rationale (تعليل) for His saying: وَلا تَنْقُصُوا الْمِكْيالَ وَالْمِيزانَ.

It is possible to generalize "goodness" (الخير) to mean that you are encompassed by Allah's care and graced by His blessings; He has given you intellect and guidance and provided you with sustenance. Thus, there is no justification for you to worship gods besides Him, to associate others with Him, or to corrupt the land by reducing the measure and the balance. According to this, it would be the rationale for both preceding sentences—namely, His saying: اعْبُدُوا اللهَ etc., and His saying: وَلا تَنْقُصُوا etc.—just as His saying: وَإِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذابَ يَوْمٍ مُحِيطٍ — "and I fear for you the punishment of an all-encompassing day" (11:84) is also a rationale.

The summary of His saying: إِنِّي أَراكُمْ until the end of the verse is that there are two deterrents that ought to restrain you from disobeying Allah: First, that you are in a state of prosperity and have no need to diminish people's wealth through unlawful means. Second, that behind the violation of Allah's command lies an all-encompassing day whose punishment is to be feared.

It is not far-fetched to suggest that His saying: إِنِّي أَراكُمْ بِخَيْرٍ means: "I look upon you with a gaze of goodness," i.e., I look at you as a sincere and compassionate advisor whose gaze is accompanied only by goodness and who desires nothing for you but happiness. Based on this, His saying: وَإِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذابَ يَوْمٍ مُحِيطٍ would be like an explanatory conjunction (عطف التفسير) to it.

His saying: وَإِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذابَ يَوْمٍ مُحِيطٍ refers to the Day of Resurrection or the day of the descent of the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال). The meaning of the day—which is the day of judgment with punishment—being "all-encompassing" (محيط) is that there is no exit from it, no escape, and no refuge besides Allah; no helper or supporter can repel it, and neither repentance nor intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — will avail therein. The meaning of "encompassing" ultimately implies that the punishment is certain and inescapable. The meaning of the verse is that for disbelief and transgression, there is a punishment that cannot be turned back, and I fear that it will strike you.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيا قَوْمِ أَوْفُوا الْمِكْيالَ وَالْمِيزانَ بِالْقِسْطِ وَلا تَبْخَسُوا النَّاسَ أَشْياءَهُمْ — "‘O my people! Observe fully the measure and the balance, with justice, and do not cheat the people of their goods’" (11:85) etc. Fulfilling (الإيفاء) means giving the right in its entirety, and cheating (البخس) means reduction. He repeated the statement regarding the measure and the balance by moving from the general to the specific to emphasize the importance of a matter that their society could not do without. This is because he first called them to reform by forbidding the reduction of the measure and the balance, and then returned to command the fulfillment of the measure and the balance and forbade cheating people of their goods. This indicates that merely refraining from reducing the measure and the balance is not sufficient to give this matter its due right—he first forbade it so that there would be a general understanding serving as a premise for knowing the obligation in detail—rather, the one who measures and weighs must fulfill his measure and balance and give them their due, and not cheat or reduce the things attributed to people through transaction, until they know they have rendered to people their goods and returned to them their property as it truly is.

Regarding His saying: وَلا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ — "‘and do not act wickedly on the earth, causing corruption’" (11:85), al-Raghib said: al-‘ayth (العيث) and al-‘athi (العثي) are close in meaning, like jadhab and jabadh [to pull], except that al-‘ayth is mostly used for corruption perceived by the senses, while al-‘athi is for that which is perceived by judgment. It is said: ‘athiya ya‘tha ‘athyan. Based on this, وَلا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ comes from ‘atha ya‘thu ‘athwan. End quote.

Accordingly, His saying: مُفْسِدِينَ — "causing corruption" is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the pronoun in لا تَعْثَوْا to provide emphasis, similar to the emphasis in our saying: "Do not corrupt with corruption."

The sentence, namely His saying: وَلا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ, is a renewed prohibition against corruption in the land, whether through killing, wounding, or any injustice regarding wealth, status, or honor. However, it is not unlikely to infer from the context that the sentence is an explanatory conjunction for the previous prohibition, thus being an emphatic prohibition against defrauding and reducing the measure and the balance, as it is a form of corruption in the land.

To explain this: Civil society (الاجتماع المدني) that revolves among the individuals of the human species is fundamentally built upon exchange (المبادلة). There is no connection or bond between two individuals of the species except that it involves giving and taking. Those in society continue to cooperate in the affairs of their lives; one benefits another so that he may benefit from him in a similar or greater manner, and he renders a benefit to him so that he may attract a benefit to himself—this is transaction and exchange.

One of the most manifest instances of this exchange is financial transactions, especially in goods that have volume or weight, which are measured or weighed. This is among the oldest things man realized the necessity of applying the tradition of exchange to.

Thus, financial transactions, especially buying and selling, are among the pillars of human social life. An individual estimates what he needs for his necessary life by measure or weight, and what he must spend in exchange for it of price, and then proceeds in his life building it upon this estimation and management.

If his transacting partner betrays him and reduces the measure and the balance without him realizing, he has corrupted his management and nullified his estimation. Through this, the order of his livelihood is disrupted from both sides: from the side of the necessities of life he acquires by purchasing, and from the side of the excess price he spends, which he exhausted himself to obtain through earning. Thus, he is robbed of sound judgment and good management in his life, and he stumbles blindly in its course—and this is corruption (الفساد).

When this becomes widespread in a society, corruption has spread among them, and it will not be long before trust, confidence, and mutual reliance are stripped away. Consequently, public security departs from among them, which is a comprehensive calamity that encompasses the righteous and the wicked, the defrauder and the one who fulfills the measure and the balance alike. Their gathering then becomes a gathering for deception and the corruption of life, rather than a gathering for cooperation toward its happiness. The Exalted said: وَأَوْفُوا الْكَيْلَ إِذا كِلْتُمْ وَزِنُوا بِالْقِسْطاسِ الْمُسْتَقِيمِ ذلِكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَحْسَنُ تَأْوِيلاً — "Fill the measure when you measure, and weigh with an even balance. That is better and more favorable in outcome" (17:35).

His saying, the Exalted: بَقِيَّتُ اللهِ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ وَما أَنَا عَلَيْكُمْ بِحَفِيظٍ — "‘What remains of Allah’s provision is better for you, should you be believers, and I am not a keeper over you’" (11:86). "The remainder" (البقية) means that which remains, and what is intended is the profit obtained by the seller, which is what stays with him after the completion of the transaction to be used for his needs. This is because exchange, although not initially established primarily for the sake of profit-seeking—rather, an individual would acquire some goods of life, and if they exceeded what he needed, he would exchange the surplus for other goods he needed but did not possess—later, trade itself and the exchange of goods for prices were taken up as a profession to earn money and acquire wealth. An individual would take goods of one type or various types and offer them to those in need for exchange, adding to his capital a certain profit in return for his labor in collecting and offering them. People, as buyers, accepted this because of the ease it brought to the process of exchange. Thus, the merchant has a legitimate profit in his trade that society accepts according to their primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — which sustains his livelihood and through which wealth is transferred to him to maintain his life.

So the meaning is that the profit, which is a divine remainder (بقية إلهية) that Allah has guided you to through your primordial nature, is better for you than the wealth you acquire through defrauding and reducing the measure and the balance, if you are believers. For the believer only benefits from wealth through the legitimate means that Allah has directed to him through lawful channels. As for anything else that neither Allah nor people—according to their primordial nature—approve of, there is no good in it for him and he has no need for it.

It has been said that the condition of faith in His saying: إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ — "should you be believers" is to indicate that faith is a condition for the knowledge of that, not for its essence. The meaning would be: "If you are believers, you would know the truth of my saying: 'What remains of Allah’s provision is better for you.'"

It has also been said that the meaning of the verse is the reward for obeying Allah—with "the remainder" meaning the reward for obedience that remains—is better for you if you are believers. Other interpretations have also been mentioned.

His saying: وَما أَنَا عَلَيْكُمْ بِحَفِيظٍ — "and I am not a keeper over you," meaning: nothing of what you possess—whether your souls, actions, obedience, sustenance, or blessings—returns to my power. I am but a messenger whose only duty is the clear delivery of the message. It is for you to choose what contains your guidance and goodness, or to fall into the abyss of destruction, without me being able to bring goodness to you or repel evil from you. This is like His saying, the Exalted: فَمَنْ أَبْصَرَ فَلِنَفْسِهِ وَمَنْ عَمِيَ فَعَلَيْها وَما أَنَا عَلَيْكُمْ بِحَفِيظٍ — "Whoever sees, it is to his own benefit, and whoever is blind, it is to his own detriment, and I am not a keeper over you" (6:104).

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا يا شُعَيْبُ أَصَلاتُكَ تَأْمُرُكَ أَنْ نَتْرُكَ ما يَعْبُدُ آباؤُنا — "They said, ‘O Shu‘ayb, does your prayer command you that we should abandon what our fathers used to worship’" (11:87) until the end of the verse. This is their rejection of Shu‘ayb's argument against them, and it is of the most subtle composition. The core of their intent was: "We are free in what we choose for ourselves of religion or how we dispose of our wealth in various ways. You do not own us such that you can command us with everything you like or forbid us from everything you dislike. If something you witness from us displeases you because of what you pray and use to draw near to your Lord, and you wish to command and forbid, then do not exceed yourself, for you own nothing but yourself."

They expressed this intent in a remarkable form mixed with both sarcasm and blame, cast in the mold of a rhetorical question (الاستفهام الإنكاري). It is as if they said: "That which you want from us—abandoning the worship of idols and abandoning whatever disposal of our wealth we wish—is what your prayer has incited you toward and made ugly in your eyes, thus commanding you to do so because it has taken possession of you. But you have demanded from us what your prayer demanded of you, yet neither you nor your prayer own us, because we are free in our perception and will. We have the right to choose any religion we wish and to dispose of our wealth in any way we want without restriction or prevention. We have only adopted our religion, which is the religion of our fathers, and we have only disposed of our own wealth, and there is no restriction on a possessor of wealth regarding his wealth."

"So what is the meaning of your prayer commanding you with something, while we are the ones to comply with what it commanded you? In other words, what is the meaning of your prayer commanding you regarding our actions that subsist in us, not you? Is this anything but foolishness of opinion? And yet you are indeed the clement and the right-minded! A clement person (الحليم) does not hasten to rebuke one he sees as an evildoer or take revenge on one he sees as a criminal until the face of correctness becomes clear to him; and a right-minded person (الرشيد) does not embark upon a matter containing error and misguidance. So how did you embark upon such a foolish matter that has no form except ignorance and error?"

From this explanation, several points become clear: First, they attributed the command to "prayer" (الصلاة) because of its role in inciting and calling toward opposing the people in their worship of idols and their defrauding in measure and balance. This is the secret behind their expression: أَصَلاتُكَ تَأْمُرُكَ أَنْ نَتْرُكَ — "does your prayer command you that we should abandon" etc., rather than saying: "Does your prayer forbid you that we worship what our fathers worshipped?" even though expressing prevention by forbidding an act is closer to natural disposition than expressing it by commanding its abandonment. For this reason, Shu‘ayb expressed it as a prohibition in his response to their statement when he said: وَما أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُخالِفَكُمْ إِلى ما أَنْهاكُمْ عَنْهُ — "I do not desire to oppose you by doing what I forbid you" (11:88), and he did not say "to what I command you to abandon." The intent—in any case—was his preventing them from idol worship and defrauding. Understand this, for it is among the subtleties of this verse, which is filled with subtlety and beauty.

Second, they said: أَنْ نَتْرُكَ ما يَعْبُدُ آباؤُنا — "that we should abandon what our fathers used to worship" rather than saying: "that we should abandon our gods" or "that we should abandon the idols," in order to point to the argument for it: that these idols have been worshipped continuously by our fathers, so it is a national tradition (سنة قومية) for us. There is no harm in following a national tradition inherited by the later generations from the earlier ones, upon which generation after generation has been raised. For we worship our gods and persist in our religion, which is the religion of our fathers, and we preserve a communal custom from being lost.

Third, they said: أَنْ نَفْعَلَ فِي أَمْوالِنا — "that we should [not] do [as we wish] with our wealth," mentioning "wealth" (الأموال) as attributed to themselves to hint at the argument: that when a thing becomes the property of someone, no doubter doubts that he has the right to dispose of it, and no one else who acknowledges its ownership to him has the right to oppose him in that. A person has the right to proceed in the course of life and manage the affair of livelihood with whatever skill and ingenuity he can, and whatever intelligence and shrewdness guide him to.

Fourth, their saying: أَصَلاتُكَ تَأْمُرُكَ until His saying: إِنَّكَ لَأَنْتَ الْحَلِيمُ الرَّشِيدُ — "Indeed you are the clement and the right-minded" is built upon sarcasm and mockery. However, the sarcasm lies in their linking the command of prayer to Shu‘ayb with their abandonment of what their fathers worshipped, as well as in attributing the command to prayer alone. As for the attribution of clemency (الحلم) and right-mindedness (الرشد) to him, there is no sarcasm or mockery in it. For this reason, the statement إِنَّكَ لَأَنْتَ الْحَلِيمُ الرَّشِيدُ was emphasized with inna and the lam, and the predicate was brought as a nominal sentence to be stronger in affirming clemency and right-mindedness for him. This makes the blame and rejection of him more eloquent: that one whose clemency and right-mindedness are beyond doubt, it is ugly for him to embark upon such a foolish matter and to rise up to strip people of their freedom and independence in perception and will.

Thus, it becomes clear that what many [commentators] have mentioned—that they described him as clement and right-minded by way of mockery, meaning he is described by their opposites, namely ignorance and error—is not correct.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ يا قَوْمِ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي وَرَزَقَنِي مِنْهُ رِزْقاً حَسَناً — "He said, ‘O my people! Tell me, should I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord, and He has provided me a good provision from Himself’" (11:88) until the end of the verse. What is intended by his being "on a manifest proof (بينة) from his Lord" is his possessing a clear sign, which is the sign of prophethood and the miracle (المعجزة) indicating the truthfulness of the prophet in his claim to prophethood. What is intended by his being "provided a good provision (رزقاً حَسَناً) from Allah" is that Allah gave him from His presence the revelation of prophethood, which encompasses the principles of knowledge and divine laws (الشرائع). The clarification of similar terms has already passed.

The meaning is: "Tell me, if I am a messenger from Allah to you, and He has singled me out with the revelation of knowledge and laws, and supported me with a manifest proof indicating the truthfulness of my claim, am I then foolish in my opinion? And is that which I call you to a foolish call? And is there in that any arbitrary rule from me over you or a stripping of your freedom? For it is only Allah Who is the Owner of everything, and you are not free in relation to Him; rather, you are His servants. He commands you as He wills, and to Him belongs the judgment, and to Him you shall return."

His saying: وَما أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُخالِفَكُمْ إِلى ما أَنْهاكُمْ عَنْهُ — "I do not desire to oppose you by doing what I forbid you" (11:88). The verb "oppose" (المخالفة) is made transitive with "to" (إلى) because it implies the meaning of what is made transitive by it, such as "inclining" (الميل) and the like. The estimation is: "I oppose you while inclining toward what I forbid you," or "I incline toward what I forbid you while opposing you."

The sentence is a response to what they accused him of—that he wanted to strip them of freedom in their actions, enslave them, and rule over them arbitrarily. Its summary is that if he had intended that, he would have opposed them in what he forbade them, but he does not intend to oppose them, so he does not intend what they accused him of; rather, he only intends reform as much as he can.

To clarify: Although divine creation (الصنع الإلهي) has made man an agent with choice (مختار) in his acts and free in his work—having the ability to incline in the instances of action toward either side of doing or refraining—he possesses, according to this creation, complete freedom in comparison to his fellow human beings who are his likes and counterparts in creation; they have what he has and owe what he owes. Thus, no one has the right to rule over another based on personal whim.

However, He created him with a social nature (أفطره على الاجتماع), so life is not complete for him except in a society of individuals of the species, in which everyone cooperates to fulfill the needs of everyone. Then each of them is specialized with his portion according to his social weight. It is self-evident that society cannot stand except by traditions and laws (سنن وقوانين) that run within it, and a government administered by some of them that preserves order and executes the laws—all of that according to what the interests of society demand.

Thus, it is inevitable that those in society sacrifice some of their freedom in exchange for the law and the prevailing tradition, by being deprived of absolute license and unrestraint, so that they may thereby succeed in attaining some of their desires and reviving the remaining part of their freedom.

Therefore, social man has no freedom in the face of vital issues demanded by the interests and benefits of society. That which the government dictates in that regard of command and prohibition is not a form of enslavement or arrogance at all, since it only dictates in matters where social man has no freedom. Likewise, if an individual among the socialized people sees from the actions of his social brothers that which harms the state of society or does not benefit it because it nullifies one of the pillars of the fundamental interests therein, and that incites him to advise them with what guides them to follow the path of right-mindedness—so he commands them with what they must do and forbids them from committing what they must refrain from—this individual is not ruling arbitrarily out of personal whim or enslaving the free socialized members of his species. For they have no freedom in the face of the high interests and necessary rulings to be observed in their society. What he conveys to them of command and prohibition in this regard is not truly a command or prohibition of his own; rather, they are a command and prohibition arising from the call of the aforementioned interests, subsisting in society as society with its expansive personality. The individual who conveys the command and prohibition to them is merely like a speaking tongue, nothing more.

The sign of this is that he himself complies with what he commands and he himself refrains from what he forbids, without his word opposing his deed or his view opposing his action. For man is naturally disposed to preserve his benefits and observe his interests; so if there were goodness in the action he calls others to, and it is shared between them, he would not oppose it in his own person, nor would he abandon for himself what he deems good for others. For this reason, he (peace be upon him) said in the response he conveyed to them: وَما أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُخالِفَكُمْ إِلى ما أَنْهاكُمْ عَنْهُ — "I do not desire to oppose you by doing what I forbid you," and he also said, as Allah recounted to complete the benefit and repel any accusation directed at him: وَما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلى رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ — "I do not ask you any reward for it; my reward lies only with the Lord of all the worlds" (26:180).

Thus, he (peace be upon him) indicates by his saying: وَما أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُخالِفَكُمْ etc., that what he forbids them from are matters containing the reform of their society, of which he is one member, and which everyone must observe and adhere to. It is not an enslaving proposal out of personal whim. Therefore, he followed it with his saying: إِنْ أُرِيدُ إِلَّا الْإِصْلاحَ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُ — "I only desire reform as much as I can" (11:88).

The summary of the situation is that when they heard from Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him) the call to abandon idol worship and defrauding, they rejected it on the grounds that it was a proposal from him that contradicted the human freedom they possessed, which allowed them to worship whom they wished and do with their wealth what they wished.

Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him) responded to them that what he calls them to is not from himself such that their being asked for it would contradict their freedom and nullify their independence in perception and will; rather, he is a messenger from their Lord to them, and he has a manifest proof for that. What he brought them is from Allah, Who owns them and owns everything, and they are His servants who have no freedom in His face, nor any choice in what He wants from them.

Furthermore, what he conveyed to them are matters containing the reform of their society and the happiness of their souls in this world and the Hereafter. The sign of this is that he does not desire to oppose them in what he forbids them; rather, he is like them in acting upon it. He only desires reform as much as he can, and he does not ask them for any reward for it; his reward is only with the Lord of all the worlds.

His saying: وَما تَوْفِيقِي إِلَّا بِاللهِ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَإِلَيْهِ أُنِيبُ — "My success (توفيقي) — the divine harmonizing of causes to achieve a righteous end — is only through Allah. In Him I have put my trust and to Him I turn" (11:88). This is in the position of an exception to "capability" (الاستطاعة). For when he (peace be upon him) mentioned to them that he desires the reform of their society through beneficial knowledge and righteous deeds (الصالحات) according to the measure of capability he possesses, and in its light he affirmed for himself capability and power—and the servant does not possess capability independently and by himself apart from Allah, the Glorified—he completed the deficiency and limitation in his speech with his saying: وَما تَوْفِيقِي إِلَّا بِاللهِ. That is, that which emanates from my will through a capability from me—of managing the affairs of your society and the harmonizing of causes (توفيق الأسباب) with one another resulting in its happiness—is only through Allah, the Glorified. There is no independence from Him, no exit from His encompassment, and no independence in any matter apart from Him. He is the One Who gave me what I have of capability, and He is the One Who harmonizes the causes through my capability. So my capability is from Him and my success is through Him.

He (peace be upon him) explained this reality and confessed that his success is through Allah. This is a branch of His being, the Exalted, the Originator (الفاطر) of every soul, its Preserver, and the One Who stands over every soul for what it has earned, as He said: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ فاطِرِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "All praise belongs to Allah, the Originator of the heavens and the earth" (35:1), and He said: وَرَبُّكَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ حَفِيظٌ — "and your Lord is watchful over all things" (34:21), and He said: أَفَمَنْ هُوَ قائِمٌ عَلى كُلِّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ — "Is He who stands over every soul for what it has earned [like him who does not?]" (13:33), and He said: إِنَّ اللهَ يُمْسِكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ أَنْ تَزُولا — "Indeed Allah sustains the heavens and the earth lest they should fall apart" (35:41). The summary of this is that He, the Exalted, is the One Who originated things, their actions, and the relations between them, and brought them forth into existence (الوجود). He is the One Who has grasped everything, sustaining it and sustaining its effects and the relations between them lest they vanish and disappear behind the veil of nullity.

The necessary consequence of this is that He, the Exalted, is the Trustee (وكيل) of everything in managing its affairs. They are attributed to Him, the Exalted, in their realization and the realization of the relations between them, because He encompasses them and dominates them. Yet, they also have an attribution to that thing by His permission, the Exalted.

It is obligatory for the servant who knows the station of his Lord and recognizes this reality to represent it by initiating trust (التوكل) in his Lord and turning (الإنابة) and returning to Him.
