Therefore, when Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him) mentioned that his success is through Allah, he followed it by initiating trust and turning, saying: عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَإِلَيْهِ أُنِيبُ.

( A Discussion on the Meaning of Human Freedom in Action )

Man, according to his creation, is a being possessed of perception and will; he has the right to choose for himself whatever action he wishes. In other words, in every action he encounters, he has the choice to take the side of doing or the side of refraining. Every action that is possible to perform, when presented to him, finds him—according to nature—standing at a point where two paths meet: doing and refraining. He is compelled (مضطر) in being characterized by the principle of choice (الاختيار), but he is an agent with choice in the actions attributed to him that proceed from him by his choice. That is, he is free-reined regarding doing and refraining according to his primordial nature, not restricted by either side nor shackled. This is what is meant by the ontological freedom (حرية الإنسان تكوينا) of man.

The necessary consequence of this ontological freedom is another legislative freedom (حرية تشريعية) that he assumes in his social life: that he has the right to choose for himself whatever ways of life he wishes and perform whatever work he wishes. No one of his fellow species has the right to exalt himself over him, enslave him, or possess his will and action, thereby imposing upon him through personal whim what he dislikes. For the individuals of the species are likes; each of them has the same free nature as the other, and the same obligations. The Exalted said: وَلا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنا بَعْضاً أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ — "and that none of us take others for lords besides Allah" (3:64), and He said: ما كانَ لِبَشَرٍ — "It does not behoove any human" until He said: ثُمَّ يَقُولَ لِلنَّاسِ كُونُوا عِباداً لِي مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ — "and then say to the people, ‘Be my servants instead of Allah’" (3:79).

This is what man possesses in comparison to his likes among his fellow species. As for his relation to the cosmic causes and means (العلل والأسباب الكونية) that brought the human nature into existence, he has no freedom in their face. For they possess him and encompass him from all sides, turning him over. It is they who, through their origination and the execution of their command, did with man what they did, bringing him forth with his structure and properties, without him having any choice in his affair to accept what he loves and reject what he dislikes. Rather, he was as was intended, not as he intended. Even the voluntary actions of man—which are the field of human freedom—only obey man in what these causes and means have permitted. Thus, not everything man loves and intends occurs, nor is he successful in everything he chooses for himself, as is apparent.

These causes and means are what equipped man with systems that remind him of his needs and the deficiencies of his existence, and incite him toward actions containing his happiness and the removal of his deficiencies and needs—such as the nutritive system, for example, which reminds him of hunger and thirst and guides him to bread and water to achieve satiety and quenching. So it is with the other systems in his existence.

Furthermore, these causes and means have imposed a legislative necessity (إيجاباً تشريعياً) upon the individual man regarding matters of real interest that he cannot deny or refuse out of a sense of self-sufficiency—such as eating, drinking, seeking shelter, and protecting oneself from heat and cold, and defending against everything that opposes the benefits of his existence.

Then, they created him with a social life, so he acknowledged the necessity of establishing domestic and civil society and proceeding in the path of cooperation and transaction. This compels him to be deprived of the gift of freedom from two sides:

First: Society is not completed by the individual except by his giving the individuals cooperating with him reciprocal rights respected by him, so that they may give him in return rights they respect. This is by him working for people as they work for him, and benefiting them to the extent he benefits from them, and being deprived of unrestraint and license in action to the extent he deprives them. Thus, he does not have the right to do as he wishes and judge as he intends; rather, he is free in what does not interfere with the freedom of others. This is a deprivation of some freedom to obtain some of it.

Second: Society cannot maintain its strength without the running of traditions and laws (سنن وقوانين) within it, accepted by the socialized individuals or most of them. Those traditions and laws guarantee their general benefits according to whether the society has a high or low and base life, and through them, their high social interests are preserved.

It is well known that respecting traditions and laws strips freedom from those in society in their respective instances. So whoever establishes a tradition or legislates a law—whether it be the general public, their delegates, the sovereign, or Allah and His Messenger, depending on the variation of traditions and laws—deprives people of some of their freedom to preserve another part of it. Allah the Exalted said: وَرَبُّكَ يَخْلُقُ ما يَشاءُ وَيَخْتارُ ما كانَ لَهُمُ الْخِيَرَةُ — "Your Lord creates whatever He wishes and chooses: they have no choice" (28:68), and the Exalted said: وَما كانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ وَلا مُؤْمِنَةٍ إِذا قَضَى اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ أَمْراً أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُمُ الْخِيَرَةُ مِنْ أَمْرِهِمْ وَمَنْ يَعْصِ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ ضَلالاً مُبِيناً — "A believing man or woman has no choice in their matter when Allah and His Apostle have ordained a matter; and whoever disobeys Allah and His Apostle has certainly strayed into manifest error" (33:36).

In summary, man is only free in comparison to the members of his species regarding what they propose out of personal whim. As for what is necessitated by his binding interests, especially the general social interests as guided by the causes and means, he has no freedom whatsoever. Nor is the call to a tradition or any action that accords with human interests—from the side of the law, or the one in whose hand is its execution, or the volunteer advisor who commands the right or forbids the wrong while adhering to a manifest proof—a form of false arbitrary rule or the stripping of legitimate freedom in any way.

Furthermore, the aforementioned causes and means and the interests they guide to are instances of the will of Allah, the Glorified, or His permission—as guided and explained by the teaching of monotheism (التوحيد) in Islam. So He, the Glorified, is the Owner in the absolute sense, and for others there is nothing but being owned from every side, and for man there is nothing but pure servitude (العبودية). His absolute ownership strips any imagined freedom for man in relation to his Lord, just as it gives him freedom in comparison to the rest of his fellow species, as the Exalted said: أَلَّا نَعْبُدَ إِلَّا اللهَ وَلا نُشْرِكَ بِهِ شَيْئاً وَلا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنا بَعْضاً أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ — "that we should worship no one but Allah, and not ascribe any partner to Him, and that none of us take others for lords besides Allah" (3:64).

Thus, He, the Glorified, is the Judge in the absolute sense and the One to be obeyed without restriction or condition, as He said: إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ — "Judgment belongs only to Allah" (12:40). He has given the right of command, prohibition, and obedience to His messengers, to the holders of authority (أولي الأمر), and to the believers of the Islamic nation. Thus, no one has freedom in the face of the word of truth they bring and call toward. The Exalted said: أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ — "Obey Allah and obey the Apostle and those vested with authority among you" (4:59), and the Exalted said: وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتُ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ يَأْمُرُونَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ — "The faithful, men and women, are comrades of one another: they bid what is right and forbid what is wrong" (9:71).

His saying, the Exalted: وَيا قَوْمِ لا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شِقاقِي أَنْ يُصِيبَكُمْ مِثْلُ ما أَصابَ قَوْمَ نُوحٍ — "‘O my people! Let not my rift with you bring upon you the like of what befell the people of Noah’" (11:89). Al-jarm (الجرم)—as mentioned by al-Raghib—is the picking of fruit from the tree, and it has been used metaphorically for every acquisition of something disliked. Al-shiqaq (الشقاق) is opposition and enmity. The meaning is: Beware lest your opposition and enmity toward me, because of what I call you to, cause you to acquire a calamity like the calamity of the people of Noah, which was drowning; or the people of Hud, which was the barren wind; or the people of Salih, which was the Cry and the earthquake.

His saying: وَما قَوْمُ لُوطٍ مِنْكُمْ بِبَعِيدٍ — "‘and the people of Lot are not far from you’" (11:89), meaning: there is no great gap between their time and your time. The temporal gap between the two peoples was less than three centuries; Lot was a contemporary of Abraham (peace be upon them), and Shu‘ayb was a contemporary of Moses (peace be upon them).

It has been said: What is intended is the denial of spatial distance, pointing out that their ruined cities are close to you because Madyan is near Sodom, which is in the Holy Land. So the meaning is: "The place of the people of Lot is not far from you; you witness their overturned cities and their remaining manifest ruins." However, the context does not support this, and the estimation [of "place"] is contrary to the original [wording] and is not resorted to except with evidence.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوا إِلَيْهِ إِنَّ رَبِّي رَحِيمٌ وَدُودٌ — "‘And plead with your Lord for forgiveness, then turn to Him penitently. My Lord is indeed all-merciful, all-affectionate’" (11:90). The discussion on the meaning of His saying: وَاسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوا إِلَيْهِ has already passed, meaning: ask Allah for forgiveness for your sins and return to Him by believing in Him and His messenger. Indeed, Allah is the Possessor of mercy and affection; He has mercy on those who seek forgiveness and repent, and He loves them.

He first said: اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ, attributing "Lord" to them, then said in the position of its rationale: إِنَّ رَبِّي رَحِيمٌ وَدُودٌ. Perhaps the reason for this is that in the stage of commanding forgiveness and repentance from Allah, the Glorified, he mentioned the attribute of Lordship (الربوبية) because it is the attribute to which worship—including seeking forgiveness and repentance—is linked. He attributed His Lordship to them by saying رَبَّكُمْ to emphasize the link and to hint that He is their Lord, not the lords they take besides Allah.

It would have been appropriate for the speech to say in its rationale: "Indeed your Lord is all-merciful, all-affectionate," but since it was, along with being a rationale, a praise of Allah, the Glorified, and he had previously affirmed that He is the Lord of the people, he attributed it a second time to himself so that the speech in its entirety would convey the meaning that "your Lord and my Lord is all-merciful, all-affectionate."

Furthermore, in this attribution, there is a sense of knowledge and experience, thus providing support for the truth of the statement. For it is in the sense that He, the Exalted, is all-merciful and all-affectionate, and how could He not be? For He is my Lord, and I know Him by these two descriptions.

Al-Wadud (الودود) is among the names of Allah, the Exalted. It is a form of al-wudd (الود), meaning love. However, what is inferred from its instances of usage is that it is a specific type of love, which is the love that has manifest effects and consequences, such as intimacy, frequenting, and benevolence. The Exalted said: وَمِنْ آياتِهِ أَنْ خَلَقَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَزْواجاً لِتَسْكُنُوا إِلَيْها وَجَعَلَ بَيْنَكُمْ مَوَدَّةً وَرَحْمَةً — "And of His signs is that He created for you mates from your own selves that you may take comfort in them, and He ordained affection and mercy between you" (30:21).

Allah, the Glorified, loves His servants and manifests the effects of His love by bestowing His blessings upon them: وَإِنْ تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ لا تُحْصُوها — "and if you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them" (14:34). Thus, He, the Exalted, is Wadud (affectionate) toward them.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا يا شُعَيْبُ ما نَفْقَهُ كَثِيراً مِمَّا تَقُولُ وَإِنَّا لَنَراكَ فِينا ضَعِيفاً — "They said, ‘O Shu‘ayb! We do not understand much of what you say, and we indeed see you to be weak among us’" (11:91) until the end of the verse. Al-fiqh (الفقه) is more eloquent and stronger than al-fahm (understanding). A man's raht (رهط) is his clan and people; it has been said it is from three to seven or ten. Based on this, in their saying رهطك (your clan), there is a hint at their small number and the insignificance of their affair. Al-rajm (الرجم) is stoning with rocks.

When Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him) argued with them and exhausted them with his argument, they found no way but to cut off his speech through means other than argument. So they mentioned to him:

First: That much of what he says is not understood by them, so his speech goes to waste as something without effect. This is a metaphor for him speaking that which contains no benefit.

Then they followed it with their saying: وَإِنَّا لَنَراكَ فِينا ضَعِيفاً — "and we indeed see you to be weak among us," meaning: we do not understand what you say, and you are not powerful among us such that your power would compel us to strive in understanding your speech, to be concerned with taking it, and to listen and accept it. For we see you among us only as a weak person whose affair is not cared for and whose word is not heeded.

Then they threatened him with their saying: وَلَوْ لا رَهْطُكَ لَرَجَمْناكَ — "‘and were it not for your clan, we would have surely stoned you’" (11:91), meaning: were it not for this small number of people who are your clan, we would have stoned you, but we observe their side regarding you. In the belittling of the clan, there is a hint that if they wanted to kill him one day, they would kill him without caring about his clan; rather, what restrained them from killing him was a type of respect and honoring from them for his clan.

Then they followed it with their saying: وَما أَنْتَ عَلَيْنا بِعَزِيزٍ — "‘and you are not dear to us’" (11:91) to emphasize their saying: لَوْ لا رَهْطُكَ لَرَجَمْناكَ. That is: you are not powerful or protected in your side against us such that it would prevent us from killing you with the worst of killings; rather, what prevents us is the observance of the side of your clan. The summary of their statement is the humiliation of Shu‘ayb and that they do not care for him or what he said; they only observe the side of his clan in refraining from attacking him.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ يا قَوْمِ أَرَهْطِي أَعَزُّ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنَ اللهِ وَاتَّخَذْتُمُوهُ وَراءَكُمْ ظِهْرِيًّا — "He said, ‘O my people! Is my clan more dear to you than Allah? And you have cast Him behind your backs [neglectfully]’" (11:92). Al-Zihri (الظهري) is attributed to the back (al-zahr), and the vowel is changed in the attribution. It is the thing that is behind the back, so it is left as something forgotten. It is said: "He took it behind him zihriyan," meaning he forgot it, did not mention it, and did not pay attention to it.

This is a refutation from Shu‘ayb of their saying: وَلَوْ لا رَهْطُكَ لَرَجَمْناكَ. That is: How can you hold my clan dear and respect their side, yet you do not hold Allah, the Glorified, dear nor respect His side, while I am the one calling you to Him from His side? Is my clan more dear to you than Allah? And you have made Him something forgotten, which you have no right to do, and it was not for you to do it. Indeed, my Lord encompasses what you do, through His encompassment of everything in existence, knowledge, and power. In the verse, there is a critique of their opinion as foolish, just as they critiqued his opinion in the previous verse as insignificant.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيا قَوْمِ اعْمَلُوا عَلى مَكانَتِكُمْ إِنِّي عامِلٌ — "‘O my people! Act according to your ability; I [too] am acting’" (11:93) until the end of the verse. It is said in al-Majma‘: al-makanah (المكانة) is the state by which its possessor is enabled to act. End quote. It is originally—as has been said—from makuna makanatan, like dakhuma dakhamatan, when one is powerful in action with all strength. It is said: "He was enabled (tamakkana) of such-and-law," meaning he encompassed it with power.

This is a threat from Shu‘ayb to them of the severest kind. For it indicates that he is certain of what he says, and neither anxiety nor agitation takes hold of him because of their disbelief in him and their rebellion against his call. So let them act according to the power and enablement they have; they have their work and he has his work. Soon a humiliating punishment will surprise them, and they will know then who it is that the punishment takes. Them or him? And they will know who is a liar. So let them watch, and he is a watcher with them, not parting from them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمَّا جاءَ أَمْرُنا نَجَّيْنا شُعَيْباً — "When Our edict came, We delivered Shu‘ayb" (11:94) until His saying: جاثِمِينَ — "lying prostrate" (11:94). What clarifies the meaning of the verse has already passed.

His saying, the Exalted: كَأَنْ لَمْ يَغْنَوْا فِيها أَلا بُعْداً لِمَدْيَنَ كَما بَعِدَتْ ثَمُودُ — "as if they had never lived there. Behold! Far be Madyan [from Allah’s mercy], as Thamud were far [from it]!" (11:95). Ghani (غني) in a place means to reside in it. His saying: أَلا بُعْداً لِمَدْيَنَ etc., contains a curse upon them just as Thamud was cursed. Some discussion on this has passed in the previous stories.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, he said: He said: Allah sent Shu‘ayb to Madyan—which is a village on the road to Syria—but they did not believe in him.

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, narrated from Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn ‘Isa, from some of his companions, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah: إِنِّي أَراكُمْ بِخَيْرٍ — "Indeed I see you in prosperity," he said: "Their prices were low."

In the same source, from Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl, from al-Rida (peace be upon him), he said: I asked him about the awaiting of the relief (انتظار الفرج), and he said: "Do you not know that the awaiting of the relief is part of the relief?" Then he said: "Indeed Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, says: وَارْتَقِبُوا إِنِّي مَعَكُمْ رَقِيبٌ — ‘And watch! I too am watching with you’ (11:93)."

I say: His saying laysa ta‘lam means la ta‘lam (do you not know), and it is a post-classical linguistic usage.

In al-Ma‘ani, with its chain of narration from ‘Abdullah ibn al-Fadl al-Hashimi, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: I said: What of His saying, Mighty and Majestic is He: وَما تَوْفِيقِي إِلَّا بِاللهِ — "My success is only through Allah," and His saying, Mighty and Majestic is He: إِنْ يَنْصُرْكُمُ اللهُ فَلا غالِبَ لَكُمْ ـ وَإِنْ يَخْذُلْكُمْ فَمَنْ ذَا الَّذِي يَنْصُرُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ — "If Allah helps you, no one can overcome you, but if He forsakes you, who is it that can help you after Him?" (3:160)? He replied: "When the servant performs what Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, commanded of obedience—his action is in accord (wafqan) with the command of Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, and the servant is called successful (muwaffaqan). And when the servant intends to enter into something of the acts of disobedience to Allah—and Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, intervenes between him and that act of disobedience so that he abandons it—his abandoning it is by the success (tawfiq) of Allah, the Exalted. But whenever He leaves him to the act of disobedience and does not intervene between him and it—until he commits it—then He has forsaken him (khadhalahu), not helped him, and not granted him success."

I say: The summary of his explanation (peace be upon him) is that His success (توفيق) and His forsaking (خذلان) are among His active attributes (صفاته الفعلية). Success is His ordering of causes such that they lead the servant to a righteous deed, or His not bringing about some of the causes that are used for disobedience. Forsaking is the opposite of that. Accordingly, the object of success is the causes, because it is the bringing about of harmony (tawafuq) between them, and they are what are characterized by it. As for describing the servant with it, it is of the type of describing a state by the state of its related object.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu Nu‘aym narrated in al-Hilyah from ‘Ali, who said: I said: O Messenger of Allah, advise me. He said: "Say: 'My Lord is Allah,' then remain steadfast." I said: "My Lord is Allah, and my success is only through Allah—in Him I have put my trust and to Him I turn." He said: "May knowledge be pleasant for you, O Abu al-Hasan—you have indeed drunk knowledge in gulps and quenched your thirst with it."

I say: A portion of the meaning of this sentence has already been pointed out.

In the same source, al-Wahidi and Ibn ‘Asakir narrated from Shaddad ibn Aws, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him) wept out of love for Allah until he became blind—so Allah restored his sight to him. Then Allah revealed to him: 'O Shu‘ayb, what is this weeping? Is it out of longing for Paradise or fear of the Fire?' He replied: 'No, but I have bound Your love to my heart, so if I look upon You, I do not care what You do with me.' Then Allah revealed to him: 'O Shu‘ayb, if that be true—then congratulations to you for meeting Me. O Shu‘ayb, for that reason I have made Moses ibn ‘Imran, My interlocutor, your servant.'"

I say: What is intended by "looking upon Him," the Exalted, is the looking of the heart, not sensory looking which necessitates corporeality—Exalted is He above that. This has been clarified in the interpretation of His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمَّا جاءَ مُوسى لِمِيقاتِنا — "When Moses arrived at Our tryst" (7:143) in the eighth volume of this book.

In the same source, Abu al-Shaykh narrated from ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib (رضي الله عنه) that he delivered a sermon and recited this verse regarding Shu‘ayb: وَإِنَّا لَنَراكَ فِينا ضَعِيفاً — "and we indeed see you to be weak among us." He said: "He was blind, so they attributed weakness to him." وَلَوْ لا رَهْطُكَ لَرَجَمْناكَ — "‘and were it not for your clan, we would have surely stoned you’." ‘Ali said: "By Allah, besides Whom there is no other god, they did not fear the majesty of their Lord—they feared only the clan."

( A Discussion on the Story of Shu‘ayb and His People in the Quran in Chapters )

1 — He (peace be upon him) is the third of the messengers from the Arabs whose names are mentioned in the Quran, who are Hud, Salih, Shu‘ayb, and Muhammad (peace be upon them). Allah, the Exalted, mentioned parts of his stories in the Surahs of al-A‘raf, Hud, al-Shu‘ara’, al-Qasas, and al-‘Ankabut.

He (peace be upon him) was from the people of Madyan—a city on the road to Syria from the Peninsula—and he was a contemporary of Moses (peace be upon him). He married one of his two daughters to him on the condition that he work for him for eight years, and if he completed ten, it would be of his own accord (28:27). So Moses served him for ten years, then bid him farewell and traveled with his family to Egypt.

His people from the inhabitants of Madyan used to worship idols. They were a people blessed with security, prosperity, fertility, and low prices. Thus, corruption spread among them, as did defrauding by reducing the measure and the balance (11:84 and others). So Allah sent Shu‘ayb to them and commanded him to forbid them from idol worship, from corruption in the land, and from reducing the measure and the balance. He called them to what he was commanded and exhorted them with warning and glad tidings, reminding them of what befell the people of Noah, the people of Hud, the people of Salih, and the people of Lot.

He (peace be upon him) went to great lengths in arguing with them and exhorting them, but it only increased them in rebellion, disbelief, and transgression (al-A‘raf, Hud, and other Surahs). Only a few of them believed in him, so they began to harm them, mock them, and threaten them against following Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him). They used to sit on every path, threatening and barring from the path of Allah those who believed in Him, seeking to make it crooked (7:86).

They began to accuse him (peace be upon him) of being bewitched and of being a liar (26:185, 186). They frightened him with stoning and threatened him and those who believed with him with expulsion from their town, or that they must return to their creed (7:88). They persisted with him until he despaired of their faith, so he left them to themselves (11:93) and called upon Allah for judgment, saying: "Our Lord! Judge between us and our people with justice, and You are the best of judges" (7:89).

So Allah sent down upon them the punishment of the day of the cloud (الظلة) — the day of the overshadowing gloom that brought forth a scorching fire — فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَخَذَهُمْ عَذَابُ يَوْمِ الظُّلَّةِ ۚ إِنَّهُ كَانَ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ "But they impugned him, so the punishment of the day of the cloud overtook them. Indeed it was the punishment of a terrible day" (26:189). They had been mocking him, saying: "Then cause fragments of the sky to fall on us, if you are truthful." Then the Cry (الصيحة) — the celestial blast of divine wrath — overtook them (11:94), as did the earthquake (الرجفة) (7:91, 29:37), and they lay lifeless prostrate in their dwellings. Allah delivered Shu‘ayb and those who believed with him (11:94). فَتَوَلَّىٰ عَنْهُمْ وَقَالَ يَا قَوْمِ لَقَدْ أَبْلَغْتُكُمْ رِسَالَاتِ رَبِّي وَنَصَحْتُ لَكُمْ ۖ فَكَيْفَ آسَىٰ عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ كَافِرِينَ "So he left them, and said, ‘O my people! I certainly delivered to you the messages of my Lord and advised you. So how should I grieve for a faithless lot?’" (7:93).

2 – His Spiritual Personality: He (عليه السلام) was among the company of the honored messengers, and Allah the Exalted included him in the beautiful praise with which He lauded them in His Book. Allah has narrated from him—especially in his discourses with his people in the chapters of al-A‘raf, Hud, and al-Shu‘ara’—a great deal of the realities of divine gnosis (المعارف), sciences (العلوم الإلهية), and exquisite refinement (الأدب البارع) toward his Lord and toward people.

He referred to himself as "the trustworthy messenger" (26:178), a "reformer" (11:88), and as being "among the righteous" (26:27); Allah narrated these descriptions from him as a form of endorsement (إمضاء). Furthermore, the Interlocutor (الكليم) — the one who spoke directly with Allah — Moses son of ‘Imran (عليه السلام), served him for nearly ten years, peace be upon him.

3 – His Mention in the Torah: The Torah does not relate his story with his people; it only refers to him within the narrative of Moses killing the Egyptian and his flight from Egypt to Midian. It names him "Reuel, the priest of Midian." (1)

(1) Exodus, Chapter 2, from the Torah.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ (٩٦)
Certainly We sent Moses with Our signs and a manifest authority (96)
إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦ فَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَ فِرْعَوْنَ ۖ وَمَآ أَمْرُ فِرْعَوْنَ بِرَشِيدٍ (٩٧)
to Pharaoh and his elite, but they followed Pharaoh’s dictates, and Pharaoh’s dictates were not right-minded. (97)
يَقْدُمُ قَوْمَهُۥ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ ٱلنَّارَ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْوِرْدُ ٱلْمَوْرُودُ (٩٨)
On the Day of Resurrection he will lead his people and conduct them into the Fire: an evil goal for the incoming! (98)
وَأُتْبِعُوا۟ فِى هَـٰذِهِۦ لَعْنَةً وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ بِئْسَ ٱلرِّفْدُ ٱلْمَرْفُودُ (٩٩)
They are pursued by a curse in this [world], as well as on the Day of Resurrection; evil is the award conferred [upon them]! (99)

(Commentary)

This is an allusion to the story of Moses (موسى) — the one to whom Allah spoke (الكليم) — peace be upon him (عليه السلام). He is the most frequently mentioned of the prophets in the Qur’an; he is mentioned by name in one hundred and thirty-odd places across some thirty-odd chapters. The Qur’an has paid more attention to the details of his story than any other, yet the Exalted has summarized the account in this chapter, contenting Himself with a brief allusion to it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِآيَاتِنَا وَسُلْطَانٍ مُبِينٍ "Certainly We sent Moses with Our signs and a manifest authority" (11:96). The preposition bi (ب) in His saying بِآيَاتِنَا (with Our signs) denotes accompaniment; that is, We sent Moses accompanied by Our signs. This is because the prophets and messengers whom Allah sent and supported with miraculous signs (الآيات المعجزة) fall into two categories. One category consists of those who were given a miraculous sign according to what their people proposed, such as Salih (صالح), peace be upon him (عليه السلام), who was supported by the sign of the she-camel. The other category consists of those supported by signs at the very beginning of their mission, such as Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad, peace be upon them (عليه السلام). As the Exalted said, addressing Moses (عليه السلام): اذْهَبْ أَنْتَ وَأَخُوكَ بِآيَاتِي "Go, you and your brother, with My signs" (20:42). Regarding Jesus (عليه السلام), He said: وَرَسُولًا إِلَىٰ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ أَنِّي قَدْ جِئْتُكُمْ بِآيَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ "and [as] an apostle to the Children of Israel, [saying]: 'I have certainly brought you a sign from your Lord...'" (3:49). Regarding Muhammad, may Allah bless him and his family and grant them peace (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), He said: هُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُ بِالْهُدَىٰ "It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance" (61:9). Guidance (الهدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — here refers to the Qur’an, as evidenced by His saying: ذَٰلِكَ الْكِتَابُ لَا رَيْبَ ۛ فِيهِ ۛ هُدًى لِلْمُتَّقِينَ "This is the Book, there is no doubt in it, a guidance to the God-conscious" (2:2). The Exalted also said: وَاتَّبَعُوا النُّورَ الَّذِي أُنْزِلَ مَعَهُ "and follow the light that has been sent down with him" (7:157).

Thus, Moses (عليه السلام) was sent with signs and a manifest authority. It is evident that these signs (الآيات) refer to the supernatural phenomena (الأمور الخارقة) that occurred at his hand, as indicated by the context of his stories in the Noble Qur’an.

As for authority (السلطان) — the decisive proof and conclusive argument that dominates minds and understandings — it encompasses both the miraculous sign and the rational argument. If taken in this sense, its conjunction with "signs" would be an instance of conjoining the general (العام) to the specific (الخاص).

It is not improbable that being sent with "manifest authority" means that Allah, the Glorified, gave him mastery over the prevailing circumstances between him and the House of Pharaoh — that tyrannical oppressor (الجبار الطاغي) whose like no messenger was tested with except Moses (عليه السلام). Yet Allah the Exalted granted Moses victory over him until He drowned him and his hosts and delivered the Children of Israel by his hand. This meaning is suggested by His saying: قَالَا رَبَّنَا إِنَّنَا نَخَافُ أَنْ يَفْرُطَ عَلَيْنَا أَوْ أَنْ يَطْغَىٰ قَالَ لَا تَخَافَا ۖ إِنَّنِي مَعَكُمَا أَسْمَعُ وَأَرَىٰ "They said, 'Our Lord! We are indeed afraid that he will forestall us or become [more] rebellious.' He said, 'Do not be afraid, for I am indeed with you, and I hear and see'" (20:45-46), and His saying to Moses (عليه السلام): لَا تَخَفْ إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْأَعْلَىٰ "Do not be afraid, for you will indeed have the upper hand" (20:68).

In this verse and its counterparts, there is a clear indication that the mission of Moses (عليه السلام) was not restricted to his people from the Children of Israel, but rather encompassed them and others.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَئِهِ فَاتَّبَعُوا أَمْرَ فِرْعَوْنَ ۖ وَمَا أَمْرُ فِرْعَوْنَ بِرَشِيدٍ "to Pharaoh and his elite, but they followed Pharaoh’s dictates, and Pharaoh’s dictates were not right-minded" (11:97). Attributing his mission to Pharaoh and his elite (الملأ) — the nobles and dignitaries of the people who fill hearts with awe — rather than all his people, is perhaps to indicate that the commoners were merely followers who had no opinion except what their dignitaries saw for them.

Regarding His saying: فَاتَّبَعُوا أَمْرَ فِرْعَوْنَ (but they followed Pharaoh’s dictates), it appears that "dictate" or "command" (الأمر) here is more general than mere speech, encompassing both word and deed. This is similar to what Allah narrated from Pharaoh in his saying: قَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ مَا أُرِيكُمْ إِلَّا مَا أَرَىٰ وَمَا أَهْدِيكُمْ إِلَّا سَبِيلَ الرَّشَادِ "Pharaoh said, 'I only show you what I see [to be right], and I only guide you to the way of rectitude'" (40:29). Thus, it applies to the tradition (السنة) and the path (الطريقة) that he adopted and commanded. It is as if the verse is a counter-statement to this claim of Pharaoh, whereby Allah the Exalted belied him by saying: وَمَا أَمْرُ فِرْعَوْنَ بِرَشِيدٍ (and Pharaoh’s dictates were not right-minded).

The term rashid (الرشيد) is a paradigm of fa'il derived from rushd (الرشد) — rectitude and right-mindedness — which is the opposite of error (الغي). That is, Pharaoh’s command was not possessed of rectitude such that it could lead to the truth; rather, it was possessed of error and ignorance. It has also been said that rashid means "guiding" (المرشد).

In this sentence — namely, وَمَا أَمْرُ فِرْعَوْنَ بِرَشِيدٍ — the manifest noun (الظاهر) is placed where a pronoun (المضمر) would normally be; the original structure would be "his command" (أمره). Perhaps the benefit in this is that the name "Pharaoh" itself serves as evidence for the lack of rectitude in the command, a nuance that could never be conveyed by a pronoun.

His saying, the Exalted: يَقْدُمُ قَوْمَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ النَّارَ ۖ وَبِئْسَ الْوِرْدُ الْمَوْرُودُ "On the Day of Resurrection he will lead his people and conduct them into the Fire: an evil place to be conducted to!" (11:98). That is, Pharaoh will lead his people because they followed his command; thus, he became an imam for them among the leaders of misguidance. The Exalted said: وَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَدْعُونَ إِلَى النَّارِ "We made them leaders who invite to the Fire" (28:41).

His saying: فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ النَّارَ (and conduct them into the Fire) is a consequence of the preceding; meaning, he leads them and thus conducts them into the Fire. The use of the past tense (الماضي) is to denote the certainty of its occurrence (تحقق الوقوع). It has also been suggested that it is a consequence of His saying: فَاتَّبَعُوا أَمْرَ فِرْعَوْنَ (but they followed Pharaoh’s dictates); meaning, they followed him, and that following conducted them into the Fire. To support this meaning, some have cited His saying: وَحَاقَ بِآلِ فِرْعَوْنَ سُوءُ الْعَذَابِ النَّارُ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْهَا غُدُوًّا وَعَشِيًّا ۖ وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ أَدْخِلُوا آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ أَشَدَّ الْعَذَابِ "and a terrible punishment beset Pharaoh’s clan: the Fire, to which they are exposed morning and evening; and on the day when the Hour sets in [it will be said], 'Admit Pharaoh’s clan into the severest punishment!'" (40:45-46). These verses indicate their punishment from the moment of death before the Day of Resurrection. However, it is not hidden that the verses clearly indicate the opposite of what was argued, as they describe the punishment before the Day of Resurrection as "exposure" (العرض) morning and evening, while on the Day of Resurrection it is "entry" (الدخول) into the severest punishment, which is recorded therein as being the Fire.

Regarding His saying: وَبِئْسَ الْوِرْدُ الْمَوْرُودُ (an evil place to be conducted to!), the term wird (الورد) refers to the water that thirsty animals or humans approach for drinking. Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "The origin of wurud (الورود) is the seeking of water, then it is used for other things. It is said: 'I reached (وردت) the water,' and I am the one reaching (وارد), and the water is the place reached (مورود). The camels were brought to the water. He said: وَلَمَّا وَرَدَ مَاءَ مَدْيَنَ 'And when he arrived at the well of Midian...' (28:23). The wird is the water prepared for being reached." [End of quote].

Based on this, there is a subtle metaphor (استعارة لطيفة) in the speech, comparing the ultimate goal that a person seeks in life through their exerted efforts to the water sought by a thirsty person. Thus, the sweetness of the happiness that a person seeks through their deeds is a watering place (ورد) they reach. The ultimate happiness of man is the pleasure of Allah and Paradise. However, when they went astray by following Pharaoh’s command and missed the path of true happiness, their goal was transformed into the Fire. Thus, the Fire became the watering place they reach — and an evil watering place it is! For a watering place (الورد) is that which extinguishes the burning of the chest and quenches the thirsty core; it is sweet water and a pleasant, easy-flowing source. But if it is transformed into the punishment of the Fire, then it is an evil watering place to be conducted to.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأُتْبِعُوا فِي هَٰذِهِ لَعْنَةً وَيَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ ۚ بِئْسَ الرِّفْدُ الْمَرْفُودُ "They were pursued by a curse in this [world], and [they will be] on the Day of Resurrection; evil is the award they will be awarded!" (11:99). That is, they followed Pharaoh’s command, so they were pursued by a curse (لعنة) from Allah in this world — a distancing from His mercy and an expulsion from the court of His proximity. The manifestation of the curse that pursued them was the drowning, or it is the decree from the Exalted to distance them from mercy, written in the records of their deeds, the effects of which include the drowning and the punishment of the Hereafter.

His saying: وَيَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ ۚ بِئْسَ الرِّفْدُ الْمَرْفُودُ (and on the Day of Resurrection; evil is the award they will be awarded!). The term rifd (الرفد) means a gift (العطية), and its linguistic root means assistance (العون). A gift is called rifd or marfud because it assists the recipient in their needs. The meaning is: evil is their award on the Day of Resurrection, which is the Fire in which they are fueled. This verse is similar to His saying in another place: وَأَتْبَعْنَاهُمْ فِي هَٰذِهِ الدُّنْيَا لَعْنَةً ۖ وَيَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ هُمْ مِنَ الْمَقْبُوحِينَ "We made a curse to follow them in this world, and on the Day of Resurrection they will be among the loathed" (28:42).

It is also possible to take "the Day of Resurrection" (يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ) as an adverbial qualification (ظرف), making the verse connected to His saying أُتْبِعُوا (they were pursued) or to His saying لَعْنَةً (a curse), similar to His saying فِي هَٰذِهِ (in this [world]). The meaning would then be: Allah pursued them in the world and the Hereafter with a curse, or Allah pursued them with the curse of the world and the Hereafter. Then a new sentence begins: "Evil is the award they will be awarded," referring to the curse that pursued them or the pursuit itself with the curse.

Completed, and praise be to Allah.

Ayahs

ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلْقُرَىٰ نَقُصُّهُۥ عَلَيْكَ ۖ مِنْهَا قَآئِمٌ وَحَصِيدٌ (١٠٠)
These are from the accounts of the townships, which We recount to you. Of them, there are some that still stand, and some that have been mown down. (100)
وَمَا ظَلَمْنَـٰهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ ۖ فَمَآ أَغْنَتْ عَنْهُمْ ءَالِهَتُهُمُ ٱلَّتِى يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ لَّمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ ۖ وَمَا زَادُوهُمْ غَيْرَ تَتْبِيبٍ (١٠١)
We did not wrong them, but they wronged themselves. When your Lord’s edict came, their gods whom they would invoke besides Allah were of no avail to them in any wise, and they did not increase them in anything but ruin. (101)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَخْذُ رَبِّكَ إِذَآ أَخَذَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ وَهِىَ ظَـٰلِمَةٌ ۚ إِنَّ أَخْذَهُۥٓ أَلِيمٌ شَدِيدٌ (١٠٢)
Such is the seizing of your Lord when He seizes the townships that are wrongdoing. Indeed His seizing is painful and severe. (102)
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّمَنْ خَافَ عَذَابَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَّجْمُوعٌ لَّهُ ٱلنَّاسُ وَذَٰلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَّشْهُودٌ (١٠٣)
There is indeed a sign in that for those who fear the punishment of the Hereafter. That is a day on which all mankind will be gathered, and it is a day witnessed [by all creatures]. (103)
وَمَا نُؤَخِّرُهُۥٓ إِلَّا لِأَجَلٍ مَّعْدُودٍ (١٠٤)
And We do not defer it but for a determinate term. (104)
يَوْمَ يَأْتِ لَا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۚ فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِىٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ (١٠٥)
The day it comes, no one shall speak except by His leave. [On that day,] some of them will be wretched and [some] felicitous. (105)
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ شَقُوا۟ فَفِى ٱلنَّارِ لَهُمْ فِيهَا زَفِيرٌ وَشَهِيقٌ (١٠٦)
As for the wretched, they shall be in the Fire: their lot therein will be groaning and wailing. (106)
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا مَا دَامَتِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ رَبُّكَ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ فَعَّالٌ لِّمَا يُرِيدُ (١٠٧)
They shall remain in it for as long as the heavens and the earth endure—except what your Lord may wish; indeed your Lord does whatever He desires. (107)
۞ وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ سُعِدُوا۟ فَفِى ٱلْجَنَّةِ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا مَا دَامَتِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ رَبُّكَ ۖ عَطَآءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ (١٠٨)
As for the felicitous, they will be in paradise. They will remain in it for as long as the heavens and the earth endure—except what your Lord may wish—an endless bounty. (108)

(Commentary)

In these verses, there is a return to the preceding stories from a universal perspective (بمنظر كلي) that summarizes the custom (سنة) of Allah regarding His servants, and the consequence of polytheism (الشرك) in unjust nations—namely, destruction in this world and eternal punishment in the Hereafter—so that those who take heed may find a lesson therein.

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ مِنْ أَنْباءِ الْقُرى نَقُصُّهُ عَلَيْكَ مِنْها قائِمٌ وَحَصِيدٌ "That is from the news of the towns which We relate to you; of them some are standing and some mown down" (11:100). The word "that" (ذلك) refers to the stories mentioned previously. The preposition "from" (من) is partitive (تبعيضية), meaning: that which We have related to you is some of the news of the cities and lands, or their inhabitants, which We relate to you.

His saying: مِنْها قائِمٌ وَحَصِيدٌ "of them some are standing and some mown down." Harvesting (الحصد) is the cutting of crops; He likened the towns to crops that are either standing (قائم) or harvested (حصيد). The meaning—if "towns" refers to the physical places themselves—is that among the towns whose news We have related to you are those that are still standing, whose remaining traces that point to them have not entirely vanished, such as the towns of the people of Lot at the time their story was revealed in the Quran, as He said: وَلَقَدْ تَرَكْنا مِنْها آيَةً بَيِّنَةً لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ "Certainly We have left of it a manifest sign for a people who exercise their reason" (29:35), and He said: وَإِنَّكُمْ لَتَمُرُّونَ عَلَيْهِمْ مُصْبِحِينَ وَبِالَّيْلِ أَفَلا تَعْقِلُونَ "Indeed you pass by them at dawn and at night. Do you not exercise your reason?" (37:137-138). And among them are those whose traces have been erased and whose landmarks have been obliterated, such as the towns of the people of Noah and ‘Ad.

If "towns" refers to their inhabitants, then the meaning is that among those nations and generations are those who are "standing," whose root was not entirely cut off, such as the nations of Noah and Salih; and among them are those whose root Allah cut off, such as the people of Lot, from whom none were saved except the household of Lot, and Lot was not originally of them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما ظَلَمْناهُمْ وَلكِنْ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ "We did not wrong them, but they wronged themselves" (11:101) to the end of the verse. That is, We did not wrong them in sending down the punishment upon them and destroying them following their polytheism (الشرك) and transgression (الفسوق); rather, they wronged themselves when they associated partners with Allah and departed from the state of servitude (زي العبودية). Whenever there is an act and a penalty for it, one of the two must be an injustice (ظلم)—either the act or the penalty. Since the penalty was not an injustice, the injustice was the act itself which necessitated the penalty.

The gist of the matter is that We punished them because of their injustice; therefore, He followed it with His saying: فَمَا أَغْنَتْ عَنْهُمْ آلِهَتُهُمُ "so their gods... did not avail them" (11:101) etc. This is because the logical structure is: We seized them, and their gods did not avail them. Thus, the consequence is derived from what is indicated by His saying: وَما ظَلَمْناهُمْ "We did not wrong them" etc. The meaning is: We seized them, and their gods—whom they used to invoke besides Allah to bring them good and repel evil from them—did not suffice them. They availed them nothing when the command of your Lord and His judgment to seize them arrived, or when the punishment of your Lord came.

His saying: وَما زادُوهُمْ غَيْرَ تَتْبِيبٍ "and they did not increase them in anything but ruin." Ruin (التتبيب) is destruction and annihilation, derived from al-tabb (التب), which originally means cutting off. This is because their worship of idols was a sin necessitating their punishment; when they felt the punishment and misery, they sought refuge in the idols and invoked them to remove it. Their invocation was another sin that increased the intensity of the punishment upon them and the severity of the penalty for them; thus, they increased them in nothing but ruin.

Attributing ruin (التتبيب) to their gods is metaphorical (مجاز); in reality, it is attributed to their act of invoking them, which is an act performed by the invoker, not the one invoked.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذلِكَ أَخْذُ رَبِّكَ إِذا أَخَذَ الْقُرى وَهِيَ ظالِمَةٌ إِنَّ أَخْذَهُ أَلِيمٌ شَدِيدٌ "Such is the seizing of your Lord when He seizes the towns while they are wrongdoing. Indeed His seizing is painful and severe" (11:102).

The reference is to the previously mentioned news of the towns. That is one of the instances (مصاديق) of His seizing by punishment, by which He compared His absolute seizing of towns in its being painful and severe. This is of the nature of universalizing a judgment (التشبيه الكلي) based on some of its instances to indicate that the judgment is general and encompasses all individuals; this is a common type of rhetorical comparison. His saying: إِنَّ أَخْذَهُ أَلِيمٌ شَدِيدٌ "Indeed His seizing is painful and severe" is an explanation of the point of resemblance (وجه الشبه), which is pain and severity.

The meaning is: just as Allah, the Glorified, seized these unjust nations—the people of Noah, Hud, Salih, Lot, Shu‘ayb, and the people of Pharaoh—with a painful and severe seizing, so too does He seize all other unjust towns when He seizes them. Therefore, let those who take heed learn from this.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِمَنْ خافَ عَذابَ الْآخِرَةِ "There is indeed a sign in that for him who fears the punishment of the Hereafter" (11:103) to the end of the verse. The reference is to what Allah has related of the stories of those unjust towns which He seized for their injustice with a painful and severe seizing. He informed that His seizing is always thus. In that is a sign (آية) for him who fears the punishment of the life to come, and a mark (علامة) indicating that Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, will seize the criminals in the Hereafter for their crimes, and that His seizing will be painful and severe. This necessitates that one takes heed and guards against that which entails the wrath of Allah, the Exalted.

His saying: ذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَجْمُوعٌ لَهُ النَّاسُ "That is a day on which mankind shall be gathered" (11:103). That is, that day in which the punishment of the Hereafter occurs is a day for which mankind shall be gathered. The reference is to the day indicated by the mention of the punishment of the Hereafter, "and for this reason the masculine pronoun was used," as has been said. It is also possible that the masculine gender of the demonstrative pronoun is used to match the predicate (الخبر) with the subject (المبتدأ).

Describing the Last Day as being "gathered for it" (مجموع له) rather than saying "mankind will be gathered" (سيجمع) or "mankind are gathered" (يجمع) is to indicate that the gathering of people for it is one of its necessary attributes (أوصافه المقضية) that is inseparable from it, without needing to be reported as a separate piece of news.

The distinguishing feature of this day is that people are gathered for its sake—the "for" (اللام) denoting purpose (للغاية). Thus, the Day has a status that is not complete except by the gathering of all people, such that not one of them is left behind nor does anyone fail to attend. And the people have a status in which every individual is linked to the whole, where the first is blended with the last and the last with the first, and the whole is mixed with the part and the part with the whole. This status is the accounting of their deeds in terms of faith (الإيمان) and disbelief (الكفر), obedience (الطاعة) and disobedience (المعصية)—in short, in terms of felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاوة).

For it is clear that a single deed from a single human being draws from all his previous deeds linked to his internal states, and all his subsequent deeds—also linked to his heart's states—draw from it. Likewise, the deed of one individual in relation to the deeds of his fellow humans in terms of influence and being influenced; and likewise, the deeds of the predecessors in relation to the deeds of the successors, and the deeds of the successors in relation to the deeds of the predecessors. Among the predecessors are the leaders of guidance and misguidance who are questioned about the deeds of the successors, and among the successors are the followers and subordinates who are questioned about the deception of their preceding leaders. The Exalted said: فَلَنَسْئَلَنَّ الَّذِينَ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَنَسْئَلَنَّ الْمُرْسَلِينَ "We will surely question those to whom [Our messengers] were sent, and We will surely question the messengers" (7:6), and He said: وَنَكْتُبُ ما قَدَّمُوا وَآثارَهُمْ وَكُلَّ شَيْءٍ أَحْصَيْناهُ فِي إِمامٍ مُبِينٍ "We write down what they have sent ahead and their tracks, and We have counted everything in a manifest leader" (36:12).

Then the recompense (الجزاء) does not fail the decisive judgment (الحكم الفصل).

This status, in this manner, is not complete except by the gathering of all people such that no one is excluded.

From here it becomes clear that the questioning of individuals in their graves and their recompense therein with some reward or punishment—as indicated by the verses of the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — and mentioned in detail in the narrations from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام—is distinct from what Allah has informed regarding the accounting of the Day of Resurrection and the recompense decreed there of eternal Paradise or Fire. For what awaits a person in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) is the questioning to complete the record of his deeds to be stored for the Day of Decisive Judgment. The Paradise or Fire in which one resides in the intermediate realm is like a preliminary lodging for the guest prepared for the meeting and the judgment. It is not a complete accounting, nor a final judgment, nor a definitive recompense, as indicated by verses such as: النَّارُ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْها غُدُوًّا وَعَشِيًّا وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ أَدْخِلُوا آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ أَشَدَّ الْعَذابِ "The Fire, to which they are exposed morning and evening; and on the day when the Hour sets in: ‘Admit Pharaoh’s clan into the severest punishment!’" (40:46), and His saying: يُسْحَبُونَ فِي الْحَمِيمِ ثُمَّ فِي النَّارِ يُسْجَرُونَ "they are dragged into the boiling water, then they are kindled in the Fire" (40:71-72). You see the verse expresses their punishment as being "exposed" to the Fire, then on the Day of Resurrection by "entering" it, which is the severest punishment; and it expresses their punishment by being "dragged" in boiling water, then by being "kindled" in the Fire, which is burning. And the Exalted said: بَلْ أَحْياءٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ فَرِحِينَ بِما آتاهُمُ اللهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ وَيَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِالَّذِينَ لَمْ يَلْحَقُوا بِهِمْ مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ أَلَّا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ "rather they are alive with their Lord, provided for, rejoicing in what Allah has given them out of His grace, and they rejoice for those who have not yet joined them from [those left] behind them, that they shall have no fear, nor shall they grieve" (3:169-170). This verse is explicit regarding the world of the grave (عالم القبر) and does not mention an accounting or the eternal Paradise; it only mentions a form of enjoyment in general.

And the Exalted said: حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ قالَ رَبِّ ارْجِعُونِ لَعَلِّي أَعْمَلُ صالِحاً فِيما تَرَكْتُ كَلَّا إِنَّها كَلِمَةٌ هُوَ قائِلُها وَمِنْ وَرائِهِمْ بَرْزَخٌ إِلى يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ "When death comes to one of them, he says, ‘My Lord! Take me back, that I may act righteously in what I have left behind.’ ‘By no means! These are mere words that he says.’ And before them is a barrier until the day they will be resurrected" (23:99-100). The verse mentions that after death they are in a barzakh-life (حياة برزخية) intermediate between the worldly life—which is play and diversion—and the life of the Hereafter, which is the true life, as He said: وَما هذِهِ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوانُ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ "The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play, but the abode of the Hereafter is indeed Life (itself), had they known" (24:64).

In summary, the world is the abode of action (دار عمل), the intermediate realm (البرزخ) is the abode of preparation for accounting and recompense, and the Hereafter is the abode of accounting and recompense (دار حساب وجزاء). The Exalted said: يَوْمَ لا يُخْزِي اللهُ النَّبِيَّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ نُورُهُمْ يَسْعى بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمانِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنا أَتْمِمْ لَنا نُورَنا وَاغْفِرْ لَنا "the day Allah will not let down the Prophet and the faithful who are with him. Their light will move in front of them and to their right, and they will say, ‘Our Lord! Perfect our light for us, and forgive us’" (66:8). They attend it with the light they earned in the world and prepared in the intermediate realm (البرزخ), then they ask Him on the Day of Resurrection to perfect their light and remove the remnants of the world of diversion and play.

His saying: وَذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَشْهُودٌ "and that is a day witnessed" (11:103) appears to follow from the previous sentence: ذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَجْمُوعٌ لَهُ النَّاسُ "That is a day on which mankind shall be gathered," since gathering necessitates witnessing. However, the word is not restricted to mankind; its absolute use (إطلاقه) suggests it is witnessed by everyone capable of witnessing, such as humans, angels, and jinn. The numerous verses indicating the gathering of jinn and devils and the presence of angels there support the absolute sense of "witnessed" as mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما نُؤَخِّرُهُ إِلَّا لِأَجَلٍ مَعْدُودٍ "And We do not delay it except for a numbered term" (11:104). That is, for that day there is a term (أجل) decreed by Allah that it shall not occur before its arrival. Allah judges, and there is none to reverse His judgment nor to repel His decree. He does not delay the Day except for a term that He counts; when the count is complete and the term arrives, the word is fulfilled and the Day occurs.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ يَأْتِ لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ "The day it comes, no soul shall speak except by His leave" (11:105). The subject of "comes" (يأتي) is a pronoun referring to the previously mentioned "term" (الأجل). That is, the day the term arrives—to which the Resurrection is delayed—no soul shall speak except by His leave. The Exalted said: مَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا لِقاءَ اللهِ فَإِنَّ أَجَلَ اللهِ لَآتٍ "Whoever expects to encounter Allah [should know that] Allah’s [appointed] time will indeed come" (29:5).

Some have mentioned, as in al-Majma‘, that the meaning is: the day the Resurrection and recompense come. This necessitates referring the pronoun to the Resurrection and recompense because the preceding speech points to them in a way; however, this is an unnecessary affectation (تكلف).

Others have mentioned—as in the Tafsir of the author of al-Manar—that the meaning is: at the time when that specific day arrives, no speaking soul shall speak except by the leave of Allah, the Exalted. Thus, "day" (يوم) in the verse refers to absolute time (مطلق الوقت)—i.e., the undefined—because it is the vessel for the defined day described by what was mentioned, which is the subject of "comes."

This is an error, as it necessitates the day being a vessel for itself (ظرفية اليوم لليوم), for the meaning would essentially return to our saying: "in the time when that specific time arrives" or "the day when that specific day arrives." Distinguishing between the two "days" by making one specific and defined and the other general and undefined does not help in repelling the impossibility of a thing being its own vessel, or time—which is a vessel by its nature—being a vessel for another time. This is an impossibility that does not become possible by changing the wording.

What he mentioned regarding the distinction between the two days by being absolute or defined is mere conceptualization that avails nothing. For the day in which that described day arrives and that described day itself are equal in terms of being absolute, defined, breadth, and narrowness. Yes, sometimes time is taken as identical to the events occurring within it, thus becoming one of the events and its vessel-nature is ignored, making it a content (مظروف) for another time, as when it is said: "the Day of Sacrifice is in the month of Dhu al-Hijjah" or "the Day of ‘Ashura is in Muharram." The Exalted said: وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ "And on the day when the Hour sets in" (45:27). If this consideration is valid in the verse, it would be possible for the pronoun of "comes" to refer to the Day.

His saying: لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ "no soul shall speak except by His leave." That is, no soul among those present shall speak except by the leave of Allah, the Glorified. The deletion of one of the two ta's (تاءين) that meet in the future tense of the tafa‘‘ul (تفعل) form is common and regular.

The preposition "by" (بـ) in His saying: بِإِذْنِهِ "by His leave" denotes accompaniment (المصاحبة). Thus, the exception is in reality from the speech, not from the speaker, as in His saying: لا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمنُ "they do not speak, except him whom the All-beneficent has permitted" (78:38). The meaning is: no soul shall speak any speech except with the speech that accompanies His leave—unlike the world, where one speaks what he chooses and intends, whether Allah has permitted it by legislative leave (إذن تشريع) or not.

This attribute—namely, the lack of any soul speaking except by His leave—has been mentioned as one of the defining characteristics of the Day of Resurrection. However, it is not exclusive to it, for no soul ever speaks, nor does any event ever occur, except by His leave, without this being exclusive to the Day of Resurrection.

It has been previously mentioned in some of our earlier discussions that most of what is mentioned in the Noble Quran as defining characteristics of the Day of Resurrection, in the context of attributes specific to it, actually encompasses it and other times. For example, His saying: يَوْمَ هُمْ بارِزُونَ لا يَخْفى عَلَى اللهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْءٌ "the day when they will emerge: nothing about them will be hidden from Allah" (40:16), and His saying: يَوْمَ تُوَلُّونَ مُدْبِرِينَ ما لَكُمْ مِنَ اللهِ مِنْ عاصِمٍ "the day when you will turn back to flee, with no one to protect you from Allah" (40:33), and His saying: يَوْمَ لا تَمْلِكُ نَفْسٌ لِنَفْسٍ شَيْئاً وَالْأَمْرُ يَوْمئِذٍ لِلَّهِ "the day when no soul will be able to do anything for another soul, and the command that day will belong to Allah" (82:19), among other verses. It is well known that nothing is ever hidden from Him, and nothing ever has a protector from Him, and no soul ever possesses anything for another soul except by His leave, and to Him belong creation and command always.

However, that which contemplation (التدبر) leads to in verses such as: لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ "You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today" (50:22), and His saying relating the words of the criminals: رَبَّنا أَبْصَرْنا وَسَمِعْنا فَارْجِعْنا نَعْمَلْ صالِحاً إِنَّا مُوقِنُونَ "Our Lord! We have seen and heard. So send us back [to the world] that we may act righteously. Indeed we [now] have certainty" (32:12), and His saying: وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعاً ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا مَكانَكُمْ أَنْتُمْ وَشُرَكاؤُكُمْ فَزَيَّلْنا بَيْنَهُمْ "On the day We will muster them all together, then We will say to the polytheists, ‘Stay where you are, you and your partners!’ Then We will separate them from each other"—until He says—هُنالِكَ تَبْلُوا كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما أَسْلَفَتْ وَرُدُّوا إِلَى اللهِ مَوْلاهُمُ الْحَقِّ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ "There every soul will examine what it has sent ahead, and they will be returned to Allah, their true Master, and what they used to fabricate will forsake them" (10:28-30)—is that the Day of Resurrection is a vessel in which Allah gathers the servants and removes the cover and veil from them. Thus, realities appear therein with complete clarity, and that which is behind the veil of the Unseen (الغيب) in this life is revealed. At that point, no doubt or suspicion will stir in their breasts, nor will any thought occur to their hearts; they will witness that Allah is the Manifest Truth (الحق المبين), and they will see that all power belongs to Allah, and that sovereignty, protection, command, and subjugation belong to Him alone, without any partner.

Causes (الأسباب) will fall away from the independence that was imagined for them in the life of the world; the connection will be severed, and the links of influence between things will vanish. At that point, the stars of causes will be scattered, and the stars by which delusions were guided in their darkness will be obliterated. No possessor of sovereignty will have a sovereignty by which he is independent, nor will any possessor of power have that with which he is emboldened. Nothing will have a refuge or sanctuary to flee to, nor a protection to be guarded by, nor a veil to hide anything from another. The command will be entirely for Allah, the One, the All-paramount; none possesses anything but He (1).

This is the meaning of His saying: يَوْمَ هُمْ بارِزُونَ لا يَخْفى عَلَى اللهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْءٌ "the day when they will emerge: nothing about them will be hidden from Allah", and His saying: ما لَكُمْ مِنَ اللهِ مِنْ عاصِمٍ "with no one to protect you from Allah", and His saying: يَوْمَ لا تَمْلِكُ نَفْسٌ لِنَفْسٍ شَيْئاً وَالْأَمْرُ يَوْمئِذٍ لِلَّهِ "the day when no soul will be able to do anything for another soul, and the command that day will belong to Allah", and other such verses. They all negate what the delusions of people decorate in this worldly life—which is nothing but diversion and play—namely, that these causes possess the meaning of influence, and are clothed with the attributes of sovereignty, power, protection, might, and honor in a real, independent manner, and that they are the givers, withholders, benefactors, and harmers, with no goal beyond them and no good besides them.

From here, one can find familiarity with the meaning of His saying: يَوْمَ يَأْتِ لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ "The day it comes, no soul shall speak except by His leave." This meaning has been repeated in other verses with similar wording, such as: لا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمنُ وَقالَ صَواباً "they do not speak, except him whom the All-beneficent has permitted and who says what is right" (78:38), and His saying: هذا يَوْمُ لا يَنْطِقُونَ "This is a day they will not speak" (77:35).

This is because Allah, the Exalted, says in describing this day: يَوْمَ تُبْلَى السَّرائِرُ "the day when the secrets are examined" (86:9), and He says: إِنْ تُبْدُوا ما فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحاسِبْكُمْ بِهِ اللهُ "Whether you reveal what is in your souls or hide it, Allah will bring you to account for it" (2:284). Thus, He explains that the accounting on that day is based on what is in the souls of states and accidents (الأحوال والأعراض), whether good or evil, not by what is discovered from them through the means of discovery as in this worldly life.

So, that which was under the veils of secrecy in the world—the hidden things of the souls and the folded contents of the hearts—is manifest and uncovered on the Day of Resurrection. What is "unseen" (غيب) today is "witnessed" (شهادة) tomorrow. The speaking (التكلم) that we humans practice among ourselves is only through the use of composed sounds that indicate, by way of convention and agreement (الوضع والاعتبار), meanings that reside in our inner selves. The motive for us to establish and practice it is the social need for some of us to be guided to what is in the minds of others, due to the impossibility of the senses perceiving it.

Speaking is one of the social causes (الأسباب الاجتماعية) we use to reveal the hidden meanings in the mind; it is predicated on what is in the minds being outside the direct perception of a human being. If we were aided by a sense that could reach mental meanings and witness them—just as, for example, sight reaches light and colors, and touch reaches heat, cold, roughness, and smoothness—we would not need to establish languages and speak them, nor would there be among us what is called a "word" or "speech." Likewise, if the human species lived its worldly life in an individualistic rather than social manner, there would be no news of speech, nor would its seed have been formed.

All of this is because the worldly life is composed of the witnessed (شهادة) and the unseen (غيب)—that which is sensed and witnessed, and that which is beyond the senses. People are in dire need of revealing and knowing the intentions in their minds. If a realm of life were assumed to be purely witnessed, composed of witnessed matters, there would be nothing in it necessitating speaking and speech. If we were to apply the term "speech" to any of the states existing there, its instance (مصداق) would be the appearance of some of what is in the souls of people to others and the awareness of those others of it.

This realm described as such is the realm of Resurrection (نشأة القيامة), according to what Allah, the Glorified, describes in verses like: يَوْمَ تُبْلَى السَّرائِرُ "the day when the secrets are examined." This is what appears from His saying: لا يُسْئَلُ عَنْ ذَنْبِهِ إِنْسٌ وَلا جَانٌّ "On that day neither man nor jinn will be questioned about his sin"—until He says—يُعْرَفُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ بِسِيماهُمْ فَيُؤْخَذُ بِالنَّواصِي وَالْأَقْدامِ "The guilty will be recognized by their marks; so they will be seized by their forelocks and their feet" (55:39-41).

If you say: "Based on this, there is no meaning for the occurrence of lying and falsehood there, yet the Noble Quran has explicitly mentioned it," as in His saying: وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعاً ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا أَيْنَ شُرَكاؤُكُمُ الَّذِينَ كُنْتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ ثُمَّ لَمْ تَكُنْ فِتْنَتُهُمْ إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا وَاللهِ رَبِّنا ما كُنَّا مُشْرِكِينَ انْظُرْ كَيْفَ كَذَبُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ "On the day We muster them all together, We will say to those who ascribed partners [to Allah], ‘Where are your partners whom you used to claim?’ Then their only excuse will be to say, ‘By Allah, our Lord, we were not polytheists.’ See how they lie against themselves" (6:22-24), and His saying: يَوْمَ يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللهُ جَمِيعاً فَيَحْلِفُونَ لَهُ كَما يَحْلِفُونَ لَكُمْ وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ عَلى شَيْءٍ أَلا إِنَّهُمْ هُمُ الْكاذِبُونَ "The day Allah resurrects them all together, they will swear to Him as they swear to you, and they will think that they are on some [solid] footing. Look! They are indeed the liars" (58:18).

I say: This is from the manifestation of ingrained dispositions (الملكات). Just as a human being, when thinking, witnesses the hidden parts of his soul without needing to inform himself of what he is thinking or reveal what is in his mind to himself through speaking—because he is in a state of witnessing (شهادة) his inner self, not in the unseen (غيب)—he nevertheless conceives an image of speech that indicates the mental meanings he is observing. He may even speak with his tongue what occurs to his mind of the parts of the thought, and the motive for this is what he has become accustomed to of speaking and uttering when he expresses what is in his mind to others.

These polytheists and hypocrites, having become accustomed to lying in their worldly life and having lived on the lies of delusion, this manifests from them on the day when dispositions and psychological habits manifest. Otherwise, it is impossible for a human being to stand before his Lord—while He, the Exalted, witnesses his inner and outer self, his deeds are present, his record is spread open, the witnesses are standing, his limbs are speaking of what they did, and causes (including lying) have fallen away and perished—and then lie in the hope of deceiving Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, prevailing over Him with a deceptive, false argument, and being saved thereby.

This is similar to their being called on the Day of Resurrection to prostrate and then being unable to do so. The Exalted said: يَوْمَ يُكْشَفُ عَنْ ساقٍ وَيُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ خاشِعَةً أَبْصارُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ وَقَدْ كانُوا يُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ وَهُمْ سالِمُونَ "The day the catastrophe occurs and they are summoned to prostrate themselves, they will not be able [to do it]. Their looks will be humbled, and abasement will overcome them. They were summoned to prostrate themselves while they were yet sound" (68:42-43). Their inability to prostrate is only due to the rootedness of the disposition of arrogance (ملكة الاستكبار) in their souls. If it were due to a new prevention from His side, the Exalted, they would have an argument against Him.

If you say: "If it were as you mentioned, and there were no need for speaking there nor any instance for it, then what is the meaning of the exception in His saying: لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ "no soul shall speak except by His leave" and similar verses? And what is the meaning of what is repeated in various places of His speech relating their sayings?"

I say: There is no doubt that a human being, while in this life, is an agent with choice (مختار) in his actions, among which is speaking. He has an equal relation to every one of his acts and its omission; both are equal in relation to him. When he commits the act, for example, one of the two sides becomes determined by necessity (تعيناً اضطرارياً), and there is no news of choice after that. The necessary effects that follow the act—among which is the recompense earned by the act—have the same status as the act after its realization.

The realm of the Hereafter is the abode of recompense (دار جزاء), not the abode of action (دار عمل). Thus, there is no news of human choice there; there is only the human being and his deed which he brought and which has become necessarily inseparable from him, and that which the deed is linked to of records and witnesses, and his Lord to Whom the matter returns and in Whose hand is the Decisive Judgment. When he is called, he responds by necessity, as the Exalted said: يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَّبِعُونَ الدَّاعِيَ لا عِوَجَ لَهُ "On that day they will follow the caller in whom there is no crookedness" (20:108), whereas they used to be called to the truth in the world and would not respond. When he speaks in response to a question, it is not of the same nature as worldly speaking which originated from his choice and revealed something hidden in his soul. For his mouth has been sealed and he has no way to speak what he wants, how he wants. The Exalted said: الْيَوْمَ نَخْتِمُ عَلى أَفْواهِهِمْ وَتُكَلِّمُنا أَيْدِيهِمْ وَتَشْهَدُ أَرْجُلُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ "Today We seal their mouths, and their hands speak to Us, and their feet bear witness to what they used to earn" (36:65). And He said: هذا يَوْمُ لا يَنْطِقُونَ وَلا يُؤْذَنُ لَهُمْ فَيَعْتَذِرُونَ "This is a day they will not speak, nor will they be permitted to offer excuses" (77:35-36). For an excuse (العذر) only exists in a recompense where there is a trace of choice, and for whose realization there is the possibility of existence or non-existence. As for the evil deed that is finished and the recompense that follows it by necessity, there is no room for an excuse therein. The Exalted said: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لا تَعْتَذِرُوا الْيَوْمَ إِنَّما تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ "O you who disbelieve! Do not make any excuses today. You are only being requited for what you used to do" (66:7). That is, your recompense is the very deed you performed; it does not change by an excuse or a pretext. It would only change if it were a worldly recompense whose matter was in the hand of the judging requiter who chooses what he sees and wills.

In summary: if he speaks in response to a question, his speaking is out of necessity and corresponds to the manifest deed he has, which has no cover over it there at all. If he speaks a lie, it is of the nature of the manifestation of dispositions, as previously mentioned, and one of his deeds that manifests as a manifestation, not as speech considered an answer to a question. Thus, his mouth is sealed, and his hearing, sight, skin, hand, and foot are made to speak; the deed he performed is brought forth, the witnesses are summoned, and Allah is a witness over all things.

It has been summarized from all we have presented that the meaning of His saying: لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ "no soul shall speak except by His leave" is that speaking on that day is not in the manner of worldly speaking—as an intentional revelation of what is in the mind such that the speaker can be truthful or lie. For this intentional possession (التملك الاختياري), which is among the concomitants of the abode of action, is removed there. Thus, the human being has no choice in his speaking; rather, it is contingent upon the leave and will of Allah. If you contemplate well, you will find that the outcome of this view—namely, the removal of the rule of choice from human speaking and all other actions, and the encompassing of the meaning of necessity (الاضطرار) over everything on that day—returns to what we opened the discussion with: that the characteristic of this day is the uncovering of the realities of things therein and the return of the Unseen (الغيب) to the Witnessed (شهادة). You must firmly contemplate the knowledge that the Divine Speech imparts regarding the Return (المعاد), for it is a difficult, intricate, and deep matter.

Some have mentioned that the meaning of His saying: لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ "no soul shall speak except by His leave" is that they do not speak therein except with good speech permitted by the Law (شرعاً), because people are compelled there to abandon foul things, so no foul thing occurs from them; as for what is not foul, it is permitted.

In this is the objection that it is a specification without a specifier (تخصيص من غير مخصص). The Day is not a day of action such that leave is given for performing a good act and not for a foul one. The compulsion (الإلجاء) whose source is that the realm is one of recompense, not action, does not distinguish between a good and a foul act, given that both are intentional, because "good" and "foul" are only applied to intentional acts.

Furthermore, Allah, the Exalted, says: هذا يَوْمُ لا يَنْطِقُونَ وَلا يُؤْذَنُ لَهُمْ فَيَعْتَذِرُونَ "This is a day they will not speak, nor will they be permitted to offer excuses." It is well known that offering excuses is not a foul act in any way.

Others have said: The meaning of the verse is that no one speaks in the Hereafter with beneficial speech—such as intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — or a means—except by His leave.

This reduces the verse in terms of its indication to verses like: يَوْمَئِذٍ لا تَنْفَعُ الشَّفاعَةُ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمنُ "Intercession will not avail that day except from him whom the All-beneficent has permitted" (20:109). In this is the objection that it is a restriction without evidence. If that were intended, the speech would properly have been: "no soul shall speak for a soul or regarding a soul except by His leave," as occurred in its counterpart: لا تَمْلِكُ نَفْسٌ لِنَفْسٍ شَيْئاً "no soul will be able to do anything for another soul."

From what we have presented, the way to reconcile the verses affirming speaking on the Day of Resurrection and the verses negating it has been obtained.

To clarify: the verses dealing with the issue of speaking therein are of two types: one type negates speaking or affirms it for individuals without exception, such as: لا يُسْئَلُ عَنْ ذَنْبِهِ إِنْسٌ وَلا جَانٌّ "neither man nor jinn will be questioned about his sin" (55:39), and His saying: يَوْمَ تَأْتِي كُلُّ نَفْسٍ تُجادِلُ عَنْ نَفْسِها "The day when every soul will come pleading for itself" (16:111).

The other type negates speech of any kind, whether truthful or false, such as: هذا يَوْمُ لا يَنْطِقُونَ "This is a day they will not speak" (77:35), and His saying: فَما لَنا مِنْ شافِعِينَ وَلا صَدِيقٍ حَمِيمٍ "Now we have no intercessors, nor any sympathetic friend" (26:100-101).

The first type is reconciled between its two sides by verses like: لا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمنُ "they do not speak, except him whom the All-beneficent has permitted" (78:38). The second type has the contradiction between its sides removed by the verse under discussion: يَوْمَ يَأْتِ لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ "The day it comes, no soul shall speak except by His leave"—but based on what was previously clarified regarding the meaning of speaking being contingent upon His leave, such that it implies they are compelled in what they speak, forced into what Allah, the Glorified, permits.
