For this reason, you see that in most instances where He mentions their difference in the Book, He follows it with their doubt (ارتياب) concerning it, such as His saying in the verses mentioned previously: وَلَقَد آتَينا موسَى الكِتابَ فَاختُلِفَ فيهِ وَلَولا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَت مِن رَبِّكَ لَقُضِيَ بَينَهُم وَإِنَّهُم لَفي شَكٍّ مِنهُ مُريبٍ "Certainly We gave Moses the Book, but differences arose concerning it; and had it not been for a prior decree from your Lord, the matter would have been decided between them. Indeed they are in a grave doubt concerning it." (11:110). He has repeated this meaning in several places in His speech.

The Exalted also said: عَمَّ يَتَساءَلُونَ عَنِ النَّبَإِ الْعَظِيمِ الَّذِي هُمْ فِيهِ مُخْتَلِفُونَ "What is it that they are questioning each other about? About the great news, concerning which they differ." (78:1-3). That is, each of them brings a statement that distances them from the truth, so they divide. And He said: إِنَّكُمْ لَفِي قَوْلٍ مُخْتَلِفٍ يُؤْفَكُ عَنْهُ مَنْ أُفِكَ قُتِلَ الْخَرَّاصُونَ "Indeed you are in a discordant view. He who is perverted is perverted from it. Perish the liars!" (51:7-10). That is, a view that does not stand upon a single face nor is it built upon knowledge; rather, it is conjecture (الخرص) and opinion that has produced it among you.

In this same vein is the saying of the Exalted: يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ لِمَ تَلْبِسُونَ الْحَقَّ بِالْباطِلِ وَتَكْتُمُونَ الْحَقَّ وَأَنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ "O People of the Book! Why do you confound the truth with falsehood, and conceal the truth while you know [it]?" (3:71). This censured confounding (اللبس) on their part was by manifesting a statement that resembles the truth but is not; it is the casting of division by which the truth is hidden.

Thus, the intended meaning of their "difference" is their invention of statements and opinions by which they diverge from the truth and through which doubt appears. Because of their following of whims that oppose the truth, they manifest their false opinions in various forms that simulate the form of truth, in order to veil it from people's understanding out of rebellion and enmity after they had known the truth. Thus, it is their difference regarding the truth after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves.

It becomes clear from what has preceded at length that the indication in His saying: وَلِذلِكَ خَلَقَهُمْ "and for that He created them" (11:119) is to the "mercy" (الرحمة) indicated by His saying: "except those on whom your Lord has mercy." The grammatical femininity of the word "mercy" (الرحمة) does not contradict the masculinity of the demonstrative pronoun (ذلك) because the verbal noun (المصدر) allows for both genders. The Exalted said: إِنَّ رَحْمَتَ اللهِ قَرِيبٌ مِنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ "Indeed Allah’s mercy is close to the virtuous." (7:56). This is because you have realized that this difference is a rebellion on their part that divides them from the truth, conceals it, and manifests falsehood. It is not permissible for falsehood to be a true objective (غاية حقيقية) for the Truth, the Exalted, in His creation. There is no sense in Allah, the Glorified, bringing the human world into existence so that they may rebel, kill the truth, and revive falsehood, only for Him to destroy them and then punish them with an eternal fire. The Noble Quran refutes this with all its explanations.

Furthermore, the context of the verses — even disregarding what has been mentioned — refutes that [other interpretation]. For they are in the position of explaining that Allah, the Exalted, calls people through His kindness and mercy to that which contains their good and happiness, without wanting any injustice or evil for them. However, through their injustice and their difference regarding the truth, they disdain His call, deny His signs, worship other than Him, and spread corruption on the earth, thereby deserving punishment. "Your Lord would never destroy the towns unjustly while their people were righteous," nor would He create them to rebel and corrupt so that He might destroy them. Thus, that which is from Him is mercy and guidance, while that which results from their rebellion, difference, and injustice returns to themselves. This is what the context of the verses provides.

The fact that mercy — meaning guidance — is an intended goal in creation is only because of its connection to the ultimate goal, which is happiness (السعادة), as in His saying recounting the words of the people of Paradise: وَقالُوا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي هَدانا لِهذا "and they will say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who guided us to this’" (7:43). This is similar to considering worship (العبادة) as a goal for creation because of its connection to happiness in His saying: وَما خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ "I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me." (51:56).

His saying: وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَةُ رَبِّكَ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَالنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ "and the word of your Lord has been fulfilled: ‘I will surely fill hell with jinn and humans, all together.’" (11:119). That is, His word, the Exalted, became due and found its realization (مصداقها) among them because of their injustice and their difference regarding the truth after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves. The "word" (الكلمة) is His saying: "I will surely fill hell..." etc.

The verse is similar to His saying: وَلَوْ شِئْنا لَآتَيْنا كُلَّ نَفْسٍ هُداها وَلكِنْ حَقَّ الْقَوْلُ مِنِّي لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَالنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ "Had We wished, We would have given every soul its guidance, but My word became due: ‘I will surely fill hell with jinn and humans, all together.’" (32:13). The origin of this word is what Allah, the Exalted, cast to Iblis — may Allah curse him — when he said: فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ قالَ فَالْحَقُّ وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنْكَ وَمِمَّنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ "He said, ‘By Your might, I will surely pervert them all, except Your devoted servants among them.’ He said, ‘The truth is—and the truth I say—I will surely fill hell with you and with those of them who follow you, all together.’" (38:82-85). These verses are identical in content, each explaining the other.

This is the summary of what contemplation provides regarding the meaning of the two verses, and it has been concluded thereby:

First: That the intended meaning of His saying: "Had your Lord wished, He would have made mankind one community" is their unification by removing division and difference from among them. It has been said that the meaning is compelling them to Islam and removing free will (الاختيار), but this contradicts religious obligation (التكليف), which is why He did not do it; this is attributed to Qatadah. It has also been said: The meaning is that had He wished, He would have gathered you in Paradise, but He wanted the higher of the two ranks for you so that you might enter it through acquisition as a reward for your deeds; this is attributed to Abu Muslim. You are aware that the context of the verses does not support either of these two meanings.

Second: That the intended meaning of His saying: "but they continue to differ" is their persistence in difference regarding religion, which means diverging from the truth and veiling it by depicting it in various false forms that resemble the truth. Some have said: It is the difference in provisions and circumstances — in short, non-religious difference; this is attributed to al-Hasan. You have already seen that this is foreign to the context of the preceding verses. Others said: The meaning of "differing" (مختلفين) is that some of them succeed others in imitating their predecessors and engaging in their falsehood; this, like the one before it, is foreign to the course of the verses, which include His saying: "Certainly We gave Moses the Book, but differences arose concerning it..." the verse.

Third: That the intended meaning of His saying: "except those on whom [your Lord] has mercy" is except those whom Allah has guided from among the believers.

Fourth: That the indication in His saying: "and for that He created them" is to mercy, which is the goal Allah intended for His creation so that they might achieve their happiness thereby. Some mentioned that the meaning is He created them for difference; this is attributed to al-Hasan and ‘Ata’. You have already seen that this is very weak and poor. Yes, if it were permissible for the pronoun in "created them" to refer to the remaining people after the exception, it would be possible to count difference as a goal for their creation, and the verse would be close in content to His saying: وَلَقَدْ ذَرَأْنا لِجَهَنَّمَ كَثِيراً مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ "Certainly We have winnowed out for hell many of the jinn and humans..." (7:179).

Others mentioned: The indication is to the sum of what the discourse points to regarding His will, the Exalted, in creating them with the aptitude for difference and division in their sciences, cognitions, opinions, and perceptions, and what follows that of their will and choice in their actions; among these are religion, faith, obedience, and disobedience. In short, the goal is absolute difference, whether in religion or otherwise.

This is attributed to Ibn Abbas based on what is narrated from him that he said: "He created them as two groups: a group He has mercy upon, so they do not differ, and a group He does not have mercy upon, so they differ." It is also attributed to Malik ibn Anas when he said regarding the meaning of the verse: "He created them so that there would be a group in Paradise and a group in the Blaze." You have already seen the aspects of weakness in this, so we shall not prolong by repeating.

Fifth: That the intended meaning of the "fulfillment of the word" is its realization and its finding of its manifestation.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding the saying of the Exalted: وَإِنَّ كُلًّا لَمَّا لَيُوَفِّيَنَّهُمْ رَبُّكَ "Indeed your Lord will pay each of them in full [the reward of] their deeds" (11:111), he said: He (peace be upon him) said: "On the Day of Resurrection."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from al-Hasan who said: "When this verse was revealed: فَاسْتَقِمْ كَما أُمِرْتَ وَمَنْ تابَ مَعَكَ 'So be steadfast as you have been commanded—[you] and whoever has turned [to Allah] with you' (11:112), he [the Prophet] said: 'Prepare yourselves, prepare yourselves!' and he was never seen laughing [after that]."

In al-Majma‘, regarding the saying of the Exalted: فَاسْتَقِمْ كَما أُمِرْتَ (11:112), Ibn Abbas said: "No verse was revealed to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that was harder upon him or more difficult than this verse. For this reason, he said to his companions when they said to him: 'Gray hair has come quickly to you, O Messenger of Allah': 'Surah Hud and al-Waqi‘ah have made me gray.'"

I say: The hadith is famous with some variations in wording: "Hud and its sisters have made me gray." The fact that al-Waqi‘ah does not contain what corresponds to His saying: "So be steadfast as you have been commanded" makes it unlikely that it is the point of reference in the hadith. That which is common between the two surahs is the account of the Resurrection; and Allah knows best.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding the saying of the Exalted: وَلا تَرْكَنُوا "And do not incline..." (11:113), he said: He (peace be upon him) said: "An inclination of affection, advice, and obedience."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Majma‘ as a disconnected report (مرسلاً) from them (peace be upon them).

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Uthman ibn Isa, from a man, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): وَلا تَرْكَنُوا إِلَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا فَتَمَسَّكُمُ النَّارُ "Do not incline toward the wrongdoers, lest the Fire should touch you" (11:113). He said: "Behold, He did not make it eternal [punishment], but said: 'lest the Fire should touch you,' so do not incline toward them."

I say: Meaning, rather He said: "the Fire should touch you," so He made it a "touching" (مساً).

In the same source, from Jarir, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: أَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ طَرَفَيِ النَّهارِ "Maintain the prayer at the two ends of the day" (11:114) — and its two ends are the Sunset (المغرب) and the Morning (الغداة) — وَزُلَفاً مِنَ اللَّيْلِ "and during the early hours of the night" (11:114) — and it is the late Night prayer (العشاء الآخرة).

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain to Zurarah, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him): In a hadith regarding the five daily prayers: "And the Exalted said regarding that: أَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ طَرَفَيِ النَّهارِ وَزُلَفاً مِنَ اللَّيْلِ and it is the late Night prayer (العشاء الآخرة)."

I say: The hadith is not without an apparent indication in interpreting the "two ends of the day" as that which is before noon and that which is after it, so as to include the times of the five prayers.

In al-Ma‘ani, with its chain to Ibrahim ibn Umar, from one who narrated to him, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): Regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: إِنَّ الْحَسَناتِ يُذْهِبْنَ السَّيِّئاتِ "Indeed good deeds efface misdeeds" (11:114). He said: "The prayer of the believer at night effaces the sins he committed during the day."

I say: The hadith is narrated in al-Kafi, Tafsir al-Ayyashi, Amali al-Mufid, and Amali al-Shaykh.

In al-Majma‘, from al-Wahidi, with his chain to Hammad ibn Salamah, from Ali ibn Zayd, from Abu Uthman, who said: "I was with Salman under a tree, and he took a dry branch from it and shook it until its leaves fell off. Then he said: 'O Abu Uthman, will you not ask me why I did this?' I said: 'And why did you do it?' He said: 'This is how the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) did it while I was with him under a tree; he took a dry branch from it and shook it until its leaves fell off. Then he said: "Will you not ask me, O Salman, why I did this?" I said: "And why did you do it?" He said: "Indeed, when a Muslim performs ablution (الوضوء) and performs it well, then prays the five prayers, his sins fall away just as these leaves fell away." Then he recited this verse: وَأَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ to its end.'"

I say: It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Tayalisi, Ahmad, al-Darimi, Ibn Jarir, al-Tabarani, al-Baghawi in his Mu‘jam, and Ibn Mardawayh, though not as a continuous chain (غير مسلسل).

In the same source, from him, with his chain to al-Harith, from Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him), who said: "We were with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the mosque waiting for the prayer. A man stood up and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, I have committed a sin.' He [the Prophet] turned away from him. When the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) finished the prayer, the man stood up and repeated his statement. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Have you not prayed this prayer with us and performed the purification well for it?' He said: 'Yes.' He said: 'Then it is the expiation for your sin.'"

I say: The narration is reported through many paths from Ibn Mas‘ud, Abu Umamah, Mu‘adh ibn Jabal, Ibn Abbas, Buraydah, Wathilah ibn al-Asqa‘, Anas, and others. There is some variation in the wording of the story's narration. It was narrated by al-Tirmidhi and others from Abu al-Yasar, who was the person in the story.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu Hamzah al-Thumali, who said: I heard one of them (peace be upon them) saying: "Ali (peace be upon him) turned to the people and said: 'Which verse in the Book of Allah is the most hopeful in your view?' Some of them said: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes" (4:48). He said: 'It is good, but it is not it.' Some of them said: يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ لا تَقْنَطُوا مِنْ رَحْمَةِ اللهِ "O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah" (39:53). He said: 'It is good, but it is not it.' Some of them said: وَالَّذِينَ إِذا فَعَلُوا فاحِشَةً أَوْ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ ذَكَرُوا اللهَ فَاسْتَغْفَرُوا لِذُنُوبِهِمْ "and those who, when they commit an indecency or wrong themselves, remember Allah and plead for forgiveness for their sins" (3:135). He said: 'It is good, but it is not it.'

He said: Then the people held back. He said: 'What is the matter with you, O assembly of Muslims?' They said: 'No, by Allah, we have nothing else.' He said: 'I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying: The most hopeful verse in the Book of Allah is: وَأَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ طَرَفَيِ النَّهارِ وَزُلَفاً مِنَ اللَّيْلِ "Maintain the prayer at the two ends of the day, and during the early hours of the night" (11:114).' He recited the entire verse and said: 'O Ali, by Him who sent me with the truth as a bearer of good news and a warner, indeed when one of you stands for his ablution, the sins fall from his limbs. When he turns with his face and heart [to Allah], he does not turn away from his prayer with any of his sins remaining upon him; he becomes as he was on the day his mother gave birth to him. If he commits something between the two prayers, he receives the like of that' — until he counted the five prayers.

Then he said: 'O Ali, the status of the five prayers for my community is like a flowing river at the door of one of you. What would one of you think if there were dirt on his body and he bathed in that river five times a day? Would any dirt remain on his body? Likewise, by Allah, are the five prayers for my community.'"

I say: The parable mentioned at the end of the hadith has been narrated through Sunni paths from several companions such as Abu Hurayrah, Anas, Jabir, and Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In the same source, from Ibrahim al-Karkhi, who said: I was with Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) when a man from the people of Medina entered upon him. Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said to him: "O so-and-so, from where have you come?" He did not say [anything] in his reply. Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "You have come from here and there. Look at what you spend your day with, for you have an appointed angel with you who preserves and writes what you do. Do not belittle a misdeed even if it is small, for it will grieve you one day. And do not belittle a righteous deed (حسنة), for there is nothing more intense in its seeking than a righteous deed — it overtakes the great, old sin, deletes it, drops it, and removes it from you. That is the saying of Allah: إِنَّ الْحَسَناتِ يُذْهِبْنَ السَّيِّئاتِ ذلِكَ ذِكْرى لِلذَّاكِرِينَ 'Indeed good deeds efface misdeeds. That is an admonition for the mindful.'" (11:114).

In the same source, from Sama‘ah ibn Mihran, who said: A man from the people of the mountains asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about a man who acquired wealth from the works of the ruler (السلطان), so he gives charity from it, maintains ties with his kin, and performs the Hajj so that what he earned might be forgiven, and he says: إِنَّ الْحَسَناتِ يُذْهِبْنَ السَّيِّئاتِ. Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "Indeed, a sin does not expiate a sin, but a righteous deed expiates a sin."

Then Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "If one mixes the lawful (الحلال) with the prohibited (حراماً) and they are completely mixed such that the lawful is not distinguished from the prohibited, then there is no harm."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad and al-Tabarani narrated from Abu Umamah who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "There is no Muslim man for whom the time of an obligatory prayer (صلاة مكتوبة) arrives, so he stands and performs ablution and performs it well, and prays and performs the prayer well, except that he is forgiven for what was between it and the prayer that was before it of his sins."

I say: The narrations in this chapter are numerous; whoever wishes to investigate them should refer to the compendiums of hadith.

In the same source, al-Tabarani, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Daylami narrated from Jarir who said: I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) being asked about the interpretation of this verse: وَما كانَ رَبُّكَ لِيُهْلِكَ الْقُرى بِظُلْمٍ وَأَهْلُها مُصْلِحُونَ (11:117). The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "And its people are fair to one another."

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Abdullah ibn Sinan, who said: Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) was asked about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: وَلَوْ شاءَ رَبُّكَ لَجَعَلَ النَّاسَ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً وَلا يَزالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ رَبُّكَ. He said: "They were one community, then Allah sent the prophets to establish the proof (الحجة) against them."

I say: Al-Saduq narrated it in al-Ma‘ani from him (peace be upon him) similarly.

In al-Ma‘ani, with its chain to Abu Basir, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَما خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ (51:56). He said: "He created them to command them to worship." He said: And I asked him about His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: وَلا يَزالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ رَبُّكَ وَلِذلِكَ خَلَقَهُمْ (11:118-119). He said: "He created them to do that which would make them deserve His mercy, so He has mercy upon them."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, from Abu al-Jarud, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), he said: وَلا يَزالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ رَبُّكَ "meaning the Progeny of Muhammad (آل محمد) and their followers. Allah says: وَلِذلِكَ خَلَقَهُمْ meaning the people of mercy do not differ in religion."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Ya‘qub ibn Sa‘id, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: I asked him about the saying of Allah: وَما خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ. He said: "He created them for worship." I said: His saying: وَلا يَزالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ رَبُّكَ وَلِذلِكَ خَلَقَهُمْ. He said: "This was revealed after that one."

I say: He is pointing to the second verse — meaning His saying: "except those on whom your Lord has mercy, and for that He created them" — being a specific verse that abrogates (ناسخة) the first general verse. It has already been mentioned in the discussion on abrogation (النسخ) that in their terminology (peace be upon them), it is broader than what the scholars of principles (علماء الأصول) have termed. The specific ontological verses (الآيات الخاصة التكوينية) are clearly dominant in their ruling over general verses, for specific factors and causes are more effective in their ruling than general ones; so understand this.

Ayahs

وَكُلًّا نَّقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلرُّسُلِ مَا نُثَبِّتُ بِهِۦ فُؤَادَكَ ۚ وَجَآءَكَ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْحَقُّ وَمَوْعِظَةٌ وَذِكْرَىٰ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٢٠)
Whatever We relate to you of the accounts of the apostles are those by which We strengthen your heart, and there has come to you in this [surah] the truth and an advice and admonition for the faithful. (120)
وَقُل لِّلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ ٱعْمَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَكَانَتِكُمْ إِنَّا عَـٰمِلُونَ (١٢١)
Say to those who do not have faith, ‘Act according to your ability; we too are acting. (121)
وَٱنتَظِرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا مُنتَظِرُونَ (١٢٢)
And wait! We too are waiting.’ (122)
وَلِلَّهِ غَيْبُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَإِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُ ٱلْأَمْرُ كُلُّهُۥ فَٱعْبُدْهُ وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَيْهِ ۚ وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (١٢٣)
To Allah belongs the Unseen of the heavens and the earth, and to Him all matters are returned. So worship Him and trust in Him. Your Lord is not oblivious of what you do. (123)

(Commentary)

The verses summarize for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) the discourse regarding the purpose of the Surah whose verses have been narrated to him. They inform him that the Surah clarifies for him the truth (الحق) concerning the Origin (المبدأ) — the source of all existence, Allah the Exalted — and the Return (المعاد) — the ultimate destination of all creation in the Hereafter, as well as the established Sunnah (سنة الله) — the habitual and unchanging way of Allah’s dealing with His creation — that governs His servants. Thus, in relation to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), it is a teaching of the truth; in relation to the believers (المؤمنين), it is an admonition (موعظة) and a reminder (ذكرى); and in relation to the disbelievers (الكافرين) who disdain faith, it is a final termination of dispute (قطع خصام). Therefore, say to them as the final point of your argumentation: "Act according to what you see fit, and we shall act according to what we see fit, and we all await the realization of what Allah has narrated to us of His established Sunnah in His creation—granting felicity to the righteous (المصلحين) and wretchedness to the corruptors (المفسدين)." The Surah concludes by commanding him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to worship Him and to place his trust in Him, for the entirety of the matter returns to Him.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكُلًّا نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ أَنْباءِ الرُّسُلِ ما نُثَبِّتُ بِهِ فُؤادَكَ — "And We relate to you every story from the news of the apostles by which We strengthen your heart" (11:120) until the end of the verse. That is, We relate all the stories to you, whether in detail or in summary. His saying: مِنْ أَنْباءِ الرُّسُلِ (from the news of the apostles) is an explanation (بيان) of that to which "every" (كل) was annexed. His saying: ما نُثَبِّتُ بِهِ فُؤادَكَ (by which We strengthen your heart) is an explanatory apposition (عطف بيان) for "the news" (الأنباء), pointing toward the benefit of the stories in relation to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), which is the strengthening of his heart and the severing of the roots of anxiety and agitation within him.

The meaning is: We relate to you the news of the messengers to strengthen your heart and to fortify your resolve in the course you are taking—the path of calling to the truth, the uprising to uproot the sources of corruption, and the endurance of the trials caused by the harm of your people.

Then, the Exalted mentions the benefit of the Surah that encompasses him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his people, both believers and disbelievers. Regarding the benefit of the Surah’s revelation for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), He says: وَجاءَكَ فِي هذِهِ الْحَقُ — "and in this there has come to you the truth" (11:120). The demonstration (الإشارة) refers either to the Surah, the verses revealed within it, or the news (الإنباء) in a certain respect. The arrival of the truth within it refers to what Allah the Exalted has clarified—within the stories and before and after them—of the realities of knowledge (حقائق المعارف) concerning the Origin and the Return, and His established Sunnah in His creation regarding the sending of messengers and the spreading of the call, followed by the felicity of the believers in this world through salvation and in the Hereafter through Paradise, and the wretchedness of the wrongdoers (الظالمين) through their seizure in this world and eternal punishment in the Hereafter.

Regarding the believers, He says: وَمَوْعِظَةٌ وَذِكْرى لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "and an advice and an admonition for the faithful" (11:120). For indeed, the realities of knowledge mentioned therein serve as a reminder (تذكرة) for the believers, by which they recall what they have forgotten of the sciences of the primordial nature (علوم الفطرة) — the innate knowledge of truth placed by Allah in the human soul — regarding the Origin, the Return, and what pertains to them. Furthermore, the stories and lessons (العبر) mentioned therein serve as an admonition (موعظة) by which they take heed.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقُلْ لِلَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ اعْمَلُوا عَلى مَكانَتِكُمْ إِنَّا عامِلُونَ وَانْتَظِرُوا إِنَّا مُنْتَظِرُونَ — "And say to those who do not believe, ‘Act according to your ability; we too are acting. And wait! We too are waiting.’" (11:121-122). This pertains to the non-believers. He commands His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to conclude the argumentation with them and terminate their dispute—after having recited the stories to them—with these sentences. He says to them: "If you will not believe and will not desist from polytheism (الشرك) and corruption despite the reminder and lessons I have presented to you, and if you will not believe what Allah has narrated of the news of the nations and informed of His established Sunnah among them, then act according to your position (المكانة) and status, and do what you deem best for yourselves; we too are acting. And wait for the consequence of your deeds that shall meet you; we too are waiting." Thus, you shall soon know the truth or falsehood of the divine report.

This is a termination of dispute and a form of threat (تهديد) that Allah presented in the previous stories—the stories of Nuh, Hud, and Salih (عليه السلام)—and in the story of Shu’ayb (عليه السلام), quoting him: وَيا قَوْمِ اعْمَلُوا عَلى مَكانَتِكُمْ إِنِّي عامِلٌ سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ يَأْتِيهِ عَذابٌ يُخْزِيهِ وَمَنْ هُوَ كاذِبٌ وَارْتَقِبُوا إِنِّي مَعَكُمْ رَقِيبٌ — "O my people! Act according to your ability; I too am acting. Soon you will know who will be overtaken by a punishment that will disgrace him, and who is a liar. And watch out! I too am watching out with you." (11:93).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِلَّهِ غَيْبُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَإِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُ الْأَمْرُ كُلُّهُ — "To Allah belongs the Unseen of the heavens and the earth, and to Him all matters are returned" (11:123). Since Allah’s command to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to tell them to act as they desire and to wait—while informing them that he and those who believe with him are also acting and waiting—carries the meaning of commanding him and his followers to act and wait, He followed it with these two sentences so that he might be of good cheer and firm of heart, knowing that the final turn of fortune (الدائرة) will be for him against them.

The meaning is: "Act and wait, you and those who follow you, for the Unseen (غيب) of the heavens and the earth—which includes the ultimate outcome of your affair and theirs—is possessed only by your Lord, who is Allah, the Glorified, and not by their deities whom they associate with Him, nor by the causes (الأسباب) upon which they rely to direct the turn of fortune for themselves and shift the outcome to their benefit. And to your Lord, who is Allah, the entirety of the matter returns; thus, He shall manifest from His Unseen the outcome of the matter according to what He willed and informed. Therefore, the final victory is yours against them." This is a remarkable mode of expression.

From here, the reason for replacing the word "your Lord" (ربك)—which was repeated in these verses—with the majestic name "Allah" (الله) becomes apparent. This is because the name "Allah" carries an intimation of encompassing (الإحاطة) everything, whether subtle or grand, in a way that no other name does. The context necessitates relying upon and seeking refuge in a sanctuary that no subduer can overcome and no victor can defeat, and that is Allah, the Glorified. For this reason, you see that after these sentences conclude, He returns to the attribute of "Lordship" (الرب) which He had been repeating, in His saying: وَما رَبُّكَ بِغافِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ — "and your Lord is not oblivious of what you do" (11:123).

His saying, the Exalted: فَاعْبُدْهُ وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَيْهِ — "So worship Him and trust in Him" (11:123). It appears that this is a derivation (تفريع) from His saying: وَإِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُ الْأَمْرُ كُلُّهُ (and to Him all matters are returned). That is, if the entirety of the matter is returned to Him, the Exalted, then no one else possesses anything nor acts independently (يستقل) in anything. Therefore, worship Him, the Glorified, and take Him as your guardian (وكيلا) in all affairs. Do not rely upon any of the causes (الأسباب) besides Him, for they are causes only through His causation and are not independent of Him. Thus, it is ignorance to rely upon any of them. وَما رَبُّكَ بِغافِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (And your Lord is not oblivious of what you do); therefore, it is not permissible to be negligent in His worship (العبادة) or in trusting (التوكل) in Him.
